I would like to see less of a fixation on Wen (2016), and more awareness of other work on NLG and DBPedia. (2) Microplanning tends to be very domain/genre dependent. For example, pronouns are used much more often in novels than in aircraft maintenance manuals. This is why so much work has focused on domain-dependent resources. So there are some real questions about whether it is possible even in theory to train a "wide-coverage microplanner". The authors do not discuss this at all; they need to show they are aware of this concern. (3) I would be concerned about the quality of the texts obtained from crowdsourcing. A lot of people dont write very well, so it is not at all clear to me that gathering example texts from random crowdsourcers is going to produce a good corpus for training microplanners. Remember that the ultimate goal of microplanning is to produce texts that are easy to *read*. Imitating human writers (which is what this paper does, along with most learning approaches to microplanning) makes sense if we are confident that the human writers have produced well-written easy-to-read texts. Which is a reasonable assumption if the writers are professional journalists (for example), but a very dubious one if the writers are random crowdsourcers. From a presentational perspective, the authors should ensure that all text in their paper meets the ACL font size criteria. Some of the text in Fig 1 and (especially) Fig 2 is tiny and very difficult to read; this text should be the same font size as the text in the body of the paper. I will initially rate this paper as borderline. I look forward to seeing the author's response, and will adjust my rating accordingly.

Title: Loss-aware Binarization of Deep Networks Authors: John Doe, Jane Smith JEL Classification: C45, C63, D81 Executive Summary: The paper presents a new binarization method for deep neural networks using a loss-aware approach. The proposed method is compared against existing binarization techniques and is shown to achieve state-of-the-art performance on various image classification tasks. The authors claim that their approach can reduce the storage and computation costs of deep neural networks while providing comparable performance to full-precision networks. Strengths: 1. The paper presents a novel binarization method that extends an existing method and achieves superior results on a number of benchmark datasets. 2. The authors provide detailed explanations of the proposed algorithms and its components, making it easy to understand and replicate their results. 3. The experiments performed in this paper are comprehensive, including a detailed comparison with existing binarization methods and a thorough analysis of different factors that can impact the performance of the proposed approach. 4. The authors demonstrate that their method outperforms several state-of-the-art methods by a significant margin on various machine learning tasks. Weaknesses: 1. While the authors provide a thorough comparison with existing binarization methods, there is no comparison with deep neural networks that use full-precision weights. This limits the conclusion that the proposed approach can provide comparable performance to full-precision networks. 2. The approach is evaluated on image classification tasks only. It would be interesting to see how it performs on other tasks such as object detection or segmentation. 3. The paper could benefit from an analysis of the computational and memory overhead associated with the proposed method. Overall Assessment: The paper provides a novel and well-explained method for binarizing deep neural networks. The authors present comprehensive experimental results and demonstrate that their approach significantly outperforms existing methods. While the paper has some weaknesses that could be addressed in future work, it makes a valuable contribution to the field of binarization of deep neural networks. Therefore, I recommend that the paper be accepted for publication after minor revisions. The paper titled "Loss-aware Binarization of Deep Networks" addresses the problem of reducing memory usage and computational complexity in deep neural networks (DNNs) while maintaining their accuracy. Specifically, the paper presents a method for binarizing the weights and activations of DNNs by minimizing the loss function with a soft-binarization approach, resulting in a more compact network. The strengths of this paper are that it proposes a novel technique for binarizing DNNs with an emphasis on loss-awareness, which allows for better accuracy retention. The authors compare their method with existing binarization techniques and demonstrate superior performance in terms of model size, speed, and accuracy on benchmark datasets. Additionally, they provide a detailed analysis of the effects of loss-aware binarization on DNNs' various layers and activation functions. However, there are also some potential weaknesses that need further clarification. First, the experiments are only conducted on a limited number of datasets, which makes it difficult to evaluate the proposed method's effectiveness on a larger scale. Also, the paper lacks a thorough discussion of the practical implications and limitations of the proposed method. Overall, the paper is well-written, and the proposed technique for loss-aware binarization of DNNs is promising. Further research in this area will undoubtedly lead to more effective and practical methods for reducing memory usage and computational complexity in deep neural networks. The paper titled "Loss-aware Binarization of Deep Networks" proposes a proximal Newton algorithm that minimizes the loss with respect to the binarized weights. The authors argue that existing binarization schemes ignore the effect of binarization on the loss and are based on simple matrix approximations. The proposed algorithm directly addresses this issue by using second-order information to obtain diagonal Hessian approximation that is more efficient than existing binarization schemes. The authors applied the proposed algorithm on both feedforward and recurrent networks and demonstrated its superiority compared to existing binarization schemes. The paper is well written and easy to follow. The motivation behind the proposed algorithm is clear and the experiments are well designed to evaluate its effectiveness. The authors provide extensive experimental results to support their claims. The paper also provides a detailed description of the proposed algorithm and the algorithm's advantages over existing binarization schemes. However, there are several areas that could be improved. First, the authors could provide more details of the experimental setup, especially the pre-processing steps and the initialization setting. Secondly, the authors could include a comparative analysis of the proposed algorithm with state-of-the-art non-binarization algorithms. Finally, the paper could be strengthened by providing more theoretical justification of the algorithm's superiority. In summary, the proposed algorithm for loss-aware binarization of deep networks is an interesting approach that addresses a fundamental issue in existing binarization schemes. The experiments demonstrate the algorithm's effectiveness, and the theoretical basis is well presented. The paper could benefit from additional details and theoretical analysis. The paper "Loss-aware Binarization of Deep Networks" proposes a novel binarization algorithm that not only reduces the size of deep neural network models but also directly minimizes the loss with respect to the binarized weights. Existing binarization schemes are based on simple matrix approximations, which ignore the effect of binarization on network loss. The authors have proposed a proximal Newton algorithm that uses a diagonal Hessian approximation to minimize the loss w.r.t. binarized weights. The underlying proximal step has an efficient closed-form solution, and second-order information is obtained from the Adam optimizer's second moments. Their proposed algorithm has been benchmarked on both feedforward and recurrent networks and outperforms existing binarization schemes. The strengths of this paper are: - The proposed algorithm addresses the existing limitations of other binarization schemes and considers the effect of binarization on network loss, which is an essential aspect previously ignored by other approaches. - The authors have also provided a thorough mathematical explanation for their proposed algorithm in the paper. - The results presented in this paper demonstrate that the proposed algorithm performs exceptionally well compared to existing binarization schemes. - They have shown that the proposed algorithm is robust for wide and deep networks, which is a significant contribution. However, there are some potential weaknesses of this paper, which can be improved in future work: - The authors have used only Adam optimizer for all of their experiments. It would have been beneficial if they had compared their proposed algorithm with other optimization methods to evaluate its performance robustness. - The authors did not provide a detailed comparison of their proposed algorithm with state-of-the-art pruning methods. Their proposed algorithm could be compared with the recently proposed neural network pruning algorithms to assess its competitiveness. - The authors have evaluated their proposed algorithm for feedforward and recurrent networks only. Extending the evaluation on more complex architectures such as convolutional neural networks and transformer models would make the findings more generalizable. Overall, this paper's contributions take a significant step towards efficient deep neural network model designing and training, and it would be interested to see continued work in this direction.

This paper presents a novel deep learning approach to keyphrase generation. The authors propose a model that leverages a hierarchical encoder-decoder architecture to generate keyphrases from a given document. The model is trained on a large corpus of documents and keyphrases to learn the keyphrase generation task. The paper is well written and the authors have done a good job of presenting the proposed model in detail. The authors have also provided a thorough evaluation of the model, comparing it to existing keyphrase generation models. The results show that the proposed model outperforms existing models, demonstrating its effectiveness. The authors have also provided a thorough discussion of the results and the implications of their work. They have discussed the limitations of their approach, as well as potential future improvements. Overall, this paper provides a novel approach to keyphrase generation and provides a thorough evaluation of the proposed model. The authors have done a good job of presenting their work and the results are impressive. I recommend this paper for publication. This paper addresses the problem of deep keyphrase generation, which is the automatic generation of keyphrases from a given document. Strengths: The paper presents a novel approach to deep keyphrase generation which uses a combination of a convolutional neural network and a recurrent neural network. The paper also provides an extensive evaluation of the proposed approach and compares it to existing methods. Weaknesses: The paper does not provide any discussion of the potential applications of the proposed approach. Additionally, the paper does not provide any discussion of the limitations of the proposed approach. This paper presents a deep learning model to generate keyphrases from text content. The proposed model is based on an encoder-decoder framework and is named deep keyphrase generation. The authors conducted empirical analysis on six datasets to demonstrate the effectiveness of the proposed model. The paper is well-written and the proposed model is clearly explained. The authors present the motivation of the paper, the proposed model and the experimental results in a logical and comprehensive way. The authors also provide the code and dataset for the proposed model. The paper is overall well-structured and the proposed model is innovative. The authors also provided a thorough evaluation on six datasets to demonstrate the effectiveness of the proposed model. There are a few areas that can be improved. First, the authors should provide more detailed analysis of the results, such as the comparison between different methods and the comparison between different datasets. Second, the authors should provide more insights into the proposed model, such as the effects of different hyper-parameters. Overall, this is an interesting paper and the proposed model has potential applications in text summarization. The authors should address the aforementioned suggestions to make the paper even better. This paper addresses the problem of automated keyphrase extraction, which is a challenge in understanding, organizing, and retrieving text content. The authors propose a generative model for keyphrase prediction with an encoder-decoder framework, which they name \textit{deep keyphrase generation}. The strengths of this paper are the proposed model and its empirical analysis on six datasets. The model is shown to be able to effectively extract keyphrases that appear in the source text, as well as generate absent keyphrases based on the semantic meaning of the text. The datasets used for the empirical analysis are also publicly available, which is a great contribution. The weaknesses of this paper are the lack of comparison to existing models and the lack of a detailed explanation of how the model works. The authors should provide more details on how the model works, as well as comparison to existing models to better demonstrate the effectiveness of the proposed model.

This paper proposed a very interesting idea of using cognitive features for sentiment analysis and sarcasm detection. More specifically, the eye-movement patterns of human annotators are recorded to derive a new set of features. The authors claim that this is the first work to include cognitive features into the NLP community. Strength: 1. The paper is generally well written and easy to follow 2. Very interesting idea which may inspire research in other NLP tasks. Weakness: 1. The motivation of using cognitive features for sentiment analysis is not very well justified. I can imagine these features may help reflect the reading ease, but I don't see why they are helpful in detecting sentiment polarities. 2. The improvement is marginal after considering cognitive features by comparing Sn+Sr+Gz with Sn+Sr. 3. Although the authors discussed about the feasibility of the approach in Section 7, but I'm not convinced, especially about the example given in section 7.2, I don't see why this technique is helpful in such a scenario. This paper is about introducing eye-tracking features for sentiment analysis as a type of cognitive feature. I think that the idea of introducing eye-tracking features as a proxy for cognitive load for sentiment analysis is an interesting one. I think the discussion on the features and comparison of feature sets is clear and very helpful. I also like that the feasibility of the approach is addressed in section 7. I wonder if it would help the evaluation if the datasets didn't conflate different domains, e.g., the movie review corpus and the tweet corpus. For one it might improve the prediction of movie review (resp. tweets) if the tweets (resp. movie reviews) weren't in the training. It would also make the results easier to interpret. The results in Table 2 would seem rather low compared to state-of-the art results for the Pang and Lee data, but look much better if compared to results for Twitter data. In Section 3.3, there are no overlapping snippets in the training data and testing data of datasets 1 and 2, right? Even if they come from the same sources (e.g., Pang & Lee and Sentiment 140). Minor: some of the extra use of bold is distracting (or maybe it's just me);

This paper presents a new semi-supervised learning method based on temporal ensembling. The method is evaluated on two real-world datasets, and the results show that it is a viable alternative to existing semi-supervised learning methods. The paper is well-written and the results are well-supported by data and analysis. The authors clearly explain the motivation for their work and the approach they take to address the problem. The results are presented in a clear and concise manner, and the paper is well-organized. Overall, this paper presents a promising new approach to semi-supervised learning that is worth further investigation. This paper addresses the problem of semi-supervised learning with temporal data. The main strength of the paper is that it proposes a new approach to this problem that uses temporal ensembles. This is a clever idea that has not been explored before. The paper is well-written and the results are interesting. However, there are some weaknesses. First, the paper does not provide a clear motivation for the problem it is trying to solve. Second, the experimental results are not strong enough to justify the claims made in the paper. Finally, the paper does not provide enough information about the proposed approach for the reader to be able to reproduce the results. This paper presents a new approach for training deep neural networks in a semi-supervised setting where only a small portion of training data is labeled. The main idea is to use self-ensembling, where the network's outputs on different epochs and under different regularization and input augmentation conditions are combined to form a consensus prediction for the unknown labels. This ensemble prediction is then used as a target for training. The paper sets new records for two standard semi-supervised learning benchmarks, reducing the (non-augmented) classification error rate from 18.44% to 7.05% in SVHN with 500 labels and from 18.63% to 16.55% in CIFAR-10 with 4000 labels, and further to 5.12% and 12.16% by enabling the standard augmentations. The paper also demonstrates good tolerance to incorrect labels. This paper addresses the problem of training deep neural networks (DNNs) in a semi-supervised setting where only a small portion of training data is labeled. The paper presents a simple and efficient method for training DNNs using self-ensembling, where a consensus prediction of the unknown labels is formed using the outputs of the network-in-training on different epochs, and under different regularization and input augmentation conditions. The ensemble prediction can be expected to be a better predictor for the unknown labels than the output of the network at the most recent training epoch, and can thus be used as a target for training. Using the proposed method, the paper sets new records for two standard semi-supervised learning benchmarks, reducing the (non-augmented) classification error rate from 18.44% to 7.05% in SVHN with 500 labels and from 18.63% to 16.55% in CIFAR-10 with 4000 labels, and further to 5.12% and 12.16% by enabling the standard augmentations. The paper additionally obtains a clear improvement in CIFAR-100 classification accuracy by using random images from the Tiny Images dataset as unlabeled extra inputs during training. Finally, the paper demonstrates good tolerance to incorrect labels. Strengths: The paper presents a simple and efficient method for training DNNs in a semi-supervised setting where only a small portion of training data is labeled. The use of self-ensembling, where a consensus prediction of the unknown labels is formed using the outputs of the network-in-training on different epochs, and under different regularization and input augmentation conditions, is a clever approach that is not widely used in the literature. The paper sets new records for two standard semi-supervised learning benchmarks, reducing the (non-augmented) classification error rate from 18.44% to 7.05% in SVHN with 500 labels and from 18.63% to 16.55% in CIFAR-10 with 4000 labels, and further to 5.12% and 12.16% by enabling the standard augmentations. The improvement over the previous state-of-the-art is clear, and the results are well-supported with extensive experimental validation. Weaknesses: There are a few weaknesses in the paper. First, the experimental results are not well-explained. It is not clear why the proposed method works well, and it is not clear how the results compare to other semi-supervised learning methods. Second, the experimental setup is not well-controlled. There are a number of parameters that are not well-defined, and it is not clear how the results vary with different parameter settings. Finally, the paper does not discuss the practical implications of the proposed method, and it is not clear how the method could be used to improve the state-of-the-art in semi-supervised learning.

The paper is clearly written, and the claims are well-supported. The Related Work in particular is very thorough, and clearly establishes where the proposed work fits in the field. I had two main questions about the method: (1) phrases are mentioned in section 3.1, but only word representations are discussed. How are phrase representations derived? (2) There is no explicit connection between M^+ and M^- in the model, but they are indirectly connected through the tanh scoring function. How do the learned matrices compare to one another (e.g., is M^- like -1*M^+?)? Furthermore, what would be the benefits/drawbacks of linking the two together directly, by enforcing some measure of dissimilarity? Additionally, statistical significance of the observed improvements would be valuable. Typographical comments: - Line 220: "word/phase pair" should be "word/phrase pair" - Line 245: I propose an alternate wording: instead of "entities are translated to," say "entities are mapped to". At first, I read that as a translation operation in the vector space, which I think isn't exactly what's being described. - Line 587: "slightly improvement in F-measure" should be "slight improvement in F-measure" - Line 636: extraneous commas in citation - Line 646: "The most case" should be "The most likely case" (I'm guessing) - Line 727: extraneous period and comma in citation - Strengths: 1. Interesting research problem 2. The method in this paper looks quite formal. 3. The authors have released their dataset with the submission. 4. The design of experiments is good. - Weaknesses: 1. The advantage and disadvantage of the transductive learning has not yet discussed. - General Discussion: In this paper, the authors introduce a transductive learning approach for Chinese hypernym prediction, which is quite interesting problem. The authors establish mappings from entities to hypernyms in the embedding space directly, which sounds also quite novel. This paper is well written and easy to follow. The first part of their method, preprocessing using embeddings, is widely used method for the initial stage. But it's still a normal way to preprocess the input data. The transductive model is an optimization framework for non-linear mapping utilizing both labeled and unlabeled data. The attached supplementary notes about the method makes it more clear. The experimental results have shown the effectiveness of the proposed method in this paper. The authors also released dataset, which contributes to similar research for other researchers in future.

- Strengths: - this article puts two fields together: text readability for humans and machine comprehension of texts - Weaknesses: - The goal of your paper is not entirely clear. I had to read the paper 4 times and I still do not understand what you are talking about! - The article is highly ambiguous what it talks about - machine comprehension or text readability for humans - you miss important work in the readability field - Section 2.2. has completely unrelated discussion of theoretical topics. - I have the feeling that this paper is trying to answer too many questions in the same time, by this making itself quite weak. Questions such as “does text readability have impact on RC datasets” should be analyzed separately from all these prerequisite skills. - General Discussion: - The title is a bit ambiguous, it would be good to clarify that you are referring to machine comprehension of text, and not human reading comprehension, because “reading comprehension” and “readability” usually mean that. - You say that your “dataset analysis suggested that the readability of RC datasets does not directly affect the question difficulty”, but this depends on the method/features used for answer detection, e.g. if you use POS/dependency parse features. - You need to proofread the English of your paper, there are some important omissions, like “the question is easy to solve simply look..” on page 1. - How do you annotate datasets with “metrics”?? - Here you are mixing machine reading comprehension of texts and human reading comprehension of texts, which, although somewhat similar, are also quite different, and also large areas. - “readability of text” is not “difficulty of reading contents”. Check this: DuBay, W.H. 2004. The Principles of Readability. Costa Mesa, CA: Impact information. - it would be good if you put more pointers distinguishing your work from readability of questions for humans, because this article is highly ambiguous. E.g. on page 1 “These two examples show that the readability of the text does not necessarily correlate with the difficulty of the questions” you should add “for machine comprehension” - Section 3.1. - Again: are you referring to such skills for humans or for machines? If for machines, why are you citing papers for humans, and how sure are you they are referring to machines too? - How many questions the annotators had to annotate? Were the annotators clear they annotate the questions keeping in mind machines and not people?

The paper proposes an approach to sequence labeling with multitask learning, where language modeling is uses as the auxiliary objective. Thus, a bidirectional neural network architecture learns to predict the output labels as well as to predict the previous or next word in the sentence. The joint objectives lead to improvements over the baselines in grammatical error detection, chunking, NER, and POS tagging. - Strengths: The contribution is quite well-written and easy to follow for the most part. The model is exposed in sufficient detail, and the experiments are thorough within the defined framework. The benefits of introducing an auxiliary objective are nicely exposed. - Weaknesses: The paper shows very limited awareness of the related work, which is extensive across the tasks that the experiments highlight. Tables 1-3 only show the three systems proposed by the contribution (Baseline, +dropout, and +LMcost), while some very limited comparisons are sketched textually. A contribution claiming novelty and advancements over the previous state of the art should document these improvements properly: at least by reporting the relevant scores together with the novel ones, and ideally through replication. The datasets used in the experiments are all freely available, the previous results well-documented, and the previous systems are for the most part publicly available. In my view, for a long paper, it is a big flaw not to treat the previous work more carefully. In that sense, I find this sentence particularly troublesome: "The baseline results are comparable to the previous best results on each of these benchmarks." The reader is here led to believe that the baseline system somehow subsumes all the previous contributions, which is shady on first read, and factually incorrect after a quick lookup in related work. The paper states "new state-of-the-art results for error detection on both FCE and CoNLL-14 datasets". Looking into the CoNLL 2014 shared task report, it is not straightforward to discern whether the latter part of the claim does holds true, also as per Rei and Yannakoudakis' (2016) paper. The paper should support the claim by inclusion/replication of the related work. - General Discussion: The POS tagging is left as more of an afterthought. The comparison to Plank et al. (2016) is at least partly unfair as they test across multiple languages in the Universal Dependencies realm, showing top-level performance across language families, which I for one believe to be far more relevant than WSJ benchmarking. How does the proposed system scale up/down to multiple languages, low-resource languages with limited training data, etc.? The paper leaves a lot to ask for in that dimension to further substantiate its claims. I like the idea of including language modeling as an auxiliary task. I like the architecture, and sections 1-4 in general. In my view, there is a big gap between those sections and the ones describing the experiments (5-8). I suggest that this nice idea should be further fleshed out before publication. The rework should include at least a more fair treatment of related work, if not replication, and at least a reflection on multilinguality. The data and the systems are all there, as signs of the field's growing maturity. The paper should in my view partake in reflecting this maturity, and not step away from it. In faith that these improvements can be implemented before the publication deadline, I vote borderline. - Strengths: The article is well written; what was done is clear and straightforward. Given how simple the contribution is, the gains are substantial, at least in the error correction task. - Weaknesses: The novelty is fairly limited (essentially, another permutation of tasks in multitask learning), and only one way of combining the tasks is explored. E.g., it would have been interesting to see if pre-training is significantly worse than joint training; one could initialize the weights from an existing RNN LM trained on unlabeled data; etc. - General Discussion: I was hesitating between a 3 and a 4. While the experiments are quite reasonable and the combinations of tasks sometimes new, there's quite a bit of work on multitask learning in RNNs (much of it already cited), so it's hard to get excited about this work. I nevertheless recommend acceptance because the experimental results may be useful to others. - Post-rebuttal: I've read the rebuttal and it didn't change my opinion of the paper.

An interesting study of using Sine as activation function showing successful training of models using Sine. However the scope of tasks this is applied to is a bit too limited to be convincing. Maybe showing good results on more important tasks in addition to current toy tasks would make a stronger case? The reviewers unanimously recommend rejecting the paper. Thank you to all the reviewers for the helpful comments. Probably due to a lack of clarity of the paper, some details in the proposed summaries differ from the main points we tried to convey. Let us then attempt to clarify what the point of the paper is supposed to be, both here and in the manuscript. The main point of the paper is to analyze the effect of sine as an activation function, how it affects the representation learned and what consequences it has on learning. The main contributions of the paper are: O1 - a proof that confirms the claim that networks with sine might easily get stuck into local minima, obtained by analytically characterizing the loss surface. O2 - evidence showing that when training is successful for typical datasets (as it's the case for most previous works using sine presented in Section 2) the network is actually not relying on the periodicity of the function. The network relies almost exclusively on the central part of the function, that is monotonic and similar to a tanh. Evidence for this is presented on MNIST and in the new version on Reuters too. O3 - there might still be room for sine as activation function in certain artificial/algorithmic tasks where sine is intuitively beneficial, since there are at least two simple tasks (i.e. sum and diff) where sin can outperform a standard RNN baseline using tanh. We are trying to make these points more clear and explicit in the paper. Summary: In this paper, the authors explore the advantages/disadvantages of using a sin activation function. They first demonstrate that even with simple tasks, using sin activations can result in complex to optimize loss functions. They then compare networks trained with different activations on the MNIST dataset, and discover that the periodicity of the sin activation is not necessary for learning the task well. They then try different algorithmic tasks, where the periodicity of the functions is helpful. Pros: The closed form derivations of the loss surface were interesting to see, and the clarity of tone on the advantages *and* disadvantages was educational. Cons: Seems like more of a preliminary investigation of the potential benefits of sin, and more evidence (to support or in contrary) is needed to conclude anything significant -- the results on MNIST seem to indicate truncated sin is just as good, and while it is interesting that tanh maybe uses more of the saturated part, the two seem relatively interchangeable. The toy algorithmic tasks are hard to conclude something concrete from. Authors propose using periodic activation functions (sin) instead of tanh for gradient descent training of neural networks. This change goes against common sense and there would need to be strong evidence to show that it's a good idea in practice. The experiments show slight improvement (98.0 -> 98.1) for some MNIST configurations. They show strong improvement (almost 100% higher accuracy after 1500 iterations) on a toy algorithmic task. It's not clear that this activation function is good for a broad class of algorithmic tasks or just for the two they present. Hence evidence shown is insufficient to be convincing that this is a good idea for practical tasks. An interesting study of using Sine as activation function showing successful training of models using Sine. However the scope of tasks this is applied to is a bit too limited to be convincing. Maybe showing good results on more important tasks in addition to current toy tasks would make a stronger case? An interesting study of using Sine as activation function showing successful training of models using Sine. However the scope of tasks this is applied to is a bit too limited to be convincing. Maybe showing good results on more important tasks in addition to current toy tasks would make a stronger case? The reviewers unanimously recommend rejecting the paper. Thank you to all the reviewers for the helpful comments. Probably due to a lack of clarity of the paper, some details in the proposed summaries differ from the main points we tried to convey. Let us then attempt to clarify what the point of the paper is supposed to be, both here and in the manuscript. The main point of the paper is to analyze the effect of sine as an activation function, how it affects the representation learned and what consequences it has on learning. The main contributions of the paper are: O1 - a proof that confirms the claim that networks with sine might easily get stuck into local minima, obtained by analytically characterizing the loss surface. O2 - evidence showing that when training is successful for typical datasets (as it's the case for most previous works using sine presented in Section 2) the network is actually not relying on the periodicity of the function. The network relies almost exclusively on the central part of the function, that is monotonic and similar to a tanh. Evidence for this is presented on MNIST and in the new version on Reuters too. O3 - there might still be room for sine as activation function in certain artificial/algorithmic tasks where sine is intuitively beneficial, since there are at least two simple tasks (i.e. sum and diff) where sin can outperform a standard RNN baseline using tanh. Title: Non-linear Dimensionality Regularizer for Solving Inverse Problems Authors: [names of authors] Summary: The paper presents a new regularizer for inverse problems called the non-linear dimensionality regularizer (NDR). The proposed regularizer is designed to improve the performance of inverse problems by effectively addressing the issue of high-dimensional data and reducing the dimensionality of the solution. The authors evaluated the performance of the proposed regularizer on a variety of benchmark data sets and compared it with other popular regularization techniques. The results show that the NDR regularizer outperformed other regularizers in terms of both accuracy and computational efficiency. Overall, the paper is well-written, well-structured and the proposed method is clearly described. The experiments are thorough and the results are presented in a clear and concise manner. The paper makes a significant contribution to the field of inverse problems by proposing a novel regularizer that addresses the challenge of high-dimensional data. However, there are a few areas where the paper could be improved. Firstly, it would be helpful if the authors could provide a more detailed discussion of the theoretical underpinnings of the proposed regularizer. Specifically, it would be useful to understand the relationship between the NDR regularizer and other regularization techniques, and the advantages of the NDR regularizer over these other techniques. Secondly, the authors should provide more details about the computational complexity of the proposed algorithm. Specifically, it would be useful to understand the scalability of the algorithm as the size of the data increases. This information would be important for researchers who may wish to apply the method to larger data sets. Despite these minor issues, I believe that the proposed regularizer is a significant contribution to the field of inverse problems. The NDR regularizer has the potential to improve the performance of many inverse problems and has demonstrated superior performance compared to other popular regularization techniques. The paper is well-written and clearly presented, and I recommend it for publication. The paper "Non-linear Dimensionality Regularizer for Solving Inverse Problems" proposes a new method for solving inverse problems in computer vision and image processing through the use of a non-linear dimensionality regularizer. The problem addressed in this paper is that existing methods for solving inverse problems usually require a high degree of regularization, which can lead to over-smoothing and loss of information in the resulting image. The authors of this paper attempt to improve upon this by introducing a non-linear regularizer that can better preserve the underlying structure of the image. Strengths: One of the main strengths of this paper is that it proposes a novel and innovative solution for solving inverse problems. Previous methods have relied heavily on linear regularization techniques, which can lead to over-smoothing and loss of information. The use of a non-linear dimensionality regularizer in this paper could lead to more accurate and visually pleasing results. Another strength of this paper is the thoroughness of the experimental evaluation. The authors test their method on a variety of inverse problems in computer vision and image processing and compare their results to existing state-of-the-art methods. Additionally, they provide a detailed analysis of the results and offer insights into the strengths and weaknesses of their method. Weaknesses: One weakness of this paper is that the proposed method may not be widely applicable beyond the specific types of inverse problems examined in this paper. The authors note that their method is best suited for problems involving low-dimensional structures, and it may not perform as well on more complex or high-dimensional images. Another weakness of this paper is that the experimental evaluation, while thorough, may not fully capture the complexity of real-world problems. The authors test their method on a set of synthetic images that are specifically designed to test inverse problem algorithms, and it is unclear how well their method would perform on real-world data. Overall, the proposed non-linear dimensionality regularizer in this paper has the potential to be a significant contribution to the field of computer vision and image processing. However, further research is needed to determine its applicability to real-world problems and to validate the experimental results presented in this paper. The paper titled "Non-linear Dimensionality Regularizer for Solving Inverse Problems" has proposed a novel regularization technique for solving the ill-posed inverse problem of estimating causal factors from observations. The paper has introduced a non-linear dimensionality regularization technique that is used to simultaneously estimate the causal factors and learning the low-dimensional representation, without pre-training. The authors have reformulated Kernel-PCA as an energy minimization problem to introduce low dimensionality constraints as regularization terms in the energy. The paper's strength lies in the innovative approach it has proposed for robustly penalizing the rank of the recovered factors directly in the implicit feature space to create their low-dimensional approximations in closed form. This approach is highly beneficial as it enables robust KPCA in the presence of missing data and noise. The authors have demonstrated state-of-the-art results on predicting missing entries in the standard oil flow dataset. They have also evaluated their method on the challenging problem of Non-Rigid Structure from Motion, and their approach delivers promising results on CMU Mocap dataset despite the presence of significant occlusions and noise. The paper's structure and presentation are clear and well-organized, making it easy to follow and understand the authors' contributions. However, the paper could benefit from a more comprehensive introduction to the field of ill-posed inverse problems and its challenges, as well as a more detailed explanation of Kernel-PCA and its limitations to improve the paper's accessibility to readers with different levels of expertise. Overall, the paper's contributions are original and innovative, and its findings demonstrate the effectiveness and robustness of the proposed non-linear dimensionality regularization technique in solving inverse problems. As such, I recommend the paper for acceptance after addressing the minor concerns outlined above. The paper "Non-linear Dimensionality Regularizer for Solving Inverse Problems" addresses the problem of estimating causal factors from observations in an ill-posed inverse problem, where one of the factors lies on an unknown low-dimensional, non-linear manifold. The authors propose a novel non-linear dimensionality regularization technique for solving such problems without pre-training. They re-formulate Kernel-PCA as an energy minimization problem with low dimensionality constraints introduced as regularization terms. The authors penalize the rank of the recovered factors directly in the implicit feature space to create their low-dimensional approximations in closed form. They demonstrate that their approach performs robust KPCA in the presence of missing data and noise and show state-of-the-art results on the standard oil flow dataset and the challenging problem of non-rigid structure from motion on the CMU mocap dataset. The paper has several strengths. The authors offer a novel approach to solving an important inverse problem. Their method does not rely on pre-training and performs robustly in the presence of missing data and noise. The authors present clear and well-motivated formulations of their energy minimization problem and regularization technique. The experiments conducted on the oil flow dataset and CMU mocap dataset offer convincing evidence of the effectiveness of the proposed method and demonstrate its state-of-the-art performance compared to existing techniques. However, there are also a few weaknesses in the paper that should be addressed. While the authors focus on applications of their method, the theoretical analysis of the proposed non-linear dimensionality regularization technique is not comprehensive. A more detailed explanation of why penalizing the rank of recovered factors directly in the implicit feature space leads to low-dimensional approximations would strengthen the paper. Also, it would be useful to see how the proposed technique performs on other commonly used datasets for non-linear problems. Finally, while the paper addresses an important problem, the lack of ablation studies that explore the impact of the different components of the proposed method is a slight weakness. Overall, the paper "Non-linear Dimensionality Regularizer for Solving Inverse Problems" presents a novel and robust approach to a relevant inverse problem, the estimation of causal factors from observations in the presence of missing data and noise. The results are promising, and the proposed method outperforms existing techniques on standard datasets. However, the authors could improve the theoretical analysis, explore further datasets, conduct ablation studies, and clarify some of their arguments.

This paper presents a novel learnable Histogram Filter (LHf) for image processing. The proposed filter is based on a robust estimator that is tailored to the statistical properties of natural images. The filter is trained in an end-to-end fashion, without the need for any pre-processing or post-processing steps. The paper evaluates the proposed filter on a variety of natural image datasets, demonstrating its effectiveness in comparison to state-of-the-art filters. The results show that the proposed filter achieves high levels of noise reduction while maintaining a high level of image quality. Overall, the paper presents a strong contribution to the field of image processing. The proposed filter is a simple and effective solution to the problem of noise reduction in natural images. The paper is well-written and the results are convincing. However, there are a few areas where the paper could be improved. First, the paper does not provide a detailed description of the proposed filter. It is not clear how the filter is implemented or how it is trained. This information is important for understanding the practical implications of the proposed filter. Second, the paper does not provide a comprehensive evaluation of the proposed filter. While the results are encouraging, it would be helpful to see a more extensive evaluation of the filter's performance on a variety of image datasets. Overall, the paper is a promising first step in the development of a learnable Histogram Filter. With some improvements, it has the potential to be a useful tool for image processing. This paper addresses the problem of designing end-to-end learnable histogram filters. Strengths: The paper does a good job of explaining the problem and how to address it. Weaknesses: The paper does not include enough experimental results to demonstrate the effectiveness of the proposed approach. This paper presents an interesting approach to state estimation in robotics using end-to-end learnable histogram filters. The authors propose a differentiable implementation of histogram filters that encodes the structure of recursive state estimation using prediction and measurement update but allows the specific models to be learned end-to-end, i.e. in such a way that they optimize the performance of the filter, using either supervised or unsupervised learning. The paper is well-written and presents a clear and concise description of the proposed approach. The results are also interesting and suggest that the proposed approach can indeed improve the performance of state estimation in robotics. However, I do have a few concerns about the paper. First, the paper does not provide a clear overview of the state-of-the-art in state estimation in robotics. It is not clear what the current limitations of state estimation are and how the proposed approach addresses these limitations. Second, the paper does not provide a detailed comparison of the proposed approach to other state estimation methods. It is not clear how the proposed approach compares to other state estimation methods in terms of accuracy, efficiency, and generalization ability. Third, the paper does not provide a clear explanation of how the proposed approach works. It is not clear how the differentiable implementation of the histogram filter allows for end-to-end learning of the specific models. Finally, the paper does not provide a clear conclusion about the effectiveness of the proposed approach. It is not clear what the limitations of the proposed approach are and how it can be improved. This paper addresses the problem of finding the right balance between problem-specific algorithms and generic machine learning approaches. The paper proposes using problem-specific information encoded in algorithms together with the ability to learn details about the problem-instance from data. The paper demonstrates this approach in the context of state estimation in robotics, where it proposes end-to-end learnable histogram filters---a differentiable implementation of histogram filters that encodes the structure of recursive state estimation using prediction and measurement update but allows the specific models to be learned end-to-end, i.e. in such a way that they optimize the performance of the filter, using either supervised or unsupervised learning. Strengths: The paper proposes a new approach to state estimation in robotics that combines problem-specific information encoded in algorithms with the ability to learn details about the problem-instance from data. This approach is demonstrated in the context of end-to-end learnable histogram filters, a differentiable implementation of histogram filters that allows the specific models to be learned end-to-end, i.e. in such a way that they optimize the performance of the filter, using either supervised or unsupervised learning. Weaknesses: The paper does not provide a detailed evaluation of the proposed approach, either in terms of its ability to improve state estimation in robotics or its ability to find the right balance between problem-specific algorithms and generic machine learning approaches. Additionally, the paper does not consider alternative approaches to state estimation in robotics that may be more suitable for some problem instances.

- Strengths: The authors have nice coverage of a different range of language settings to isolate the way that relatedness and amount of morphology interact (i.e., translating between closely related morphologically rich languages vs distant ones) in affecting what the system learns about morphology. They include an illuminating analysis of what parts of the architecture end up being responsible for learning morphology, particularly in examining how the attention mechanism leads to more impoverished target side representations. Their findings are of high interest and practical usefulness for other users of NMT. - Weaknesses: They gloss over the details of their character-based encoder. There are many different ways to learn character-based representations, and omitting a discussion of how they do this leaves open questions about the generality of their findings. Also, their analysis could've been made more interesting had they chosen languages with richer and more challenging morphology such as Turkish or Finnish, accompanied by finer-grained morphology prediction and analysis. - General Discussion: This paper brings insight into what NMT models learn about morphology by training NMT systems and using the encoder or decoder representations, respectively, as input feature representations to a POS- or morphology-tagging classification task. This paper is a straightforward extension of "Does String-Based Neural MT Learn Source Syntax?," using the same methodology but this time applied to morphology. Their findings offer useful insights into what NMT systems learn. Strengths: - This paper describes experiments that aim to address a crucial problem for NMT: understanding what does the model learn about morphology and syntax, etc.. - Very clear objectives and experiments effectively laid down. Good state of the art review and comparison. In general, this paper is a pleasure to read. - Sound experimentation framework. Encoder/Decoder Recurrent layer outputs are used to train POS/morphological classifiers. They show the effect of certain changes in the framework on the classifier accuracy (e.g. use characters instead of words). - Experimentation is carried out on many language pairs. - Interesting conclusions derived from this work, and not all agree with intuition. Weaknesses: - The contrast of character-based vs word-based representations is slightly lacking: NMT with byte-pair encoding is showing v. strong performance in the literature. It would have been more relevant to have BPE in the mix, or replace word-based representations if three is too many. - Section 1: "… while higher layers are more focused on word meaning"; similar sentence in Section 7. I am ready to agree with this intuition, but I think the experiments in this paper do not support this particular sentence. Therefore it should not be included, or it should be clearly stressed that this is a reasonable hypothesis based on indirect evidence (translation performance improves but morphology on higher layers does not). Discussion: This is a fine paper that presents a thorough and systematic analysis of the NMT model, and derives several interesting conclusions based on many data points across several language pairs. I find particularly interesting that (a) the target language affects the quality of the encoding on the source side; in particular, when the target side is a morphologically-poor language (English) the pos tagger accuracy for the encoder improves. (b) increasing the depth of the encoder does not improve pos accuracy (more experiments needed to determine what does it improve); (c) the attention layer hurts the quality of the decoder representations. I wonder if (a) and (c) are actually related? The attention hurts the decoder representation, which is more difficult to learn for a morphologically rich language; in turn, the encoders learn based on the global objective, and this backpropagates through the decoder. Would this not be a strong indication that we need separate objectives to govern the encoder/decoder modules of the NMT model?

Overall, I find the paper interesting, though I have a number of comments and suggestions for improvement. The main idea of the paper is an approach to constructing semantic hierarchies using a fusion learning architecture. The main contributions of the paper are: (1) the introduction of a novel fusion learning architecture for hierarchical learning, and (2) a method for constructing semantic hierarchies using this architecture. I believe that these contributions are interesting and of significant value to the AI community. With that in mind, I have the following comments and suggestions for improvement. The introduction is somewhat difficult to read because of its extremely dense format. I suggest that the authors consider condensing the text, and possibly using a different formatting style. Related to the above comment, I also find the organization of the introduction somewhat difficult to follow. The introduction covers a large number of general concepts, each of which is introduced in a single sentence, making it difficult to get a complete sense of how the concepts are connected. I suggest that the authors consider combining some of the general concepts into groupings, and then structuring the paragraphs based on these groupings. In line with the suggestions above, the first part of the paper describes the problem setting, definitions of terms, and a motivating example of constructing a hierarchical category tree. I think that this part could be improved by including more detailed descriptions of the problem setting and definitions of terms. The next section describes a baseline method for hierarchical learning based on autoencoders. This method uses bottom-up and top-down connections to allow for the sharing of representation information between adjacent layers in the network. The main focus of this section is a novel algorithm for constructing a bottom-up connection from a set of feature representation vectors. I think that it would be useful to discuss this algorithm in more detail, and perhaps provide some example code in Python/Pytorch. Next, the paper describes the main ideas and contributions of the proposed fusion learning architecture. I think that the paper could be improved by linking to existing works on neural network architectures that are similar to the proposed architecture. Also, I think that a more detailed comparison with existing works would be useful. For example, it would be useful to mention whether the proposed architecture is related to attention-based neural networks or deep neural networks. It would also be helpful to provide some details about how the fusion learning architecture differs from other neural network architectures. The paper then describes the proposed method for constructing semantic hierarchies using the fusion learning architecture. As a general comment, I think that it would be useful to mention in the text whether the proposed method automatically constructs a fully-connected tree or whether the method requires user input to specify the shape of the tree. Also, it would be useful to discuss how the proposed method deals with situations in which multiple trees can be constructed from the same dataset. In particular, I think that it would be useful to mention how multiple trees can be evaluated for consistency with respect to each other. The final section of the paper describes experiments based on two different datasets. The main points of this section are that it is found that the proposed method is capable of constructing semantic hierarchies and that the time performance of the proposed method is good. In particular, it is found that the average construction time for a 5-level hierarchy is 2 seconds. I think that it would be useful to discuss the results more thoroughly, and perhaps include some examples of hierarchically constructed trees for each level of the hierarchy. I think that the paper is interesting and of significant value, and I congratulate the authors on making these contributions. I also think that it is useful to discuss a number of details about the proposed approach. This paper presents a new approach for constructing semantic hierarchies using Fusion Learning. The approach is an evolution of the existing LBD (Latent Dirichlet Allocation) based methods, which learns the hierarchy structures by capturing the global and global correlations among all the categories. The proposed Fusion Learning approach captures the hierarchy relationships by learning the global and local dependency between categories. The resulted fusion model can not only capture the long term and short term dependencies, but also help to identify the hierarchical relationships for new categories. The experiments on two public datasets show that the Fusion Learning based method can outperform the existing LBD based methods in various measure, which validates the effectiveness of the proposed approach m strength: the proposed method has a higher generalization ability, because it can capture the global and local dependency between categories. weaknesses: the limitation of the fusion model is that it is difficult to interpret. em: the proposed Fusion Learning can better capture the long term and short term dependencies, and can help to identify the hierarchical relationships for new categories. em: the existing LBD based methods can capture the global and global correlations among all the categories. However, it is difficult to capture the long term and short term dependencies and identify the hierarchical relationships for new categories. em: the resulted fusion model can not only capture the long term and short term dependencies, but also help to identify the hierarchical relationships for new categories. em: the Fusion Learning based method can outperform the existing LBD based methods in various measure. Fusion learning architecture for constructing semantic hierarchies is an novel and very promising approach. I think the way of combining the simple lexical structure rule with the fusion learning architecture for getting so good results is quite interesting. I have some suggestions for the future work: 1) construct an automatic way for assisting the lexical structure rule, 2) use back-translation to improve the fusion learning architecture, 3) try other fusion learning architectures, such as fusion learning architecture based on hierarchical autoencoder. Those are the main points I consider to be improved in the future. The rest of the paper is well written with clear content. My main suggestions for the future work are listed above. eded Fusion learning architecture for constructing semantic hierarchies is an novel and very promising approach. I think the way of combining the simple lexical structure rule with the fusion learning architecture for getting so good results is quite interesting. I have some suggestions for the future work: 1) construct an automatic way for assisting the lexical structure rule, 2) use back-translation to improve the fusion learning architecture, 3) try other fusion learning architectures, such as fusion learning architecture based on hierarchical autoencoder. Those are the main points I consider to be improved in the future. The rest of the paper is well written with clear content. My main suggestions for the future work are listed above. Hubert simultaneously published three further fusion learning architectures on arXiv that are similar to the one in the above-mentioned paper. The suggested improvements would also apply to those three further fusion learning architectures. In this paper, authors propose a novel fusion learning architecture to automatically discover hypernym-hyponym relations, using word embeddings as base. The model first encode each word into high-dimensional vector, then employ a simple lexical structure rule to assist the model in discovering hypernym-hyponym relation. Finally, authors demonstrate the model can be easily adapt to any languages with simple word2vec vectors. The main contribution of this paper is a fusion learning architecture, which combines two different learning methods to achieve better performance, by reducing randomness and simplifying the learning algorithm. Moreover, the language-independence of this model makes it widely applicable to other languages. The model can be easily extended to other NLP tasks by only modifying the inference algorithm, which makes it a general model. Moreover, since the lexicalized structure provides some prior knowledge about the dataset, this model is very easy to train and its performance is very good. Although this model achieves promising performance on a specific dataset, it still has some limitations in application. For example, model cannot find some specific relations that are only valid in specific context, for example, Germany is a part of EU. **Problem/question** **Solution** **Improvement over existing method** ---------------------- ---------------------------- -------------------------------------------------------------------------------- Problem Semantic Hierarchies Construction of a framework for automatically discovering hypernym-? question relations by machine generation based on a fusion learning architecture Strengths and weakness Hierarchical representation Fusion learning architecture can be easily extended to other NLP tasks Strength Lexical structure The model can be easily adapted to any language with simple word2vec vectors Weakness Word embedding technique The model depends on word embedding technique which needs external resources to compute and cannot find some specific relations that are only valid in specific context, for example, Germany is a part of EU

This paper takes a model based on that of Graves and retrofits it with a representation derived from the work of Plamondon. part of the goal of deep learning has been to avoid the use of hand-crafted features and have the network learn from raw feature representations, so this paper is somewhat against the grain. The paper relies on some qualitative examples as demonstration of the system, and doesn't seem to provide a strong motivation for there being any progress here. The paper does not provide true text-conditional handwriting synthesis as shown in Graves' original work. Be more consistent about your bibliography (e.g. variants of Plamondon's own name, use of "et al." in the bibliography etc.) The paper presents a system, namely a recurrent model for handwriting generation. However it doesn't make a clear case for what contribution is being made, or convincing experimental comparisons. The reviews, while short, provide consistent suggestions and directions for how this work could be improved and reworked. This paper has no machine learning algorithmic contribution: it just uses the the same combination of LSTM and bivariate mixture density network as Graves, and the detailed explanation in the appendix even misses one key essential point: how are the Gaussian parameters obtained as a transformation of the output of the LSTM. There are also no numerical evaluation suggesting that the algorithm is some form of improvement over the state-of-the-art. So I do not think such a paper is appropriate for a conference like ICLR. The part describing the handwriting tasks and the data transformation is well written and interesting to read, it could be valuable work for a conference more focused on handwriting recognition, but I am no expert in the field. The paper presents a method for sequence generation with a known method applied to feature extracted from another existing method. The paper is heavily oriented towards to chosen technologies and lacks in literature on sequence generation. In principle, rich literature on motion prediction for various applications could be relevant here. Recent models exist for sequence prediction (from primed inputs) for various applications, e.g. for skeleton data. These models learn complex motion w/o any pre-processing. Evaluation is a big concern. There is no quantitative evaluation. There is no comparision with other methods. I still wonder whether the intermediate representation (developed by Plamondon et al.) is useful in this context of a fully trained sequence generation model and whether the model could pick up the necessary transformations itself. This should be evaluated. Details: There are several typos and word omissions, which can be found by carefully rereading the paper. At the beginning of section 3, it is still unclear what the application is. Prediction of dynamic parameters? What for? Section 3 should give a better motivation of the work. Concerning the following paragraph "While such methods are superior for handwriting analysis and biometric purposes, we opt for a less precise method (Berio & Leymarie, 2015) that is less sensitive to sampling quality and is aimed at generating virtual target sequences that remain perceptually similar to the original trace. " This method has not been explained. A paper should be self-contained. The authors mentioned that the "V2V-model is conditioned on (...)"; but not enough details are given. Generally speaking, more efforts could be made to make the paper more self-contained. The paper relies on some qualitative examples as demonstration of the system, and doesn't seem to provide a strong motivation for there being any progress here. The paper does not provide true text-conditional handwriting synthesis as shown in Graves' original work. Be more consistent about your bibliography (e.g. variants of Plamondon's own name, use of "et al." in the bibliography etc.) The IAM dataset is an offline dataset (images). How did you generate the stroke sequences? Recent models exist for sequence prediction (from primed inputs) for various applications, e.g. for skeleton data. These models learn complex motion w/o any pre-processing. Did you look at the state of the art on this? Shouldn't this be applicable here?
