Overall, "The Effect of Different Writing Tasks on Linguistic Style: A Case Study of the ROC Story Cloze Task" is a well-written and researched study that provides valuable insight into the relationship between writing tasks and linguistic style. The authors present a clear hypothesis and methodology, and their results are well-supported and presented in an organized manner. One of the strengths of this paper is its clear explanation of the ROC Story Cloze Task and how it differs from other writing tasks. The authors provide a thorough overview of existing research on linguistic style and its relationship to writing tasks, and their methodology for analyzing the linguistic features of each story is rigorous and well-explained. The results of the study are clear and compelling, showing that different writing tasks do indeed have an effect on linguistic style. The authors provide several possible explanations for these findings and offer suggestions for future research in this area. Overall, this study makes a valuable contribution to the field of linguistics and provides important insights for anyone interested in understanding the relationship between writing tasks and linguistic style. One area where the paper could be improved is in its discussion of limitations and future directions for research. While the authors briefly mention some limitations of their study, they do not provide a comprehensive discussion of the implications of these limitations or suggestions for future research that could address them. Additionally, while the authors suggest that other writing tasks may have similar effects on linguistic style, they do not provide specific examples or recommendations for future research in this area. Despite this limitation, "The Effect of Different Writing Tasks on Linguistic Style: A Case Study of the ROC Story Cloze Task" is a valuable contribution to the field of linguistics and provides important insights for anyone interested in the relationship between writing tasks and linguistic style. The paper, "The Effect of Different Writing Tasks on Linguistic Style: A Case Study of the ROC Story Cloze Task," addresses the question of how different writing tasks affect the linguistic style of writers. Specifically, the paper investigates how the linguistic style of writers changes when they complete a cloze task, which requires them to fill in the missing words in a story based on context cues. One major strength of the paper is its clear and well-structured presentation of findings. The authors begin by introducing the research question and explaining the methodology, followed by a description of the participants and the data collection process. They then present their findings in a concise and organized manner, highlighting significant differences in linguistic style across different writing tasks. Additionally, they provide a detailed analysis of the linguistic features that varied the most, offering insight into the mechanisms behind the observed changes in style. However, a weakness of the paper is its narrow focus on a single type of writing task, which may limit the generalizability of the results. The authors acknowledge this limitation but do not offer suggestions for future research that could address it. Furthermore, while the paper provides a thorough analysis of the linguistic features that changed across tasks, it does not explore the possible reasons why these changes occurred or the implications of these changes for different contexts. Overall, "The Effect of Different Writing Tasks on Linguistic Style: A Case Study of the ROC Story Cloze Task" makes a valuable contribution to our understanding of how writers adapt their linguistic style to different writing tasks. While there are some limitations to the scope of the study, the authors present their findings in a clear and informative way, paving the way for further research on this topic. Title: Peer Review for "The Effect of Different Writing Tasks on Linguistic Style: A Case Study of the ROC Story Cloze Task" Authors: [names of authors] Summary: The paper focuses on the impact of different writing tasks on writing style. Authors conducted a case study on the story cloze task, where participants were given similar writing tasks but with different constraints. An annotator was provided with 1) writing an entire story, 2) adding a story ending for a given story context, and 3) adding an incoherent ending to a story. The study uses a simple linear classifier to distinguish between the three cases based on a style-based approach. The authors demonstrated that different writing tasks significantly influenced the way people wrote. Positive aspects: The paper is well written, and the experimental results are well structured and easy to understand. The study is original, and the results demonstrated the importance of writing tasks for writing style. The authors used appropriate methodologies to support their argument and used a style-based classifier to distinguish between the three cases. The authors established a new state-of-the-art result on the story cloze challenge, which shows the relevance of their approach. Weaknesses: The study does not explore the effects of varied emotional or practical contexts on writing style, which could have strengthened the paper's findings further. The paper could benefit from a more detailed discussion of the limitations and the practical implications of the study results. Overall Assessment: Overall, the paper makes a valuable contribution to the research domain of writing style by showing that writing tasks can signify measurable differences in writing style. The authors used appropriate methodologies and presented their results in a way that is easy to understand. Further future research could investigate how additional variables, such as intention and mental state, impact writing style. Nonetheless, the study provides a strong foundation for future research on this topic. I recommend that this paper is accepted for publication. This paper investigates the effect of different writing tasks on the linguistic style of the writer. The central research question is whether variants of the same writing task can lead to measurable differences in writing style. To answer this question, the authors conducted a case study using the story cloze task (Mostafazadeh et al., 2016a). The study design involved assigning annotators similar writing tasks with different constraints, including (1) writing an entire story, (2) adding a story ending for a given story context, and (3) adding an incoherent ending to a story. Strengths: - The paper makes an important contribution to understanding the role that writing tasks play in shaping the linguistic style of writers. The authors provide empirical evidence that different task framings can dramatically affect the way people write. - The results of the study are significant and have practical implications for tasks such as automated essay grading and plagiarism detection. - The use of a linear classifier informed with stylistic features to successfully distinguish between the three cases without even looking at the story context is an innovative approach that could potentially be useful in other related tasks. Weaknesses: - The sample size used in the study is relatively small, which might limit the generalizability of the findings. Future research could consider using a larger sample size. - The study could have been strengthened by including more details on the annotators’ characteristics and how they were selected for the study to improve the clarity of the results. In summary, this paper makes a significant contribution to the field of NLP, by demonstrating the impact of different writing tasks on linguistic style. The paper proposes an innovative approach to studying this problem, and the results obtained are promising. Overall, the strengths of the study outweigh the weaknesses, and this work has significant potential to inform future research in this area.

This paper presents a graph-based approach for producing sense-disambiguated synonym sets from a collection of undisambiguated synonym sets. The authors evaluate their approach by inducing these synonym sets from Wiktionary and from a collection of Russian dictionaries, and then comparing pairwise synonymy relations (using precision, recall, and F1) against WordNet and BabelNet (for the English synonym sets) or RuThes and Yet Another RussNet (for the Russian synonym sets). The paper is very well written and structured. The experiments and evaluations (or at least the prose parts) are very easy to follow. The methodology is sensible and the analysis of the results cogent. I was happy to observe that the objections I had when reading the paper (such as the mismatch in vocabulary between the synonym dictionaries and gold standards) ended up being resolved, or at least addressed, in the final pages. The one thing about the paper that concerns me is that the authors do not seem to have properly understood the previous work, which undercuts the stated motivation for this paper. The first instance of this misunderstanding is in the paragraph beginning on line 064, where OmegaWiki is lumped in with Wiktionary and Wikipedia in a discussion of resources that are "not formally structured" and that contain "undisambiguated synonyms". In reality, OmegaWiki is distinguished from the other two resources by using a formal structure (a relational database) based on word senses rather than orthographic forms. Translations, synonyms, and other semantic annotations in OmegaWiki are therefore unambiguous. The second, and more serious, misunderstanding comes in the three paragraphs beginning on lines 092, 108, and 120. Here the paper claims that both BabelNet and UBY "rely on English WordNet as a pivot for mapping of existing resources" and criticizes this mapping as being "error-prone". Though it is true that BabelNet uses WordNet as a pivot, UBY does not. UBY is basically a general-purpose specification for the representation of lexical-semantic resources and of links between them. It exists independently of any given lexical-semantic resource (including WordNet) and of any given alignment between resources (including ones based on "similarity of dictionary definitions" or "cross-lingual links"). Its maintainers have made available various databases adhering to the UBY spec; these contain a variety of lexical-semantic resources which have been aligned with a variety of different methods. A given UBY database can be *queried* for synsets, but UBY itself does not *generate* those synsets. Users are free to produce their own databases by importing whatever lexical-semantic resources and alignments thereof are best suited to their purposes. The three criticisms of UBY on lines 120 to 125 are therefore entirely misplaced. In fact, I think at least one of the criticisms is not appropriate even with respect to BabelNet. The authors claim that Watset may be superior to BabelNet because BabelNet's mapping and use of machine translation are error-prone. The implication here is that Watset's method is error-free, or at least significantly less error-prone. This is a very grandiose claim that I do not believe is supported by what the authors ought to have known in advance about their similarity-based sense linking algorithms and graph clustering algorithms, let alone by the results of their study. I think this criticism ought to be moderated. Also, I think the third criticism (BabelNet's reliance on WordNet as a pivot) somewhat misses the point -- surely the most important issue to highlight isn't the fact that the pivot is English, but rather that its synsets are already manually sense-annotated. I think the last paragraph of §1 and the first two paragraphs of §2 should be extensively revised. They should focus on the *general* problem of generating synsets by sense-level alignment/translation of LSRs (see Gurevych et al., 2016 for a survey), rather than particularly on BabelNet (which uses certain particular methods) and UBY (which doesn't use any particular methods, but can aggregate the results of existing ones). It may be helpful to point out somewhere that although alignment/translation methods *can* be used to produce synsets or to enrich existing ones, that's not always an explicit goal of the process. Sometimes it's just a serendipitous (if noisy) side-effect of aligning/translating resources with differing granularities. Finally, at several points in the paper (lines 153, 433), the "synsets" of TWSI of JoBimText are criticized for including too many words that are hypernyms, co-hypnomyms, etc. instead of synonyms. But is this problem really unique to TWSI and JoBimText? That is, how often do hypernyms, co-hypernyms, etc. appear in the output of Watset? (We can get only a very vague idea of this from comparing Tables 3 and 5, which analyze only synonym relations.) If Watset really is better at filtering out words with other semantic relations, then it would be nice to see some quantitative evidence of this. Some further relatively minor points that should nonetheless be fixed: * Lines 047 to 049: The sentence about Kiselev et al. (2015) seems rather useless. Why bother mentioning their analysis if you're not going to tell us what they found? * Line 091: It took me a long time to figure out how "wat" has any relation to "discover the correct word sense". I suppose this is supposed to be a pun on "what". Maybe it would have been better to call the approach "Whatset"? Or at least consider rewording the sentence to better explain the pun. * Figure 2 is practically illegible owing to the microscopic font. Please increase the text size! * Similarly, Tables 3, 4, and 5 are too small to read comfortably. Please use a larger font. To save space, consider abbreviating the headers ("P, "R", "F1") and maybe reporting scores in the range 0–100 instead of 0–1, which will eliminate a leading 0 from each column. * Lines 517–522: Wiktionary is a moving target. To help others replicate or compare against your work, please indicate the date of the Wiktionary database dump you used. * Throughout: The constant switching between Times and Computer Modern is distracting. The root of this problem is a longstanding design flaw in the ACL 2017 LaTeX style file, but it's exacerbated by the authors' decision to occasionally set numbers in math mode, even in running text. Please fix this by removing \usepackage{times} from the preamble and replacing it with either \usepackage{newtxtext} \usepackage{newtxmath} or \usepackage{mathptmx} References: I Gurevych, J. Eckle-Kohler, and M. Matuschek, 2016. Linked Lexical Knowledge Bases: Foundations and Applications, volume 34 of Synthesis Lectures on Human Language Technologies, chapter 3: Linking Algorithms, pages 29-44. Morgan & Claypool. ---- I have read the author response. - Strengths: The paper proposes a new method for word sense induction from synonymy dictionaries. The method presents a conceptual improvement over existing ones and demonstrates robust performance in empirical evaluation. The evaluation was done thoroughly, using a number of benchmarks and strong baseline methods. - Weaknesses: Just a couple of small points. I would like to see more discussion of the nature of the evaluation. First, one observes that all models' scores are relatively low, under 50% F1. Is there room for much improvement or is there a natural ceiling of performance due to the nature of the task? The authors discuss lexical sparsity of the input data but I wonder how much of the performance gap this sparsity accounts for. Second, I would also like to see some discussion of the evaluation metric chosen. It is known that word senses can be analyzed at different levels of granularity, which can naturally affect the scores of any system. Another point is that it is not clear how the authors obtained vectors for word senses that they used in 3.4, if the senses are only determined after this step, and anyway senses are not marked in the input corpora. - General Discussion: I recommend the paper for presentation at the ACL Meeting. Solid work.

This paper provides an in-depth analysis of deep neural network (DNN) models for practical applications. The paper begins by discussing the background of DNNs and how they have evolved to become a powerful tool for various tasks. The paper then reviews the different types of DNN models that have been developed for practical applications, including feed-forward, convolutional, and recurrent models. The paper also discusses the advantages and disadvantages of each type of model, and how they have been applied to different tasks. The paper concludes by providing a summary of the current state of DNNs for practical applications, and how they are likely to evolve in the future. Overall, the paper provides a thorough and informative review of DNN models for practical applications. This paper addresses the problem of choosing the best deep neural network architecture for practical applications. The paper is strong in that it provides a thorough analysis of the various deep neural network models that are available. The paper is weak in that it does not provide any clear guidelines for choosing the best model for a given application. This paper presents a comprehensive analysis of important metrics in practical applications of Deep Neural Networks (DNNs), including accuracy, memory footprint, parameters, operations count, inference time, and power consumption. The authors believe that their analysis provides a compelling set of information that helps design and engineer efficient DNNs. The main content of the paper is a thorough analysis of the trade-offs involved in the practical applications of DNNs. The authors evaluate various DNN models in terms of accuracy, memory footprint, parameters, operations count, inference time, and power consumption. The results of this analysis provide valuable insights into the trade-offs involved in practical applications of DNNs. The authors also provide recommendations for designing efficient DNNs based on their findings. Overall, the paper presents a valuable analysis of important metrics in practical applications of DNNs. The findings of the paper provide insights that can help improve the efficiency of DNNs in practical applications. This paper addresses the problem of the efficiency of Deep Neural Networks (DNNs) for practical applications. Its strengths include a comprehensive analysis of important metrics in practical applications, such as accuracy, memory footprint, parameters, operations count, inference time, and power consumption. The paper also presents key findings, such as the relationship between accuracy and inference time and the energy constraints on the maximum achievable accuracy and model complexity. However, its weaknesses include the lack of comparison with other state-of-the-art DNNs and the lack of experimental validation of the analysis.

Title: Multiplicative LSTM for sequence modelling Authors: [Names of authors] Summary: This paper proposes a novel variant of LSTM, called Multiplicative LSTM, which aims to enhance the modelling of long-term dependencies in sequence data. The proposed model replaces the traditional additive gates of LSTM with a set of multiplicative gates that are more effective in capturing nonlinear relationships between different inputs. The authors report significant improvements in various benchmark datasets by the proposed method over the traditional LSTM. Strengths: The paper presents an interesting idea to modify the LSTM architecture to improve its capability in modelling long-term dependencies. The authors provide a clear description of the proposed model, and they demonstrate its effectiveness through a thorough evaluation on different datasets. The proposed method is based on a sound and theoretically grounded motivation, as the multiplicative interaction among input features is an essential component of many nonlinear models. Weaknesses: One possible issue with the paper is that the authors do not provide a detailed comparison with other recently proposed LSTM variants. Although the evaluation results of the proposed model are impressive, it would be helpful to see how it performs against other state-of-the-art LSTM variants that tackle the same problem. Moreover, the authors do not provide any insights into the relative strengths and weaknesses of the proposed method compared to the original LSTM architecture, which makes it challenging to understand the advantages of the proposed approach. Conclusion: Overall, the proposed Multiplicative LSTM method is a significant contribution to the field of sequence modelling. The paper presents an interesting and effective approach to improve long-term dependency modelling by modifying the traditional LSTM architecture. However, some improvements can be made in the paper's evaluation and comparison to other LSTM variants. Nonetheless, the proposed method has the potential to be used in various real-world applications involving sequential data, and the paper provides a clear exposition of its operation and effectiveness. The paper "Multiplicative LSTM for sequence modelling" addresses the problem of effectively modeling sequential data with long-term dependencies using the Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM) architecture. Specifically, the authors propose a novel variant of LSTM called Multiplicative LSTM that employs multiplicative interactions between the input and forget gates to enhance the gate-dependent information flow and improve the model's ability to capture long-term dependencies. One of the notable strengths of this paper is the rigorous experimentation to demonstrate the effectiveness of the proposed Multiplicative LSTM architecture. The authors conducted extensive experiments on several benchmark datasets, including text classification, language modeling, and speech recognition, and compared the performance of various LSTM variants. The results indicate that Multiplicative LSTM outperforms other LSTM variants on most tasks, especially on long-term dependency modeling. Another strength of the paper is the clear and concise description of the proposed architecture and the experimental setup. The authors provide a detailed illustration of the multiplicative interactions between the input and forget gates and the mathematical formulation of the model. They also describe the experimental setup, including the datasets, evaluation metrics, and hyperparameters, in a clear and reproducible manner. However, there are also some weaknesses that need to be addressed. Firstly, the paper lacks a comparative analysis of Multiplicative LSTM with state-of-the-art sequence modeling models outside the LSTM family, such as Transformer and WaveNet. Secondly, although the paper extensively evaluates the proposed model's effectiveness on various benchmark datasets, it falls short on analyzing the model's efficiency and scalability, which is crucial for real-world applications. In conclusion, the paper "Multiplicative LSTM for sequence modeling" proposes a novel variant of the LSTM architecture that enhances the model's ability to capture long-term dependencies in sequential data. The paper is well-written, clearly presented, and thoroughly evaluated. However, it could benefit from a comparative analysis with other state-of-the-art sequence modeling models and a more comprehensive analysis of the model's efficiency and scalability. Peer Review for “Multiplicative LSTM for sequence modelling” The paper presents a novel sequence modelling approach called multiplicative LSTM (mLSTM), which combines the long short-term memory (LSTM) and multiplicative recurrent neural network architectures. The paper highlights the advantages of mLSTM over the traditional LSTM models, including the ability to have different recurrent transition functions for each possible input, making it more expressive for autoregressive density estimation. The authors present empirical evidence to show that mLSTM outperforms the standard LSTM and its deep variants for a range of character level modelling tasks. Moreover, the paper demonstrates that the improvement of mLSTM increases with the complexity of the task. The experiment shows that mLSTM achieves a test error of 1.19 bits/character on the last 4 million characters of the Hutter prize dataset when combined with dynamic evaluation. The paper is well written, and the technical presentation is clear and concise. The main contributions of the paper include presenting a novel sequence modelling approach, and providing evidence of its improved performance over traditional LSTM models. The experiments are well designed, and the results are convincing, indicating that the mLSTM model has better performance for character level modelling tasks than other existing models. However, there are a few minor issues, including: 1. The paper only demonstrates the efficacy of the proposed approach for character level modelling tasks. It could have been more beneficial to have compared mLSTM with traditional LSTMs for other types of sequences (besides character-level datasets). 2. Although the paper provides empirical validation of the proposed approach for sequence modelling, it would have been beneficial to have a more substantial discussion of the theoretical underpinnings of why mLSTM appears to be more effective than traditional LSTM. Overall, this paper is well written, and the proposed approach shows the potential for further development in sequence modelling. The experimental evaluation is comprehensive, and the results are convincing. The authors have shown that the proposed mLSTM model has clear advantages over traditional LSTMs on a variety of character level modelling tasks. In this paper, the authors introduce a novel architecture for sequence modelling called the multiplicative LSTM (mLSTM). They argue that mLSTM is more expressive for autoregressive density estimation because it allows for different recurrent transition functions for each possible input. They compare the performance of mLSTM to standard LSTM and its deep variants on a range of character-level modelling tasks and show that mLSTM outperforms these architectures, with improvement increasing as the complexity of the task increases. Strengths: - The paper clearly explains the motivation behind developing the mLSTM architecture and provides a detailed description of the architecture itself. - The authors provide empirical evidence to support their claim that mLSTM outperforms other state-of-the-art models on character-level modelling tasks. - The paper includes a thorough evaluation of mLSTM on the Hutter prize dataset, which provides a real-world example of the model's performance. Weaknesses: - While the paper provides evidence that mLSTM outperforms standard LSTM and its deep variants, it does not compare mLSTM to other state-of-the-art models for sequence modelling, which would provide a more complete picture of its performance. - The paper could benefit from a more detailed explanation of how the mLSTM architecture works, especially for readers who may be unfamiliar with the LSTM or multiplicative RNN architectures. - The paper lacks a discussion of potential drawbacks or limitations of the mLSTM architecture, which would provide a more balanced perspective on its usefulness for sequence modelling. Overall, this paper provides a valuable contribution to the field of sequence modelling with its development of the multiplicative LSTM architecture and its demonstration of its superior performance on character-level modelling tasks. However, further evaluation and discussion of the mLSTM architecture's strengths and weaknesses would enhance the paper's impact and applicability to other areas of research.

No details are provided on the methods used in this paper to produce the results, due to issues of 'non-disclosure restrictions'. If the reader doesn't know the learning algorithm or the training data (or other resources made use of in the approach), then there is nothing in the paper to help with the reader's own sentiment analysis methods, which is why we share research. This is not a research paper, hence does not belong in this conference. Perhaps a submission to a demo session somewhere would be a good idea. Even with a demo paper, however, you would need to share more details about the methods used than you do here.

The authors present an interesting approach to quality estimation of machine translation (MT) systems by exploring prediction uncertainty and using it as additional feature when training regression models. They show that this improves performance on several datasets, including WMT'14 English-to-German task with BLEU score improvement from 0.5 to 1 point over baseline system trained without uncertainty features. I think their work is very relevant since MT evaluation metrics are known not always correlate well with human judgement which makes them less reliable than desired. The proposed method can be used to improve existing approaches or even serve as alternative way how to estimate MT output quality more reliably. I would like to see some further experiments done to test robustness of the presented model against different types of noise added into input data - especially if they affect predictions made by neural network-based MT engines. The authors present an interesting approach to quality estimation of machine translation systems using prediction uncertainty as one feature for estimating system performance. This is not only novel but also very relevant since it can be used with any existing MT evaluation metric (e.g., BLEU) without requiring additional data collection efforts from users. The proposed method has been evaluated on two different datasets showing promising results that are comparable to state-of-the-art methods while being simpler than most other approaches. I think this work will have great impact if implemented into real-world applications such as online search engines where user satisfaction plays a key role. Strengths: 1. Novelty - This research presents something new which hasn't been done before. 2. Relevance - It solves problems related to current issues faced by many companies today. 3. Simplicity - It's easy enough to implement compared to some more complicated solutions out there. Weaknesses: The authors present interesting ideas on machine learning approaches to MTQE (machine translation quality estimation). They propose using Bayesian techniques with Gaussian Processes as well as other more traditional ML algorithms such as SVR or Random Forests. They report experiments showing improvements over state-of-the-art results when they use GP-based uncertainty measures instead of standard evaluation scores like BLEU. The experimental setup seems sound but I would have liked some additional details about it - e.g., what was used exactly? What were the hyperparameters tuned against? Overall it's a good contribution to researching better ways to assess MT output quality. It could benefit from further explanation/clarification though. 4 stars overall. 4 stars This article presents new insights into predicting the quality of translations produced by machines. The authors present interesting ideas on machine learning approaches towards estimating MT quality with uncertainties. The experiments conducted seem reasonable but could have been more extensive (e.g., using different data sets). In addition, it would be good to see some discussion about possible applications where such uncertainty estimation may prove beneficial. Strengths: This research presents new ways of thinking about predicting MT quality from source sentences. It shows how these probabilities can help improve decision making under uncertain conditions. Weaknesses: There seems to be no clear explanation why they chose to use linear regression instead of other techniques like neural networks as used elsewhere. Also there does not appear any comparison between the two types of algorithms. Finally, I think the results presented here should be compared against those obtained when applying similar techniques to human translations rather than just comparing against random guesses.

The authors have developed two useful applications that can be used to analyze sentiment in tweets or app reviews. The first, App2Check is an application which allows users to upload their own apps (or those from other sources) along with user generated comments on these apps into a database where they are analyzed using various techniques including text mining algorithms. Tweet2Check uses similar methods but focuses more specifically on analyzing Twitter data related to specific topics such as movies/TV shows/music etc.. Both systems use supervised classification models trained over large datasets containing positive and negative examples. These results were then compared against human labeled ground truth data sets showing high accuracy rates across all experiments performed by the researchers. Overall this research provides some interesting insights about how people feel towards different products based upon what they say online via social media platforms like twitter and/or through third party websites/app stores. This work could potentially help companies improve customer service levels while also helping them better understand consumer behavior. I would recommend publishing it! The authors present two new applications that use deep neural networks to analyze sentiment in text reviews from mobile apps (app2check) as well as tweets about movies/TV shows/music artists (tweet2check). The results are very promising. Strengths: 1. Both papers provide an in-depth description on how their models work. 2. They also showcase some interesting examples where they apply these techniques. 3. Finally, both papers have good experimental setups with large datasets which makes it easier to reproduce experiments. Weaknesses: No competing approaches were considered against app2check/tweet2check so it's not clear if there is room for improvement over existing methods. Overall I think this research has great potential but more effort should be put into making sure other researchers can easily replicate your findings The authors have done an excellent job with their work. The article provides useful information regarding how they developed these two new programs (Tweet2check & App2Check) along with some interesting findings from using them. I would recommend publishing it! Thanks again! Yes I think it's ready now :) No problem at all if you need any further clarifications. Thanks once again! Yes, I'm happy to let you know when we're going to publish your manuscript. Thank you so much! Best regards, Editor-in-Chief Yes ,we will send you soon .Thank You ! Yes , thanks for letting us know . We'll be waiting for your email then .Best Regards ,Editor-in-Chief Yes ,we'll send you shortly after receiving final approval from other editors .No Problem at All If Need Any Further Clarifications . The authors have done an excellent job with their work. The article provides detailed information regarding how they developed two new tools (Tweet2check & App2Check) using Machine Learning algorithms. They also provide details on how these tools were tested against other existing tools like SentiStrength, TextBlob etc., alongwith providing comparisons between them based upon various parameters including accuracy rate achieved during testing phase. I would recommend publishing it after making some minor changes suggested below: 1) It will be good if you can add few screenshots showing how your tool works when applied over real world data sets i.e. Twitter feeds and/or Android application store reviews. This may help readers understand better how exactly does your system work? 2) You should mention whether any kind of pre-processing was performed before feeding input text into each algorithm used within your proposed framework

This paper proposes a new approach for low-resource POS tagging using cross-lingual projection. The approach is evaluated on the WSJ and the neighborhoods of out-of-vocabulary words, and the results are compared with other state-of-the-art methods. The paper is well-written and the results are interesting. However, there are some concerns that should be addressed before the paper is accepted. First, it is not clear how the cross-lingual projection is learned. The paper mentions that "a method of moments is used to learn the linear projections", but it is not clear how this is done. Second, it is not clear how the cross-lingual projection is used to improve low-resource POS tagging. The paper mentions that "the cross-lingual projection is used to correct errors made by the distant supervision", but it is not clear how this is done. Third, it is not clear what "a method of moments" is. The paper mentions that "a method of moments is used to learn the linear projections", but it is not clear what this means. This paper addresses the problem of how to learn when to trust distant supervision in low-resource POS tagging using cross-lingual projection. The paper's strength lies in its thorough experimental evaluation. The paper's weakness is that it does not provide a clear explanation of how the cross-lingual projection is used to learn when to trust distant supervision. This paper introduces a novel approach to sequence tagging that learns to correct the errors from cross-lingual projection using an explicit debiasing layer. This is framed as joint learning over two corpora, one tagged with gold standard and the other with projected tags. The evaluation with only 1,000 tokens tagged with gold standard tags, along with more plentiful parallel data, shows that the proposed method equals or exceeds the state-of-the-art on eight simulated low-resource settings, as well as two real low-resource languages, Malagasy and Kinyarwanda. The main contribution of this paper is a novel approach to sequence tagging that learns to correct the errors from cross-lingual projection using an explicit debiasing layer. This is framed as joint learning over two corpora, one tagged with gold standard and the other with projected tags. The evaluation with only 1,000 tokens tagged with gold standard tags, along with more plentiful parallel data, shows that the proposed method equals or exceeds the state-of-the-art on eight simulated low-resource settings, as well as two real low-resource languages, Malagasy and Kinyarwanda. The proposed method is evaluated on eight simulated low-resource settings, as well as two real low-resource languages, Malagasy and Kinyarwanda, and achieves state-of-the-art results. This paper addresses the problem of learning when to trust distant supervision in low-resource settings. Strengths: - The paper proposes a novel approach to sequence tagging that learns to correct the errors from cross-lingual projection using an explicit debiasing layer. - The evaluation results show that the proposed method achieves state-of-the-art performance on eight simulated low-resource settings, as well as two real low-resource languages, Malagasy and Kinyarwanda. Weaknesses: - The paper does not include any human evaluation or user studies to validate the effectiveness of the proposed method. - The paper does not discuss any negative impacts of the proposed method. - The paper does not explore any other methods for correcting the errors from cross-lingual projection.

The paper "Epitomic Variational Autoencoders" presents a new approach to variational autoencoders (VAEs) that uses epigenetic loss to regularize the training process. The paper is well-written and provides a clear explanation of the new approach. The results are interesting and show that the new approach can improve the quality of VAEs. However, there are some areas where the paper could be improved. First, it is not clear how the new approach is different from other VAE regularization methods. The paper does not provide a clear comparison between the new approach and other methods. Second, the results are not fully convincing. The paper only shows results for one type of data and does not show results for other types of data. Third, the paper does not provide enough details about the implementation of the new approach. The paper should include more details about the architecture of the new approach and the training process. This paper addresses the problem of epitomic variational autoencoders, and how they can be used to learn representations of words and phrases. Strengths: -The paper does a good job of explaining the problem and how epitomic variational autoencoders can be used to learn representations of words and phrases. Weaknesses: -The paper could have more detail about how the results were obtained, and what the data set was. This paper proposes a new variational autoencoder (VAE) architecture called Epitomic VAE (eVAE). eVAE is composed of a number of sparse VAEs called "epitomes," where each epitome shares part of its encoder-decoder architecture with other epitomes in the composition. The main advantage of the proposed approach is that it avoids the common problem in VAEs of model over-pruning, which degrades their performance. The paper substantiates that eVAE is efficient in using its model capacity and generalizes better than VAE, by presenting qualitative and quantitative results on MNIST and TFD datasets. Overall, the paper is well-written and the proposed model is interesting and has potential. However, there are some aspects that could be improved. First, it is not clear how the proposed model differs from other VAE architectures that have been proposed in the past, such as hierarchical VAEs or dynamic VAEs. Second, the paper does not provide a detailed description of the model architecture, which makes it difficult to understand how the model works. Third, the paper does not provide enough empirical results to justify the claims made about the superiority of the proposed model over other VAE architectures. This paper addresses the problem of efficiently using a probabilistic generative model's capacity to generalize. Strengths: The paper clearly defines the problem being addressed and why it is important. It also proposes a clear solution and evaluates it with both qualitative and quantitative results on two datasets. Weaknesses: While the evaluation results are generally positive, they could be stronger. For example, it would be helpful to see more results on different datasets or more detailed evaluation results.

This paper proposes SEM, a simple large-size multilabel learning algorithm which models the probability of each label as softmax(sigmoid(W^T X) + b), so a one-layer hidden network. This in and of itself is not novel, nor is the idea of optimizing this by adagrad. Though it's weird that the paper explicitly derives the gradient and suggests doing alternating adagrad steps instead of the more standard adagrad steps; it's unclear whether this matters at all for performance. The main trick responsible for increasing the efficiency of this model is the candidate label sampling, which is done in a relatively standard way by sampling labels proportionally to their frequency in the dataset. Given that neither the model nor the training strategy is novel, it's surprising that the results are better than the state-of-the-art in quality and efficiency (though non-asymptotic efficiency claims are always questionable since implementation effort trades off fairly well against performance). I feel like this paper doesn't quite meet the bar. This is largely a well written paper proposing a sensible approach for multilabel learning that is shown to be effective in practice. However, the main technical elements of this work: the model used and its connections to basic MLPs and related methods in the literature, the optimization strategy, and the speedup tricks are all familiar from prior work. Hence the reviewers are somewhat unanimous in their view that the novelty aspect of this paper is its main shortcomings. The authors are encouraged to revise the paper and clarify the precise contributions. The paper proposes a semantic embedding based approach to multilabel classification. Conversely to previous proposals, SEM considers the underlying parameters determining the observed labels are low-rank rather than that the observed label matrix is itself low-rank. However, It is not clear to what extent the difference between the two assumptions is significant SEM models the labels for an instance as draws from a multinomial distribution parametrized by nonlinear functions of the instance features. As such, it is a neural network. The proposed training algorithm is slightly more complicated than vanilla backprop. The significance of the results compared to NNML (in particular on large datasets Delicious and EUrlex) is not very clear. The paper is well written and the main idea is clearly presented. However, the experimental results are not significant enough to compensate the lack of conceptual novelty. The paper presents the semantic embedding model for multi-label prediction. In my questions, I pointed that the proposed approach assumes the number of labels to predict is known, and the authors said this was an orthogonal question, although I don't think it is! I was trying to understand how different is SEM from a basic MLP with softmax output which would be trained with a two step approach instead of stochastic gradient descent. It seems reasonable given their similarity to compare to this very basic baseline. Regarding the sampling strategy to estimate the posterior distribution, and the difference with Jean et al, I agree it is slightly different but I think you should definitely refer to it and point to the differences. One last question: why is it called "semantic" embeddings? usually this term is used to show some semantic meaning between trained embeddings, but this doesn't seem to appear in this paper. Peer review of "ENet: A Deep Neural Network Architecture for Real-Time Semantic Segmentation" In this paper, the authors proposed a new deep neural network architecture named "ENet" for real-time semantic segmentation of images. The design of ENet is based on a compact and efficient model that performs well in terms of accuracy and speed. Overall, the paper is well-written, and the proposed method is presented clearly. However, there are several concerns that need to be addressed before this work can be considered ready for publication. First, the authors do not provide a comprehensive comparison of their method with existing state-of-the-art methods on widely used benchmark datasets. Although the authors demonstrated the effectiveness of the proposed method on two small datasets, notable challenges exist when scaling up the method to larger, more complex datasets, such as PASCAL VOC and Cityscapes. A comprehensive evaluation of the proposed method on such benchmark datasets will strengthen the contribution of this work. Second, more detailed information on the hyperparameters used in the proposed method, such as the optimizer, learning rate, weight decay, and batch size, would be helpful for readers interested in reproducing the results presented in the paper. In addition, a description of the computational resources required to train and test the model would be useful for researchers attempting to implement the approach. Third, the authors provide limited discussion of the limitations of the proposed method. While the paper demonstrates its effectiveness on a few datasets, there may be other scenarios where the proposed method may not perform well, and this warrants further investigation. Overall, I believe that this paper presents a promising method for real-time semantic segmentation, but further improvements and evaluations are required before it can be recognized as a significant contribution to the field. The paper "ENet: A Deep Neural Network Architecture for Real-Time Semantic Segmentation" addresses the problem of semantic segmentation in real-time. Semantic segmentation is a process of assigning a semantic label to every pixel in an image, making it an important task for various computer vision applications such as self-driving cars, surveillance, and medical imaging. However, the real-time processing of high-resolution images causes significant computational expense, making it a challenging issue to address. The paper presents a novel architecture called ENet, which is specifically designed to perform real-time semantic segmentation. The authors integrate and adapt various techniques from other architectures, including encoder-decoder networks and residual connections, while also proposing a new bottleneck layer to reduce computation costs. The authors extensively evaluate ENet on multiple benchmark datasets and compare its performance to other state-of-the-art segmentation methods. Strengths: - The authors propose a novel architecture specifically designed for real-time semantic segmentation. ENet is optimized for memory usage and computation, resulting in significant speed improvements compared to previous methods. - The experimental evaluation is thorough and includes comparisons to state-of-the-art methods on multiple benchmark datasets. The results demonstrate that ENet achieves better performance or similar results while being significantly faster than other methods. - The authors provide detailed visualizations of the segmentation and intermediate activations, allowing the reader to better understand the network's behavior. Weaknesses: - The authors do not provide a comprehensive analysis of the network's limitations or failure modes. While they discuss some potential issues, more analysis would have helped to understand where and why the network may struggle. - The description of the proposed architecture and its components can be dense and difficult to follow, making it challenging for readers with limited experience in deep learning to understand the network's design. Overall, the paper "ENet: A Deep Neural Network Architecture for Real-Time Semantic Segmentation" presents a significant contribution to the field of real-time semantic segmentation. The proposed architecture demonstrates remarkable speed and competitive performance, making it suitable for various real-time applications. The thorough experimental evaluation and visualizations make this paper an excellent reference for researchers in the field. Review for "ENet: A Deep Neural Network Architecture for Real-Time Semantic Segmentation" Summary: The paper introduces a new deep neural network architecture, named ENet (efficient neural network), which is designed for real-time semantic segmentation tasks. Compared to existing models, ENet requires 75x less FLOPs, has 79x less parameters, and is up to 18x faster while providing similar or better accuracy. The authors demonstrate the effectiveness of ENet on standard datasets and provide performance measurements on embedded systems. Possible software improvements to further increase the processing speed of ENet are also discussed. Strengths: 1. The paper addresses an important problem in mobile applications where real-time pixel-wise semantic segmentation is required. 2. The proposed ENet architecture is specifically designed for low latency operation and outperforms existing models in terms of speed, accuracy, and parameters required. 3. The authors provide a comprehensive evaluation of ENet on several standard datasets and demonstrate its effectiveness in real-world applications. 4. The performance measurements on embedded systems provide insights into the practicality of ENet in mobile devices. Weaknesses: 1. The paper lacks detailed explanations of the specific architectural and operational differences between ENet and existing models, making it difficult to understand the reasons behind ENet's superior efficiency. 2. The authors could have performed a more in-depth analysis of the trade-offs between accuracy and processing time of ENet, especially in scenarios where strict real-time requirements may not be necessary. 3. Some of the figures and tables in the paper have low quality, making it difficult to read and interpret the results. Overall, the paper presents a novel deep neural network architecture for real-time semantic segmentation tasks, demonstrating its effectiveness and practicality on standard datasets and embedded systems. However, more detailed explanations of the architecture and analysis of the trade-offs between accuracy and processing time could be added to improve the clarity and completeness of the paper. Paper review: ENet: A Deep Neural Network Architecture for Real-Time Semantic Segmentation Problem statement: The paper addresses the critical problem of semantic segmentation in real-time applications such as autonomous driving and augmented reality. The existing deep neural networks for semantic segmentation are often computationally expensive, making them unsuitable for low-powered devices. Therefore, the authors propose a novel neural network architecture, ENet, specifically designed for semantic segmentation tasks requiring low latency operation. Strengths: 1. The paper proposes an innovative neural network architecture, ENet, which outperforms existing state-of-the-art models in terms of speed, efficiency, and accuracy of semantic segmentation. 2. The authors provide a detailed comparison of the proposed model with existing models on various datasets, including CamVid, Cityscapes, and SUN datasets. The results demonstrate that ENet performs significantly better than other models while requiring fewer resources. 3. The paper presents a thorough analysis of the proposed architecture’s performance on embedded systems, and the results show that it outperforms other models even on low-powered devices. Weaknesses: 1. Although the authors provide a comprehensive performance comparison between ENet and existing models, they do not perform the same analysis on all datasets, which may raise questions regarding the generalizability of ENet. 2. The claims made in the paper regarding the efficiency and accuracy of ENet are based on experiments conducted on specific datasets and may not be generalizable to other datasets. Overall, the paper presents an important contribution to the field of semantic segmentation, specifically addressing the need for real-time performance in low-power devices. The authors provide a novel architecture, ENet, which significantly improves the speed, accuracy, and resource efficiency of semantic segmentation. However, further analysis and experimentation are required to ensure that the proposed architecture’s claims hold true for other datasets as well.

This paper presents a novel approach to host-based intrusion detection using LSTM-based system-call language modeling and ensemble methods. The authors propose a two-stage detection framework that combines LSTM-based language modeling with an ensemble of machine learning classifiers. The authors evaluate the proposed framework on two publicly available datasets and demonstrate that it outperforms existing approaches in terms of accuracy and F1-score. The paper is well-structured and the authors provide a clear explanation of the proposed approach. The authors also provide a detailed description of the datasets and the evaluation metrics used. The experimental results are clearly presented and discussed. The authors have done a good job of presenting their approach and the results of their experiments. However, there are a few areas that could be improved. First, the authors could provide more details about the implementation of the proposed framework, such as the exact parameters used in the LSTM model and the exact hyperparameters used for the ensemble. Second, the authors could provide more details about the datasets used, such as the exact number of samples and the exact distribution of the classes. Finally, the authors could provide more details about the evaluation metrics used, such as the exact formulas used to calculate the metrics. Overall, this is a well-written paper that presents a novel approach to host-based intrusion detection. The authors provide a clear explanation of their approach and the results of their experiments are clearly presented and discussed. With some minor improvements, this paper could be an excellent contribution to the field. This paper addresses the problem of host-based intrusion detection using an ensemble method based on Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM) system-call language modeling. The authors propose an approach that uses a combination of LSTM and ensemble methods to detect intrusions in a host-based system. Strengths: The paper provides a detailed description of the proposed method, including an overview of the data preprocessing and feature engineering techniques used. The authors also provide a thorough evaluation of the proposed method, comparing it to several existing methods. Furthermore, the authors provide an analysis of the results, discussing the advantages and disadvantages of their approach. Weaknesses: The paper does not provide any information about the implementation details of the proposed method, such as the specific parameters used. Additionally, the authors do not provide any information about the scalability of the proposed method. Finally, the authors do not discuss any potential applications of their method in the real world. This paper presents a novel system-call language-modeling approach for designing anomaly-based host intrusion detection systems. The authors propose an ensemble method that blends multiple thresholding classifiers into a single one in order to reduce false-alarm rates. The proposed system-call language model has various advantages leveraged by the fact that it can learn the semantic meaning and interactions of each system call. Through experiments on public benchmark datasets, the authors demonstrate the validity and effectiveness of the proposed method. The paper is well-written and the proposed method is clearly described. The authors provide a comprehensive overview of the related work and clearly explain the proposed approach. The experiments and results are also presented in a clear and concise manner. The paper could be improved by providing more details about the proposed ensemble method and how it works. In addition, the authors should provide more details about the datasets used in the experiments and the metrics used to evaluate the performance of the proposed method. Overall, this is a well-written paper with interesting ideas and results. This paper addresses the problem of designing a robust intrusion detection system for computer security. It proposes a system-call language-modeling approach for designing anomaly-based host intrusion detection systems and a novel ensemble method that blends multiple thresholding classifiers into a single one. The paper has several strengths. First, it provides a comprehensive overview of the related work and describes the proposed system-call language model in detail. Second, the paper includes a thorough analysis of the performance of the proposed method on public benchmark datasets. Third, the paper shows that the proposed model has high portability, which is an important factor for successful intrusion detection systems. The paper also has some weaknesses. First, the paper does not provide a detailed description of the implementation of the proposed system-call language model. Second, the paper does not compare the proposed method with other existing methods for intrusion detection. Third, the paper does not discuss the scalability of the proposed method.

This paper proposed to use unsupervised learning to learn features in a reinforcement learning setting. It is unclear what "unsupervised" means here since the "causality prior" uses reward signals for training. This is reinforcement learning, not unsupervised learning. The experiments are also very premature. The task is as simple as moving the head of the robot left or right. There is also no comparison to baselines. In conclusions section, the authors claim the proposed method can be used for transfer learning without experiments to backup the claim. Overall this paper is confusing and premature. The authors apply an already-published method for state representation learning in a very simple experimental scenario. They give no additional contribution or comparison, nor do they offer any empirical or analytical study. Dear reviewers, we agreed that comparisons with other works need to be done and that other tasks have to be tested to improve evaluation and validation. It will be done in future experiments. This paper implements the method of Jonschkowski & Brock to learn a low-dimensional state representation represented as the last layer of a neural network. The experiments apply the method for learning a one-dimensional state representation of a simulated robot’s head position from synthetic images. Learning state representations is an active and useful area of research for learning representations in interactive domains such as robotics. However, there seems to be no novelty in the method, over Jonschkowki & Brock. The primary contribution is the experimental evaluation performed on one task, where the paper evaluates the correlation between the learned state representation and the ideal state representation for the task (which is the robot’s head position). As acknowledged by the authors, the experiments are very preliminary, only showing one simple task with a one-dimensional learned representation and a two-dimensional discrete action space. To make the experiments compelling, there need to be comparisons to prior methods such as Lange et al. ’12, Watter et al. NIPS ’15, and Finn et al. ICRA ’16 which also learn state representations from raw images. PCA on the images would also be a useful comparison, especially for simple tasks. Without these comparisons, it is impossible to evaluate the effectiveness of the method. Lastly, as mentioned in the pre-review questions, the related work should include a discussion of other state representation learning methods such as Watter et al. NIPS ’15, Finn et al. ICRA ’16, and van Hoof et al. IROS ’16. In summary, this paper lacks novelty and significance, as the paper implements an existing method and demonstrates results on only one simple task. Without comparisons, the results are impossible to interpret. More challenging tasks and experimental comparisons would significantly improve the paper. Additionally, this paper does not introduce any novel contributions to state representation learning for solving challenges in this domain. One pro is that the paper is generally written clearly. The paper proposes to use the representation learning approach of [Jonschkowski & Brock, 2015] with a deep network as function approximator. The general task and approach are interesting, but contribution of this work is limited, and experimental evaluation is absolutely unsatisfactory, so the paper cannot be accepted for publications. The approach is tested on a simple synthetic task with very small training and test sets and very little variation in the data. The authors admitted themselves that the results are preliminary. The proposed method is not compared with existing approaches or simple hand-crafted baselines. It is impossible to judge if the proposed method is useful and/or performs well compared to existing approaches. This makes the paper unfit for publication. With proper experiments, and if the method works in interesting realistic scenarios, this could become a good paper. This paper proposed to use unsupervised learning to learn features in a reinforcement learning setting. It is unclear what "unsupervised" means here since the "causality prior" uses reward signals for training. This is reinforcement learning, not unsupervised learning. The experiments are also very premature. The task is as simple as moving the head of the robot left or right. There is also no comparison to baselines. In conclusions section, the authors claim the proposed method can be used for transfer learning without experiments to backup the claim. Overall this paper is confusing and premature. In conclusions section, the authors claim the proposed method can be used for transfer learning without experiments to backup the claim. Overall this paper is confusing and premature. The authors apply an already-published method for state representation learning in a very simple experimental scenario. They give no additional contribution or comparison, nor do they offer any empirical or analytical study. Dear reviewers, we agreed that comparisons with other works need to be done and that other tasks have to be tested to improve evaluation and validation. It will be done in future experiments.
