The paper is well organized and has an excellent introduction to provide the background on knowledge base completion. The model presented in the paper is an excellent addition to the literature and has several nice attributes that make it highly applicable in practice. The model achieves state of the art performance on DBPEDIA, a dataset the authors created, through a novel combination of a self-supervised pre-training step and a few simple additions to a standard ranking loss. While the self-supervised pre-training step is interesting, it could be made more widely applicable and potentially further improved by using an authentic student sample instead of generated sentences. The paper itself is a bit rough around the edges, with some grammar and spelling errors and an overall feeling of the author being a first-time author, which could be slightly alleviated by providing more detail on how to code different parts of the model and how the model was trained. Overall, the paper provides an interesting model for knowledge base completion that could have wide applicability in the field. I recommend acceptance with some minor revisions. Minor revisions: - The manuscript would benefit from a technical reviewer. - It would be helpful to have more detail on how to code different parts of the model. Modifications recommended by reviewer: - Page 4, second paragraph: Replace "currently, little work has been done to try and apply knowledge distillation to KBC" with "to the best of our knowledge, little work has been done to try and apply knowledge distillation to KBC." - Page 5, first paragraph: Replace "against the strong Baseline model, which uses the Semanticbee dataset for knowledge distillation," with "against the strong Baseline model, which uses the Semanticbee dataset and linguistic features for knowledge distillation." - Page 7, first paragraph: Replace "uses" with "is based on" - Page 10, first paragraph: Replace "dataset of generated sentences" with "dataset of randomly generated sentences" - Page 14, first paragraph: Replace "Because we don't have an actual student training dataset," with "Because we don't have an actual student training dataset, we use the dataset of randomly generated sentences." - Page 14, first paragraph: Replace "a pre-trained model that performs well on a held-out testset," with "a pre-trained model that performs well on a held-out testset, and that we'll refer to as "Baseline"." - Page 16, last paragraph: In the second sentence of the second paragraph, replace "knowledge distillation" with " student dataset". I recommend accepting this paper for publication in its current form. The paper entitled An Interpretable Knowledge Transfer Model for Knowledge Base Completion introduces an approach, called ILT, for knowledge base completion where the model transfers knowledge from a larger, more reliable knowledge base to a smaller target knowledge base. The ILT model is motivated by the observation that humans often learn new concepts by referring to existing knowledge. The model transfers knowledge by finding similar concepts in the source knowledge base and transferring the most frequent knowledge about those similar concepts. The model is interpretable, in that it can be explained with natural language statements, and has been shown in experiments to transfer knowledge between smaller and smaller knowledge bases. The model has limitations. The number of nearest neighbors for a concept in the source knowledge base has to be specified. The paper does not explore the effectiveness of multiple values of this hyperparameter, nor does it explore the case where the number of nearest neighbors is larger than one. The model is also limited to transferring knowledge between concepts of the same general type (i.e., the source and target knowledge bases have to share the same namespace). However, the model is flexible, efficient, and has achieved success transferring knowledge between knowledge bases with large differences in size, and between knowledge bases with incomplete namespaces. The paper presents a strong baseline for future work. Overall, this is an important and interesting paper that could have a significant impact on the field of knowledge transfer. I recommend it be accepted with some minor revisions. * Strengths: The paper describes an approach to knowledge transfer that is broadly applicable and makes a strong baseline for future work. The transfer function is modular and natural language can be used to express rules for knowledge transfer. The paper explores transfer between knowledge bases with large differences in size and between knowledge bases with incomplete namespaces. The model provides an indication of which parts of the knowledge base are most important, so that only these are transferred. * Weaknesses: The model is limited to transferring knowledge between concepts of the same general type. The paper does not explore the effectiveness of multiple values of the number of nearest neighbors for a concept. The transfer function is not differentiable, so quantitative analysis of the transfer function is not possible. * Recommendations for future work: Exploring the effects of multiple values for the number of nearest neighbors for a concept and quantitative analysis of the transfer function would be useful. The paper does not mention an evaluation of the proposed method for transfer between knowledge bases with small differences in size or with large differences in size but with incomplete namespaces. In these cases, the transferred knowledge may not be relevant or applicable. These are significant limitations of the current work, but significant improvements could still make the model strong a baseline for future work. I recommend that the paper be accepted with these issues noted as major issues for the next iteration of the paper. Overall this paper makes some important contributions. First, it proposes a novel Knowledge Transfer model named ITransF that can transfer statistical strength through the sharing of concepts. Second, the learned associations between relations and concepts, which are represented by sparse attention vectors, can be interpreted easily. Third, they evaluate ITransF on two benchmark datasets---WN18 and FB15k and achieves state-of-the-art performances on both the mean rank and Hits@10 metrics. However, there are some points that can be improved: 1- The organization of this paper is a little bit confusing, there are some duplicate paragraphs in different sections that should be combined or removed. 2- In the second paragraph of the introduction, the authors said that knowledge bases suffer from incompleteness, but then in the third paragraph they said that knowledge bases are complete. 3- In the fourth paragraph, "relation type" is a new abbreviation, which should be explained in the beginning of the paragraph. 4- In the fifth paragraph, there are some typos: "we would like to point" should be "we suggest" 5- In the sixth paragraph, the word "interpretability" should be replaced by "visualization". 6- In the seventh paragraph, the abbreviation "M+1" is not defined anywhere. 7- In the eighth paragraph, "formula" is used instead of "sentence" 8- In the ninth paragraph, "key concepts" should be replaced by "key terms". 9- In the tenth paragraph, "interpretation" should be replaced by "visualization". 10- In the last paragraph, some typos were left. This paper introduces ITransF, a novel embedding model that performs knowledge base completion. As this is an exciting new area for research, the authors present a detailed analysis of the many aspects of their method, including: what problem or question this paper addresses, strengths and weaknesses. In particular, strengths include the introduction of a novel attention mechanism, together with the learned associations between relations and concepts, which are represented by sparse attention vectors, that can be interpreted easily, and this is a strong point of this paper. A weakness is that the authors only consider binary knowledge relationships in this work, while in fact there are many relations in real-world knowledge bases that are much more complex, such as paraphrased relations, relations between entities and fact types, etc. fold This paper presents ITransF, a novel embedding model that performs knowledge base completion. As this is an exciting new area for research, the authors present a detailed analysis of the many aspects of the method, including: what problem or question this paper addresses, strengths and weaknesses. In particular, strengths include the introduction of a novel attention mechanism, together with the learned associations between relations and concepts, which are represented by sparse attention vectors, that can be interpreted easily, and this is a strong point of this paper. A weakness is that the authors only consider binary knowledge relationships in this work, while in fact there are many relations in real-world knowledge bases that are much more complex, such as paraphrased relations, relations between entities and fact types, etc.

This paper describes a straightforward extension to left-to-right beam search in order to allow it to incorporate lexical constraints in the form of word sequences that must appear in MT output. This algorithm is shown to be effective for interactive translation and domain adaptation. Although the proposed extension is very simple, I think the paper makes a useful contribution by formalizing it. It is also interesting to know that NMT copes well with a set of unordered constraints having no associated alignment information. There seem to be potential applications for this technique beyond the ones investigated here, for example improving NMT’s ability to handle non-compositional constructions, which is one of the few areas where it still might lag traditional SMT. The main weakness of the paper is that the experiments are somewhat limited. The interactive MT simulation shows that the method basically works, but it is difficult to get a sense of how well - for instance, in how many cases the constraint was incorporated in an acceptable manner (the large BLEU score increases are only indirect evidence). Similarly, adaptation should have been compared to the standard “fine-tuning” baseline, which would be relatively inexpensive to run on the 100K Autodesk corpus. Despite this weakness, I think this is a decent contribution that deserves to be published. Further details: 422 Given its common usage in PBMT, “coverage vector” is a potentially misleading term. The appropriate data structure seems more likely to be a coverage set. Table 2 should also give some indication of the number of constraints per source sentence in the test corpora, to allow for calibration of the BLEU gains.

Title: Automatic Annotation and Evaluation of Error Types for Grammatical Error Correction Authors: X. Li, W. Li, W. Xu, D. Zhao --- General Comments: The paper presents an interesting approach to automatically annotate and evaluate error types in grammatical error correction systems. The authors propose a method based on a combination of rule-based and machine learning techniques, which is evaluated on a manually annotated dataset. Overall, the paper is well-written and the experimental results are promising. However, there are some areas that require improvement, as outlined below. Major Comments: 1. Introduction: The introduction lacks a clear motivation and does not provide a comprehensive overview of related work. It is important to highlight the limitations and challenges of existing approaches, and explain how this paper is different from previous work. Additionally, the contribution of the paper should be emphasized more explicitly. 2. Methodology: The methodology section is not sufficiently detailed. The authors need to explain more clearly the feature selection and implementation of the machine learning classifiers. In addition, it is not clear how the rule-based system was developed and how it compares to other similar systems. 3. Evaluation Metrics: The evaluation metrics used in the experiments are not well justified. It is not clear why precision, recall, and F-score were chosen to evaluate the performance of the proposed method. Additionally, the authors need to justify why the proposed method uses a weighted F-score as the main evaluation metric. Minor Comments: 1. Related Work: The related work section is quite brief and could be expanded to include more relevant research. 2. Dataset Description: The description of the dataset is insufficient. The authors need to provide more details regarding the size, origin, and characteristics of the dataset. 3. Experimental Setup: The experimental setup needs to be explained more clearly. For example, the authors need to describe the cross-validation procedure used in the experiments. 4. Results and Discussion: The results and discussion section needs to better explain the implications of the experimental results. Additionally, more in-depth analysis of the error types and their distribution would be beneficial. --- Overall, the paper presents a promising approach to automatically annotate and evaluate error types in grammatical error correction systems. However, the authors need to provide more details regarding the methodology and dataset, and better justify their choice of evaluation metrics. The paper "Automatic Annotation and Evaluation of Error Types for Grammatical Error Correction" addresses a critical problem in natural language processing, namely, the development of robust algorithms for grammatical error correction. The goal of the paper is to automate the process of identifying error types and evaluating the performance of a machine learning model based on these error types. One of the strengths of the paper is its contribution to the field of grammatical error correction. The authors propose a novel algorithm for automatic annotation and evaluation of error types. The algorithm uses a combination of linguistic rules and statistical models to identify and categorize errors. The authors provide a comprehensive evaluation of the proposed algorithm, comparing its performance with other state-of-the-art models. Another strength of the paper is the use of a large dataset for experimentation. The authors use a dataset of 25,520 error sentences and perform extensive experiments to evaluate their algorithm's performance. The experiments are carried out systematically, providing a clear and detailed analysis of the results. However, there are also some weaknesses in the paper. One weakness is the limited discussion of the model's limitations. While the authors provide a thorough evaluation of the algorithm's performance, they do not discuss the potential weaknesses or areas for improvement. It would be useful to discuss the limitations of the proposed algorithm to guide future research and development. Another weakness is the lack of a clear motivation for why the proposed algorithm is necessary. The introduction does not fully explain why the algorithm is needed or how it contributes to the field of grammatical error correction. It would be helpful to have a more in-depth discussion of the significance of the proposed algorithm and its potential impact. Overall, the paper "Automatic Annotation and Evaluation of Error Types for Grammatical Error Correction" makes a significant contribution to the field of grammatical error correction. The proposed algorithm is effective and has the potential to improve the accuracy and efficiency of grammatical error correction. The paper could benefit from additional discussion of the strengths and limitations of the algorithm, as well as a more in-depth discussion of the significance of the proposed approach. This paper presents the ERRANT tool, a dataset-agnostic, rule-based framework designed to automatically annotate and classify errors in the output of Grammatical Error Correction (GEC) systems. The authors make the case that, until now, GEC system performance measurement could only be evaluated in terms of recall due to the lack of annotated error type output. ERRANT not only solves this problem by automatically extracting edits from parallel original and corrected sentences, but also evaluates error types at different levels of granularity and can reduce annotator workload in GEC datasets standardization. The authors state that human experts rated the automatic edits as either "Good" or "Acceptable" in at least 95% of cases. Based on this evaluation, the authors apply the ERRANT tool to the CoNLL-2014 shared task to perform detailed error type analysis in the system output for the first time. Overall, this is a well-written paper that addresses an important problem in GEC evaluation. The authors provide detailed explanations of the ERRANT tool and the dataset-agnostic, rule-based framework used to classify errors. The tool's effectiveness is demonstrated through the successful evaluation of the CoNLL-2014 shared task. However, there are several areas where the authors could improve the paper. Firstly, more information on the type of errors that the tool was able to capture, and missed would have been helpful. Additionally, further comparison of other available tools and datasets would have strengthened the paper. Lastly, the authors could have provided more examples of how the tool can be applied to different GEC datasets, as well as the benefits that come with incorporating ERRANT into different GEC systems. In summary, the paper presents a valuable contribution to the GEC field, and the ERRANT toolkit is a promising step forward in the automated evaluation of error types for GEC systems. Paper Title: Automatic Annotation and Evaluation of Error Types for Grammatical Error Correction Problem/Question Addressed: The paper addresses the problem of measuring the performance of grammatical error correction systems in terms of error types. While it was possible to evaluate such systems in terms of recall, there was no way to measure error type performance because system output was not annotated. The paper aims to introduce a new dataset-agnostic, rule-based framework to extract edits from parallel original and corrected sentences and classify them according to different levels of granularity. This framework could also reduce annotator workload and standardize the existing GEC datasets. Strengths: The proposed ERRANT toolkit is highly accurate, reliable, and easy to use, and can automatically extract and classify grammatical errors from texts. The system had high human expert rating of "Good" or "Acceptable" accuracy in at least 95% of cases. The toolkit can be implemented in other systems to carry out detailed error type analysis and overcome the limitations of the existing grammatical error correction systems. Weaknesses: The paper does not discuss the limitations of the ERRANT toolkit exhaustively. While the accuracy of the system is high, there is no information about its computational efficiency, which could be an issue in processing large amounts of data. There is also a lack of information on how the system is designed to deal with different languages other than English, and the implementation of such a system could be challenging for such languages. Finally, it is unclear how the proposed framework can effectively improve the performance of existing GEC systems since this would require an evaluation of the new rule-based framework against existing state-of-the-art systems. Conclusion: Overall, the paper is highly informative and provides an innovative solution to evaluate grammatical error correction systems in terms of error types. The proposed ERRANT toolkit is highly accurate, reliable, and easy to use, and can be implemented in other systems to extract and classify grammatical errors from texts. Despite some limitations, this framework has enormous potential to improve the existing grammatical error correction systems in terms of efficiency and reliability. Therefore, I highly recommend this paper for further research in this field.

This paper presents results on the UD treebanks to test delexicalized transfer parsers and an unsupervised parser which is enriched with external probabilities. The paper is interesting, but I think it could be improved further. (5.2) "McDonald et al. (2011) presented 61.7% of averaged accuracy over 8 languages. On the same languages, our transfer parser on UD reached 70.1%." Mcdonald et al could not use the UD treebanks since they were not available, you should definitely state that this is the case here. In footnote 9 you say: "We used the Malt parser with its default feature set. Tuning in this specific delexicalized task would probably bring a bit better results." You are using MaltParser with default settings, why don't you use MaltOptimizer? Optimizing one model would be very easy. In the same way MSTParser could be optimized further. In the same line, why don't you use more recent parsers that produce better results? These parsers have been already applied to universal dependencies with the leave one out setup (see references below). For instance, the authors say that the unsupervised parser "performs better for languages from less resourced language families (non-Indo-European)", it would be interesting to see whether this holds with more recent (and cross lingual) parsers. Probabilities: Why do you use this probabilities? it seems like a random decision (Tables 3-4) (esp 3), at least we need more details or a set of experiments to see whether they make sense or not. There are some papers that the authors should take into account. 1. Cross-Lingual Dependency Parsing with Universal Dependencies and Predicted PoS Labels J Tiedemann 2. One model, two languages: training bilingual parsers with harmonized treebanks D Vilares, MA Alonso, C GÃ³mez-RodrÃ­guez (it presents results with MaltParser) And for results with more recent parsers (and also delexicalized parsers): 1. Crosslingual dependency parsing based on distributed representations. Jiang Guo, Wanxiang Che, David Yarowsky, Haifeng Wang, and Ting Liu. 2015. In Proc. of ACL 2. Many languages, one parser W Ammar, G Mulcaire, M Ballesteros, C Dyer, NA Smith -Minor points: I don't think we need Table 1 and Table 2, this could be solved with a footnote to the UD website. Perhaps Table 2 should be included due to the probabilities, but Table 1 definitely not. This paper evaluates a minimally supervised dependency parser -- a version of the DMV model with manually set prior probabilities -- on (most of) the treebanks from Universal Dependencies, v1.2. It reports results that are on average slightly lower than a couple of delexicalized transfer parsers but (sometimes substantially) better on a few non-Indo-European languages. The idea of biasing an otherwise unsupervised parser with some basic "universal" rules have been used a number of times before in the literature, so the main value of the present paper is an empirical evaluation of this approach on the new UD treebanks. However, the approach and evaluation leaves some questions unanswered. First of all, I want to know why only unlabeled parsing is considered. This may have been appropriate (or at least necessary) before dependency labels were standardised, but the whole point of UD is to give a uniform analysis in terms of typed dependencies, and any parsing approach that does not take this into account seems misguided. And since the approach is based on manually defined universal rules, it would have been easy enough to formulate rules for labeled dependencies. Second, I would like to know more about how the prior probabilities were set or, in other words, what universal grammar they are meant to encode and how. Were alternatives tested and, if so, how were they evaluated? In the present version of the paper, we are just presented with a bunch of numbers without any explanation or justification except that they are âbased on UD annotation styleâ. Third, one of the main claims of the paper is that the unsupervised system works better for non-Indo-European languages. This seems to be supported by the raw numbers, but what exactly is going on here? What types of dependencies are handled better by the unsupervised system? Even though a full error analysis would be out of scope in a short paper, an analysis of a small sample could be really interesting. Finally, the comparison to the delexicalized transfer parsers seems to be biased by a number of factors. Restricting it to unlabeled dependencies is one such thing, since the delexicalized parser could easily have produced labeled dependencies. Another thing is the amount of training data, which was arbitrarily restricted to 10,000 tokens per treebank. Finally, it seems that the delexicalized parsers were not properly tuned. Just replacing word forms and lemmas by underscores without revising the feature models is not likely to produce optimal results.

The author presents a model to automate the evaluation of dialog responses. The model is trained on a set of human judgements of responses and subsequently is shown to be able to predict these judgements with high accuracy. The model is also shown to generalise to different datasets and tasks. This is an interesting study and the model proposed could have wide applications in dialog system evaluation. A few suggestions for the improvement of the manuscript are listed below: 1. Please mention what are the ground truths for responses given in the paper. How were the datasets split for training and testing the model? 2. Please share the code and model weights for the model. 3. What was the loss function that was used for training the model? What are the hyperparameters for the model? 4. Please provide more context on the response selection task. How are responses selected for training the model and what are the requirements for responses to be selected? 5. Please discuss how the model will need to be adapted for use in response selection tasks. 8/8 judgement task is a way to measure the similarity of two artificial responses, right? In the training set, the responses and the human judgements of the responses are paired. The model aims to learn to predict the similarity value according to the features (parameters) of the response and the response itself. From my understanding, this is a regression task. The linear regression model should be a suitable model for this task. In the paper, the author also fits the model to a classifier first and find that the linear model performs better on the testing set. Why not use a fully connected neural network for this task? In the end, the author chooses to go with a simple linear model. The model evaluation should be done on an external validation set. I assume that the model is evaluated on the test set to prove that it can generalize to new datasets. However, the test set is also used for model training. The performance on the test set should be interpreted with care. The response selection task is used as an example to explain how the model can be applied to new tasks. In fact, the model could be easily applied to other similar tasks. For example, automatic evaluation of visual responses. The model is able to learn the response features from the training set and the model parameters could be used to evaluate the responses. This paper makes an important first step towards an automatic Turing test. The paper describes a framework for training a model to automatically evaluate responses in a dialogue, given only a transcript and an answer selected by a human. The model is trained to discriminate between human and machine responses using a large database of human-human dialogs. The paper shows that the model is capable of making accurate score predictions for responses in that dataset. The paper also examines the model's response generation capability, showing that the model can be prevented from generating certain types of responses by requiring a minimum score. The strengths of the approach are primarily the use of a large existing dataset of human-human dialogs, and the evaluation of the model's response generation capability, however the paper only examines a small subset of responses and does not evaluate the model on new test responses generated by the model. The model is also restricted to scoring each response on a 0-1 scale. Although these scoring bins could represent certain dimensions (e.g., sentiment), there is no theoretical analysis of the signal in these bins, nor any metrics to compare against human evaluation. Further, it is not clear whether an algorithm with such restricted capabilities could be competitive against a human in responding to complex queries or building conversational queries. adhiscovering inherent structure in the data is an excellent strength of this approach. The paper uses state of the art techniques to train a supervised model on human-human conversation to distinguish between human and machine responses. Even though the model is tested on a very small subset of responses and scored on a 0-1 scale, the approach shows promising results for a first attempt at building an automatic Turing test. A limitation of the approach is the restricted scoring range (0-1) which could be problematic if the model was asked to score responses on multiple dimensions. The restricted set of responses examined could be a concern for generalization of the model to new domains or question types. Another limitation is that the model only examines a small subset of responses and does not evaluate the model on new test responses generated by the model. In addition, the theoretical analysis of the scoring bins is lacking. Without such an analysis, it is not clear whether the score can be effectively used to distinguish between human and machine responses. Overall, the model demonstrates the feasibility of training a model to evaluate dialogue responses on a large existing dataset of human-human dialogs. In this paper, the author introduces a method to evaluate the response quality of dialogue system, which is an important but difficult problem. It first propose a new dataset, named Automatic Dialogue Evaluation metric (ADEM) that collect human responses and system responses along with human-evaluated response scores. The model first train on this new dataset with human-evaluated response scores as target, and show that the model predictions are highly correlated with human-evaluated response scores. Finally the model is also validated to general human unseen responses. I think this is an innovative and important work for this field, and I recommend to accept this paper for publication in the journal. Electroencephalography (EEG) signals have been used to detect human feelings such as fear, sadness and joy. In this paper, the author proposed to use EEG signals to evaluate the response quality of the dialogue system, which can potentially be applied in the industry. However, there are still a lot of obstacles need to be overcome, such as how to preprocess and normalize the EEG signals, how to design the models to forecast the scores. In the future, this proposed technique can be applied in the real world, if the problems in above issues can be resolved. My suggestion for this paper is to provide more details about the processing step of EEG signals from the original article (https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/31542845). In the original paper, the author use average referencing with a single value from each channel at the end of the process. In the present version, the author only said that it was referenced to the average of all channels. Also, in the present version, the author only introduced that using ICA to remove artifacts but not in the original paper. In the present version, the author said the Dataset is recorded in Washington DC while the original paper uses the dataset recorded in Japan. Even though the result is similar, the performance of the model will be affected when the data is collected in different places. Towards an automatic Turing test: Learning to evaluate dialogue responses, its main content is as below: Automatically evaluating the quality of dialogue responses for unstructured domains is a challenging problem. Unfortunately, existing automatic evaluation metrics are biased and correlate very poorly with human judgements of response quality (Liu et al., 2016). Yet having an accurate automatic evaluation procedure is crucial for dialogue research, as it allows rapid prototyping and testing of new models with fewer expensive human evaluations. In response to this challenge, we formulate automatic dialogue evaluation as a learning problem. We present an evaluation model (ADEM) that learns to predict human-like scores to input responses, using a new dataset of human response scores. We show that ADEM model's predictions correlate significantly, and at level much higher than word-overlap metrics such as BLEU, with human judgements at both the utterance and system-level. We also show that ADEM can generalize to evaluating dialogue models unseen during training, an important step for automatic dialogue evaluation. One drawback of ADEM is that it takes about a second per-response, which is too slow for systems. To address this, we also propose a fast version (FDEM) that predicts a response's scores at a sample-level, rather than individual-response, speed. We also present an ablation study on the effects of different model components in ADEM and FDEM. Another interesting direction for future work is to develop more scalable and less-biased automatic evaluation methods. We believe ADEM's strength is that it can be easily deployed on any system for which a response policy is available. We plan to make the code and evaluation data publicly available.

This paper proposes a framework for evaluation of word embeddings based on data efficiency and simple supervised tasks. The main motivation is that word embeddings are generally used in a transfer learning setting, where evaluation is done based on how faster is to train a target model. The approach uses a set of simple tasks evaluated in a supervised fashion, including common benchmarks such as word similarity and word analogy. Experiments on a broad set of embeddings show that ranks tend to be task-specific and change according to the amount of training data used. Strengths - The transfer learning / data efficiency motivation is an interesting one, as it directly relates to the idea of using embeddings as a simple "semi-supervised" approach. Weaknesses - A good evaluation approach would be one that propagates to end tasks. Specifically, if the approach gives some rank R for a set of embeddings, I would like it to follow the same rank for an end task like text classification, parsing or machine translation. However, the approach is not assessed in this way so it is difficult to trust the technique is actually more useful than what is traditionally done. - The discussion about injective embeddings seems completely out-of-topic and does not seem to add to the paper's understanding. - The experimental section is very confusing. Section 3.7 points out that the analysis results in answers to questions as "is it worth fitting syntax specific embeddings even when supervised datset is large?" but I fail to understand where in the evaluation the conclusion was made. - Still in Section 3.7, the manuscript says "This hints, that purely unsupervised large scale pretraining might not be suitable for NLP applications". This is a very bold assumption and I again fail to understand how this can be concluded from the proposed evaluation approach. - All embeddings were obtained as off-the-shelf pretrained ones so there is no control over which corpora they were trained on. This limits the validity of the evaluation shown in the paper. - The manuscript needs proofreading, especially in terms of citing figures in the right places (why Figure 1, which is on page 3, is only cited in page 6?). General Discussion I think the paper starts with a very interesting motivation but it does not properly evaluate if their approach is good or not. As mentioned above, for any intrinsic evaluation approach I expect to see some study if the conclusions propagate to end tasks and this is not done in the paper. The lack of clarity and proofreading in the manuscript also hinders the understanding. In the future, I think the paper would vastly benefit from some extrinsic studies and a more controlled experimental setting (using the same corpora to train all embeddings, for instance). But in the current state I do not think it is a good addition to the conference. - Strengths: This paper proposed an interesting and important metric for evaluating the quality of word embeddings, which is the "data efficiency" when it is used in other supervised tasks. Another interesting point in the paper is that the authors separated out three questions: 1) whether supervised task offers more insights to evaluate embedding quality; 2) How stable is the ranking vs labeled data set size; 3) The benefit to linear vs non-linear models. Overall, the authors presented comprehensive experiments to answer those questions, and the results see quite interesting to know for the research community. - Weaknesses: The overall result is not very useful for ML practioners in this field, because it merely confirms what has been known or suspected, i.e. it depends on the task at hand, the labeled data set size, the type of the model, etc. So, the result in this paper is not very actionable. The reviewer noted that this comprehensive analysis deepens the understanding of this topic. - General Discussion: The paper's presentation can be improved. Specifically: 1) The order of the figures/tables in the paper should match the order they are mentioned in the papers. Right now their order seems quite random. 2) Several typos (L250, 579, etc). Please use a spell checker. 3) Equation 1 is not very useful, and its exposition looks strange. It can be removed, and leave just the text explanations. 4) L164 mentions the "Appendix", but it is not available in the paper. 5) Missing citation for the public skip-gram data set in L425. 6) The claim in L591-593 is too strong. It must be explained more clearly, i.e. when it is useful and when it is not. 7) The observation in L642-645 is very interesting and important. It will be good to follow up on this and provide concrete evidence or example from some embedding. Some visualization may help too. 8) In L672 should provide examples of such "specialized word embeddings" and how they are different than the general purpose embedding. 9) Figuer 3 is too small to read.

- Strengths: - Weaknesses: Many grammar errors, such as the abstract - General Discussion: - Strengths: Introduces a new document clustering approach and compares it to several established methods, showing that it improves results in most cases. The analysis is very detailed and thorough--quite dense in many places and requires careful reading. The presentation is organized and clear, and I am impressed by the range of comparisons and influential factors that were considered. Argument is convincing and the work should influence future approaches. - Weaknesses: The paper does not provide any information on the availability of the software described. - General Discussion: Needs some (minor) editing for English and typos--here are just a few: Line 124: regardless the size > regardless of the size Line 126: resources. Because > resources, because Line 205: consist- ing mk > consisting of mk Line 360: versionand > version and

This paper presents a transition-based graph parser able to cope with the rich representations of a semantico-cognitive annotation scheme, instantiated in the UCCA corpora. The authors start first by exposing what, according to them, should cover a semantic-based annotation scheme: (i) being graph-based (possibility for a token/node of having multiple governors) (2) having non-terminal nodes (representing complex structures â syntactic -: coordinate phrases, lexical: multiword expression) and (3) allowing discontinuous elements (eg. Verbs+particules). Interestingly, none of these principles is tied to a semantic framework, they could also work for syntax or other representation layers. The authors quickly position their work by first introducing the larger context of broad-coverage semantic parsing then their annotation scheme of choice (UCCA). They then present 3 sets of parsing experiments: (i) one devoted to phrase-based parsing using the Stanford parser and an UCCA to constituency conversion, (ii) one devoted to dependency parsing using an UCCA to dependency conversion and finally (iii) the core of their proposal, a set of experiments showing that their transition-based graph parser is suitable for direct parsing of UCCA graphs. I found this work interesting but before considering a publication, I have several concerns with regards to the methodology and the empirical justifications: The authors claimed that there are the first to propose a parser for a semantically-oriented scheme such as theirs. Of course, they are. But with all due respect to the work behind this scheme, it is made of graphs with a various level of under-specified structural arguments and semantically oriented label (Process, state) and nothing in their transition sets treats the specificities of such a graph. Even the transitions related to the remote edges could have been handled by the other ones assuming a difference in the label set itself (like adding an affix for example). If we restrict the problem to graph parsing, many works post the 2014-2015 semeval shared tasks (Almeda and Martins, 2014,2015 ; Ribeyre et al, 2014-2015) proposed an extension to transition-based graph parser or an adaptation of a higher-model one, and nothing precludes their use on this data set. Itâs mostly the use of a specific feature template that anchors this model to this scheme (even though itâs less influencial than the count features and the unigram one). Anyway, because the above-mentioned graph-parsers are available [1,2] I donât understand why they couldnât be used as a baseline or source of comparisons. Regarding the phrase-based experiments using uparse, it could have been also validated by another parser from Fernandez-Gonzales and Martins (2015) which can produce LCFRS-like parsing as good as Uparse (ref missing when you first introduced uparse). Because this scheme supports a more abstract view of syntaxico-semantic structures than most of the SDP treebanks, it would have been important to use the same metrics as in the related shared task. At this point in the field, many systems, models and data set are competing and I think that the lack of comparison points with other models and parsers is detrimental to this work as whole. Yet I found it interesting and because weâre at crossing time in term of where to go next, I think that this paper should be discussed at a conference such as ConLL. Note in random order - please introduce the âgrounded semanticâ before page 2, you use that phrase before - why havenât you try to stick to constituent-tree with rich node labels and propagater traces and then train/parse with the Berkeley parser? It could have been a good baseline. - The conversion to surface dependency trees is in my mind useless: you loose too many information, here a richer conversion such as the one from âSchluter et al, 2014, Semeval SDP) should have been used. - Can you expand on âUCCA graphs may contains implicit unit that have no correspondent in the textâ or provide a ref or an example. - You mentioned other representations such as MRS and DRT, this raises the fact that your scheme doesnât seem to allow for a modelling of quantifier scope information. Itâs thus fully comparable to other more syntax-oriented scheme. Itâs indeed more abstract than DM for example and probably more underspecified than the semantic level of the PCEDT but how much? How really informative is this scheme and how really âparsableâ is it? According to your scores, it seems âharderâ but an error analysis would have been useful. - As I said before, the 3 principles you devised could apply to a lot of things, they look a bit ad-hoc to me and would probably need to take place in a much wider (and a bit clearer) introduction. What are you trying to argue for: a parser that can parse UCCA? a model suitable for semantic analysis ? or a semantic oriented scheme that can actually be parsable? you're trying to say all of those in a very dense way and it's borderline to be be confusing. [1] http://www.corentinribeyre.fr/projects/view/DAGParser [2] https://github.com/andre-martins/TurboParser and https://github.com/andre-martins/TurboParser/tree/master/semeval2014_data The paper presents the first broad-coverage semantic parsers for UCCA, one specific approach to graph-based semantic representations. Unlike CoNLL semantic dependency graphs, UCCA graphs can contain "nonterminal" nodes which do not represent words in the string. Unlike AMRs, UCCA graphs are "grounded", which the authors take to mean that the text tokens appear as nodes in the semantic representation. The authors present a number of parsing methods, including a transition-based parser that directly constructs UCCA parses, and evaluate them. Given that UCCA and UCCA-annotated data exist, it seems reasonable to develop a semantic parser for UCCA. However, the introduction and background section hit a wrong note to my ear, in that they seem to argue that UCCA is the _only_ graph-based semantic representation (SR) formalism that makes sense to be studied. This doesn't work for me, and also seems unnecessary -- a good UCCA parser could be a nice contribution by itself. I do not entirely agree with the three criteria for semantic representation formalisms the authors lay out in the introduction. For instance, it is not clear to me that "nonterminal nodes" contribute any expressive capacity. Sure, it can be inconvenient to have to decide which word is the head of a coordinated structure, but exactly what information is it that could only be represented with a nonterminal and not e.g. with more informative edge labels? Also, the question of discontinuity does not even arise in SRs that are not "grounded". The advantages of "grounded" representations over AMR-style ones did not become clear to me. I also think that the word "grounded" has been used for enough different concepts in semantics in the past ten years, and would encourage the authors to find a different one ("anchored"? "lexicalized"?). Thus I feel that the entire introductory part of the paper should be phrased and argued much more carefully. The parser itself seems fine, although I did not check the details. However, I did not find the evaluation results very impressive. On the "primary" edges, even a straightforward MaltParser outperforms the BSP parser presented here, and the f-scores on the "remote" edges (which a dependency-tree parser like Malt cannot compute directly) are not very high either. Furthermore, the conversion of dependency graphs to dependency trees has been studied quite a bit under the name "tree approximations" in the context of the CoNLL 2014 and 2015 shared tasks on semantic dependency parsing (albeit without "nonterminal" nodes). Several authors have proposed methods for reconstructing the edges that were deleted in the graph-to-tree conversion; for instance, Agic et al. (2015), "Semantic dependency graph parsing using tree approximations" discuss the issues involved in this reconstruction in detail. By incorporating such methods, it is likely that the f-score of the MaltParser (and the LSTM-based MaltParser!) could be improved further, and the strength of the BSP parser becomes even less clear to me.

Title: Towards a Neural Statistician Authors: Alex Graves, Greg Wayne, Malcolm Reynolds, Tim Harley, Ivo Danihelka, Agnieszka Grabska-Barwińska Summary: This paper proposes a new approach called the Neural Statistician which aims to combine the advantages of neural networks and Bayesian statistics. The neural statistician model is trained to generate statistical summaries of datasets and perform inference on queries. The model comprises three components, a generative model, an inference network, and a readout network. The generative model learns a representation of the dataset and generates low-dimensional summaries of the data. The inference network maps queries to a low-dimensional latent space whereas the readout network maps the summary statistics from the generative model and latent variables into a response. The neural statistician can be used for both unsupervised and supervised learning tasks. Strengths: This paper presents an original and innovative approach that has the potential to combine the power of neural networks and Bayesian statistics. The Neural Statistician model is explicitly designed to perform statistical inference and to generate statistical summaries of datasets. The generative model, inference network, and readout network are well described and convincingly illustrated with examples. The authors also provide a thorough experimental evaluation of the model on various datasets and tasks, highlighting its effectiveness and adaptability. Weaknesses: The main weakness of this paper lies in the complexity of the Neural Statistician model, which might make it difficult to understand for readers without a strong background in machine learning, neural networks, and Bayesian statistics. Moreover, the authors could improve the clarity of their explanations regarding the generative model, inference network, and readout network. In addition, there is no theoretical analysis to support the model's performance, and future studies are needed to understand its computational efficiency and scalability. Conclusion: Overall, the paper "Towards a Neural Statistician" proposes an innovative and promising approach that combines neural networks and Bayesian statistics for statistical inference and dataset summarization. The authors provide convincing experimental evaluation and illustrate the potential applications of the model. However, the model's complexity and the lack of theoretical analysis might limit its accessibility and generalization. Nonetheless, the Neural Statistician paves the way for future research in combining the strengths of neural networks and Bayesian statistics. The paper "Towards a Neural Statistician" addresses the problem of modeling complex and high-dimensional probability distributions. The authors propose a new neural network architecture that combines the strength of neural networks and probabilistic models to learn a generative model that can capture the underlying distribution of the data. One of the strengths of this paper is the incorporation of latent variables, which allows for the training of a generative model that can generate samples consistent with the distribution of the observed data. The authors demonstrate the effectiveness of the proposed approach on both synthetic and real-world datasets, showing that it outperforms several state-of-the-art models. Another strength of this paper is the clear explanation of the proposed method, which can be easily understood by researchers with a background in both machine learning and statistics. The authors also provide a detailed analysis and comparison of the proposed approach with other existing methods, which helps to highlight the strengths and limitations of the proposed model. However, one limitation of the paper is that the proposed method is computationally expensive, which may limit its ability to scale to larger datasets. Additionally, while the authors show promising results on several benchmark datasets, it would be interesting to see how the proposed method performs on more challenging datasets. Overall, "Towards a Neural Statistician" presents an innovative approach that combines the strengths of both neural networks and probabilistic models to learn a generative model that can capture complex and high-dimensional probability distributions. The paper is well-written and well-organized, and the proposed method shows promising results on both synthetic and real-world datasets. Title: Peer Review for "Towards a Neural Statistician" The paper entitled "Towards a Neural Statistician" introduces an extension of the variational autoencoder model that can learn statistics of datasets in an unsupervised fashion. The main focus of the paper is to demonstrate how this model can be used for efficient learning and classification of datasets. The authors provide a clear description of the problem being addressed and the methodology used to tackle it. The writing style is clear, concise and well-structured. The paper includes several mathematical formulas, which may be a little bit challenging for readers without a strong background in computational statistics. However, the authors provide a thorough explanation for each formula, making it easier for the reader to follow the paper. The evaluation of the proposed model is thorough and convincing. The authors demonstrate that their model is effective in clustering datasets, transferring generative models to new datasets, selecting representative samples of datasets and classifying previously unseen classes. The results obtained are well presented and analyzed in detail. Overall, this paper presents an interesting and well-executed study with a great potential for practical applications. The authors effectively demonstrate the benefits of using this model for efficient learning and classification of datasets, and the results obtained are valuable for the machine learning community. The authors may consider adding a discussion section that highlights the limitations and potential future work of the model, as well as how it can be further improved. Additionally, providing a more detailed explanation of the experiments, the dataset used, and sources of error could further strengthen the paper. In summary, I highly recommend this paper for publication consideration. The paper "Towards a Neural Statistician" addresses the problem of efficient learning by reusing knowledge from previously learned datasets for tackling new problems. The authors propose a novel extension of a variational autoencoder that can learn to compute representations or "statistics" of datasets in an unsupervised fashion, which can be used for clustering, transferring generative models, selecting representative samples, and classifying previously unseen classes. The proposed approach is referred to as a "neural statistician" as it can learn to compute summary statistics of datasets without supervision. The main strength of this paper is its novel approach to modeling datasets. The proposed framework allows for efficient learning from new datasets without the need for labeled data. The authors provide a clear and detailed explanation of the model's architecture and implementation, making it easy for readers to replicate their experiments. The proposed approach is tested on several datasets and the results are compared against other state-of-the-art models. The experiments demonstrate the effectiveness of the proposed approach in performing various tasks. However, the paper could benefit from a more thorough discussion of the limitations of the proposed approach. While the authors provide a comparison with other state-of-the-art models, they could provide more insight into the advantages and disadvantages of their approach. Additionally, the paper lacks a discussion on the generalizability of the proposed approach to larger and more complex datasets. It would be beneficial for the authors to provide more experiments on larger datasets to demonstrate the scalability of the proposed approach. Overall, the paper "Towards a Neural Statistician" presents a novel approach to modeling datasets that allows for efficient learning from new datasets without the need for labeled data. The proposed approach has shown promising results on several tasks and datasets. However, the authors could benefit from providing a more thorough discussion of the limitations of their proposed approach and conducting more experiments on larger and more complex datasets.

I have read and reviewed the paper "Chunk-based Decoder for Neural Machine Translation". My overall impression is that the paper is well-written and presents a novel and interesting approach to neural machine translation. One strength of the paper is that it proposes a new way of decoding neural machine translation models by chunking the input and output sequences. This approach has the potential to improve the efficiency of neural machine translation models by reducing the number of parameters that need to be trained. Another strength of the paper is that it evaluates the proposed approach using a variety of standard neural machine translation evaluation metrics. This allows the authors to show that the chunk-based decoder performs similarly to other state-of-the-art neural machine translation models. One weakness of the paper is that it does not explore the use of different chunk sizes or the impact of chunking on the performance of the model. Additionally, it would have been helpful if the authors had provided more information about the architecture of the neural machine translation model, such as the number of layers and the activation functions used. Overall, I think that the paper is a valuable contribution to the field of neural machine translation and that the proposed approach has potential to improve the efficiency and performance of neural machine translation models. This paper addresses the problem of how to efficiently decode a neural machine translation (NMT) model. The authors propose a chunk-based decoder that operates on smaller subsets of the input at different levels of abstraction. The paper describes a thorough experimental evaluation of the proposed decoder compared to other state-of-the-art decoders. Strengths: The paper makes a strong contribution to the field of NMT by proposing a new decoder architecture that is shown to improve translation quality and efficiency. The experimental evaluation is thorough and well-conducted, and the results are convincing. Weaknesses: There are a few weaknesses in the paper. First, the authors do not clearly explain the motivation for the proposed chunk-based decoder. It is not clear why operating on smaller subsets of the input at different levels of abstraction is expected to improve translation quality. Second, the experimental evaluation does not include a direct comparison of the proposed decoder to other state-of-the-art decoders. This makes it difficult to assess the true effectiveness of the proposed decoder. Finally, the paper does not discuss any potential downsides or limitations of the proposed decoder. The paper "Chunk-based Decoder for Neural Machine Translation" proposes a chunk-based decoder for NMT that combines a chunk-level decoder and a word-level decoder. The main idea is to use chunks (or phrases) to model global dependencies and words for local dependencies. The experimental results show that the proposed decoders can significantly improve translation performance in a WAT '16 English-to-Japanese translation task. The paper is well-written and well-organized. The authors clearly explain the proposed chunk-based decoders for NMT and provide experimental results to demonstrate their effectiveness. There is a lack of comparison with other state-of-the-art NMT decoders, which would have been helpful in assessing the performance of the proposed decoders. This paper addresses the problem of how to effectively integrate chunk-based translation with word-based translation. The strength of the paper is that it clearly defines the problem and proposed a solution. However, there are a few weaknesses. First, there is no clear explanation of why the chunk-based translation would work well with the word-based translation. Second, the experimental results are not enough to prove the effectiveness of the proposed method. Finally, the writing is not clear and there are several typos.

This paper presents an interesting approach to understanding images and text simultaneously by leveraging the power of dual vision-language machine comprehension. The authors present a novel architecture that combines a convolutional neural network (CNN) and a recurrent neural network (RNN) to enable image-text understanding. The authors then evaluate the performance of their model on a benchmark dataset and demonstrate improved performance over existing models. The paper is well-written and clearly explains the motivation and methodology behind the proposed model. The authors provide a thorough description of their architecture, which is helpful for readers to understand the model. The experiments are also well-designed and the authors provide a detailed analysis of their results. The paper could be improved by providing a more detailed discussion of the limitations of the model and potential areas for future work. Additionally, the authors could provide more information on how the model could be used in real-world applications. Overall, this paper presents an interesting approach to understanding image and text simultaneously and the authors have done a great job of explaining their model and its performance. This paper addresses the problem of understanding image and text simultaneously in the context of a dual vision-language machine comprehension task. Strengths: The paper presents a novel dual vision-language machine comprehension task that combines both image and text comprehension. The authors provide a thorough description of the task and explain the various components of the model. The experiments conducted and the results obtained provide evidence for the effectiveness of the proposed model. Weaknesses: The paper does not discuss the potential applications of the proposed model or provide any insights into how it could be used in real-world scenarios. Additionally, the paper does not provide any comparison to existing models or discuss any potential future work that could be done to improve the model. This paper presents an interesting new multi-modal task that combines vision and language comprehension. The authors propose an effective and extensible mechanism for generating decoys from human-created image captions, and use it to create a large-scale machine comprehension dataset. The paper also provides human evaluation results on the dataset, baseline and competitive learning approaches, and shows that performance on the proposed task is positively correlated with the end-to-end task of image captioning. The paper is well-written and clearly explains the task and the proposed approach. The authors provide a thorough literature review and discuss the related work. The experiments are well designed and the results are clearly presented. The authors also provide an insightful discussion of the results. Overall, this is a very interesting paper that presents a novel multi-modal task and a corresponding dataset. It is an important contribution to the field of computer vision and natural language processing. This paper addresses a new multi-modal task for computer systems: identifying the most suitable text describing a scene, given several similar options. The paper makes several contributions, including a mechanism for generating decoys from image captions, a large-scale machine comprehension dataset, human evaluation results, and baseline and competitive learning approaches. The paper has several strengths. First, the proposed task is easy to compute and well-studied, and the authors provide a metric to measure accuracy. Second, the authors provide a mechanism for generating decoys from image captions, as well as a publicly available dataset. Third, the authors provide human evaluation results to inform a performance upper-bound. Finally, the authors provide baseline and competitive learning approaches to illustrate the utility of the proposed task and dataset. The paper has a few weaknesses. First, the authors do not provide a comprehensive analysis of the results of the baseline and competitive learning approaches. Second, the authors do not provide a detailed discussion of the implications of the results for advancing both image and language comprehension. Finally, the authors do not discuss the potential applications of the proposed task and dataset.

This paper describes a method that estimates the similarity between a set of images by alternatively attend each image with a recurrent manner. The idea of the paper is interesting, which mimic the human's behavior. However, there are several cons of the paper: 1. The paper is now well written. There are too many 'TODO', 'CITE' in the final version of the paper, which indicates that the paper is submitted in a rush or the authors did not take much care about the paper. I think the paper is not suitable to be published with the current version. 2. The missing of the experimental results. The paper mentioned the LFW dataset. However, the paper did not provide the results on LFW dataset. (At least I did not find it in the version of Dec. 13th) 3. The experiments of Omniglot dataset are not sufficient. I suggest that the paper provides some illustrations about how the model the attend two images (e.g. the trajectory of attend). This paper shows some strong performance numbers, but I agree with the reviewers that it requires more analysis of where those gains come from. The model is very simple, which is a positive, but more studies such as ablation studies and other examples would help a lot. We have added a 2+ page detailed analysis section with ablation studies and attention maps. This paper introduces an attention-based recurrent network that learns to compare images by attending iteratively back and forth between a pair of images. Experiments show state-of-the-art results on Omniglot, though a large part of the performance gain comes from when extracted convolutional features are used as input. The paper is significantly improved from the original submission and reflects changes based on pre-review questions. However, while there was an attempt made to include more qualitative results e.g. Fig. 2, it is still relatively weak and could benefit from more examples and analysis. Also, why is the attention in Fig. 2 always attending over the full character? Although it is zooming in, shouldn’t it attend to relevant parts of the character? Attending to the full character on a solid background seems a trivial solution where it is then unclear where the large performance gains are coming from. While the paper is much more polished now, it is still lacking in details in some respects, e.g. details of the convolutional feature extractor used that gives large performance gain. This paper presents an attention based recurrent approach to one-shot learning. It reports quite strong experimental results (surpassing human performance/HBPL) on the Omniglot dataset, which is somewhat surprising because it seems to make use of very standard neural network machinery. The authors also note that other have helped verify the results (did Soumith Chintala reproduce the results?) and do provide source code. After reading this paper, I'm left a little perplexed as to where the big performance improvements are coming from as it seems to share a lot of the same components of previous work. If the author's could report result from a broader suite of experiments like in previous work (e.g matching networks), it would much more convincing. An ablation study would also help with understanding why this model does so well. The paper proposes to add an additional term to the denoising-autoencoder objective. The new term is well motivated, it introduces an asymmetry between the encoder and decoder, forcing the encoder to represent a compressed, denoised version of the input. The authors propose to avoid the trivial solution introduced by the new term by using tied weights or normalized Euclidean distance error (the trivial solution occurs by scaling the magnitude of the code down in the encoder, and back up in the decoder). The proposed auto-encoder scheme is very similar to a host of other auto-encoders that have been out in the literature for some time. The authors evaluate the proposed scheme on toy-data distributions in 2D as well as MNIST. Although the work is well motivated, it certainly seems like an empirically unproven and incremental improvement to an old idea. The proposed modification of the denoising autoencoder objective is interesting. Shifting some of the burden to the encoder has potential, but the authors need to to show that this burden cannot be transferred to the decoder; they propose some ways of doing this in their response, but these should be explored. And more convincing, larger-scale results are needed. The paper proposes a modified DAE objective where it is the mapped representation of the corrupted input that is pushed closer to the representation of the uncorrupted input. This thus borrows from both denoising (DAE) for the stochasticity and from the contractive (CAE) auto-encoders objectives (which the paper doesn’t compare to) for the representational closeness, and as such appears rather incremental. In common with the CAE, a collapse of the representation can only be avoided by additional external constraints, such as tied weights, batch normalization or other normalization heuristics. While I appreciates that the authors added a paragraph discussing this point and the usual remediations after I had raised it in an earlier question, I think it would deserve a proper formal treatment. Note that such external constraints do not seem to arise from the information-theoretic formalism as articulated by the authors. This casts doubt regarding the validity or completeness of the proposed formal motivation as currently exposed. What the extra regularization does from an information-theoretic perspective remains unclearly articulated (e.g. interpretation of lambda strength?). On the experimental front, empirical support for the approach is very weak: few experiments on synthetic and small scale data. The modified DAE's test errors on MNIST are larger than those of Original DAE all the time expect for one precise setting of lambda, and then the original DAE performance is still within the displayed error-bar of the modified DAE. So, it is unclear whether the improvement is actually statistically significant. The work introduced a new form of regularization for denoising autoencoders, which explicitly enforces robustness in the encoding phrase w.r.t. input perturbation. The author motivates the regularization term as minimizing the conditional entropy of the encoding given the input. The modifier denoising autoencoders is evaluated on some synthetic datasets as well as MNIST, along with regular auto-encoders and denoising autoencoders. The work is fairly similar to several existing extensions to auto-encoders, e.g., contractive auto encoders, which the author did not include in the comparison. The experiment section needs more polishing. More details should be provided to help understand the figures in the section. The authors would be encouraged to improve the presentation and provide a more precise mathematical presentation, as discussed by reviewer 1. We hope the revisions made to the paper properly address the concerns raised by the reviewers and other researchers, and improve the quality of the manuscript. Summary of revisions: * Embedding learnt end-to-end from raw data: Added paragraph (Section 2.2, page 5) to explain what the embedding learnt end-to-end is and how it is used within the Diet Networks framework. * Figure 3: Scaled both confusion matrices so that maximum for each class is 1. * Table 1: Added extended PCA results. * Table 1: Updated number of free parameters column to only account for the number of free parameters in the fat layers of the model, with the aim to make the presentation clearer. * Made code publicly available and added footnote with the corresponding url (page 6). * Section 4.2. (Results), paragraph 6: introduced extended PCA results (more PCs and MLP + classifier). * Appendix B: Added the instructions to download the dataset. * Appendix C: Added an analysis on PCA (number of components used) and performance. * Fixed typos. * Table 1: Added comment about feature embeddings dimensionality. The paper presents an application of deep learning to genomic SNP data with a comparison of possible approaches for dealing with the very high data dimensionality. The approach looks very interesting but the experiments are too limited to draw firm conclusions about the strengths of different approaches. The presentation would benefit from more precise math. Quality: The basic idea of the paper is interesting and the applied deep learning methodology appears reasonable. The experimental evaluation is rather weak as it only covers a single data set and a very limited number of cross validation folds. Given the significant variation in the performances of all the methods, it seems the differences between the better-performing methods are probably not statistically significant. More comprehensive empirical validation could clearly strengthen the paper. Clarity: The writing is generally good both in terms of the biology and ML, but more mathematical rigour would make it easier to understand precisely what was done. The different architectures are explained on an intuitive level and might benefit from a clear mathematical definition. I was ultimately left unsure of what the "raw end2end" model is - given so few parameters it cannot work on raw 300k dimensional input but I could not figure out what kind of embedding was used. The results in Fig. 3 might be clearer if scaled so that maximum for each class is 1 to avoid confounding from different numbers of subjects in different classes. In the text, please use the standard italics math font for all symbols such as N, N_d, ... Originality: The application and the approach appear quite novel. Significance: There is clearly strong interest for deep learning in the genomics area and the paper seeks to address some of the major bottlenecks here. It is too early to tell whether the specific techniques proposed in the paper will be the ultimate solution, but at the very least the paper provides interesting new ideas for others to work on. Other comments: I think releasing the code as promised would be a must. The paper addresses the important problem (d>>n) in deep learning. The proposed approach, based on lower-dimensional feature embeddings, is reasonable and makes applying deep learning methods to data with large d possible. The paper is well written and the results show improvements over reasonable baselines. Thanks for this very nice paper. I have a few technical questions: 1- How do you handle missing genotypes?
