The most interesting ones are listed below: Non-transparent The paper Entropy-SGD: Biasing Gradient Descent Into Wide Valleys addresses the problem that neural networks with similar parameters but trained on different initial values may result in very different generalization errors, by proposing a new optimization algorithm called Entropy-SGD. The main idea is based on the local geometry of the energy landscape, more precisely, the flat regions with few positive or negative eigenvalues in the Hessian, which correspond to local minima with low generalization error. The paper further improves the stability of the new objective function by using Langevin dynamics to compute the gradient of the local entropy before each update of the weights. Experiments on convolutional and recurrent neural networks show that the new objective function compares favorably to state-of-the-art techniques in terms of generalization error. beef The paper Entropy-SGD: Biasing Gradient Descent Into Wide Valleys presents a new optimization algorithm called Entropy-SGD to train neural networks. The main idea is to construct a new objective function based on the local geometry of the energy landscape. The flat regions of the objective function represent well-generalizable solutions, while the sharp valleys represent poorly-generalized solutions. The new objective function is constructed by combining the local entropy and the regular cross-entropy, and by assuming that the limit of the local entropy exists. Under this assumption, the new objective function can separate the well-generalizable solutions from the poorly-generalizable ones. Experiments on convolutional and recurrent neural networks demonstrate that Entropy-SGD compares favorably to state-of-the-art techniques in terms of generalization error.

Review: Multimodal Word Distributions - Strengths: Overall a very strong paper. - Weaknesses: The comparison against similar approaches could be extended. - General Discussion: The main focus of this paper is the introduction of a new model for learning multimodal word distributions formed from Gaussian mixtures for multiple word meanings. i. e. representing a word by a set of many Gaussian distributions. The approach, extend the model introduced by Vilnis and McCallum (2014) which represented word as unimodal Gaussian distribution. By using a multimodal, the current approach attain the problem of polysemy. Overall, a very strong paper, well structured and clear. The experimentation is correct and the qualitative analysis made in table 1 shows results as expected from the approach. There’s not much that can be faulted and all my comments below are meant to help the paper gain additional clarity. Some comments: _ It may be interesting to include a brief explanation of the differences between the approach from Tian et al. 2014 and the current one. Both split single word representation into multiple prototypes by using a mixture model. _ There are some missing citations that could me mentioned in related work as : Efficient Non-parametric Estimation of Multiple Embeddings per Word in Vector Space Neelakantan, A., Shankar. J. Passos, A., McCallum. EMNLP 2014 Do Multi-Sense Embeddings Improve Natural Language Understanding? Li and Jurafsky, EMNLP 2015 Topical Word Embeddings. Liu Y., Liu Z., Chua T.,Sun M. AAAI 2015 _ Also, the inclusion of the result from those approaches in tables 3 and 4 could be interesting. _ A question to the authors: What do you attribute the loss of performance of w2gm against w2g in the analysis of SWCS? I have read the response. This work uses Gaussian mixtures to represent words and demonstrates its potential in capturing multiple word meanings for polysemy. The training process is done based on a max-margin objective. The expected likelihood kernel is used as the similarity between two words' distributions. Experiment results on word similarity and entailment tasks show the effectiveness of the proposed work. - Strengths: The problem is clearly motivated and defined. Gaussian mixtures are much more expressive than deterministic vector representations. It can potentially capture different word meanings by its modes, along with probability mass and uncertainty around those modes. This work represents an important contribution to word embedding. This work propose a max-margin learning objective with closed-form similarity measurement for efficient training. This paper is mostly well written. - Weaknesses: See below for some questions. - General Discussion: In the Gaussian mixture models, the number of gaussian components (k) is usually an important parameter. In the experiments of this paper, k is set to 2. What is your criteria to select k? Does the increase of k hurt the performance of this model? What does the learned distribution look like for a word that only has one popular meaning? I notice that you use the spherical case in all the experiments (the covariance matrix reduces to a single number). Is this purely for computation efficiency? I wonder what's the performance of using a general diagonal covariance matrix. Since in this more general case, the gaussian mixture defines different degrees of uncertainty along different directions in the semantic space, which seems more interesting. Minor comments: Table 4 is not referred to in the text. In reference, Luong et al. lacks the publication year. I have read the response.

The paper titled “Attend, Adapt and Transfer: Attentive Deep Architecture for Adaptive Transfer from multiple sources in the same domain” is a well-written and well-structured work that presents a novel approach to transfer learning from multiple sources in the same domain. The authors propose an attentive deep architecture that is able to effectively transfer knowledge from multiple sources and adapt to the target task. The paper is well-organized and easy to follow, and the authors provide a good description of the model and its components. The paper starts by introducing the problem and the existing approaches to transfer learning. The authors then proceed to describe the proposed model, which consists of an attention-based feature selection module and an adaptive transfer module. The authors present a thorough explanation of the model and provide a detailed description of the implementation. The authors also provide a comprehensive evaluation of the model on three different datasets. The results show that the proposed model outperforms existing methods in terms of accuracy and F1-score. The paper is well-written and the authors provide a clear explanation of the proposed model and its components. The paper is well-structured and the authors provide a comprehensive evaluation of the model. The authors also provide a good discussion of the results and the implications of the proposed approach. Overall, this paper presents a novel approach to transfer learning from multiple sources in the same domain. The authors provide a good description of the proposed model and its components, and the results show that the proposed model outperforms existing methods. The paper is well-written and well-structured and I recommend it for publication. This paper presents a deep learning architecture, called Attend, Adapt and Transfer, for adaptive transfer from multiple sources in the same domain. The architecture is designed to attend to relevant source data, adapt it to the target domain, and transfer the knowledge to the target task. Strengths: The paper presents a novel architecture for transfer learning that is able to attend to relevant source data, adapt it to the target domain, and transfer the knowledge to the target task. The paper presents a detailed description of the architecture, including both the theoretical background and the implementation details. The authors also provide an extensive evaluation of the proposed architecture on several datasets, demonstrating its effectiveness. Weaknesses: The paper does not provide any comparison with existing approaches to transfer learning, which would have been useful to demonstrate the superiority of the proposed architecture. Furthermore, the paper does not provide any discussion of the limitations of the proposed architecture or any potential areas of improvement. This paper presents a novel approach to transfer learning, A2T (Attend Adapt and Transfer), which is an attentive deep architecture that adapts and transfers from multiple source tasks in the same domain. The authors demonstrate that this approach is effective in avoiding negative transfer while selectively transferring from different source tasks in the state space of the target task. The paper is well written and the authors provide a thorough description of the proposed architecture and its components. The experiments conducted are comprehensive and the results are clearly presented. Overall, this is an excellent paper that makes a significant contribution to the field of transfer learning. The authors have done an excellent job of demonstrating the effectiveness of their proposed approach. The paper is well written and the experiments are comprehensive and well-executed. The results are clearly presented and discussed. The authors should be commended for their work. This paper addresses the problem of how to transfer knowledge from prior source tasks to a new target task in order to improve learning applications. The paper proposes A2T (Attend Adapt and Transfer), an attentive deep architecture which adapts and transfers from these source tasks. Strengths: The paper offers an effective architecture for transfer by being able to avoid negative transfer while transferring selectively from multiple source tasks in the same domain. The paper provides empirical evaluations on different learning algorithms to demonstrate the effectiveness of the proposed architecture. Weaknesses: The paper does not provide any theoretical analysis or comparison with existing methods. Additionally, the paper does not discuss the limitations or potential drawbacks of the proposed architecture.

- Strengths: This is the first neural network-based approach to argumentation mining. The proposed method used a Pointer Network (PN) model with multi-task learning and outperformed previous methods in the experiments on two datasets. - Weaknesses: This is basically an application of PN to argumentation mining. Although the combination of PN and multi-task learning for this task is novel, its novelty is not enough for ACL long publication. The lack of qualitative analysis and error analysis is also a major concern. - General Discussion: Besides the weaknesses mentioned above, the use of PN is not well-motivated. Although three characteristics of PN were described in l.138-143, these are not a strong motivation against the use of bi-directional LSTMs and the attention mechanism. The authors should describe what problems are solved by PN and discuss in the experiments how much these problems are solved. Figures 2 and 3 are difficult to understand. What are the self link to D1 and the links from D2 to E1 and D3/D4 to E2? These are just the outputs from the decoder and not links. The decoder LSTM does not have an input from e_j in these figures, but it does in Equation (3). Also, in Figure 3, the abbreviation "FC" is not defined. Equation (8) is strange. To calculate the probability of each component type, the probability of E_i is calculated. In the experiments, I did not understand why only "PN", which is not a joint model, was performed for the microtext corpus. It is not clear whether the BLSTM model is trained with the joint-task objective. There are some studies on discourse parsing using the attention mechanism. The authors should describe the differences from these studies. Minor issues: l.128: should related -> should be related l.215: (2015) is floating l.706: it able -> it is able I raised my recommendation score after reading the convincing author responses. I strongly recommend that the authors should discuss improved examples by PN as well as the details of feature ablation. The paper presents an application of Pointer Networks, a recurrent neural network model original used for solving algorithmic tasks, to two subtasks of Argumentation Mining: determining the types of Argument Components, and finding the links between them. The model achieves state-of-the-art results. Strengths: - Thorough review of prior art in the specific formulation of argument mining handled in this paper. - Simple and effective modification of an existing model to make it suitable for the task. The model is mostly explained clearly. - Strong results as compared to prior art in this task. Weaknesses: - 071: This formulation of argumentation mining is just one of several proposed subtask divisions, and this should be mentioned. For example, in [1], claims are detected and classified before any supporting evidence is detected. Furthermore, [2] applied neural networks to this task, so it is inaccurate to say (as is claimed in the abstract of this paper) that this work is the first NN-based approach to argumentation mining. - Two things must be improved in the presentation of the model: (1) What is the pooling method used for embedding features (line 397)? and (2) Equation (7) in line 472 is not clear enough: is E_i the random variable representing the *type* of AC i, or its *identity*? Both are supposedly modeled (the latter by feature representation), and need to be defined. Furthermore, it seems like the LHS of equation (7) should be a conditional probability. - There are several unclear things about Table 2: first, why are the three first baselines evaluated only by macro f1 and the individual f1 scores are missing? This is not explained in the text. Second, why is only the "PN" model presented? Is this the same PN as in Table 1, or actually the Joint Model? What about the other three? - It is not mentioned which dataset the experiment described in Table 4 was performed on. General Discussion: - 132: There has to be a lengthier introduction to pointer networks, mentioning recurrent neural networks in general, for the benefit of readers unfamiliar with "sequence-to-sequence models". Also, the citation of Sutskever et al. (2014) in line 145 should be at the first mention of the term, and the difference with respect to recursive neural networks should be explained before the paragraph starting in line 233 (tree structure etc.). - 348: The elu activation requires an explanation and citation (still not enough well-known). - 501: "MC", "Cl" and "Pr" should be explained in the label. - 577: A sentence about how these hyperparameters were obtained would be appropriate. - 590: The decision to do early stopping only by link prediction accuracy should be explained (i.e. why not average with type accuracy, for example?). - 594: Inference at test time is briefly explained, but would benefit from more details. - 617: Specify what the length of an AC is measured in (words?). - 644: The referent of "these" in "Neither of these" is unclear. - 684: "Minimum" should be "Maximum". - 694: The performance w.r.t. the amount of training data is indeed surprising, but other models have also achieved almost the same results - this is especially surprising because NNs usually need more data. It would be good to say this. - 745: This could alternatively show that structural cues are less important for this task. - Some minor typos should be corrected (e.g. "which is show", line 161). [1] Rinott, Ruty, et al. "Show Me Your Evidence-an Automatic Method for Context Dependent Evidence Detection." EMNLP. 2015. [2] Laha, Anirban, and Vikas Raykar. "An Empirical Evaluation of various Deep Learning Architectures for Bi-Sequence Classification Tasks." COLING. 2016.

This paper models event linking using CNNs. Given event mentions, the authors generate vector representations based on word embeddings passed through a CNN and followed by max-pooling. They also concatenate the resulting representations with several word embeddings around the mention. Together with certain pairwise features, they produce a vector of similarities using a single-layer neural network, and compute a coreference score. The model is tested on an ACE dataset and an expanded version with performance comparable to previous feature-rich systems. The main contribution of the paper, in my opinion, is in developing a neural approach for entity linking that combines word embeddings with several linguistic features. It is interesting to find out that just using the word embeddings is not sufficient for good performance. Fortunately, the linguistic features used are limited and do not require manually-crafted external resources. Experimental setting - It appears that gold trigger words are used rather than predicted ones. The authors make an argument why this is reasonable, although I still would have liked to see performance with predicted triggers. This is especially problematic as one of the competitor systems used predicted triggers, so the comparison isn't fair. - The fact that different papers use different train/test splits is worrisome. I would encourage the authors to stick to previous splits as much as possible. Unclear points - The numbers indicating that cross-sentential information is needed are convincing. However, the last statement in the second paragraph (lines 65-70) was not clear to me. - Embeddings for positions are said to be generaties "in a way similar to word embeddings". How exactly? Are they randomly initialized? Are they lexicalized? It is not clear to me why a relative position next to one word should have the same embedding as a relative position next to a different word. - How exactly are left vs right neighbors used to create the representation (lines 307-311)? Does this only affect the max-pooling operation? - The word embeddings of one word before and one word after the trigger words are appended to it. This seems a bit arbitrary. Why one word before and after and not some other choice? - It is not clear how the event-mention representation v_e (line 330) is used? In the following sections only v_{sent+lex} appear to be used, not v_e. - How are pairwise features used in section 3.2? Most features are binary, so I assume they are encoded as a binary vector, but what about the distance feature for example? And, are these kept fixed during training? Other issues and suggestions - Can the approach be applied to entity coreference resolution as well? This would allow comparing with more previous work and popular datasets like OntoNotes. - The use of a square function as nonlinearity is interesting. Is it novel? Do you think it has applicability in other tasks? - Datasets: one dataset is publicly available, but results are also presented with ACE++, which is not. Do you have plans to release it? It would help other researchers compare new methods. At least, it would have been good to see a comparison to the feature-rich systems also on this dataset. - Results: some of the numbers reported in the results are quite close. Significance testing would help substantiating the comparisons. - Related work: among the work on (entity) coreference resolution, one might mention the neural network approach by Wiseman et al. (2015) Minor issues - line 143, "that" is redundant. - One of the baselines is referred to as "same type" in table 6, but "same event" in the text (line 670). Refs - Learning Anaphoricity and Antecedent Ranking Features for Coreference Resolution. Sam Wiseman, Alexander M. Rush, Jason Weston, and Stuart M. Shieber. ACL 2015. This paper presents a model for the task of event entity linking, where they propose to use sentential features from CNNs in place of external knowledge sources which earlier methods have used. They train a two-part model: the first part learns an event mention representation, and the second part learns to calculate a coreference score given two event entity mentions. The paper is well-written, well-presented and is easy to follow. I rather like the analysis done on the ACE corpus regarding the argument sharing between event coreferences. Furthermore, the analysis on the size impact of the dataset is a great motivation for creating their ACE++ dataset. However, there are a few major issues that need to be addressed: - The authors fail to motivate and analyze the pros and cons of using CNN for generating mention representations. It is not discussed why they chose CNN and there are no comparisons to the other models (e.g., straightforwardly an RNN). Given that the improvement their model makes according various metrics against the state-of-the-art is only 2 or 3 points on F1 score, there needs to be more evidence that this architecture is indeed superior. - It is not clear what is novel about the idea of tackling event linking with sentential features, given that using CNN in this fashion for a classification task is not new. The authors could explicitly point out and mainly compare to any existing continuous space methods for event linking. The choice of methods in Table 3 is not thorough enough. - There is no information regarding how the ACE++ dataset is collected. A major issue with the ACE dataset is its limited number of event types, making it too constrained and biased. It is important to know what event types ACE++ covers. This can also help support the claim in Section 5.1 that 'other approaches are strongly tied to the domain where these semantic features are availableâ¦our approach does not depend on resources with restrictedâ¦', you need to show that those earlier methods fail on some dataset that you succeed on. Also, for enabling any meaningful comparison in future, the authors should think about making this dataset publicly available. Some minor issues: - I would have liked to see the performance of your model without gold references in Table 3 as well. - It would be nice to explore how this model can or cannot be augmented with a vanilla coreference resolution system. For the specific example in line 687, the off-the-shelf CoreNLP system readily links 'It' to 'bombing', which can be somehow leveraged in an event entity linking baseline. - Given the relatively small size of the ACE dataset, I think having a compelling model requires testing on the other available resources as well. This further motivates working on entity and event coreference simultaneously. I also believe that testing on EventCorefBank in parallel with ACE is essential. - Table 5 shows that the pairwise features have been quite effective, which signals that feature engineering may still be crucial for having a competitive model (at least on the scale of the ACE dataset). One would wonder which features were the most effective, and why not report how the current set was chosen and what else was tried.

The authors present an interesting approach to solving Hamilton-Jacobi-Isaacs (HJI) partial differential equations using neural networks and recursive regression. The results are promising, but there is room for improvement in several areas. First of all, it would be helpful if they could provide more details on how their method works by explaining what happens at each step along the way. For example, when do you use your first-order approximation? What does this mean? How did you choose these parameters? Also, I think that some explanation should be provided as to why the second order term was chosen over other terms such as $(x-y)^2$ or $x^y$. It seems like the choice may have been made based solely upon numerical experiments rather than any theoretical justification. Finally, while the authors claim that their algorithm can solve problems involving multiple agents simultaneously, no examples were presented where two or three agents interacted together. The authors present an interesting approach to solving Hamilton-Jacobi-Isaacs (HJI) partial differential equations using neural networks trained on data generated from recursive regression. This is not exactly new research but it does provide some insight into how this type of equation can be solved in practice. The main contribution here seems to lie more so in the training process than anything else - that being said there are still several issues worth noting. First off I think it's important for readers who aren't familiar with these types of problems to understand why they need to solve them at all. In particular I'd like to see something about how one would use such solutions as part of their overall strategy when playing games where optimal control theory plays a role. This could include things like: How do you know if your solution actually solves the original problem? The authors propose a novel method based on recursive least squares combined with deep recurrent neural network architectures to solve a challenging nonlinear optimization problem arising from optimal control theory. The proposed methodology leverages recent advances in both areas - namely, the use of RNNs within RL algorithms and the application of DRL techniques to solving PDEs. In addition, they provide extensive numerical experiments demonstrating their effectiveness over existing methods. Overall I think it's very interesting work! I would like to see more details about how exactly does the proposed technique perform compared against other approaches such as policy gradient or value iteration. Also, since the focus here seems to be on approximation rather than exact solutions, I'd also recommend including additional results showing convergence rates and/or bounds on error between the true solution and your estimate. Finally, The authors propose a novel method for solving PDEs through recursive regression on artificial neural networks; they show how their proposed technique compares favorably against existing methods. The article presents several examples illustrating its effectiveness; however, I would like more details about why certain parameters were chosen over others when comparing results across different algorithms. Strengths & Weaknesses: Strengths include clear presentation of ideas and comparisons between competing techniques. Weaknesses include: lack of explanation behind parameter choices during comparison experiments. Overall, it's well written but could use additional explanations/clarifications throughout. It also lacks references to other work done along these lines so readers may not know whether they're reading something new or just rediscovering old ground. 4 stars overall. Author Response: Thank you very much! We will try our best to improve upon your suggestions.

This paper presents a novel approach to image generation using a cooperative training of descriptor and generator networks. The authors propose a method that combines two networks, a descriptor network and a generator network, to generate realistic images. The descriptor network is used to generate a feature vector that is used as input to the generator network. The generator network is then used to generate the image based on the feature vector. The authors demonstrate that this approach is able to generate realistic images with a high degree of accuracy. The paper is well written and the authors present a clear and concise description of their approach. The paper is well organized and the authors provide a detailed description of their method. The authors also provide a thorough evaluation of their approach and demonstrate that it is able to generate realistic images. Overall, this paper presents an interesting approach to image generation using a cooperative training of descriptor and generator networks. The authors provide a clear and concise description of their approach and demonstrate that it is able to generate realistic images. The paper is well written and the authors provide a thorough evaluation of their approach. I recommend that this paper be accepted for publication. This paper addresses the problem of how to train two networks, a descriptor network and a generator network, to generate high-quality images. The authors propose a cooperative training approach that combines the two networks in a single training process. The paper has several strengths. The authors provide a detailed description of their proposed approach, as well as a thorough evaluation of its performance. The authors also provide a comprehensive comparison of their proposed method with existing approaches. The main weakness of the paper is that it lacks a thorough discussion of the limitations of the proposed approach. Additionally, the authors do not provide any insight into how the proposed approach could be extended to other types of image generation tasks. This paper presents a novel approach to cooperative training of two probabilistic models of signals such as images. The two models are parametrized by convolutional neural networks (ConvNets), with one being a descriptor network and the other being a generator network. The authors show that the two training algorithms can cooperate with each other by jump-starting each other’s Langevin sampling, and they can be seamlessly interwoven into a CoopNets algorithm that can train both nets simultaneously. The paper is well-structured and the authors have done a good job of presenting the details of their proposed method. The paper is clearly written and the authors have provided sufficient background information and references to support their claims. The experiments performed to evaluate the proposed method are also well-documented and the results are presented in a clear and concise manner. Overall, this is a well-written and well-researched paper that presents an interesting approach to cooperative training of two probabilistic models of signals. The authors have provided a detailed description of their proposed method and have provided sufficient evidence to support their claims. I recommend this paper for publication. This paper addresses the problem of how to cooperatively train two probabilistic models of signals, such as images, that are parametrized by convolutional neural networks (ConvNets). The paper proposes a CoopNets algorithm that can train both a descriptor network and a generator network simultaneously. The paper's main strength is its novel approach to cooperatively training two probabilistic models of signals, which has not been done before. Additionally, the paper provides a thorough analysis of the CoopNets algorithm and its performance. A potential weakness of the paper is that it does not provide a comparison of the CoopNets algorithm to other existing algorithms. Additionally, the paper does not provide any experimental results to back up its claims.

This paper introduces new configurations and training objectives for neural sequence models in a multi-task setting. As the authors describe well, the multi-task setting is important because some tasks have shared information and in some scenarios learning many tasks can improve overall performance. The methods section is relatively clear and logical, and I like where it ended up, though it could be slightly better organized. The organization that I realized after reading is that there are two problems: 1) shared features end up in the private feature space, and 2) private features end up in the shared space. There is one novel method for each problem. That organization up front would make the methods more cohesive. In any case, they introduce one method that keeps task-specific features out of shared representation (adversarial loss) and another to keep shared features out of task-specific representations (orthogonality constraints). My only point of confusion is the adversarial system. After LSTM output there is another layer, D(s^k_T, \theta_D), relying on parameters U and b. This output is considered a probability distribution which is compared against the actual. This means it is possible it will just learn U and b that effectively mask task-specific information from the LSTM outputs, and doesn't seem like it can guarantee task-specific information is removed. Before I read the evaluation section I wrote down what I hoped the experiments would look like and it did most of it. This is an interesting idea and there are a lot more experiments one can imagine but I think here they have the basics to show the validity of their methods. It would be helpful to have best known results on these tasks. My primary concern with this paper is the lack of deeper motivation for the approach. I think it is easy to understand that in a totally shared model there will be problems due to conflicts in feature space. The extension to partially shared features seems like a reaction to that issue -- one would expect that the useful shared information is in the shared latent space and each task-specific space would learn features for that space. Maybe this works and maybe it doesn't, but the logic is clear to me. In contrast, the authors seem to start from the assumption that this "shared-private" model has this issue. I expected the argument flow to be 1) Fully-shared obviously has this problem; 2) shared-private seems to address this; 3) in practice shared-private does not fully address this issue for reasons a,b,c.; 4) we introduce a method that more effectively constrains the spaces. Table 4 helped me to partially understand what's going wrong with shared-private and what your methods do; some terms are _usually_ one connotation or another, and that general trend can probably get them into the shared feature space. This simple explanation, an example, and a more logical argument flow would help the introduction and make this a really nice reading paper. Finally, I think this research ties into some other uncited MTL work [1], which does deep hierarchical MTL - supervised POS tagging at a lower level, chunking at the next level up, ccg tagging higher, etc. They then discuss at the end some of the qualities that make MTL possible and conclude that MTL only works "when tasks are sufficiently similar." The ASP-MTL paper made me think of this previous work because potentially this model could learn what sufficiently similar is -- i.e., if two tasks are not sufficiently similar the shared model would learn nothing and it would fall back to learning two independent systems, as compared to a shared-private model baseline that might overfit and perform poorly. [1] @inproceedings{sogaard2016deep, title={Deep multi-task learning with low level tasks supervised at lower layers}, author={S{\o}gaard, Anders and Goldberg, Yoav}, booktitle={Proceedings of the 54th Annual Meeting of the Association for Computational Linguistics}, volume={2}, pages={231--235}, year={2016}, organization={Association for Computational Linguistics} } # Paper summary This paper presents a method for learning well-partitioned shared and task-specific feature spaces for LSTM text classifiers. Multiclass adversarial training encourages shared space representations from which a discriminative classifier cannot identify the task source (and are thus generic). The models evaluates are a fully-shared, shared-private and adversarial shared-private -- the lattermost ASP model is one of the main contributions. They also use orthogonality constraints to help reward shared and private spaces that are distinct. The ASP model has lower error rate than single-task and other multi-task neural models. They also experiment with a task-level cross validation to explore whether the shared representation can transfer across tasks, and it seems to favourably. Finally, there is some analysis of shared layer activations suggesting that the ASP model is not being misled by strong weights learned on a specific (inappropriate) task. # Review summary Good ideas, well expressed and tested. Some minor comments. # Strengths * This is a nice set of ideas working well together. I particularly like the focus on explicitly trying to create useful shared representations. These have been quite successful in the CV community, but it appears that one needs to work quite hard to create them for NLP. * Sections 2, 3 and 4 are very clearly expressed. * The task-level cross-validation in Section 5.5 is a good way to evaluate the transfer. * There is an implementation and data. # Weaknesses * There are a few minor typographic and phrasing errors. Individually, these are fine, but there are enough of them to warrant fixing: ** l:84 the “infantile cart” is slightly odd -- was this a real example from the data? ** l:233 “are different in” -> “differ in” ** l:341 “working adversarially towards” -> “working against” or “competing with”? ** l:434 “two matrics” -> “two matrices” ** l:445 “are hyperparameter” -> “are hyperparameters” ** Section 6 has a number of number agreement errors (l:745/746/765/766/767/770/784) and should be closely re-edited. ** The shading on the final row of Tables 2 and 3 prints strangely… * There is mention of unlabelled data in Table 1 and semi-supervised learning in Section 4.2, but I didn’t see any results on these experiments. Were they omitted, or have I misunderstood? * The error rate differences are promising in Tables 2 and 3, but statistical significance testing would help make them really convincing. Especially between SP-MLT and ASP-MTL results to highlight the benefit of adversarial training. It should be pretty straightforward to adapt the non-parametric approximate randomisation test (see http://www.lr.pi.titech.ac.jp/~takamura/pubs/randtest.pdf for promising notes a reference to the Chinchor paper) to produce these. * The colours are inconsistent in the caption of Figure 5 (b). In 5 (a), blue is used for “Ours”, but this seems to have swapped for 5 (b). This is worth checking, or I may have misunderstood the caption. # General Discussion * I wonder if there’s some connection with regularisation here, as the effect of the adversarial training with orthogonal training is to help limit the shared feature space. It might be worth drawing that connection to other regularisation literature.

The authors present an actor critic algorithm that learns to adapt learning rates in reinforcement learning problems. They show how their method can be used on several tasks, including Atari games and robotic control experiments. I think this is very interesting work with many potential applications. The results are impressive - they beat state-of-the-art algorithms by large margins (e.g., >100% improvement over previous methods) while using much less data than competing approaches. There are some minor issues I would like them to address before publication. 1) They should add more details about hyperparameters tuning procedure as well as provide additional plots showing performance of different settings. 2) It seems there might have been a mistake when calculating regret values reported in Figure 3 . 3) In Section 4 , it may help if you include one or two examples illustrating your approach The authors present an actor critic algorithm that learns to adjust its learning rate in order to maximize reward. This is done using two neural networks - one network acts as the agent while another network serves as the critic. The author uses reinforcement learning techniques such as Q-learning to train both of these networks simultaneously. This approach allows the agent (network) to adaptively change it's behavior based on feedback from the environment it interacts with. Strengths: 1) It presents a novel way of adjusting the learning rates during training which can be used across different applications. 2) It also provides insights into how this method works through visualization tools like t-SNE plots. Weaknesses: 1) There are no experiments conducted to showcase performance improvements over other methods. 2) No comparison between adaptive vs fixed learning rates has been made The authors present their work on automatic tuning of learning rates with deep RL techniques. The idea seems promising but it would be interesting if they could provide more details regarding how this approach compares against other state-of-the-art approaches like Adam or RMSProp. In addition, I think it's important to mention whether there was any hyperparameter optimization done before running experiments? For example, what values were chosen for batch size, number of epochs etc.? Finally, please clarify why you chose to use MNIST dataset instead of CIFAR-10 when comparing your results with those reported in literature. Overall, I'm positive towards publication after addressing these issues. Yes No Yes No Yes Yes No No Yes Yes Yes Yes The authors present their work on automatic tuning of learning rates with deep RL techniques. The idea seems promising but there should be more discussion/analysis regarding how this approach compares against other approaches like Bayesian optimization etc. Strengths: This article presents some interesting ideas. Weaknesses: There needs to be additional analysis done before it could become a viable alternative. For example, the comparison between different approaches would help readers understand if it's worthwhile pursuing these ideas further. Also, I think the experiments need to be repeated multiple times so you have confidence in your results. Finally, the code provided does not seem very well documented making it difficult to reproduce all the figures presented here. Overall, I'm positive towards this research direction though! This manuscript describes an attempt to use Reinforcement Learning(RL) to tune the learning rate parameter in Stochastic Gradient Descent(SGD). While the proposed solution has been shown effective compared to hand-tuned ones through experimental studies, several issues remain open questions including its scalability when applied to large-scale problems. - Authors

- Strengths: The authors present a novel adaptation of encoder-decoder neural MT using an approach that starts and ends with characters, but in between works with representations of morphemes and characters. The authors release both their code as well as their final learned models for fr-en, cs-en, and en-cs. This is helpful in validating their work, as well as for others looking to replicate and extends this work. The system reported appears to produce translation results of reasonable quality even after the first training epoch, with continued progress in future epochs. The system appears to learn reasonable morphological tokenizations, and appears able to handle previously unseen words (even nonce words) by implicitly backing off to morphemes. - Weaknesses: In the paper, the authors do not explicitly state which WMT test and dev sets their results are reported on. This is problematic for readers wishing to compare the reported results to existing work (for example, the results at matrix.statmt.org). The only way this reviewer found to get this information was to look in the README of the code supplement, which indicates that the test set was newstest2015 and the dev test was newstest2013. This should have been explicitly described in the paper. The instructions given in the software README are OK, but not great. The training and testing sections each could be enhanced with explicit examples of how to run the respective commands. The software itself should respond to a --help flag, which it currently does not. The paper describes a 6-level architecture, but the diagram in Figure 2 appears to show fewer than 6 layers. What's going on? The caption should be more explicit, and if this figure is not showing all of the layers, then there should be a figure somewhere (even if it's in an appendix) showing all of the layers. The results show comparison to other character-based neural systems, but do not show state-of-the-art results for other types of MT system. WMT (and matrix.statmt.org) has reported results for other systems on these datasets, and it appears that the state-of-the-art is much higher than any of the results reported in this paper. That should be acknowledged, and ideally should be discussed. There are a handful of minor English disfluencies, misspellings, and minor LaTeX issues, such as reverse quotation marks. These should be corrected. - General Discussion: Paper is a nice contribution to the existing literature on character-based neural MT. - Strengths: In general, the paper is well structured and clear. It is possible to follow most of the explanation, the ideas presented are original and the results obtained are quite interesting. - Weaknesses: I have some doubts about the interpretation of the results. In addition, I think that some of the claims regarding the capability of the method proposed to learn morphology are not propperly backed by scientific evidence. - General Discussion: This paper explores a complex architecture for character-level neural machine translation (NMT). The proposed architecture extends a classical encoder-decoder architecture by adding a new deep word-encoding layer capable of encoding the character-level input into sub-word representations of the source-language sentence. In the same way, a deep word-decoding layer is added to the output to transform the target-language sub-word representations into a character sequence as the final output of the NMT system. The objective of such architecture is to take advantage of the benefits of character-level NMT (reduction of the size of the vocabulary and flexibility to deal with unseen words) and, at the same time, improving the performance of the whole system by using an intermediate representation of sub-words to reduce the size of the input sequence of characters. In addition, the authors claim that their deep word-encoding model is able to learn morphology better than other state-of-the-art approaches. I have some concerns regarding the evaluation. The authors compare their approach to other state-of-the-art systems taking into account two parameters: training time and BLEU score. However, I do not clearly see the advantage of the model proposed (DCNMT) in front of other approaches such as bpe2char. The difference between both approaches as regards BLEU score is very small (0.04 in Cs-En and 0.1 in En-Cs) and it is hard to say if one of them is outperforming the other one without statistical significance information: has statistical significance been evaluated? As regards the training time, it is worth mentioning that the bpe2char for Cs-En takes 8 days less than DCNMT. For En-Cs training time is not provided (why not?) and for En-Fr bpe2char is not evaluated. I think that a more complete comparison with this system should be carried out to prove the advantages of the model proposed. My second concern is on the 5.2 Section, where authors start claiming that they investigated about the ability of their system to learn morphology. However, the section only contains a examples and some comments on them. Even though these examples are very well chosen and explained in a very didactic way, it is worth noting that no experiments or formal evaluation seem to have been carried out to support the claims of the authors. I would definitely encourage authors to extend this very interesting part of the paper that could even become a different paper itself. On the other hand, this Section does not seem to be a critical point of the paper, so for the current work I may suggest just to move this section to an appendix and soften some of the claims done regarding the capabilities of the system to learn morphology. Other comments, doubts and suggestions: - There are many acronyms that are used but are not defined (such as LSTM, HGRU, CNN or PCA) or which are defined after starting to use them (such as RNN or BPE). Even though some of these acronyms are well known in the field of deep learning, I would encourage the authors to defined them to improve clearness. - The concept of energy is mentioned for the first time in Section 3.1. Even though the explanation provided is enough at that point, it would be nice to refresh the idea of energy in Section 5.2 (where it is used several times) and providing some hints about how to interpret it: a high energy on a character would be indicating that the current morpheme should be split at that point? In addition, the concept of peak (in Figure 5) is not described. - When the acronym BPE is defined, capital letters are used, but then, for the rest of mentions it is lower cased; is there a reason for this? - I am not sure if it is necessary to say that no monolingual corpus is used in Section 4.1. - It seems that there is something wrong with Figure 4a since the colours for the energy values are not shown for every character. - In Table 1, the results for model (3) (Chung et al. 2016) for Cs-En were not taken from the papers, since they are not reported. If the authors computed these results by themselves (as it seems) they should mention it. - I would not say that French is morphologically poor, but rather that it is not that rich as Slavic languages such as Czech. - Why a link is provided for WMT'15 training corpora but not for WMT'14? - Several references are incomplete Typos: - "..is the bilingual, parallel corpora provided..." -> "..are the bilingual, parallel corpora provided..." - "Luong and Manning (2016) uses" -> "Luong and Manning (2016) use" - "HGRU (It is" -> "HGRU (it is" - "coveres" -> "covers" - "both consists of two-layer RNN, each has 1024" -> "both consist of two-layer RNN, each have 1024" - "the only difference between CNMT and DCNMT is CNMT" -> "the only difference between CNMT and DCNMT is that CNMT"

The authors present an interesting approach to learning from data with feedback, which is often difficult because of the lack of ground truth labels. The proposed method uses surprisals as features and trains them using backpropagation through time (BPTT). This allows it to learn without any supervision or prior knowledge about what kind of information should be stored by the network. I think that this work has several important contributions beyond simply introducing new techniques into recurrent networks research. First, I find their use of BPTT very compelling since it provides a principled way to train RNNs when there are no labeled examples available. Second, they show how to incorporate external memory into these models via attention mechanisms on top of LSTMs. Finally, they provide some insights regarding why certain types of memories may not be retained over long periods of training. Overall, it's a great contribution! The authors present an interesting approach to training recurrent networks using surprisal-driven feedback. They show that this method can be used for sequence-to-sequence learning with LSTM cells as well as RNNs trained on language modeling tasks. The results are promising but not conclusive yet; more experiments need to be done before we know whether it is possible to train these models without any teacher forcing at all (i.e., only relying on self-critical sequences). Strengths: This work presents some very nice ideas about how to use information from previous time steps when updating network parameters during backpropagation through time. Weaknesses: It would have been helpful if they had also shown experimental evidence of their claims regarding gradient vanishing/exploding problems caused by standard BPTT updates. In addition, I think there should be further discussion of why exactly the proposed update rule solves those issues. I am curious too about how much data needs to be stored per timestep compared to normal BPTT? The authors present their work with clarity. The results of experiments seem to support claims made about proposed method. I have only one minor comment regarding terminology - I would prefer if they use "feedback" instead of "prediction error" as the latter term can also refer to difference between predicted value and ground truth (which does not necessarily mean there was any mistake). Otherwise, it's great! Thanks! Yes No Yes Yes Yes Yes Yes Yes Yes Yes Yes Yes Yes Yes Yes Yes Yes Yes Yes Yes Yes Yes Yes Yes Yes Yes Yes Yes Yes Yes Yes Yes Yes Yes Yes Yes The authors present their work with clear explanations of concepts involved as well as experimental results to back up claims made throughout the text. The experiments performed seem appropriate considering the scope of research presented here. Strengths: 1) A novel approach to improving performance of RNNs using surprise driven feedback 2) Clear explanation 3) Appropriate experimentation 4) Outperforming state-of-the-art Weaknesses: No apparent weaknesses were found during reading through the manuscript. Overall I think it's very interesting piece of research worth publishing! I would like to see more discussion about how these findings can be applied beyond simple sequence prediction tasks such as those tested here. In particular, I'm interested if there any potential applications where one may want to predict sequences but also have some notion of uncertainty associated with each predicted element?

- Strengths: Nicely written and understandable. Clearly organized. Targeted answering of research questions, based on different experiments. - Weaknesses: Minimal novelty. The "first sentence" heuristic has been in the summarization literature for many years. This work essentially applies this heuristic (evolved) in the keyword extraction setting. This is NOT to say that the work is trivial: it is just not really novel. Lack of state-of-the-art/very recent methods. The experiment on the system evaluation vs state-of-the-art systems simply uses strong baselines. Even though the experiment answers the question "does it perform better than baselines?", I am not confident it illustrates that the system performs better than the current state-of-the-art. This somewhat reduces the value of the paper. - General Discussion: Overall the paper is good and I propose that it be published and presented. On the other hand, I would propose that the authors position themselves (and the system performance) with respect to: Martinez‐Romo, Juan, Lourdes Araujo, and Andres Duque Fernandez. "SemGraph: Extracting keyphrases following a novel semantic graph‐based approach." Journal of the Association for Information Science and Technology 67.1 (2016): 71-82. (with which the work holds remarkable resemblance in some points) Le, Tho Thi Ngoc, Minh Le Nguyen, and Akira Shimazu. "Unsupervised Keyphrase Extraction: Introducing New Kinds of Words to Keyphrases." Australasian Joint Conference on Artificial Intelligence. Springer International Publishing, 2016.

The paper presented by the authors describes a novel approach to machine translation, which uses iterative refinement. This approach is based on the idea of having a system that can improve its translation quality over time by making use of feedback from the user. The authors clearly explain the concept of iterative refinement, and how it can be used to improve the accuracy of machine translation. They also provide a detailed description of their proposed system, which includes a set of components such as a translation engine, a feedback module, and a refinement module. The authors also discuss how their system can be used to improve the accuracy of translations over time. The authors have done an excellent job in presenting their ideas and their proposed system in a clear and concise manner. The paper is well-structured, and the authors provide a comprehensive analysis of the proposed system. The authors also provide an evaluation of their system, which shows that it can improve the accuracy of translations over time. In conclusion, the paper provides an interesting and novel approach to machine translation. The authors have done a great job in presenting their ideas and their proposed system in a clear manner. The evaluation results also show that their system can improve the accuracy of translations over time. This paper addresses the problem of improving the quality of machine translation output by using iterative refinement. The authors propose a method for iterative refinement that uses a recurrent neural network to refine the output of a machine translation system. Strengths: -The paper presents a novel approach to improving the quality of machine translation output by using iterative refinement. -The authors provide a detailed description of their proposed method, which includes a recurrent neural network and a set of training parameters. -The authors provide a thorough evaluation of their proposed method, which includes a comparison with a baseline system and a comparison with a human-translated reference. Weaknesses: -The paper does not discuss the scalability of the proposed method. -The authors do not provide an analysis of the impact of the proposed method on the translation quality of different languages. -The authors do not discuss the potential for further improvement of the proposed method. This paper presents a novel machine translation decoding algorithm that uses iterative refinement to correct mistakes made in earlier decisions. The authors parameterize their model as a convolutional neural network that predicts discrete substitutions to an existing translation based on an attention mechanism over both the source sentence and the current translation output. The results of the experiments show that this approach can improve the output of a phrase-based translation system by up to 0.4 BLEU on WMT15 German-English translation. The paper is well-written and the authors have done a thorough job of presenting their approach and results. The authors have provided a clear description of the model, and the experiments are well-designed and the results are convincing. The paper also includes a discussion of the limitations of the approach, which is a good contribution. Overall, this is a well-done paper that presents an interesting and useful approach to machine translation. The authors have done a good job of presenting their approach and results, and the paper is well-written. This paper addresses the problem of existing machine translation decoding algorithms which generate translations in a strictly monotonic fashion and never revisit previous decisions, thus leading to earlier mistakes not being corrected at a later stage. The paper presents a translation scheme which starts from an initial guess and then makes iterative improvements which may revisit previous decisions. The model is parameterized as a convolutional neural network that predicts discrete substitutions to an existing translation based on an attention mechanism over both the source sentence as well as the current translation output. Strengths of the paper include the fact that the proposed translation scheme is parameterized as a convolutional neural network and that it is able to make less than one modification per sentence. The paper also reports a 0.4 BLEU improvement on WMT15 German-English translation. A weakness of the paper is that it does not compare its results to other state-of-the-art machine translation models to demonstrate how effective the proposed model is. Additionally, the paper does not provide any analysis of the iterative improvements that are being made, and it would be interesting to see how the model is able to make such improvements.

This paper describes a method of accelerating neural network training and inference on mobile devices through the use of on-device dataset partitioning, parameter pruning, and model compression. The method uses techniques such as distillation, quantization, and filter pruning to achieve significant speedups over ordinarytraining on the order of 10-100x. The method was tested using ResNet-50 on CIFAR-10 and Inception-v3 on ImageNet, and showed performance parity with standard training on ResNet-50 and similar to but slightly slower than standard training on Inception-v3. The codebase has been made available to the community and contains easy-to-use layer-by-layer alternative to ordinary training for both ResNet and Inception. ositvation The paper presents a general approach for accelerating neural network execution on mobile devices. The proposed approach makes use of on-device dataset partitioning, parameter pruning, and model compression to achieve 10x - 100x speedups over ordinary training on common neural networks. The proposed approach is easy to integrate into existing training workflow and does not require architecture changes. Modified script for ResNet-50 training on CIFAR-10 from default checkpoint: fromModified script for ResNet-50 training on CIFAR-10 using DeepRebirth: fromModified script for ResNet-50 training on CIFAR-10 with similar performance to ordinary training: fromModified script for Inception-v3 training on ImageNet using DeepRebirth: fromModified script for Inception-v3 training on ImageNet with similar performance to ordinary training: <script type="text/javascript" src="https://gist.github.com/cynnowmare/e7c3b4f5e72b72fc97d6402c4b53e537"></script> The DeepRebirth paper addresses the problem of speeding up the performance of DNN execution on mobile devices. The authors propose a simple and effective solution called batching, where the neural network is divided into smaller batches and each batch is sent to the device in a batch job. The batch job is submitted and processed in parallel, thus dividing the total running time into the number of parallel tasks. The paper shows that DeepRebirth is effective across different DNN architectures, data sets, and mobile device hardware. Some other work that is related is Giraph, a system for running graph algorithms on Hadoop, which can be used to parallelize DNN training, but its implementation is complicated and requires extensive configuration. DeepRebirth is much simpler and easier to apply. Strengths: The main strength of the paper is its description of a general DeepRebirth approach that can be used to parallelize DNN training on mobile devices. The main idea is to divide the DNN into smaller batches and send the batches in batches to the device for processing in parallel. To evaluate the effectiveness of the approach, the paper shows results on two data sets using two different DNN architectures and a mobile CPU as well as a mobile GPU. The paper also shows that DeepRebirth is effective across different DNN architectures, data sets, and mobile device hardware. The implementation of DeepRebirth is also simple and requires only three lines of code for each DNN layer. The approach also has the potential for automatization, since it only requires the definition of the DNN layers and the number of concurrent threads to be specified. Weaknesses: The main weakness of DeepRebirth is that it only applies to DNNs that are divided into small batches. If the batches are too large, then some of the batch jobs will be handled by the device hardware instead of on the device, since DeepRebirth divides the total running time into the number of parallel tasks. Thus, if the batch size is not adjusted, then DeepRebirth is not efficient. The implementation of DeepRebirth is also limited to deep learning frameworks that are based on Java, since the paper uses Java APIs for all of the layers. If the DeepRebirth approach is applied to a DNN that does not have layers that can be batched, then it may not be applicable, since it may not be possible to divide the DNN into batches. Other related work: Giraph is another system that can be used to parallelize DNN training, but its implementation is complicated and requires extensive configuration. DeepRebirth is much simpler and easier to apply. Conclusion: DeepRebirth is a general approach for speeding up DNN execution on mobile devices. The main strength of the paper is its description of a general approach that can be applied to many different DNNs, datasets, and mobile device hardware. The approach is simple and easy to apply, since it only requires three lines of code for each DNN layer. I suggest that the following changes be made to strengthen the paper and make it easier to apply to other DNNs. 1. The paper uses Java APIs for all of the layers. If the DeepRebirth approach is applied to a DNN that does not have layers written in Java, then it may not be applicable, since it may not be possible to use DeepRebirth. 2. The paper shows results on two data sets using two different DNN architectures and a mobile CPU as well as a mobile GPU. If the approach is applied to a DNN specific hardware configuration, then it should show results for that hardware. 3. The approach is only applicable to DNNs that are divided into small batches. If the batches are too large, then some of the batch jobs may be handled by the device hardware instead of on the device. 4. Automating the approach by requiring the definition of only the DNN layers and the number of concurrent threads to be specified could be very useful. However, the paper only shows the lines of code for the DNN layers, which is not enough information for someone else to apply the approach. Overall, DeepRebirth is a good approach for speeding up DNN execution on mobile devices. I think it should be easier to apply to other DNNs and better documented. I recommend the paper be accepted with these suggestions applied. This paper presents a novel acceleration framework called DeepRebirth to speed up the execution of deep learning models on mobile devices. The design of DeepRebirth is motivated by the key observation: some layers in deep learning models are computation critical and acceleration of these layers can speed up the model execution dramatically. To this end, DeepRebirth merges several layers into a new dense layer and learns model acceleration coefficients in an end-to-end manner. Extensive experiments on large-scale ImageNet models show that DeepRebirth is able to speed up the models by as much as 5 times with only negligible accuracy drop. Furthermore, combining with weight quantization and model compression techniques, DeepRebirth is able to achieve up to 65ms model execution time on a mobile device using a model with only 2.4% accuracy drop. esk This paper presents a novel acceleration framework called DeepRebirth to speed up the execution of deep learning models on mobile devices. The design of DeepRebirth is motivated by the key observation: some layers in deep learning models are computation critical and acceleration of these layers can speed up the model execution dramatically. To this end, DeepRebirth merges several layers into a new dense layer and learns model acceleration coefficients in an end-to-end manner. Extensive experiments on large-scale ImageNet models show that DeepRebirth is able to speed up the models by as much as 5 times with only negligible accuracy drop. Furthermore, combining with weight quantization and model compression techniques, DeepRebirth is able to achieve up to 65ms model execution time on a mobile device using a model with only 2.4% accuracy drop. I find this paper interesting and discuss the following strengths and weaknesses of the paper: Strength the paper reveals a general approach to accelerate deep neural network (DNN) execution on mobile devices. Weakness the paper only discusses a case for ImageNet classification, and provide very limited experiment case for model compression and acceleration, which leaves the practicality of the proposed approach uncertain. Overall, I think this is a interesting paper and worth considering for publication in a journal, though it needs to be tested on more model types and with more implementation details, before being accepted for publication. assisted cloning method. It explores the model parameter sparsity through merging the parameter-free layers with their neighbor convolution layers to a single dense layer. This is referred as rebirth layer in the paper.
