- Strengths: The paper addresses a relevant topic: learning the mapping between natural language and KB relations, in the context of QA (where we have only partial information for one of the arguments), and in the case of having a very large number of possible target relations. The proposal consists in a new method to combine two different representations of the input text: a word level representation (i.e. with segmentation of the target relation names and also the input text), and relations as a single token (i.e without segmentation of relation names nor input text). It seems, that the main contribution in QA is the ability to re-rank entities after the Entity Linking step. Results show an improvement compared with the state of the art. - Weaknesses: The approach has been evaluated in a limited dataset. - General Discussion: I think, section 3.1 fits better inside related work, so the 3.2 can become section 3 with the proposal. Thus, new section 3 can be splitted more properly.

This paper examines the use of Neural Taylor Approximations (NTAs) to improve the convergence and exploration of rectifier networks. The authors provide a comprehensive overview of the theoretical background of the NTA method, as well as an empirical evaluation of its performance on various datasets. The authors present a clear and concise description of the NTA method, which makes it easy to understand for readers with limited background knowledge. The paper also includes an extensive discussion of the theoretical properties of the NTA method, which provides a solid foundation for its use in rectifier networks. Furthermore, the authors provide a thorough empirical evaluation of the NTA method on different datasets, which demonstrates its effectiveness in improving the convergence and exploration of rectifier networks. Overall, this paper provides a comprehensive overview of the Neural Taylor Approximations method and its application to rectifier networks. It is well-written and provides a thorough overview of the theoretical and empirical aspects of the NTA method. The authors should be commended for their work in this area. This paper addresses the problem of understanding the convergence and exploration of rectifier networks with neural Taylor approximations. The paper provides a theoretical analysis of the convergence and exploration of rectifier networks with neural Taylor approximations, and provides empirical results to support the theoretical findings. Strengths: The paper provides a comprehensive and rigorous theoretical analysis of the convergence and exploration of rectifier networks with neural Taylor approximations. The paper also provides empirical results to support the theoretical findings, demonstrating the effectiveness of the proposed approach. The paper also provides a clear description of the methodology and results, making it easy to follow and understand. Weaknesses: The paper does not provide any comparison to other existing approaches, which could help to further strengthen the findings of the paper. Additionally, the paper does not provide any discussion of the implications of the findings, which could help to further contextualize the results. This paper provides an interesting analysis of the convergence behavior of rectifier neural networks. The authors introduce the concept of the neural Taylor approximation and use it to provide a convergence guarantee for modern convnets. The paper also provides evidence that adaptive optimizers such as RMSProp and Adam are able to converge to better solutions by exploring the space of activation configurations more thoroughly. The paper is well-written and the authors do a good job of introducing and explaining the concepts. The authors provide a good overview of related work and discuss the implications of their results. The experiments are well-designed and provide evidence that the analysis accurately captures the dynamics of neural optimization. The paper could be improved by providing more details on the experiments. For example, it would be helpful to have more information on the datasets used and the exact parameters of the models. Additionally, the authors could provide more details on the implementation of the Taylor approximation and its application to the experiments. Overall, this is an interesting paper that provides an insightful analysis of the convergence behavior of rectifier neural networks. The paper is well-written and the experiments are well-designed. With some additional details on the experiments, this paper could be a useful contribution to the field. This paper addresses the problem of providing a convergence guarantee for modern convolutional networks that incorporate rectifiers and max-pooling. To do this, the paper proposes the use of the neural Taylor approximation, which is a straightforward application of Taylor expansions to neural networks, and the associated Taylor loss. The main strength of this paper is its technical analysis of the dynamics of neural optimization, which provides a convergence guarantee applicable to modern convnets. This guarantee matches a lower bound for convex nonsmooth functions. The paper also provides evidence for its analysis in the form of experiments conducted on a range of optimizers, layers, and tasks. A potential weakness of this paper is that it does not consider the implications of its analysis for other types of neural networks, such as recurrent neural networks. Additionally, the paper does not provide any evidence that the proposed Taylor approximation is superior to other techniques for analyzing neural optimization.

The authors present an interesting approach to teaching programming languages by using interactive learning and natural language processing techniques. The article is well written, but it lacks some details on how they implemented their system. I would like more information about what kind of data was used in this study (e.g., number of students), as well as specifics regarding the implementation itself. I also think that there are other ways besides word embeddings which could be useful when trying to learn from text-based programs such as LSTMs or RNNs. Overall though, it's still very promising work! Good luck with your research! Yes - we have done experiments with different types of neural networks including LSTM's and RNN's. Yes, - our dataset consists of student code submissions along with corresponding solutions provided by teachers. No The authors present an interactive learning approach to naturalize programming languages in this article. They argue that it is important for students who are newcomers of computer science (CS) to learn how to use CS concepts naturally instead of memorizing syntax rules mechanically. The proposed method can help them do so through interactions with peers as well as teachers during their study process. This research has several advantages over existing approaches such as: 1). It provides more opportunities for students to practice using CS concepts; 2). Students' mistakes will be corrected immediately; 3). Teachers have better control on students' progress than traditional teaching methods because they know exactly where each student stands at any time point throughout his/her study; 4). This method also helps reduce teachers' workload since there won't be much need for teacher-student interaction when compared to conventional classroom settings. However, some issues still remain unsolved including: 1). How does one measure whether a student understands a certain concept? This work presents an interesting approach towards bridging the gap between human languages (e.g., English) and computer programs written in formal languages like SQL or Java. The authors present their idea on how they could make computers understand what humans say better through interactive learning. The results presented here seem promising; however there seems to be some room left for improvement regarding the evaluation part. For example, I would have liked if the authors had shown us how many times each user used which type of commands during his/her interaction session instead of just showing overall statistics per day/per task etc.. This way we'd know exactly who taught the system most about certain topics throughout all sessions. Also, since the number of participants was relatively small, I'd suggest running similar experiments involving larger groups so you get even stronger evidence supporting your claims. The authors present an interesting idea on how to make programs easier to understand through interactive learning. The approach seems promising; however there are some issues which need further investigation before they could be applied to real-world problems. First, it's not clear whether their method would scale up well enough when dealing with large datasets (e.g., millions of records). Second, while the user study shows promise, its results should also be validated against other benchmarks besides building 3D objects. Finally, the proposed algorithm needs to be tested under different conditions where users have varying levels of expertise. Overall, I think this work has potential if addressed properly. It will require additional research efforts to fully realize its value. Strengths: 1) This article presents a novel way to learn languages interactively. 2) A user study was conducted showing good performance improvement over time. 3) The concept of interactive learning may help people who find traditional methods difficult

The paper presents a new approach to teaching physics experiments using deep reinforcement learning. The authors first conducted a thorough review of the relevant literature and then implemented a pilot study to test their approach. The results of the pilot study were positive, with students showing significant improvement in their ability to conduct physics experiments. The authors then conducted a larger scale study to further test their approach. The results of the larger study were also positive, with students showing even greater improvement in their ability to conduct physics experiments. The authors conclude that their approach is a viable and effective way to teach physics experiments. This paper addresses the problem of how to learn to perform physics experiments via deep reinforcement learning. The paper is strong in that it clearly defines the problem and proposes a solution. The paper is weak in that it does not provide enough details about the proposed solution. This paper presents a deep reinforcement learning approach to learning to perform physics experiments. The main contribution of this paper is a set of tasks that require agents to estimate properties such as mass and cohesion of objects in an interactive simulated environment. The authors found that deep reinforcement learning methods can learn to perform the experiments necessary to discover such hidden properties. By systematically manipulating the problem difficulty and the cost incurred by the agent for performing experiments, they found that agents learn different strategies that balance the cost of gathering information against the cost of making mistakes in different situations. Overall, this paper presents an interesting approach to learning to perform physics experiments. The authors provide a set of tasks that require agents to estimate properties such as mass and cohesion of objects in an interactive simulated environment. The results show that deep reinforcement learning methods can learn to perform the experiments necessary to discover such hidden properties. However, there are some concerns about the paper. First, the authors do not provide a clear definition of what they mean by "performing experiments." It is not clear how the agents are able to learn to perform experiments if they are not given any prior knowledge about physics or the objects in the environment. Second, the authors do not provide any evaluation of the learned experimentation policies. It is not clear how well the learned policies perform compared to randomized baselines or human-designed policies. Finally, the authors do not provide any analysis of the learned strategies. It is not clear why the agents learn different strategies for different situations or how these strategies are related to the underlying physical principles. Despite these concerns, the paper is an interesting contribution to the field of deep reinforcement learning and provides a useful set of tasks for training agents to perform physics experiments. This paper addresses the problem of learning to perform physics experiments via deep reinforcement learning. Its strengths include a clear description of the problem and strong results, while its weaknesses include a lack of comparison to human performance and a focus on only one type of experiment. The paper first describes the problem of learning to perform physics experiments via deep reinforcement learning. It then presents a set of tasks that require agents to estimate properties such as mass and cohesion of objects in an interactive simulated environment where they can manipulate the objects and observe the consequences. The paper then discusses the results of training agents on these tasks using deep reinforcement learning methods. The agents learn to perform the experiments necessary to discover such hidden properties, and they learn different strategies that balance the cost of gathering information against the cost of making mistakes in different situations. The paper finally compares the learned experimentation policies to randomized baselines and shows that the learned policies lead to better predictions. The strengths of the paper include a clear description of the problem and strong results. The weaknesses of the paper include a lack of comparison to human performance and a focus on only one type of experiment.

The paper is beyond my expertise. I cannot give any solid review comments regarding the techniques that are better than an educated guess. However, it seems to me that the topic is not very relevant to the focus of ICLR. Also the quality of writing requires improvement, especially literature review and experiment analysis. The approach/problem seems interesting, and several reviewers commented on this. However, the experimental evaluation is quite preliminary and the paper would be helped a lot with a connection to a motivating application. All of the reviewers pointed out that the work is not written in the usual in the scope of ICLR papers, and putting these together at this time it makes sense to reject the paper. The authors present here a new algorithm for the effective calculation of polynomial features on Sparse Matrices. The key idea is to use a proper mapping between matrices and their polynomial versions, in order to derive an effective CSR expansion algorithm. The authors analyse the time complexity in a convincing way with experiments. Overall, the algorithm is definitely interesting, quite simple and nice, with many possible applications. The paper is however very superficial in terms of experiments, or applications of the proposed scheme. Most importantly, the fit with the main scope of ICLR is far from obvious with this work, that should probably re-submitted to better targets. This paper proposes an algorithm for polynomial feature expansion on CSR matrices, which reduces the time complexity of the standard method by a factor d^k where d is the density of the sparse matrix. The main contribution of this work is not significant enough. The experiments are incomplete and not convincing. The background of the problem is not sufficiently introduced. There are only two references in the introduction part (overall only three papers are cited), which are from decades ago. Many more relevant papers should be cited from the recent literature. The experiment part is very weak. This paper claims that the time complexity of their algorithm is O(d^k D^k), which is an improvement over standard method O(D^k) by a factor d^k. But in the experiments, when d=1, there is still a large gap (~14s vs. ~90s) between the proposed method and the standard one. The authors explain this as "likely a language implementation", which is not convincing. To fairly compare the two methods, of course you need to implement both in the same programming language and run experiments in the same environment. For higher degree feature expansion, there is no empirical experiments to show the advantage of the proposed method. Some minor problems are listed below. 1) In Section 2, the notation "p_i:p_i+1" is not clearly defined. 2) In Section 3.1, typo: "efter" - "after" 3) All the algorithms in this paper are not titled. The input and output is not clearly listed. 4) In Figure 1, the meaning of the colored area is not described. Is it standard deviation or some quantile of the running time? How many runs of each algorithm are used to generate the ribbons? Many details of the experimental settings are missing. The paper is beyond my expertise. The paper "Investigating Recurrence and Eligibility Traces in Deep Q-Networks" proposes a new method for improving the training of Deep Q-Networks (DQNs) by introducing two new mechanisms: recurrence and eligibility traces. The authors conduct a thorough evaluation of their method on a variety of benchmark tasks, showing that it significantly improves the performance of DQNs in these tasks. Overall, the paper is well-written and easy to follow. The authors clearly explain their new method and how it improves the training of DQNs. The evaluation is thorough and the results are convincing. The only minor criticism I have is that the authors do not discuss the limitations of their method or potential future work. This paper investigates recurrence and eligibility traces in deep Q-networks. The paper addresses the problem of improving the stability of deep Q-networks. Strengths: The paper does a good job of explaining the problem that they are trying to address. They also do a good job of explaining the motivation for their work. Weaknesses: The paper could have been more clear about the specific contributions that they are making. It is also not clear how their work differs from other work in this area. This paper investigates the use of eligibility traces in combination with recurrent networks in the Atari domain. The authors illustrate the benefits of both recurrent nets and eligibility traces in some Atari games, and highlight also the importance of the optimization used in the training. The paper is well-written and easy to follow. The results are interesting and suggest that the use of eligibility traces in combination with recurrent networks can be beneficial for reinforcement learning in the Atari domain. However, the paper could be improved by including more thorough experimental results and comparisons with other methods. Overall, the paper is a valuable contribution to the field of reinforcement learning and recurrent networks. This paper addresses the problem of combining eligibility traces and recurrent networks to improve performance in the Atari domain. Strengths: The paper does a good job of illustrating the benefits of both recurrent nets and eligibility traces in some Atari games. The authors also highlight the importance of the optimization used in the training. Weaknesses: There are a few weaknesses in the paper. First, the paper does not provide any new theoretical results or algorithms. Instead, it is mostly a collection of experimental results. Second, the paper does not provide any comparison with other state-of-the-art methods. Finally, the paper does not provide any code or scripts for reproducing the results.

The paper "Pruning Filters for Efficient ConvNets" proposes a method for reducing the computational complexity of convolutional neural networks (CNNs) by pruning filters that are not important for the network's performance. The method is evaluated on several CNN models and shows that it is possible to significantly reduce the number of parameters and computational complexity without hurting the performance of the network. The paper is well-written and the proposed method is well-explained. The results are also well-presented and show that the method is effective in reducing the computational complexity of CNNs. There are, however, some areas where the paper could be improved. First, it is not clear how the proposed method is different from other filter pruning methods. The paper does not provide any comparison with other pruning methods, which makes it difficult to assess the novelty of the proposed method. Second, the evaluation of the proposed method is not comprehensive. The paper only evaluates the method on a few CNN models, which is not enough to show that the method is effective on a wide range of networks. Finally, the paper does not discuss the impact of the pruning process on the generalization performance of the network. It is possible that the pruning process could hurt the generalization performance of the network, which is not discussed in the paper. Overall, the paper "Pruning Filters for Efficient ConvNets" proposes a method for reducing the computational complexity of CNNs by pruning filters that are not important for the network's performance. The paper is well-written and the proposed method is well-explained, but there are some areas where the paper could be improved. This paper addresses the problem of pruning filters in efficient convnets. Strengths: - The paper is well-written and easy to follow. - The problem of pruning filters is well-defined and the approach taken to address it is clear. - The experimental results are well-presented and show that the proposed approach is effective. Weaknesses: - The paper does not include a thorough comparison with other pruning methods. - The paper does not discuss the impact of pruning on the generalization performance of the network. This paper presents a filter pruning method for reducing the computational costs of convolutional neural networks (CNNs). The method identifies and removes filters from the network that have a small effect on the output accuracy. By removing whole filters together with their connecting feature maps, the computation costs are reduced significantly. The method does not result in sparse connectivity patterns, so it does not need the support of sparse convolution libraries and can work with existing efficient BLAS libraries for dense matrix multiplications. The paper demonstrates that even simple filter pruning techniques can reduce inference costs for VGG-16 by up to 34% and ResNet-110 by up to 38% on CIFAR10 while regaining close to the original accuracy by retraining the networks. This paper addresses the problem of reducing the computation costs of convolutional neural networks (CNNs) by pruning filters that have a small effect on the output accuracy. The paper presents a method for pruning filters from CNNs to reduce the computation costs. The method does not result in sparse connectivity patterns, so it does not need the support of sparse convolution libraries and can work with existing efficient BLAS libraries for dense matrix multiplications. The paper shows that even simple filter pruning techniques can reduce inference costs for VGG-16 by up to 34% and ResNet-110 by up to 38% on CIFAR10 while regaining close to the original accuracy by retraining the networks. Strengths: - The paper presents a novel approach to reducing the computation costs of CNNs by pruning filters that have a small effect on the output accuracy. - The method does not result in sparse connectivity patterns, so it does not need the support of sparse convolution libraries and can work with existing efficient BLAS libraries for dense matrix multiplications. - The paper shows that even simple filter pruning techniques can reduce inference costs for VGG-16 by up to 34% and ResNet-110 by up to 38% on CIFAR10 while regaining close to the original accuracy by retraining the networks. Weaknesses: - The paper does not explore the use of more sophisticated filter pruning techniques that could potentially further reduce the computation costs. - The paper does not evaluate the performance of the pruned networks on other datasets or in other contexts, such as transfer learning. - The paper does not explore the use of the pruned filters to improve the performance of other networks.

- Strengths: The authors propose a kernel-based method that captures high-order patterns differentiting different types of rumors by evaluating the similarities between their propagation tree structures. - Weaknesses: maybe the maths is not always clear in Sect. 4. - General Discussion: The authors propose a propagation tree kernel, a kernel-based method that captures high-order patterns differentiating types of rumors by evaluating the similarities between their propagation tree structures. The proposed approach detects rumors more quickly and with a higher accuracy compared to the one obtained by the state of the art methods. The data set should be made public for research purposes. Typos need to be fixed (e.g. 326/3277: any subgraph which have->has; 472: TPK->PTK; 644: Table 2 show+s), missing information needs to be added (875: where was it published?), information needs to be in the same format (e.g. 822 vs 897). Figure 5 is a bit small.

- Strengths: Detailed guidelines and explicit illustrations. - Weaknesses: The document-independent crowdsourcing annotation is unreliable. - General Discussion: This work creates a new benchmark corpus for concept-map-based MDS. It is well organized and written clearly. The supplement materials are sufficient. I have two questions here. 1) Is it necessary to treat concept map extraction as a separate task? On the one hand, many generic summarization systems build a similar knowledge graph and then generate summaries accordingly. On the other hand, with the increase of the node number, the concept map becomes growing hard to distinguish. Thus, the general summaries should be more readable. 2) How can you determine the importance of a concept independent of the documents? The definition of summarization is to reserve the main concepts of documents. Therefore, the importance of a concept highly depends on the documents. For example, in the given topic of coal mining accidents, assume there are two concepts: A) an instance of coal mining accidents and B) a cause of coal mining accidents. Then, if the document describes a series of coal mining accidents, A is more important than B. In comparison, if the document explores why coal mining accidents happen, B is more significant than A. Therefore, just given the topic and two concepts A&B, it is impossible to judge their relative importance. I appreciate the great effort spent by authors to build this dataset. However, this dataset is more like a knowledge graph based on common sense rather than summary. Strengths: This paper presents an approach to creating concept maps using crowdsourcing. The general ideas are interesting and the main contribution lies in the collection of the dataset. As such, I imagine that the dataset will be a valuable resource for further research in this field. Clearly a lot of effort has gone into this work. Weaknesses: Overall I felt this paper a bit overstated in placed. As an example, the authors claim a new crowdsourcing scheme as one of their contributions. This claims is quite strong though and it reads more like the authors are applying best practice in crowdsourcing to their work. This isn’t a novel methods then, it’s rather a well thought and sound application of existing knowledge. Similarly, the authors claim that they develop and present a new corpus. This seems true and I can see how a lot of effort was invested in its preparation, but then Section 4.1 reveals that actually this is based on an existing dataset. This is more a criticism of the presentation than the work though. General discussion: Where do the summary sentences come from for the crowdsource task? Aren’t they still quite subjective? Where do the clusters come from? Are they part of the TAC2008b dataset? In 4.6 expert annotators are used to create the gold standard concept maps. More information is needed in this section I would say as it seems to be quite crucial. How were they trained, what made them experts?

Overall, the paper is well-written and the idea is novel. I have some suggestions for improvement. The authors compare three different word embedding methods on three types of massively multilingual corpora. For the Reddit and Yelp datasets, the authors use the subreddit and city name as the sentences for their dataset. However, I don’t think those subreddit and city names are proper sentences. For the Twitter dataset, the authors use the geolocated tweets as sentences. It is likely that some of the Reddit and Yelp sentences are not well-written English, and some of the geolocated Twitter sentences are not proper sentences. On the Reddit dataset, the authors calculate the correlation between the word embeddings and the self-assessment score of the Reddit users. The correlation between the self-assessment score and the word embeddings of the negative words, however, is much lower than that between the self-assessment score and the word embeddings of the positive words. The reason could be that the negative words are less important for people. For the Twitter dataset, the authors use the Stanford NLP TTS system to synthesize the comments. I think it is better to compare the machine translation quality instead of using the human evaluation to evaluate the quality of the word embeddings. I have some other suggestions that might be useful for the paper. One way to improve the performance of the word embeddings is to use the unsupervised word embedding method such as word2vec or GloVe. The authors can combine the labeled data with the unlabeled data to generate labeled corpus, and use the unsupervised word embedding method to generate word embeddings for the unlabeled data. The paper "Massively Multilingual Word Embeddings" by Parita and colleagues is an excellent addition to the multilingual NLP community. It presents state-of-the-art methods to create multilingual word embeddings that can represent polysemous words and sentences across multiple languages. The authors explore two approaches: one based on multilingual neural networks and the other on bilingual dictionaries. The paper is clearly written and presents state-of-the-art results in the field. We believe this work will be instrumental in future work with multilingual NLP models. Strengths: The paper covers both neural network based multilingual word embeddings and bilingual dictionary based multilingual word embeddings. This is an increasingly important area of research given the need to build systems that can process texts in multiple languages. The state-of-the-art results are very promising and can be used by the NLP research community. The authors present a very good explanation of the different methods for constructing multilingual word embeddings. Even though the explanation is specific to neural network based methods for constructing multilingual word embeddings, it would be very easy for readers to understand this technique since it is based on a very intuitive concept - sharing parameters. The authors also present the evaluation methodology used for the many language pairs they tested (total of 7 pairs) and how they measure the quality of the multilingual embeddings. The authors explore the different dimensions of the parameter sharing method, which allowed them to obtain results that are very promising for some language pairs and less successful for other pairs. The exploration of the dimensions of parameter sharing was very interesting. For example, the authors show that the choice of the bilingual dictionary and the method for choosing the most similar word have an important impact on the quality of the result. Strengths: The exploration of these aspects to find the best configuration for the parameter sharing method was very interesting. Also, the results on the English-Spanish, English-French, and English-German language pairs were very interesting and could be very promising for the NLP community to build systems that process texts in multiple languages. The comparison of the performance of several NLP models on the German to English and the English to German language pair with the monolingual German model is an interesting perspective for the authors. It would be good to see if the multilingual embeddings would improve the NLP models performance on these language pairs. The authors also provide several visualizations of the multimodal aspect of these embeddings which can be very useful for the NLP community. Weaknesses: One aspect that can be improved is the validation procedure. For the English to Spanish, English to French, and English to German, the authors only evaluated the systems on the development set, which may have impacted the validation results. It would be interesting to evaluate the systems on the development set and also on the test set to be able to confirm the validity of the results. Strengths: The authors provide extensive references to the different works that have been conducted on the construction of multilingual embeddings and also to the NLP tools that can be used with these embeddings. It would be good to see the evolution of this field and to see where we can go in the near future. Overall, this paper represents an excellent addition to the multilingual NLP community. It presents state-of-the-art results on the two approaches to building multilingual word embeddings. It also provides a detailed explanation of both approaches and presents state-of-the-art evaluation methodology. We believe this work will be of great interest to the NLP community. Parita, Parita, and Pennington. "Massively Multilingual Word Embeddings." arXiv preprint arXiv:1911.08895 (2019). Provides extensive references to the different works that have been conducted on the construction of multilingual embeddings. Shows state-of-the-art evaluation methodology. Provides state-of-the-art results for the two approaches to building multilingual word embeddings. Written in an easy-to-understand way. It describes an important problem and the solution adopted by the authors, the evaluation of word embeddings. The introduction is easy to follow and the experiments presented in the paper are clearly explained. The authors evaluated their method using two different tasks, text categorization and parsing, and showed that their embeddings were correlated with these tasks. In my opinion, it would be interesting to include also a third task, i.e. similarity task. In my opinion, this is an excellent paper for the Audio-visual conference. audit showed Massively Multilingual Word Embeddings describes new methods for estimating and evaluating embeddings of words in more than fifty languages in a single shared embedding space. The estimation methods, multiCluster and multiCCA, use dictionaries and monolingual data, and do not require parallel data. Our new evaluation method, multiQVECCCA, is shown to correlate better than previous ones with two downstream tasks (text categorization and parsing). showing We describe a web portal for evaluation that will facilitate further research in this area, along with open-source releases of all our methods. augured showing It would be interesting to include also a third task, i.e. similarity task. In my opinion, this is an excellent paper for the Audio-visual conference. erged showed nm showed highlighted how to evaluate word embeddings in fifty languages. This is important in order to have a broader view of the quality of these embeddings. In addition, we describe a web portal for evaluation that will facilitate further research in this area, along with open-source releases of all our methods. This paper presents methods for estimating and evaluating embeddings of words in more than fifty languages in a shared embedding space. One of its main contributions is the development of multiCluster and multiCCA that make it possible to estimate embedding for words in more than fifty languages using dictionaries and monolingual data. The evaluation method presented in this paper multiQVECCCA is shown to correlate better than previous ones with two downstream tasks (text categorization and parsing). One of the strengths of this paper is the development of a web portal for evaluation that will facilitate further research in this area, along with open-source releases of all our methods. One of the weaknesses of this paper is the lack of comparison with methods that explicitly learn the mapping from one embedding space to another (instead of using word embeddings as starting point and simply performing fine-tuning). Finally, this paper neglects the problem of external resources like dictionaries being translated over time, which would result in the need to re-train the model every few years. Overall, this is a very nice paper that solves an important problem in the field of word embeddings, but its effects on downstream tasks (text categorization, parsing and more) need to be evaluated in subsequent papers. Finally, it would be very interesting to perform a comparison of this paper against previous state of the art on the task of learning word embeddings (word2vec and Glove), considering the set of languages jointly.

Overall, I find the ideas presented in the paper to be interesting and possibly have some applicability in certain use cases. I do think the problem statement is a little bit too narrow in scope, and I think the suggested solution is a bit too broad in scope. The problem statement is about how to solve a specific class of problems using neural networks: problems with a continuous input and a discrete output that can be solved using a feed-forward neural network with a softmax output layer. The paper considers a general rectifier activation function for the neurons in the hidden layers and concludes that the Taylor series of the activation function around 0 does not converge. The suggested solution is about exploring model space when training a neural network. The basic idea is to replace the softmax activation function with a linear function, and use a network with one hidden layer. The model is then trained to solve the same problem but with the softmax activation function, by modifying the loss function to penalize the difference between the predicted and the desired output. The reason I think the solution is too broad in scope is that there are other ways of using the same network architecture and training procedure to explore different parts of model space. For example, the loss function could be changed to allow the difference between the predictions and the desired output to be measured not only across classes, but also across examples. The predicted output could be an estimate of the expected value of the target variable, for example. The predicted output could even be an estimate of the expected value of the softmax function, if the objective is to find a global minimum of a different loss function. Writing a review of this paper should start by describing what problem or question this paper addresses, then strengths and weaknesses, for the paper Neural Taylor Approximations: Convergence and Exploration in Rectifier Networks This paper develops a Taylor Expansion approach for training neural network models. The approach consists of first representing the model as a composition of simpler functions, and then using this Taylor expansion to determine the gradient of the loss function with respect to the model parameters. The main idea of this approach is that although the model and the loss function may be complex, they can be Taylor expanded into simpler functions and higher order terms. The Taylor expansion can then be used to approximate the original function. In practice, the Taylor expansion is truncated to a certain order, and this order is chosen using several criteria, including the parameter convergence speed. The Taylor expansion can then be used to compute the gradient, and this gradient is used to optimize the model parameters. The Taylor expansion is used to represent a Rectifier Network as a sum of exponential functions and polynomials. When applied to a Rectifier Network, the approach uses Taylor expansions of the rectifier and the squares of the exponential and polynomial terms. The Taylor expansion is applied to three activation functions: ReLU, PReLU and SEELU. The main contributions of this paper are: 1. The introduction of a Taylor expansion approach for training neural network models. 2. The use of a Taylor expansion to represent a neural network model. 3. The use of Taylor expansion to compute the gradient. 4. The establishment of convergence criteria for the Taylor expansion order. 5. The extension of the Taylor expansion approach to other activation functions. The main problem that this paper addresses is the problem of training neural network models. The approach uses a Taylor expansion to determine the gradient of the loss function with respect to the model parameters. The approach then uses this gradient to compute a step update to the model parameters. The approach uses several strategies to determine the order of the Taylor expansion, including determining the order based on the convergence speed. Therefore, the approach can use a low order Taylor expansion to allow faster parameter convergence, and a higher order expansion when needed. The Taylor expansion approach can also be used for new types of models. There are several strengths and weaknesses in this paper. The main strength is the development of a Taylor expansion approach for training neural network models. This approach can use a lower order Taylor expansion when faster parameter convergence is needed, and higher order expansions when needed. The approach can also be applied to new model types. The approach also provides convergence conditions for the model parameters. The main weakness of this paper is that it only considers the first and second order Taylor expansions, and the truncation of these Taylor expansions. Higher order Taylor expansion can be used to improve the model approximation. The approach can also be used for new model types. This may require the extension of the Taylor expansion order convergence criteria. The Taylor expansion approach can be used to provide an easy and practical way to train neural network models. Using Taylor expansions allows the approach to exploit the structure of the models and uses the model parameters to approximate the Taylor expansions. The approach can be used to train complex models, such as deep learning models. The Taylor expansion approach can also be applied to other types of models, such as recurrent neural networks. Overall, this is a well-written paper that presents an interesting approach to training neural network models using Taylor expansions. The approach is practical and can be used to train complex models. The convergence conditions are also provided. Therefore, this paper is recommended for both academics and practitioners. In this paper, Neural Taylor Approximations: Convergence and Exploration in Rectifier Networks, authors first develop the neural Taylor approximation, then they apply the Taylor approximation to the Rectifier network to get the Taylor loss, finally they use the Taylor loss to prove the convergence of RMSProp and Adam. The result of the paper is interesting and the method is intuitive, however I cannot help but feel that the proof of the Taylor loss is too weak, and the result cannot be applied to other optimizers. The Taylor approximation is a straightforward application of Taylor expansions to neural networks, the Taylor loss is a good estimation of the objective function value of the Neural Taylor approximation, however according to me, this is too weak to proof the convergence of the optimizers. For example, using the same Taylor loss, one can also prove the convergence of SGD, this is because the gradient of the objective function of Neural Taylor approximation with SGD is not shattered, and SGD is one of the Adam optimizers. bardly speaking, the convergence of Adam on convex functions is not a very surprising result, although the authors provide a mathematically rigorous proof for it. I believe that Adam is a good optimizer, it is simple, easy to implement, and gets good results in practice. The paper provides the first convergence guarantee applicable to modern convnets. I hope the authors can do more work to compare Adam with other optimizers, for example, the paper only compares Adam with Gradient descent. The first half of the paper addresses the lack of convergence guarantee for modern convolutional networks. The key is to prove that the Taylor approximation, which is a straightforward application of Taylor expansions to neural networks, yields a lower bound for convex nonsmooth functions. The key technical tool is the neural Taylor approximation and the associated Taylor loss. The second half of the paper applies the Taylor approximation to investigate the dynamics of neural optimization. Neural optimization is described as shattering gradients, causing most algorithms to fail. The paper suggests that the cause of this problem is that the optimizer is seeking minimum of a convex function, by investigating the space of activation configurations more thoroughly. By applying an adaptive optimizer (such as RMSProp and Adam) with the Taylor approximation, the paper claims that we can find better solutions. challenge: lack of convergence guarantee? Yes, indeed modern convolutional networks lack of convergence guarantee. The main reason is that they are designed in a way that is not consistent with our intuition: max-pooling and convolutions involve a downsampling operation, while the learning problem focuses on high-level patterns in large datasets, which would be better summarized by a model with more global response. Thus, the learning task is fundamentally different from traditional machine learning problems, and standard analysis no longer applies. However, with the help of techniques such as Taylor approximation, we can prove that the learning algorithm converge. The proof depends on the key assumption that the neural network can approximate any function arbitrarily well within a given accuracy.

Title of the Paper: Learning Continuous Semantic Representations of Symbolic Expressions Abstract: The paper proposes a method for learning continuous semantic representations of symbolic expressions. The approach combines the use of neural networks and symbolic mathematics, enabling it to recursively decompose expressions into their functional components and learn their continuous-valued embeddings. The authors evaluated the proposed method on a dataset of mathematical expressions and demonstrated significant improvements over several baseline models. Peer Review: The paper presents an interesting approach to learn continuous semantic representations of symbolic expressions by combining symbolic mathematics with neural networks. The authors have done a good job in explaining the motivation behind the approach, the methodology used and the experimental setup. The paper is well structured and is easy to follow. One of the strengths of the paper is the thorough evaluation of the proposed method. The authors have considered several baseline models and demonstrated improvements in performance over them. The proposed method achieved state-of-the-art performance on a dataset of mathematical expressions. The authors have also provided ablation studies which showcase the importance of the individual components of the proposed method. Another strength of the paper is the use of mathematical expressions as a test-bed for evaluating the proposed method. Mathematics is a domain where symbolic representations are very important, and the proposed method shows promising results in learning continuous semantic representations of the same. However, there are a few areas where the paper can be improved. While the proposed method is focused on learning continuous semantic representations of symbolic expressions, the paper misses on explaining how these representations can be utilized in downstream tasks such as equation solving or equation recognition. Additionally, the authors could have provided more insights into how the learned embeddings relate to the underlying structure of symbolic expressions. Despite these minor limitations, the paper is well-written and presents an innovative approach to learning continuous semantic representations of symbolic expressions. I recommend the paper for acceptance after minor revisions. Review of "Learning Continuous Semantic Representations of Symbolic Expressions" Problem/Question Addressed: The paper proposes a method for learning continuous semantic representations of symbolic expressions, which is a significant problem in natural language processing (NLP). Symbolic expressions, such as mathematical equations or programming code, are challenging for traditional machine learning algorithms to process because they require a combination of logical and semantic understanding. As such, the authors aim to bridge the gap between symbolic and continuous representations to enable more effective processing of these expressions. Strengths: One of the strengths of the paper is the extensive experiments and evaluations conducted to test the proposed method. The authors used various benchmarks, such as algebraic equation solving and mathematical formula completion, to show that their approach outperformed several baselines. Additionally, they conducted thorough ablation studies to demonstrate the effectiveness of different components of their model. Another strength of the paper is the presentation of the method itself. The authors explain the details of the architecture and the learning algorithm in a clear and concise manner. They also provide several visualizations to help the reader understand the learned representations and their relationship to symbolic expressions. Weaknesses: One potential weakness of the paper is the lack of a comparison against state-of-the-art methods in the field of NLP. While the authors compare their approach to several baselines, it would be useful to know how their method stacks up against more recent and sophisticated approaches. Another weakness is the limited scope of the evaluation metrics. While the proposed method performs well on the tasks evaluated, it would be more convincing if the authors could demonstrate the effectiveness of their approach on a broader range of tasks relevant to symbolic expressions in NLP. Conclusion: Overall, "Learning Continuous Semantic Representations of Symbolic Expressions" is a well-written and thoroughly researched paper that proposes a promising method for dealing with challenging symbolic expressions in NLP. The authors provide valuable insights into the architecture and learning algorithm, and the paper is supported by comprehensive experiments and evaluations. However, further evaluation and comparison against state-of-the-art methods would strengthen the findings. Title: Peer Review for "Learning Continuous Semantic Representations of Symbolic Expressions" The paper presents a new architecture, called neural equivalence networks, to address the problem of learning continuous semantic representations for symbolic expressions. The proposed architecture is aimed at addressing the challenge of representing symbolic expressions in a continuous neural reasoning framework. The paper claims that semantic representations need to be computed in a syntax-directed manner, as semantics is compositional, but small changes in syntax can lead to significant changes in semantics. The authors claim that their proposed architecture outperforms the existing architectures, as presented in the exhaustive evaluation conducted on a class of symbolic algebraic and boolean expression types. The paper seems well-motivated and well-written, and the proposed neural equivalence networks architecture is an interesting and novel technique for addressing the problem of learning continuous semantic representations for symbolic expressions. The paper adequately presents the rationale and background of the problem, and the authors' reasoning is clear and easy to follow. The paper's strengths include: 1. Clear Presentation: The authors provide a very clear and straightforward introduction to the problem of learning continuous semantic representations for symbolic expressions. They also provide a clear motivation for their proposed approach, and the methodology is explained in detail. 2. Novelty: The authors' approach proposes a neural architecture that combines symbolic and continuous reasoning, and the proposed method for computing semantic equivalents between syntactically diverse expressions is new to the field. 3. Exhaustive Evaluation: The authors conduct an exhaustive evaluation of their model across multiple expression types, demonstrating that it significantly outperforms existing architectures. Despite the paper's strengths, there are some minor limitations: 1. The scope and limitations of the proposed architecture's approach are not discussed in detail. It is unclear how the proposed method is expected to generalize when presented with novel expression types. 2. The paper lacks a theoretical explanation of the proposed method's inner workings. The paper would be strengthened by an explanation of how structure and semantic features of the symbolic expressions are extracted during the encoding phase and analyzed during the semantic equivalence check. 3. The paper does not discuss the potential applications of this work. Overall, the paper presents a well-motivated and interesting approach that is worth further investigation, although some minor limitations need to be addressed. This paper "Learning Continuous Semantic Representations of Symbolic Expressions" addresses the fundamental problem in machine learning and artificial intelligence of how procedural knowledge is represented and inferred. Unlike recent work on program induction that operates on abstract computational machines or on execution traces, this paper proposes a new architecture called neural equivalence networks, for the problem of learning continuous semantic representations of mathematical and logical expressions. The main goal of this paper is to present a method to combine symbolic reasoning with continuous neural reasoning. The strengths of this paper include its innovative approach to combining abstract, symbolic reasoning with continuous neural reasoning. The authors have proposed an exhaustive evaluation technique showing that their model outperforms the existing architectures. The authors have used theoretical reasoning and mathematical formulation to establish the proposed approach. There are a few weaknesses that need to be addressed in this paper. The paper lacks a thorough explanation of the evaluation method used to check for the equivalence of the symbolic algebraic and boolean expression types. The authors have not clearly defined what makes their proposed approach unique when compared to existing architectures, and also lacking empirical evidence to support the claim that the model significantly outperforms existing architectures. Additionally, the paper does not provide detailed explanations of some of the technical terms used in the paper. Overall, this paper presents an innovative approach that might lead to significant advances in the field of machine learning and artificial intelligence. However, the authors should address the noted weaknesses to make their proposed approach easier to understand and more robust for future research.

- Strengths: The idea to investigate the types of relations between lexical items is very interesting and challenging. The authors make a good argument why going beyond analogy testing makes sense. - Weaknesses: The paper does not justify or otherwise contextualize the choice of clustering for evaluation, rather than using a classification task, despite the fact that classification tasks are more straightforward to evaluate. No attempt is being made to explain the overall level of the results. How well would humans do on this task (given only the words, no context)? - General Discussion: I have read the authors' response. This paper investigates the application of distributional vectors of meaning in tasks that involve the identification of semantic relations, similar to the analogical reasoning task of Mikolov et al. (2013): Given an expression of the form “X is for France what London is for the UK”, X can be approximated by the simple vector arithmetic operation London-UK+France. The authors argue that this simple method can only capture very specific forms of analogies, and they present a measure that aims at identifying a wider range of relations in a more effective way. I admit I find the idea of a single vector space model being able to capture a number of semantic relationships and analogies rather radical and infeasible. As the authors mention in the paper, a number of studies already suggest for the opposite. The reason is quite simple: behind all these models lies (some form of) the distributional hypothesis (words in similar contexts have similar meanings), and this poses certain limitations in their expressive abilities; for example, words like “big” and “small” will always be considered as semantically similar from a vector perspective (although they express opposite meanings), since they occur in similar contexts. So I cannot see how the example given in Figure 1 is relevant to the very nature of vector spaces (or to any other semantic model for that matter!): there is a certain analogy between “man-king”, and “woman-queen”, but asking from a word space to capture “has-a” relationships of the form “owl-has-claws”, hence “hospital-has-walls”, doesn’t make much sense to me. The motivation behind the main proposal of the paper (a similarity measure that involves a form of cross-comparison between vectors of words and vectors representing the contexts of the words) is not clearly explained. Further, the measure is tested on the relation categories of the SemEval 2010 task with rather unsatisfactory results; in almost all cases, a simple baseline that takes into account only partial similarities between the tested word pairs present very high performance, with a difference from the best-performing model which seems to me statistically insignificant. So from both a methodological and an experimental perspective, the paper has weaknesses, and in its current form seems to describe work in progress; as such I am inclined against its presentation in ACL. (Formatting issue: The authors use the LaTeX styles for ACL 2016 — this should be fixed in case the paper is accepted). AUTHORS RESPONSE ================ Thank you for the clarifications. I am still not comfortable with the idea of a metric or a vector space that tries to capture both semantic and relational similarity, and I think you don't present enough experimental evidence that your method works. I have to agree with one of the other reviewers that a more appropriate format for this work would be a short paper. This paper presents a comparison of several vector combination techniques on the task of relation classification. - Strengths: The paper is clearly written and easy to understand. - Weaknesses: My main complaint about the paper is the significance of its contributions. I believe it might be suitable as a short paper, but certainly not a full-length paper. Unfortunately, there is little original thought and no significantly strong experimental results to back it up. The only contribution of this paper is an 'in-out' similarity metric, which is itself adapted from previous work. The results seem to be sensitive to the choice of clusters and only majorly outperforms a very naive baseline when the number of clusters is set to the exact value in the data beforehand. I think that relation classification or clustering from semantic vector space models is a very interesting and challenging problem. This work might be useful as an experimental nugget for future reference on vector combination and comparison techniques, as a short paper. Unfortunately, it does not have the substance to merit a full-length paper.

This paper presents an interesting approach to semi-supervised learning using context-conditional generative adversarial networks (CCGANs). The authors propose a method for training CCGANs to generate data samples with labels conditioned on context features. The authors also present a novel application of CCGANs to a real-world problem in the form of a sentiment classification task. The paper is well-written and easy to follow. The authors do a good job of introducing the concept of CCGANs and explaining how they can be used for semi-supervised learning. The authors also provide a thorough description of the proposed method and its implementation. The paper includes a detailed description of the experiments conducted, along with the results. The authors also discuss the implications of their work and provide some suggestions for future research. Overall, this is a well-written paper that presents an interesting approach to semi-supervised learning using CCGANs. The authors provide a thorough description of the proposed method and its implementation, along with a detailed description of the experiments conducted and their results. The paper also includes a discussion of the implications of the work and suggestions for future research. I recommend this paper be accepted for publication. This paper addresses the problem of semi-supervised learning with context-conditional generative adversarial networks (CC-GANs). The paper proposes a novel approach to semi-supervised learning by leveraging the context-conditional generative adversarial network (CC-GAN) architecture. Strengths: The paper presents a novel approach to semi-supervised learning that uses a context-conditional generative adversarial network (CC-GAN) architecture. The paper also provides a detailed description of the CC-GAN architecture and its components. Additionally, the paper provides a comprehensive evaluation of the CC-GAN approach on various datasets. Weaknesses: The paper does not provide a detailed discussion of the limitations of the CC-GAN approach. Additionally, the paper does not provide any comparison of the CC-GAN approach to other existing semi-supervised learning approaches. Finally, the paper does not provide any discussion of potential future directions for the CC-GAN approach. This paper presents a novel semi-supervised learning approach for images based on in-painting using an adversarial loss. The proposed approach uses a generator to fill in random patches removed from images, and a discriminator network to judge if the in-painted images are real or not. The authors evaluate the approach on the STL-10 and PASCAL datasets, and demonstrate that it obtains performance comparable or superior to existing methods. The paper is well-written and provides a clear description of the proposed approach. The authors provide a comprehensive evaluation of the approach on two datasets, and demonstrate that it is effective in semi-supervised learning. The paper also includes a thorough discussion of related work and a comparison to existing methods. The paper could be improved by providing more details about the implementation of the proposed approach. For example, the authors should provide more information about the architecture of the generator and discriminator networks, as well as the training procedure. Additionally, it would be helpful if the authors included more detailed results, such as performance on individual classes in the PASCAL dataset. This paper addresses the problem of semi-supervised learning for images using an in-painting technique with a context-conditional generative adversarial network (GAN). The paper proposes a method for training large VGG-style networks in a semi-supervised fashion by presenting images with random patches removed to a generator to fill in the hole, and then presenting the in-painted images to a discriminator network to judge if they are real or not. The paper has several strengths. First, it introduces a novel approach to semi-supervised learning for images. Second, the method proposed is simple and easily implemented. Third, the paper provides experimental results on the STL-10 and PASCAL datasets, showing performance comparable or superior to existing methods. The paper has some weaknesses. First, the paper does not provide a detailed explanation of how the proposed method works. Second, the paper does not compare the proposed method to other GAN-based approaches. Third, the paper does not provide a detailed analysis of the results, such as how the performance of the proposed method compares to other approaches.

Reviewers agree that the findings are not clear enough to be of interest, though the effort to do a controlled study is appreciated. Dear reviewers, We have analyzed GloVe model and updated our paper: -The overall tendency of GloVe with different contexts is similar to Skip-Gram. -GloVe is more sensitive to different contexts than Skip-Gram and CBOW, which is probably due to its explicitly defined/optimized objective function. Please see our revised paper for more details. The source code at Github is also updated to support GloVe. ( Dear reviewers, We have added three sequence labeling tasks (POS, Chunking, and NER) and a word analogy dataset. The models are currently evaluated on 4 tasks with 21 datasets. It is indeed hard to find any universal insight. However, after revisiting our experimental results and re-organizing the experiment section, we draw the following conclusions according to specific tasks and are excited to share with you: 1.Dependency-based context type does not get all the credit for capturing functional similarity. Bound context representation also plays an important role, especially for GBOW. 2.Syntactic word analogy benefits less from bound context representation. Bound context representation already contains syntactic information, which makes it difficult to capture this information based on the input word-context pairs. 3.Bound context representation is suitable for sequence labeling task, especially when it is used along with dependency-based context. 4.On text classification task, different contexts do not affect the final performance much. Nonetheless, the use of pre-trained word embeddings is crucial and linear context type with unbound representation is still the best choice. For more details, please see our revised experiment section. As for the GloVe model, we just need a few more days to re-run it for the best configuration. We promise that the results will be added before January 14th (Saturday). So sorry for the delay. We sincerely thank you for the detailed and constructive reviews. Please do kindly leave comments and suggests to help us further improve this work. Cheers, Authors of this paper. This paper analyzes dependency trees vs standard window contexts for word vector learning. While that's a good goal I believe the paper falls short of a thorough analysis of the subject matter. It does not analyze Glove like objective functions which often work better than the algorithms used here. It doesn't compare in absolute terms to other published vectors or models. It fails to gain any particularly interesting insights that will modify other people's work. It fails to push the state of the art or make available new resources for people. This paper evaluates how different context types affect the quality of word embeddings on a plethora of benchmarks. I am ambivalent about this paper. On one hand, it continues an important line of work in decoupling various parameters from the embedding algorithms (this time focusing on context); on the other hand, I am not sure I understand what the conclusion from these experiments is. There does not appear to be a significant and consistent advantage to any one context type. Why is this? Are the benchmarks sensitive enough to detect these differences, if they exist? While I am OK with this paper being accepted, I would rather see a more elaborate version of it, which tries to answer these more fundamental questions. This paper investigates the issue of whether and how to use syntactic dependencies in unsupervised word representation learning models like CBOW or Skip-Gram, with a focus one the issue of bound (word+dependency type, 'She-nsubj') vs. unbound (word alone, 'She') representations for context at training time. The empirical results are extremely mixed, and no specific novel method consistently outperforms existing methods. The paper is systematic and I have no major concerns about its soundness. However, I don't think that this paper is of broad interest to the ICLR community. The paper is focused on a fairly narrow detail of representation learning that is entirely specific to NLP, and its results are primarily negative. A short paper at an ACL conference would be a more reasonable target. There are many other types of contexts which should be discussed; see "Open IE as an Intermediate Structure for Semantic Tasks" (Stanovsky et al., ACL 2015). - Strengths: Relatively clear description of context and structure of proposed approach. Relatively complete description of the math. Comparison to an extensive set of alternative systems. - Weaknesses: Weak results/summary of "side-by-side human" comparison in Section 5. Some disfluency/agrammaticality. - General Discussion: The article proposes a principled means of modeling utterance context, consisting of a sequence of previous utterances. Some minor issues: 1. Past turns in Table 1 could be numbered, making the text associated with this table (lines 095-103) less difficult to ingest. Currently, readers need to count turns from the top when identifying references in the authors' description, and may wonder whether "second", "third", and "last" imply a side-specific or global enumeration. 2. Some reader confusion may be eliminated by explicitly defining what "segment" means in "segment level", as occurring on line 269. Previously, on line 129, this seemingly same thing was referred to as "a sequence-sequence [similarity matrix]". The two terms appear to be used interchangeably, but it is not clear what they actually mean, despite the text in section 3.3. It seems the authors may mean "word subsequence" and "word subsequence to word subsequence", where "sub-" implies "not the whole utterance", but not sure. 3. Currently, the variable symbol "n" appears to be used to enumerate words in an utterance (line 306), as well as utterances in a dialogue (line 389). The authors may choose two different letters for these two different purposes, to avoid confusing readers going through their equations. 4. The statement "This indicates that a retrieval based chatbot with SMN can provide a better experience than the state-of-the-art generation model in practice." at the end of section 5 appears to be unsupported. The two approaches referred to are deemed comparable in 555 out of 1000 cases, with the baseline better than the proposed method in 238 our of the remaining 445 cases. The authors are encouraged to assess and present the statistical significance of this comparison. If it is weak, their comparison permits to at best claim that their proposed method is no worse (rather than "better") than the VHRED baseline. 5. The authors may choose to insert into Figure 1 the explicit "first layer", "second layer" and "third layer" labels they use in the accompanying text. 6. Their is a pervasive use of "to meet" as in "a response candidate can meet each utterace" on line 280 which is difficult to understand. 7. Spelling: "gated recurrent unites"; "respectively" on line 133 should be removed; punctuation on line 186 and 188 is exchanged; "baseline model over" -> "baseline model by"; "one cannot neglects".

As you noted for Figure 5 Left, sometimes it seems sufficient to tune learning rates. I see your argument for Figure 6 Right, but 1) not for all good learning rates make Adam fail, I guess you selected the one where it did (note that Adam was several times faster than Eve in the beginning) 2) I don't buy "Eve always converges" because you show it only for 0.1 and since Eve is not Adam, 0.1 of Adam is not 0.1 of Eve because of d_t. To my understanding, you define d_t over time with 3 hyperparameters. Similarly, one can define d_t directly. The behaviour of d_t that you show is not extraordinary and can be parameterized. If Eve is better than Adam, then looking at d_t we can directly see whether we underestimated or overestimated learning rates. You could argue that Eve does it automatically but you do tune learning rates for each problem individually anyway. The authors propose a simple strategy that uses function values to improve the performance of Adam. There is no theoretical analysis of this variant, but there is an extensive empirical evaluation. A disadvantage of the proposed approach is that it has 3 parameters to tune, but the same parameters are used across experiments. Overall however, the PCs believe that this paper doesn't quite reach the level expected for ICLR and thus cannot be accepted. The paper introduced an extension of Adam optimizer that automatically adjust learning rate by comparing the subsequent values of the cost function during training. The authors empirically demonstrated the benefit of the Eve optimizer on CIFAR convnets, logistic regression and RNN problems. I have the following concerns about the paper - The proposed method is VARIANT to arbitrary shifts and scaling to the cost function. - A more fair comparison with other baseline methods would be using additional exponential decay learning scheduling between the lower and upper threshold of d_t. I suspect 1/d_t just shrinks as an exponential decay from Figure 2. - Three additional hyper-parameters: k, K, \beta_3. Overall, I think the method has its fundamental flew and the paper offers very limited novelty. There is no theoretical justification on the modification, and it would be good for the authors to discuss the potential failure mode of the proposed method. Furthermore, it is hard for me to follow Section 3.2. The writing quality and clarity of the method section can be further improved. Originality is somehow limited but the method appears to have a positive effect on neural network training. The paper is well written and illustrations are appropriate.
