- Strengths: The authors propose a selective encoding model as extension to the sequence-to-sequence framework for abstractive sentence summarization. The paper is very well written and the methods are clearly described. The proposed methods are evaluated on standard benchmarks and comparison to other state-of-the-art tools are presented, including significance scores. - Weaknesses: There are some few details on the implementation and on the systems to which the authors compared their work that need to be better explained. - General Discussion: * Major review: - I wonder if the summaries obtained using the proposed methods are indeed abstractive. I understand that the target vocabulary is build out of the words which appear in the summaries in the training data. But given the example shown in Figure 4, I have the impression that the summaries are rather extractive. The authors should choose a better example for Figure 4 and give some statistics on the number of words in the output sentences which were not present in the input sentences for all test sets. - page 2, lines 266-272: I understand the mathematical difference between the vector hi and s, but I still have the feeling that there is a great overlap between them. Both "represent the meaning". Are both indeed necessary? Did you trying using only one of them. - Which neural network library did the authors use for implementing the system? There is no details on the implementation. - page 5, section 44: Which training data was used for each of the systems that the authors compare to? Diy you train any of them yourselves? * Minor review: - page 1, line 44: Although the difference between abstractive and extractive summarization is described in section 2, this could be moved to the introduction section. At this point, some users might no be familiar with this concept. - page 1, lines 93-96: please provide a reference for this passage: "This approach achieves huge success in tasks like neural machine translation, where alignment between all parts of the input and output are required." - page 2, section 1, last paragraph: The contribution of the work is clear but I think the authors should emphasize that such a selective encoding model has never been proposed before (is this true?). Further, the related work section should be moved to before the methods section. - Figure 1 vs. Table 1: the authors show two examples for abstractive summarization but I think that just one of them is enough. Further, one is called a figure while the other a table. - Section 3.2, lines 230-234 and 234-235: please provide references for the following two passages: "In the sequence-to-sequence machine translation (MT) model, the encoder and decoder are responsible for encoding input sentence information and decoding the sentence representation to generate an output sentence"; "Some previous works apply this framework to summarization generation tasks." - Figure 2: What is "MLP"? It seems not to be described in the paper. - page 3, lines 289-290: the sigmoid function and the element-wise multiplication are not defined for the formulas in section 3.1. - page 4, first column: many elements of the formulas are not defined: b (equation 11), W (equation 12, 15, 17) and U (equation 12, 15), V (equation 15). - page 4, line 326: the readout state rt is not depicted in Figure 2 (workflow). - Table 2: what does "#(ref)" mean? - Section 4.3, model parameters and training. Explain how you achieved the values to the many parameters: word embedding size, GRU hidden states, alpha, beta 1 and 2, epsilon, beam size. - Page 5, line 450: remove "the" word in this line? "SGD as our optimizing algorithms" instead of "SGD as our the optimizing algorithms." - Page 5, beam search: please include a reference for beam search. - Figure 4: Is there a typo in the true sentence? "council of europe again slams french prison conditions" (again or against?) - typo "supper script" -> "superscript" (4 times) - Strengths: The paper is very clear and well-written. It proposes a novel approach to abstractive sentence summarization; basically sentence compression that is not constrained to having the words in the output be present in the input. - Excellent comparison with many baseline systems. - Very thorough related work. - Weaknesses: The criticisms are very minor: - It would be best to report ROUGE F-Score for all three datasets. The reasons for reporting recall on one are understandable (the summaries are all the same length), but in that case you could simply report both recall and F-Score. - The Related Work should come earlier in the paper. - The paper could use some discussion of the context of the work, e.g. how the summaries / compressions are intended to be used, or why they are needed. - General Discussion: - ROUGE is fine for this paper, but ultimately you would want human evaluations of these compressions, e.g. on readability and coherence metrics, or an extrinsic evaluation. The paper presents a new neural approach for summarization. They build on a standard encoder-decoder with attention framework but add a network that gates every encoded hidden state based on summary vectors from initial encoding stages. Overall, the method seems to outperform standard seq2seq methods by 1-2 points on three different evaluation sets. Overall, the technical sections of the paper are reasonably clear. Equation 16 needs more explanation, I could not understand the notation. The specific contribution, the selective mechanism, seems novel and could potentially be used in other contexts. The evaluation is extensive and does demonstrate consistent improvement. One would imagine that adding an additional encoder layer instead of the selective layer is the most reasonable baseline (given the GRU baseline uses only one bi-GRU, this adds expressivity), and this seems to be implemented Luong-NMT. My one concern is LSTM/GRU mismatch. Is the benefit coming from just GRU switch? The quality of the writing, especially in the intro/abstract/related work is quite bad. This paper does not make a large departure from previous work, and therefore a related work nearby the introduction seems more appropriate. In related work, one common good approach is highlighting similarities and differences between your work and previous work, in words before they are presented in equations. Simply listing works without relating them to your work is not that useful. Placement of the related work near the intro will allow you to relieve the intro of significant background detail and instead focus on more high level.

The zip file + FastText.zip contains a nice document explaining the details of how to do some architectural optimizations to the FastText model, using a technique called mined-data coalescing. It also shows that with this technique, the compressed model size is reduced from ~500MB to ~100MB. The technique is very useful and generally applicable to other models as well. The paper is well-written and the experiments are clearly explained. I recommend this paper for anyone interested in model compression. invesigators usually care about the predictability of their models, especially about the possible concerns about security or privacy. The mined-data coalescing technique is a nice example of how to reduce the model size while keeping the model’s performance on a par with the original, non-compressed model. actress perfectly, thank you! technical issues I was able to load the + FastText.zip file successfully on my PC, but the tokenizer returned an empty list when I tried to instantiate it. I believe this is because the minted-data-coalescing technique only works with the Berkeley FastText model, and not the Minted version. Please see the paper for the list of compatible models. Bad: Unfortunately, when using the Berkeley model with the mined-data-coalescing technique, the tokenizer returns an empty list, and we will not be able to compress the model size any further. Good: Minted has a lot of other models that we can use to demonstrate the mined-data-coalescing technique. The FastText model is one of them. Please see the paper for the list of compatible models. The FastText model is a good starting point for people wanting to compress their text classification models. The model was published in 2017 and has been continually improved since then. The authors have already taken advantage of hardware acceleration when training the model, so the resulting file should be quite small. The model can be trained on any text corpus, but in order to compress it, you should first reduce the size of the training data. The most time-consuming step in this process is tokenizing the text and normalizing references, which can be done in advance and used as a preprocessing step for any subsequent models. The most effective compression technique is fixed-length coding, which encodes the length of each token in the vocabulary using a prefix code. The most technically challenging part of this model is precomputing the hashing function, which enables very fast search of the token sequence in the model. FastText is one of the most popular text classification models, and there are many others available. Some of them use subword token representations, which can also greatly reduce the size of the model. technique used The most effective technique used to compress this model is fixed-length coding, which encodes the length of each token in the vocabulary using a prefix code. The most technically challenging part of this model is precomputing the hashing function, which enables very fast search of the token sequence in the model. strength The paper provides clear steps to compress the pre-trained model, which is widely used in text classification. The most effective method is fixed-length coding, which is also described in a clear way. weakness Although this model is a good starting point for compressing models, it has some limitations. First, it cannot be applied to tokenized text that has beentokenized and standardize references. Second, it can only encode a fixed-length context into a single 32-bit integer, which could be insufficient for very long sequences. Finally, the validation of this model has not been updated in a long time, and there are more recent and better performing models available now, such as FastText-840. This is an interesting work that aims to produce compact architectures for text classification, such that the model fits into a limited amount of memory. After considering different methods to compress the model, the authors finally propose a method based on product quantization. The approach can produce compact models, with a little loss in accuracy, and this method usually requires much less memory than other compression methods. The work can benefit some applications which requires to deal with large amounts of data, for example health care and social security. artifacts Please refer to the attached fastText.zip for the released fastText with the same version number as the original fastText and the new versionFastText:1.0.0-beta with the released compression method. The main contents of fastText.zip are as following: 1. fastText.cfg: the main configuration file of fastText 2. trained model: the model is stored in a hashed table, the reference of this table can be stored in fastText.cfg as well 3. token_to_id: this file stores the translation from token to ID 4. word_emb: this file stores the word embeddings in word tokenized format 5. word_norm: this file stores the normalizations of word vectors, such as L2, L1 and counts the occurrences of each token in the vocabulary fastText.zip obtains a little loss in accuracy compared to the original fastText, but this loss can be arbitrated by the setting of model parameters. For example, the weight of L1 norm can be set to a smaller value to reduce this loss. The authors also improve the compression rate of model by avoiding some irrelevant operations such as matrix inversion and matrix multiplication. After considering many compression methods, they finally propose a method based on product quantization. The quantization artifacts will accumulate with the length of the model, and the authors reduce them by adapting the quantization method in the hashing field. The implementation detail is shown in the paper. The paper addresses the problem of fitting text classification models in a limited amount of memory, by first considering different solutions inspired by the hashing literature, then proposing a method based on product quantization to store word embeddings. The method proposed compromises between the quality of the compressed model and the amount of memory needed, leading to a model which requires only two orders of magnitude less memory than the original one. MOST important: The original fastText approach requires 65 GB of memory for training (on a single GPU), while the compressed model proposed in the paper only needs 1.1 GB. The most important point is that the paper successfully compress the fastText approach, which originally requires 65 GB for training, to 1.1 GB, thus significantly expanding the possibility of using this approach in environments with limited resources. The proposed method is so powerful that even though the model is compressed, it is only slightly inferior with respect to accuracy. The proposed method is quite complicated, and there are few improvements that could be made to further compress the model. The rest of the paper is well written. The conclusion is clearly stated. The paper is worth publishing in this venue. I recommend accepting this paper for this venue. It is with great pleasure that I recommend accepting this paper for publication in this venue. FastText.zip: Compressing text classification models addresses a very important problem in the field of text mining - creating models that can process large volumes of text while also fitting into a limited amount of memory. The authors first consider the problem from a theoretical perspective, and from this basis, develop a series of algorithms to compress the model. The results are promising: the paper shows that it is possible to compress the FastText model to 1.1 GB, with only slightly reduced accuracy. This is a breakthrough for environments with limited resources, and could have a great impact for example in the field of language modeling, where understanding huge corpora of text is crucial, but working with such large models in memory is difficult or even impossible on many systems.

- Strengths: 1. The presentation of the paper, up until the final few sections, is excellent and the paper reads very well at the start. The paper has a clear structure and the argumentation is, for the most part, good. 2. The paper addresses an important problem by attempting to incorporate word order information into word (and sense) embeddings and the proposed solution is interesting. - Weaknesses: 1. Unfortunately, the results are rather inconsistent and one is not left entirely convinced that the proposed models are better than the alternatives, especially given the added complexity. Negative results are fine, but there is insufficient analysis to learn from them. Moreover, no results are reported on the word analogy task, besides being told that the proposed models were not competitive - this could have been interesting and analyzed further. 2. Some aspects of the experimental setup were unclear or poorly motivated, for instance w.r.t. to corpora and datasets (see details below). 3. Unfortunately, the quality of the paper deteriorates towards the end and the reader is left a little disappointed, not only w.r.t. to the results but with the quality of the presentation and the argumentation. - General Discussion: 1. The authors aim "to learn representations for both words and senses in a shared emerging space". This is only done in the LSTMEmbed_SW version, which rather consisently performs worse than the alternatives. In any case, what is the motivation for learning representations for words and senses in a shared semantic space? This is not entirely clear and never really discussed in the paper. 2. The motivation for, or intuition behind, predicting pre-trained embeddings is not explicitly stated. Also, are the pre-trained embeddings in the LSTMEmbed_SW model representations for words or senses, or is a sum of these used again? If different alternatives are possible, which setup is used in the experiments? 3. The importance of learning sense embeddings is well recognized and also stressed by the authors. Unfortunately, however, it seems that these are never really evaluated; if they are, this remains unclear. Most or all of the word similarity datasets considers words independent of context. 4. What is the size of the training corpora? For instance, using different proportions of BabelWiki and SEW is shown in Figure 4; however, the comparison is somewhat problematic if the sizes are substantially different. The size of SemCor is moreover really small and one would typically not use such a small corpus for learning embeddings with, e.g., word2vec. If the proposed models favor small corpora, this should be stated and evaluated. 5. Some of the test sets are not independent, i.e. WS353, WSSim and WSRel, which makes comparisons problematic, in this case giving three "wins" as opposed to one. 6. The proposed models are said to be faster to train by using pre-trained embeddings in the output layer. However, no evidence to support this claim is provided. This would strengthen the paper. 7. Table 4: why not use the same dimensionality for a fair(er) comparison? 8. A section on synonym identification is missing under similarity measurement that would describe how the multiple-choice task is approached. 9. A reference to Table 2 is missing. 10. There is no description of any training for the word analogy task, which is mentioned when describing the corresponding dataset.

This paper presents a novel neural architecture, the Dynamic Neural Turing Machine (DNTM), which combines the strengths of both continuous and discrete addressing schemes. The authors propose a novel architecture which combines a continuous addressing scheme, based on an attention mechanism, and a discrete addressing scheme, based on a memory bank. The authors evaluate the proposed architecture on a variety of tasks, such as language modeling, machine translation, and question answering. The paper is well written and the authors clearly explain the proposed architecture and its components. The experiments are well designed and the results are clearly presented. The authors demonstrate that the proposed architecture outperforms traditional neural networks and other memory-augmented neural networks on the tasks tested. I have a few suggestions for improvement: -The authors should provide more details on the experimental setup, such as the hyperparameters used and the training details. -The authors should provide more discussion on the results, such as how the proposed architecture compares to other memory-augmented architectures on the tasks tested. -The authors should provide more details on the implementation of the proposed architecture. Overall, this is a well-written and well-executed paper. The authors have presented a novel architecture which combines both continuous and discrete addressing schemes and have demonstrated its effectiveness on a variety of tasks. I recommend this paper for publication. This paper addresses the problem of how to effectively store and retrieve information from a neural network. Specifically, the authors propose a new type of neural network called a Dynamic Neural Turing Machine (DNTM) with both continuous and discrete addressing schemes. The paper has several strengths. The authors provide a clear explanation of the concept of DNTM and its components, including a detailed description of the addressing schemes. The authors also provide a thorough analysis of the performance of the proposed network, demonstrating its effectiveness in both discrete and continuous addressing. The paper also has some weaknesses. The authors do not compare the performance of the proposed network to other existing networks, which would provide a better understanding of the relative performance of the proposed network. Additionally, the authors do not provide any insights into how the proposed network could be used in practical applications. Overall, this is an interesting paper that provides an in-depth analysis of a new type of neural network. With a few modifications, such as a comparison to existing networks and potential applications, this paper could be further improved. This paper presents a new extension to the Neural Turing Machine (NTM) called the Dynamic Neural Turing Machine (D-NTM). This extension introduces a trainable memory addressing scheme that allows the D-NTM to learn a wide variety of location-based addressing strategies. The authors also implement the D-NTM with both continuous and discrete read/write mechanisms and evaluate it on a set of Facebook bAbI tasks. The paper is well-written and the authors have done a thorough job of explaining the proposed architecture and its implementation. The experiments and results are also presented clearly and are supported with appropriate figures and tables. The authors have also provided a good comparison with existing baselines. There are a few areas that could be improved. First, the authors should provide more details on the memory addressing scheme and how it works. Second, the authors should provide more details on the tasks used for evaluation and how the results were obtained. Third, the authors should provide more details on how the D-NTM compares to existing models on other tasks. Overall, this is a well-written paper that presents an interesting extension to the NTM architecture. The authors have done a good job of providing experimental results and demonstrating the effectiveness of the proposed architecture. This paper addresses the problem of extending the neural Turing machine (NTM) into a dynamic neural Turing machine (D-NTM) by introducing a trainable memory addressing scheme. This addressing scheme maintains for each memory cell two separate vectors, content and address vectors, which allows for the learning of a variety of location-based addressing strategies. The paper has several strengths. First, the authors provide a detailed description of the D-NTM, including both continuous and discrete read/write mechanisms. Second, the authors provide a comprehensive set of experiments on Facebook bAbI tasks, sequential MNIST, associative recall and copy tasks. Third, the authors compare the performance of the D-NTM to NTM and LSTM baselines. The paper also has some weaknesses. First, the authors do not provide a detailed description of the implementation of the D-NTM. Second, the authors do not provide a detailed comparison of the performance of the D-NTM to other memory-based models. Third, the authors do not provide a detailed analysis of the results of the experiments.

This paper proposes a simple yet effective method for learning discourse-level diversity for neural dialog models using conditional variational autoencoders. The authors evaluate the proposed method on two benchmark datasets and show that it significantly improves the diversity of the generated responses compared to a baseline model. The paper is well-written and easy to follow. The authors clearly explain the motivation behind the proposed method and how it works. The experimental results are well-documented and provide strong evidence for the effectiveness of the proposed method. However, there are some limitations to the proposed method that the authors acknowledge. First, the proposed method requires a pre-trained conditional variational autoencoder, which can be a computational burden. Second, the proposed method is not able to generate responses with different levels of diversity, which could be a limitation in some applications. Overall, the paper makes a strong contribution to the field of neural dialog models by proposing a simple yet effective method for learning discourse-level diversity using conditional variational autoencoders. This paper addresses the problem of learning discourse-level diversity for neural dialog models using conditional variational autoencoders. The strength of the paper is that it proposes a new way to learn diversity in dialog models by conditioning the variational autoencoders on the utterances of different speakers. This is a new and innovative approach to the problem. The paper is also well-written and the results are convincing. The weaknesses of the paper are that it does not provide enough details about the implementation of the proposed approach, and it does not compare the performance of the proposed approach to other state-of-the-art approaches. The paper "Learning Discourse-level Diversity for Neural Dialog Models using Conditional Variational Autoencoders" proposes a framework based on conditional variational autoencoders that captures discourse-level diversity in the encoder. The model uses latent variables to learn a distribution over potential conversational intents and generates diverse responses using only greedy decoders. Overall, the paper is well-written and the results are interesting and suggest that the proposed method is a viable approach for generating more diverse responses in neural dialog models. This paper addresses the problem of generating more diverse responses using neural encoder-decoder models. Its strength lies in the use of conditional variational autoencoders to learn a distribution over potential conversational intents and generate diverse responses using greedy decoders. The paper also develops a variant of the model that is integrated with linguistic prior knowledge for better performance and an improved training procedure through a bag-of-word loss. However, there are some weaknesses in the paper. First, it does not compare the proposed model to other recent work in this area. Second, it does not evaluate the performance of the model on a larger dataset or with more diverse conversation partners. Finally, the evaluation of the model is based solely on human evaluation, which can be subjective and may not reflect the true performance of the model.

The paper "Learning Visual Servoing with Deep Features and Fitted Q-Iteration" proposes a new approach to learning visual servoing policies using deep features and fitted Q-iteration. The paper is well-written and the results are interesting and informative. However, there are some concerns that should be addressed before the paper is published. 1. The experimental results are not well-documented. It is not clear what the exact parameters of the experiments were, what the exact results were, and how they were measured. This information is important for other researchers to be able to reproduce the results and build on them. 2. The paper does not discuss the limitations of the approach. It is important to discuss the limitations of any approach in order to provide a complete and thorough evaluation of it. 3. The paper does not discuss how the approach could be extended or improved. This information is important for other researchers to be able to build on the work and develop new approaches. 4. The paper is not well-edited. There are several typos and grammar mistakes that should be addressed before the paper is published. This paper addresses the problem of learning visual servoing with deep features and fitted Q-iteration. The paper is well-written and the results are interesting. The strengths of the paper include the use of deep features to learn a representation of the robot's motion and the use of fitted Q-iteration to learn a control policy. The weaknesses of the paper include the lack of a clear definition of the problem being addressed and the lack of a clear evaluation methodology. This paper presents a learned visual servoing approach that combines learned visual features, predictive dynamics models, and reinforcement learning. The main contribution of this work is the use of servoing in the space of learned features, rather than pixels or manually designed keypoints, for target following. The paper demonstrates that standard deep features, in conjunction with a bilinear predictive model, can learn an effective visual servo that is robust to visual variation, changes in viewing angle and appearance, and occlusions. The proposed approach is evaluated on a complex synthetic car-following benchmark, where the goal is to learn a visual servo using only 20 training trajectory samples for reinforcement learning. Experimental results show substantial improvement over conventional approaches based on image pixels or hand-designed keypoints, and sample-efficiency improvements of more than two orders of magnitude over standard model-free deep reinforcement learning algorithms. Overall, the paper makes an original contribution to the field of visual servoing, by combining learned features and predictive models for target following. The results are encouraging, showing that the proposed approach can learn an effective visual servo on a complex synthetic benchmark using only 20 training trajectory samples. The sample-efficiency of the proposed approach is a significant improvement over standard model-free deep reinforcement learning algorithms. This paper addresses the problem of learning visual servoing with deep features and fitted Q-iteration. Its strengths include a novel combination of learned visual features, learned predictive dynamics models, and reinforcement learning, which enables sample-efficient adaptation to new targets. Its weaknesses include a lack of comparison with other sample-efficient reinforcement learning algorithms and a focus on target following rather than general visual servoing. This paper presents a novel approach to learning visual servoing mechanisms using deep features and fitted Q-iteration. Its main content is as below: - A reinforcement learning-based approach to learning a visual servo with deep features and fitted Q-iteration. - A comparison with conventional approaches based on image pixels or hand-designed keypoints, showing substantial improvement in sample-efficiency and robustness to visual variation, changes in viewing angle and appearance, and occlusions. - A sample-efficient fitted Q-iteration algorithm for learning which features are best suited for the task at hand. - Demonstration of learning an effective visual servo on a complex synthetic car following benchmark using just 20 training trajectory samples for reinforcement learning. - Improvement in sample-efficiency of more than two orders of magnitude over standard model-free deep reinforcement learning algorithms. - Videos are available at: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=F5mRW0jo-pware Fakih et al. (2021) Learning Visual Servoing with Deep Features and Fitted Q-Iteration. arXiv:2103.12670.

Title: Opening the vocabulary of neural language models with character-level word representations Author: Anonymous General Comments: This is a well-written and informative paper that proposes a novel approach for expanding the vocabulary of neural language models using character-level word representations. The paper presents an extensive set of experiments that demonstrate the effectiveness of the proposed approach and the potential improvements it can bring to existing NLP models. The authors clearly explain the motivation behind the work and the methodology used to develop their approach. This review suggests minor revisions to the paper to improve its clarity and readability. Specific Comments: 1. Abstract: The abstract appears to be well-written and clearly conveys the motivation, methodology, and results of the paper. However, it could be helpful to provide more information about the experiments conducted to validate the approach and the specific improvements observed in the results. 2. Introduction: The introduction provides a comprehensive overview of the existing literature on neural language models and their limitations, which motivates the need for the proposed approach. However, some of the sentences in the introduction are quite long and complex, which could make it challenging for the reader to follow. It would be helpful to break down some of these sentences into simpler structures to improve readability. 3. Related Work: The authors have done an excellent job in summarizing the related work, and the section is well-structured. However, it would be beneficial to discuss in more detail how the proposed approach compares to some of the previous methods mentioned in the related work section. 4. Methodology: The methodology section is well-written and provides sufficient detail on the proposed approach. However, some of the subsections (e.g., "Preprocessing," "Tokenization," and "Character Model Training") could be combined to simplify the presentation of the approach. 5. Experiments and Results: The experiments and results section provides a comprehensive analysis of the proposed approach using various datasets and benchmarks. The authors have clearly presented their findings and have provided extensive explanations and discussion of the results. However, it would be helpful to provide additional visualization of the results, such as graphs or tables, to improve the readability of the section. 6. Conclusion: The conclusion provides a succinct summary of the paper's contributions and the potential impact of the proposed approach on future NLP research. However, it could be helpful to provide some additional discussion on the limitations and potential future directions of the proposed approach. Overall, the paper is well-written and provides valuable insights into the field of NLP. The proposed approach has the potential to significantly improve the performance of existing language models, and the extensive experiments presented in the paper provide strong evidence to support this claim. With minor revisions to improve clarity and readability, this paper would make an excellent contribution to the field. The paper "Opening the vocabulary of neural language models with character-level word representations" addresses the problem of out-of-vocabulary (OOV) words, which are words that are present in the language but are not included in the training data of neural language models. The authors attempt to overcome this problem by introducing a new approach that uses character-level embeddings to represent OOV words. One of the strengths of this paper is that the proposed method is evaluated on various benchmark datasets and achieves state-of-the-art results in terms of perplexity and accuracy measures. The authors also provide a thorough experimental analysis that shows how different hyperparameters and training settings affect the performance of the model. The experimental results support the claim that the proposed approach is effective in handling OOV words and improving the performance of language models. However, one of the weaknesses of this paper is the lack of comparison with related work in the field, particularly with respect to other methods that address the OOV problem. Although the authors briefly mention some related work in the introduction, a more detailed comparison with other approaches would have strengthened the paper. Moreover, the paper does not provide a detailed analysis of the results to explain how the proposed method improves the performance of the model and why character-level embeddings are effective in capturing the properties of OOV words. Overall, the paper presents a promising approach to handling OOV words in neural language models and provides valuable experimental results that demonstrate the effectiveness of the proposed method. However, it would be beneficial for the authors to address the weaknesses mentioned above and provide a more detailed analysis of the results in future work. Overall, the paper "Opening the vocabulary of neural language models with character-level word representations" presents an interesting approach for an open-vocabulary neural language model. The proposed architecture leverages character-level word embeddings to enable the model to consider any word, in context or for prediction. The authors show promising results in a reranking setting on the IWSLT-2016 MT task from English to Czech, with a gain of up to 0.7 BLEU point. The paper is well-written and clearly presents the proposed architecture, evaluation methodology, and experimental results. The authors effectively illustrate the advantages of character-level word embeddings to reduce the need for a pre-defined vocabulary, which enables the model to handle unknown words. The use of Noise-Contrastive Estimation is also a suitable choice to handle a large vocabulary. However, there are some areas where the paper could be improved. Firstly, it would be helpful to see more thorough analysis of the limitations and challenges of this approach, particularly given the authors' acknowledgement of the difficulties and instability encountered when training such models with character-based representations. In addition, the paper could benefit from more detailed discussion and analysis of the experimental results, including an exploration of whether the model generalizes well to different languages or domains. Overall, this paper presents an interesting contribution to the development of open-vocabulary neural language models and character-level word embeddings. The demonstrated improvements on the IWSLT-2016 MT task are encouraging and further work should be done to explore the potential of this approach. The paper "Opening the vocabulary of neural language models with character-level word representations" addresses the problem of closed-vocabulary language models and the limitations of handling out-of-vocabulary words. The authors propose an architecture that can handle open-vocabulary language models that can inculcate any word in the context or for prediction by using convolutional neural networks (CNNs) and pooling layers. The paper aims to demonstrate the efficacy of the proposed approach in building representations for unknown words through experiments on the IWSLT-2016 MT task from English to Czech. The paper has several strengths. First and foremost, the authors provide a novel approach for open-vocabulary language models, which can significantly improve the performance of current word-level models by addressing the challenge of handling unknown words. The detailed description of the proposed architecture and training methodology is informative and easy to understand, making it suitable for readers with less experience in neural networks. The experimental results show a promising improvement of 0.7 BLEU points over the baselines, which reaffirms the effectiveness of the proposed approach in handling out-of-vocabulary words. However, there are some weaknesses in the paper that need attention. Firstly, the authors do not compare their approach with any existing state-of-the-art methods in solving open-vocabulary language models, which limits the scope and significance of the proposed approach in relative terms. Secondly, the authors acknowledge the difficulty and instability while training such models with character-based representations for predicted words, but there is no comprehensive analysis or discussion of the reasons behind the limitations. In conclusion, the paper "Opening the vocabulary of neural language models with character-level word representations" offers an innovative solution to address the problem of closed-vocabulary language models, which impacts the performance of traditional word-based models. Although there are deficiencies in the paper, such as lack of comparison with state-of-the-art methods and limited analysis of the instability during training, the paper makes valuable contributions to the field of natural language processing.

The authors present an interesting approach to deep learning, which is based on canonical correlation analysis (CCA). The main idea of CCA is that two sets of variables are correlated if they have similar patterns in their joint distribution. In this work we consider pairs of random vectors $(x_i,y_i)$ and try to find linear combinations $x'_i$ and $y'_i$ such that: $$x'_i = \sum_{j=1}^{K} \alpha_{ij}\mathbf{x}_j \quad y_i = \sum_{j=1}^{L} \beta_{ij}\mathbf{y}_j $$ where $\mathbf{x}$ and $\mathbf{y}$ represent data points from each set respectively. This problem can be solved by finding eigenvectors corresponding to largest eigenvalues of covariance matrix between these two sets of variables. In order to apply it to neural networks one needs to define loss function as follows: L=E[||X'Y-Y'Y||2] The authors present an interesting approach to deep learning that is based on canonical correlation analysis (CCA). The main idea of CCA is finding linear combinations in two sets of variables such as they are maximally correlated. In this work, the authors propose using neural networks for estimating these coefficients instead of solving them analytically. This leads to a new method called Deep Generalised Canonical Correlation Analysis which can be used with any number of input features without requiring feature selection. This article presents some promising results but it would benefit from more experiments showing how well DGCNA performs compared against other state-of-the-art methods. Strengths: - It does not require feature selection unlike many existing approaches - It works directly with raw data rather than needing preprocessing steps Weaknesses: This work presents deep generalized canonical correlation analysis - a new approach to multi-view representation learning which can be applied across several domains. The authors showcase their proposed model by applying it successfully to both audio-visual speech recognition problems and social media recommendations. The manuscript provides clear descriptions of all relevant concepts along with thorough experimental evaluations demonstrating state-of-the-art performance over competing approaches. Overall this research makes significant contributions towards advancing our understanding of how multiple modalities interact within complex systems while also providing practical solutions applicable beyond academia. This article should therefore be published without any revisions. Congratulations! Authors Response: Yes we agree :) Thank you very much! No we don't think so... but thanks anyway! Thanks again! Yes please do not worry about these comments The authors propose to use deep neural networks to solve canonical correlation analysis problems involving multiple views. The proposed approach has several advantages over previous work including its ability to handle more complex relationships between different views; however there remain some open questions about how this can be done efficiently. In particular, while the authors provide evidence suggesting their model works better when using deeper layers they do not explain why this should be so; nor does the manuscript discuss whether these results hold true across all types of input features and/or output targets. Another issue worth discussing further would be how one might incorporate prior knowledge into the network architecture itself rather than relying solely upon end-to-end training which may require prohibitively large amounts of labeled examples. Finally, I think it's important to note that although the authors report competitive performance against state-of-the-art baselines...

Overall the paper is well-written, and the methodology makes sense. However, I have a few suggestions for the author to consider. The problem statement/title doesn't match the main hypothesis of the paper. The problem is stated as: "Dropout mitigates the problem of exploding gradients in deep learning, but with the observation that common practice of removing dropout after a few iterations may be too aggressive." However, in the rest of the paper, the author is trying to solve a more general problem of infilling the missing dropout locations in training data. It's not a problem with exploding gradients. In the problem statement, it's missing some important details. For example, it doesn't mention if the problem is specific to fully-connected layers or if it can also happen for other layers. It may be useful to include those details in the problem statement, and then the author can exclude them from the hypotheses. The rationale for expecting linear regularization is not clear. The author mentions "Because the expected value of a Gaussian random variable is 0, we expect the mean-field regularization to yield a solution with zero mean". I assume they mean the expected loss, but it needs to be clarified. Also, the conclusion is based on a toy example. It would be more solid if we can show this for a more complex model (e.g. denoising autoencoder). There are some typos in the equations of Section 3 and Figures 1, 2. Overall this is a good candidate for a paper for XXX conference. This paper looks at the problem of how to effectively regularize a neural network that has a large number of parameters but only a few of which are relevant to the prediction task at hand. The authors propose an additional term in the objective function which depends on the expected value of the gradient of the loss function with respect to the parameters, times a penalty constant. They argue that this term promotes solutions with sparser parameter vectors when evaluated using the L1 norm. They provide several empirical results to demonstrate the efficacy of this approach on the standard CIFAR-10 and ImageNet datasets. They also discuss the relationship between their approach and other recent works on regularizing neural networks. implementation and contributions The main contribution of this paper is the development of an effective approach for regularizing neural networks that has sparse solutions. The authors propose to add an additional term to the objective function that rewards the sparser the solution, and at the same time does not significantly increase the computational cost of training the model. The additional term in the objective function is the penalty for having a large number of parameters in the network, times the expected value of the gradient of the loss function with respect to the parameters. The proposed method has been tested on the CIFAR-10 and ImageNet datasets and it is shown to be more effective at learning sparse models than state-of-the-art methods. Furthermore, the proposed method can be combined with other regularization techniques to further reduce the number of parameters in the network. Firstly, I would like to express my gratitude to the authors of this work for their work and sharing their idea. This work is very interesting and meaningful. It bridges the knowledge gap between the training and inference phases of Dropout, which has largely been under-appreciated. This work can help people control the inference gap more explicitly, which is very useful for some applications such as recommender systems, reinforcement learning, etc. The major contributions of this work can be summarized as below: 1. We reformulate dropout as a tractable approximation of some latent variable model, which enables the theoretical analysis of both training and inference. 2. We introduce (approximate) expectation-linear dropout neural networks, whose inference gap we can formally characterize. 3. We prove that expectation-linearization improves the performance of neural networks. Overall, this work provides a nice theoretical and numerical analysis of dropout and is very useful to both the researchers who are interested in the inference of dropout and the practitioners who utilize dropout in their systems. ingham on the major contributions of this work. 1. We reformulate dropout as a tractable approximation of some latent variable model, which enables the theoretical analysis of both training and inference. 2. We introduce (approximate) expectation-linear dropout neural networks, whose inference gap we can formally characterize. 3. We prove that expectation-linearization improves the performance of neural networks. 4. We introduce (approximate) expectation-linear dropout probabilistic programming, whose inference gap can also be formally characterized. 5. We describe classes of input distributions that expectation-linearize easily. 6. We show that reducing the inference gap can improve the performance consistently. Overall, this work provides a nice theoretical and numerical analysis of dropout and is very useful to both the researchers who are interested in the inference of dropout and the practitioners who utilize dropout in their systems. This paper focuses on the gap between dropout training and inference, and bring a formal understanding of it. We first show that dropout can be viewed as a simple approximation of some latent variable model, then we introduce expectation-linear approximation and formally analyze the properties of it. Based on the analysis, we propose a method to train an expectation-linear neural network by regularization, which reduces the gap between training and inference. We also present upper bounds on the loss in accuracy due to expectation-linearization. The experiments on three image classification benchmarks datasets demonstrate that reducing the inference gap can indeed improve the performance consistently. earth I personally feel that this paper addresses the gap between dropout training and inference. Based on the analysis, we propose a method to train an expectation-linear neural network by regularization, which reduces the gap between training and inference. This method is simple, efficient and can consistently improve the performance. mais I agree. The paper is good and is a useful contribution to the field. The problem statement, proposed solution, and validation are adequately described. I have a few suggestions for improvement. The regularization term is defined as $R(x) = \alpha R_N(x)$, where $\alpha$ is the strength of the non-linear dimensionality regularizer and $R_N(x)$ is the nuclear norm. I recommend that the authors justify this specific form of regularization. In general, the nuclear norm is not jointly convex with respect to its arguments. Consequently, using the nuclear norm as a penalty term in a optimization problem can result in a sub-optimal solution. To validate the effectiveness of the proposed method, several examples are used to demonstrate improvement over state-of-the-art results. These examples include an image deblurring problem and a compressed sensing problem. The desired solution for both of these problems contain sharp transitions in the image domain, which are not observed in the reconstruction results. It could be due to the use of L2 or L1 regularization. The addition of non-linear regularizers that enforce smoothness could help to remove noise while keeping sharp edges. This could be tested on the deblurring example. The Tikhonov regularization method is used to solve the inverse problem. Tikhonov regularization is well-known to produce biased estimates if the noise is not white. Consequently, it is recommended that the authors verify the effectiveness of their method in comparison to other methods for solving the inverse problem. For example, L1 or Bayesian regularized methods could be used for comparison purposes. In recent years, deep learning has made remarkable progress in a variety of application fields such as image understanding, speech recognition and natural language processing. This success is largely due to the remarkable improvement in efficiency brought by the development of GPUs and TPUs as well as novel network designs (e.g., residual and parallel networks). However, for many machine learning tasks, especially those that require solving an inverse problem, the use of deep neural networks still suffer from the high computational cost, especially for large-scale or high-dimensional inverse problems. To solve this problem, this paper presents a non-linear dimensionality regularizer to replace the square norm of the weight vector in the cost function, which leads to significant improvements in both the efficiency and the performance of solving large-scale inverse problems. Specifically, the proposed method replaces the sum of the squares of the elements in a linear transformation of the original high-dimensional data with the sum of the absolute values of the elements in the linear transformation. The non-linearity of the proposed regularizer is similar to that of the loss function of the deep neural network, which makes it more suitable for the inverse problem. The convergence analysis and experimental results demonstrate the effectiveness of the proposed method. hg; This paper presents a non-linear dimensionality regularizer for solving inverse problems. This regularizer replaces the square of the weight vector in the cost function with the absolute value of a linear transformation of the original high-dimensional data. This makes it possible to use a deep network for solving large-scale or high-dimensional inverse problems without sacrificing efficiency. As an example, the efficiency of solving a 10,000-dimensional inverse problem with the regularized Newton method is about 50 times faster than that with the original Newton method. The regularized Newton method also has better stability and can even be used to solve 100,000-dimensional problems. The convergence analysis and experimental results demonstrate the effectiveness of the proposed method. The main content of the paper is clear and make sense. Although I have some concerns about the feasibility of the approach in solving the problem in hand, here are my recommendations for the author to improve: 1. The author may use t-SNE to give a visual impression of the recovered results. 2. The author may consider using a bigger data set to demonstrate the effectiveness of the approach. 3. The author may also consider using proper quantitative metrics to measure the effectiveness of the solution. I believe this is still a worthwhile attempt, although I would not expect the method to work perfectly in this situation. 893 Overall assessment: acceptable Constructive comments: 953 Neutral comments: 59 Negative comments: 0 894 Thank you for your review. The authors of this paper introduced a novel non-linear dimensionality regularizer for solving inverse problems such as predicting missing entries in the standard oil flow dataset and Non-Rigid Structure from Motion. This method solved the problem of kernel PCA in the presence of missing data and noise by introducing robust low-rank regularization terms in the implicit feature space. Their approach was evaluated on the challenging oil flow and Non-Rigid Structure from Motion problems and showed significant improvements over the state of the art. morphology The introduction is good, however, we cannot know whether the presented method solves the problem of kernel PCA in the presence of missing data and noise unless we perform evaluation on it. strengths and weaknesses: strengths Our approach presents several advantages over the state of the art. First, it has low computational complexity, as the regularizer is only applied in the implicit feature space. Second, it is very easy to implement: Our approach relies on robustly penalizing the rank of the recovered factors directly in the implicit feature space to create their low-dimensional approximations in closed form. This is in contrast to existing approaches, which typically involve iterative re-training of the whole model, making their application in practice challenging. Third, as shown in the experiments, it is able to deal with high-dimensional and even partial observation cases. Fourth, it is highly general as it can be applied to any kernel, and can therefore be used in many situations without the need for retraining. weaknesses Our approach relies on robustly penalizing the rank of the recovered factors to create their low-dimensional approximations in closed form. To the best of our knowledge, it is the first attempt to create a dimensionality regularizer in the KPCA framework. The introduction of robust low-rank regularization terms in the implicit feature space is an idea that is very interesting and worth pursuing. The code is not available, which is essential to evaluate the robustness of the method in practice.

- Strengths: * Outperforms ALIGN in supervised entity linking task which suggests that the proposed framework improves representations of text and knowledge that are learned jointly. * Direct comparison with closely related approach using very similar input data. * Analysis of the smoothing parameter provides useful analysis since impact of popularity is a persistent issue in entity linking. - Weaknesses: * Comparison with ALIGN could be better. ALIGN used content window size 10 vs this paper's 5, vector dimension of 500 vs this paper's 200. Also its not clear to me whether N(e_j) includes only entities that link to e_j. The graph is directed and consists of wikipedia outlinks, but is adjacency defined as it would be for an undirected graph? For ALIGN, the context of an entity is the set of entities that link to that entity. If N(e_j) is different, we cannot tell how much impact this change has on the learned vectors, and this could contribute to the difference in scores on the entity similarity task. * It is sometimes difficult to follow whether "mention" means a string type, or a particular mention in a particular document. The phrase "mention embedding" is used, but it appears that embeddings are only learned for mention senses. * It is difficult to determine the impact of sense disambiguation order without comparison to other unsupervised entity linking methods. - General Discussion: This paper addresses the problem of disambiguating/linking textual entity mentions into a given background knowledge base (in this case, English Wikipedia). (Its title and introduction are a little overblown/misleading, since there is a lot more to bridging text and knowledge than the EDL task, but EDL is a core part of the overall task nonetheless.) The method is to perform this bridging via an intermediate layer of representation, namely mention senses, thus following two steps: (1) mention to mention sense, and (2) mention sense to entity. Various embedding representations are learned for the words, the mention senses, and the entities, which are then jointly trained to maximize a single overall objective function that maximizes all three types of embedding equally. Technically the approach is fairly clear and conforms to the current deep processing fashion and known best practices regarding embeddings; while one can suggest all kinds of alternatives, it’s not clear they would make a material difference. Rather, my comments focus on the basic approach. It is not explained, however, exactly why a two-step process, involving the mention senses, is better than a simple direct one-step mapping from word mentions to their entities. (This is the approach of Yamada et al., in what is called here the ALIGN algorithm.) Table 2 shows that the two-step MPME (and even its simplification SPME) do better. By why, exactly? What is the exact difference, and additional information, that the mention senses have compare4ed to the entities? To understand, please check if the following is correct (and perhaps update the paper to make it exactly clear what is going on). For entities: their profiles consist of neighboring entities in a relatedness graph. This graph is built (I assume) by looking at word-level relatedness of the entity definitions (pages in Wikipedia). The profiles are (extended skip-gram-based) embeddings. For words: their profiles are the standard distributional semantics approach, without sense disambiguation. For mention senses: their profiles are the standard distributional semantics approach, but WITH sense disambiguation. Sense disambiguation is performed using a sense-based profile (‘language model’) from local context words and neighboring mentions, as mentioned briefly just before Section 4, but without details. This is a problem point in the approach. How exactly are the senses created and differentiated? Who defines how many senses a mention string can have? If this is done by looking at the knowledge base, then we get a bijective mapping between mention senses and entities -– that is, there is exactly one entity for each mention sense (even if there may be more entities). In that case, are the sense collection’s definitional profiles built starting with entity text as ‘seed words’? If so, what information is used at the mention sense level that is NOT used at the entity level? Just and exactly the words in the texts that reliably associate with the mention sense, but that do NOT occur in the equivalent entity webpage in Wikipedia? How many such words are there, on average, for a mention sense? That is, how powerful/necessary is it to keep this extra differentiation information in a separate space (the mention sense space) as opposed to just loading these additional words into the Entity space (by adding these words into the Wikipedia entity pages)? If the above understanding is essentially correct, please update Section 5 of the paper to say so, for (to me) it is the main new information in the paper. It is not true, as the paper says in Section 6, that “…this is the first work to deal with mention ambiguity in the integration of text and knowledge representations, so there is no exact baselines for comparison”. The TAC KBP evaluations for the past two years have hosted EDL tasks, involving eight or nine systems, all performing exactly this task, albeit against Freebase, which is considerably larger and more noisy than Wikipedia. Please see http://nlp.cs.rpi.edu/kbp/2016/ . On a positive note: I really liked the idea of the smoothing parameter in Section 6.4.2. Post-response: I have read the authors' responses. I am not really satisfied with their reply about the KBP evaluation not being relevant, but that they are interested in the goodness of the embeddings instead. In fact, the only way to evaluate such 'goodness' is through an application. No-one really cares how conceptually elegant an embedding is, the question is: does it perform better? - Strengths: Good ideas, simple neural learning, interesting performance (altough not striking) and finally large set of applications. - Weaknesses: amount of novel content. Clarity in some sections. The paper presents a neural learning method for entity disambiguation and linking. It introduces a good idea to integrate entity, mention and sense modeling within the smame neural language modeling technique. The simple training procedure connected with the modeling allows to support a large set of application. The paper is clear formally, but the discussion is not always at the same level of the technical ideas. The empirical evaluation is good although not striking improvements of the performance are reported. Although it seems an extension of (Yamada et al., CoNLL 2016), it adds novel ideas and it is of a releant interest. The weaker points of the paper are: - The prose is not always clear. I found Section 3 not as clear. Some details of Figure 2 are not explained and the terminology is somehow redundant: for example, why do you refer to the dictionary of mentions? or the dictionary of entity-mention pairs? are these different from text anchors and types for annotated text anchors? - Tha paper is quite close in nature to Yamada et al., 2016) and the authors should at least outline the differences. One general observation on the current version is: The paper tests the Multiple Embedding model against entity linking/disambiguation tasks. However, word embeddings are not only used to model such tasks, but also some processes not directly depending on entities of the KB, e.g. parsing, coreference or semantic role labeling. The authors should show that the word embeddings provided by the proposed MPME method are not weaker wrt to simpler wordspaces in such other semantic tasks, i.e. those involving directly entity mentions. I did read the author's response.

This paper proposes a new layer, called the noise adaptation layer, for training deep neural networks. The layer is designed to adapt to the noise in the training data, which can improve the network's performance on noisy data. The paper evaluates the performance of the noise adaptation layer on three different datasets, and shows that it can improve the network's performance on all three datasets. The paper also includes a detailed explanation of how the noise adaptation layer works, and how it can be implemented in a deep learning framework. This paper addresses the problem of training deep neural networks using a noise adaptation layer. The strength of the paper is that it proposes a new way to train deep neural networks that is more efficient and effective than existing methods. The paper is also well-written and easy to follow. However, there are a few weaknesses in the paper. First, the paper does not provide any theoretical background for the noise adaptation layer. This makes it difficult to understand how the layer works and how it is different from existing methods. Second, the paper does not provide any empirical results to show that the noise adaptation layer is better than existing methods. Third, the paper does not provide any code to implement the noise adaptation layer. This makes it difficult for other researchers to reproduce the results and build on the work. The paper proposes a new approach for training deep neural networks using a noise adaptation layer. The main contributions of the paper are: - The paper proposes a new approach for training deep neural networks using a noise adaptation layer. - The paper extends the proposed approach to the case where the noisy labels are dependent on the features in addition to the correct labels. - The experimental results demonstrate that the proposed approach outperforms previous methods. The paper is well-written and the results are well-presented. The paper makes a strong contribution to the field of deep learning. This paper addresses the problem of training a deep neural network (DNN) using noisy labels. The paper proposes to explicitly model the noise by an additional softmax layer that connects the correct labels to the noisy ones. This scheme is extended to the case where the noisy labels are dependent on the features in addition to the correct labels. Experimental results demonstrate that this approach outperforms previous methods. Strengths: - The paper proposes a novel approach to training a DNN using noisy labels, by explicitly modeling the noise. - The paper extends the proposed scheme to the case where the noisy labels are dependent on the features in addition to the correct labels. - Experimental results demonstrate that the proposed approach outperforms previous methods. Weaknesses: - The paper does not discuss the trade-offs involved in using the proposed approach compared to other methods. - The paper does not provide any analysis of the sensitivity of the proposed approach to the choice of hyperparameters. - The paper does not discuss the practical implications of the proposed approach, such as the time and memory requirements.

Overall, this is an interesting work, which extends the previous works on visual-semantic embedding from capturing global visual contexts to local fine-grained details. The bigImage method is pretty novel and interesting. However, I have some concerns about the generalizability of the models and the feasibility of the approaches in real-world scenarios. 1. Generalizability: When applying this method to new images, the model may give unexpected answers, especially for images that contain things that are not present in the training dataset. For example, when applying this method to an image of a dog, the model would try to extract its face, eyes, and other parts that are visible in the image. Such behaviors can be unreliable, and may cause an error for real-world applications. 2. Feasibility: According to the description in the paper, the training of the model requires a large number of images that contain the specific objects that the model should learn. Since this is an extension of the previous work that requires a large number of images to train the global visual contexts, the requirement of images that contain a specific object may increase the required number of training images, and thus the cost of obtaining those training images may become very expensive or even impossible. Nevertheless, I believe that this method is very useful for vision-based research, and I hope the author will provide some generalizable model that can show more reliable results on unseen images in the future. morphology Overall, this is an interesting work, which extends the previous works on visual-semantic embedding from capturing global visual contexts to local fine-grained details. The bigImage method is pretty novel and interesting. Although I have some concerns about the generalizability of the models and the feasibility of the approaches in real-world scenarios. 1. Generalizability: When applying this method to new images, the model may give unexpected answers, especially for images that contain things that are not present in the training dataset. For example, when applying this method to an image of a dog, the model would try to extract its face, eyes, and other parts that are visible in the image. Such behaviors can be unreliable, and may cause an error for real-world applications. 2. Feasibility: According to the description in the paper, the training of the model requires a large number of images that contain the specific objects that the model should learn. Since this is an extension of the previous work that requires a large number of images to train the local fine-grained details, the requirement of images that contain a specific object may increase the required number of training images, and thus the cost of obtaining those training images may become very expensive or even impossible. Nevertheless, I believe that this method is very useful for vision-based research, and I hope the author will provide some generalizable model that can show more reliable results on unseen images in the future. The paper “Learning Character-level Compositional Dataset for Recurrent Neural Network Models” attempts to tackle the problem of learning character-level compositional dataset for use with Recurrent Neural Networks (RNNs). To that end, the authors introduce a new dataset consisting of pairs of characters co-occurred in a same sentence, along with modified next-sentence-suggestion (NSS) parses for each character pair. The author then apply a number of preprocessing steps to the original dataset to create a more compositional version of it. The author apply the dataset to a state-of-the-art RNN architecture, Neural Compositional Learner (NCL), and show that using the proposed dataset as a part of NCL training results in further performance improvement, as well as more human-readable generated texts. The paper presents an interesting idea of modifying NSS parse for each character pair, and perform some other data preprocessing steps. The dataset and preprocessing steps might not be scalable to full training dataset, however. For example, in the NSS parsing step, each character pair is modified with respect to a single NSS parse. It is not clear how this could be applied to a full sentence. Also, the paper presents results only for a state-of-the-art NCL model, and it would be interesting to see how other NCL models perform with the proposed dataset. However, given the lack of documentation, it is not clear how the proposed dataset was created. Also, it would be interesting to see if the NCL model performance can be further improved by using other preprocessing steps on the dataset, such as token level co-occurrence statistics. Overall, this is an interesting paper showing a novel approach of modeling character compositionality. The character visual features seem to encode the character semantics in a very effective way. The novelty here is to encode the character visual features into a vector and apply it to character-level model for better performance on low resource languages, like Chinese, Japanese, and Korean. Below are some suggestions for the author to improve the paper: 1. Clarifying how the LCCHW19 dataset is different from IJCNN03. 2. More experiments on other low resource languages. 3. More analysis on the learned embedding space. 4. Future work will be comparing with the strong baseline or state of the art models. inings 1. LCCHW19 is a larger scale version of IJCNN03. So more experiments on IJCNN03 are better to demonstrate the effectiveness of the proposed model. 2. In the paper, the author only tested on Chinese, Japanese, and Korean. They plan to test the model on other low resource languages in the future. 3. In the paper, the author only analyzed the model performance on document level classification task. It will be better to do some analyses on sentence or paragraph level. 4. The baseline models should be SMRT or GPT-2. Since the LCCHW19 is a character-level only dataset, the GPT-2 may not be a good baseline. meters 1. LCCHW19 is a larger scale version of IJCNN03. So more experiments on IJCNN03 are better to demonstrate the effectiveness of the proposed model. 2. In the paper, the author only tested on Chinese, Japanese, and Korean. They plan to test the model on other low resource languages in the future. 3. In the paper, the author only analyzed the model performance on document level classification task. It will be better to do some analyses on sentence or paragraph level. 4. The baseline models should be SMRT or GPT-2. Since the LCCHW19 is a character-level only dataset, the GPT-2 may not be a good baseline. Previous work has modeled the compositionality of words by creating character-level models of meaning, reducing problems of sparsity for rare words. However, in many writing systems compositionality has an effect even on the character-level: the meaning of a character is derived by the sum of its parts. In this paper, we model this effect by creating embeddings for characters based on their visual characteristics, creating an image for the character and running it through a convolutional neural network to produce a visual character embedding. Experiments on a text classification task demonstrate that such model allows for better processing of instances with rare characters in languages such as Chinese, Japanese, and Korean. Additionally, qualitative analyses demonstrate that our proposed model learns to focus on the parts of characters that carry topical content which resulting in embeddings that are coherent in visual space. My questions for the paper are: 1. Is it right to assume that meaning is derived by the sum of its parts? 2. Would you call it a successful method to create visual character embedding? 3. What can we conclude from the results that visual characteristics are better than other methods to create visual character embedding? I also have some concerns about the paper: 1. In the introduction, you mentioned that you used CNN to produce visual character embedding. Would you please show the architecture of your model and how you initialize it? 2. The whole evaluation is based on text classification, which task is text classification but also text bias? If the data you used is in Chinese, Japanese or Korean, would there be any data leakage to the model? I also would like to suggest that you could further compare the approach of this paper to BERT, OpenAI GPT and some other approaches that focus on text encoder in NLP.

This paper tackles the problem of compressing trained convnets with the goal of reducing memory overhead and speeding up the forward pass. As I understand it, the main contribution of this work is to develop fast convolution routines for sparse conv weights int he case of general sparsity (as compared with structured sparsity). They evaluate their method on both AlexNet and GoogLeNet as well as on various platforms. The authors make code available online. The paper is well written and does a good job of putting this work in the context of past model reduction techniques. My main request of the authors would be to provide a concise summary of the speedup/memory gains achievable with this new work compared with previously published work. The authors do show the various sparsity level obtained with various methods of pruning but it is unclear to me how to translate the information given in the paper into an understanding of gains relative to other methods. While the core ideas explored in this paper are quite limited in algorithmic novelty (e.g., the direct sparse convolutions), the reviewers largely feel that the paper is well written, experiments are carefully done on multiple architectures and system issues are discussed in-depth. Given the interest in the ICLR community around performance characterization and acceleration of CNNs in particular, this paper offers an interesting perspective. The paper details an implementation of sparse-full convolutions and a model to work out the potential speed-up of various sparsity levels for CNNs. The first contribution is more about engineering, but the authors make the source code available which is greatly appreciated. The second contribution is perhaps more interesting, as so far pruning methods have focused on saving memory, with very modest speed gains. Imbuing knowledge of running speed into a pruning algorithm seems like the proper way to tackle this problem. The authors are very methodical in how they build the model and evaluate it very thoroughly. It seems that the same idea could be used not just for pruning existing models, but also when building new architectures: selecting layers and their parameters as to achieve an optimal throughput rate. This could make for a nice direction for future work. One point that is missing is some discussion of how transferable the performance model is to GPUs. This would make the technique easier to adopt broadly. Other areas for improvement: The points in Figure 4 are hard to distinguish (e.g. small red circle vs. small red square), and overall the figure could be made bigger; specifying whether the "base learning rate" in Section 3 is the start or end rate of the annealing schedule; typos: "punning" (p.4), "spares" (p.5). The authors provide a well engineered solution to exploiting sparsity in convolutional layers of a deep network by recasting it as sparse matrix-vector multiplication. This leads to very nice speedups and the analysis of when this is possible is also useful for practitioners. My main concern with this paper is that the "research" aspect of it seems rather minimal, and it's mostly about performance engineering and comparisons. It is upto the area chairs to decide how well such a paper fits in at ICLR.
