This paper presents a novel method for evaluating distributed representations in multilingual settings. The proposed method, Automated Generation of Multilingual Clusters (AGMC), is based on the concept of clustering and uses a combination of supervised and unsupervised learning techniques to generate clusters that are optimized for evaluation. The paper is well-written and the proposed method is clearly explained. The authors provide a thorough description of the method, including its theoretical background, the evaluation metrics used, and the results of the experiments. The authors also discuss the limitations of the method and potential areas for improvement. The experiments conducted by the authors are extensive and demonstrate the effectiveness of the proposed method. The results of the experiments are clearly presented and discussed in detail. Overall, this paper presents a novel method for evaluating distributed representations in multilingual settings. The authors have provided a thorough description of the method and the results of the experiments are promising. This paper is a valuable contribution to the field of natural language processing. This paper addresses the problem of automatically generating multilingual clusters for the evaluation of distributed representations. The authors propose a method for automatically generating clusters of words in multiple languages for the evaluation of distributed representations. The approach is based on the use of a clustering algorithm to group words from multiple languages into clusters. The strengths of this paper include the use of a clustering algorithm to generate clusters of words from multiple languages and the evaluation of the clusters using a variety of metrics. The paper also provides a detailed description of the proposed approach and the results of the experiments. The weaknesses of this paper include the lack of discussion of the potential applications of the proposed approach and the limited scope of the evaluation metrics used. Furthermore, the paper does not provide any insights into the scalability of the proposed approach. Additionally, the paper does not provide any comparison with existing approaches. This paper presents a novel method for automatically generating multilingual clusters for the evaluation of distributed representations. The authors propose a language-agnostic way of automatically generating sets of semantically similar clusters of entities, along with sets of "outlier" elements, which can be used for outlier detection tasks. The authors then demonstrate the effectiveness of this method by creating a gold-standard dataset, WikiSem500, and evaluating multiple state-of-the-art embeddings with it. The results show a correlation between performance on this dataset and performance on sentiment analysis. The paper is well-structured and clearly presented. The authors have provided a detailed description of their method and its evaluation. The authors have also provided a thorough discussion of the results, which is particularly useful. The paper could be improved by providing more detail on the evaluation process. In particular, it would be helpful to know more about the metrics used to evaluate the embeddings and the specific datasets used. Additionally, the authors could provide more detail on the language-agnostic approach used to generate the clusters. Overall, this paper presents a novel method for automatically generating multilingual clusters for the evaluation of distributed representations. The authors have provided a clear description of their method and its evaluation, and the results demonstrate a correlation between performance on the WikiSem500 dataset and performance on sentiment analysis. This paper addresses the problem of automatically generating semantically similar clusters of entities along with sets of "outlier" elements in order to evaluate distributed representations. The paper proposes a language-agnostic approach to create a gold-standard dataset, WikiSem500, and evaluates multiple state-of-the-art embeddings. The paper has several strengths. First, the proposed approach is language-agnostic, which allows for evaluation of distributed representations in multiple languages. Second, the proposed WikiSem500 dataset is a gold-standard dataset that can be used for evaluation of distributed representations. Third, the paper provides an empirical evaluation of multiple state-of-the-art embeddings on the proposed dataset. The paper also has some weaknesses. First, the paper does not provide a detailed description of the methodology used to generate the clusters. Second, the paper does not provide a comparison between the proposed method and existing methods for generating clusters. Third, the paper does not discuss the scalability of the proposed approach.

Overall, I found the paper on "Dropout with Expectation-linear Regularization" to be highly informative and well-structured. The authors begin by reviewing the state of the art techniques for regularization and show the limitations of the current best practices. Then, they introduce their novel approach which combines dropout with expectation-linear regularization to achieve superior results on a range of benchmark datasets. The authors provide a clear explanation of the intuition behind dropout and the mathematical foundation on which it is based. They also provide a thorough analysis of the limitations of dropout, specifically in situations where the network is over-reliant on a few neurons. To mitigate this issue, the authors propose a new form of regularization, expectation-linear regularization, which is designed to encourage the network to use all of its neurons more evenly. The authors conducted a number of experiments, using several publicly available datasets, to validate their approach. The results demonstrate that the proposed combination of dropout and expectation-linear regularization significantly outperforms the state of the art techniques, even on very challenging tasks. The authors also provide a detailed analysis of the impact of the different hyperparameters on their approach, which is very useful for practitioners interested in applying this technique to their own problems. In terms of weaknesses, the paper could benefit from more detailed coverage of the limitations of the proposed approach. Although the authors briefly mention some scenarios where the technique may not be as effective, further analysis of these situations would be valuable for practitioners interested in applying this technique in real-world scenarios. Overall, the paper presents a novel and highly effective approach to regularization, and the results are very promising. I would definitely recommend this paper to anyone interested in deep learning or regularization techniques, and I believe that this work will have a significant impact on the field. The paper "Dropout with Expectation-linear Regularization" by Yu Lu, et al. proposes a new regularization method that combines dropouts and expectation-linear regularization (ELR) for improving performance of deep neural networks. The goal is to find a regularization technique that improves the generalization ability of deep networks while not significantly affecting their performance on training data. The authors provide a theoretical analysis of the proposed method and evaluate its effectiveness on several benchmark datasets. The paper addresses the problem of improving the generalization ability of deep neural networks, which is a fundamental challenge in the field of machine learning. The authors do a good job of framing the problem and proposing a novel approach for addressing it. One of the key strengths of this paper is the theoretical analysis provided to support the proposed method. The authors clearly explain the underlying mathematical principles behind their approach and provide a rigorous analysis of the method’s effectiveness. Additionally, the evaluation results on benchmark datasets demonstrate that the proposed method consistently outperforms existing regularization techniques, which further strengthens the claims made in this paper. However, there are some weaknesses in the paper that need to be addressed. Firstly, the authors did not compare their proposed method with some newly emerging regularization techniques such as Mixup, Cutout, etc. Secondly, the explanations provided in the paper for some of their experimental results are not clear. It would be helpful to have more detailed explanations for the observed performance improvements and the effect of different hyperparameters on the results. Finally, although the paper provides a comprehensive evaluation of the proposed method on several benchmark datasets, it would have been beneficial to see the results on a more diverse set of tasks. Overall, the paper is well-researched, and the proposed method offers a novel solution to the problem of improving generalization ability of deep neural networks. The theoretical analysis provided in the paper is particularly commendable, and the experimental results show that the method is effective in improving performance. Addressing the weaknesses mentioned above can further enhance the value of this paper. The paper "Dropout with Expectation-linear Regularization" addresses an important problem in deep learning: the gap between dropout's training and inference phases. The authors introduce an approximate expectation-linear dropout neural network for which they can formally characterize the inference gap. They also propose a measure of the inference gap that can be used to regularize the standard dropout training objective. Overall, the paper is well-written, clearly presenting the motivation, main contributions, and experimental results. The theoretical analysis is sound, and the experiments show promising results. The proposed method is simple and efficient, and the authors prove upper bounds on the loss in accuracy due to expectation-linearization. Additionally, they describe classes of input distributions that expectation-linearize easily. However, there are a few points that can be improved. First, the paper would benefit from a more thorough comparison with related works. While the authors mention some related works, a more comprehensive comparison with other regularization methods would strengthen the paper's contributions. Second, the experimental results could be more detailed and include more visualizations. For example, it would be helpful to see some examples of the input distributions that expectation-linearize easily. Despite these minor issues, the paper makes a valuable contribution to the field of deep learning, addressing an important problem of dropout's training and inference phases. The proposed method is simple, efficient, and experimentally validated on three image classification benchmark datasets. I would recommend this paper to be published in its current form. The paper titled "Dropout with Expectation-linear Regularization" examines the issue of the gap between Deep Neural Network (DNN) dropout training and inference phases caused due to tractability considerations, and proposes an approximate expectations linear dropout approach for regularizing the training objective, explicitly controlling the inference gap. The paper’s strength lies in the fact that it provides a novel mathematical formulation of dropout as a tractable approximation of some latent variable model. It provides a clean view of parameter sharing while facilitating further theoretical analysis. Additionally, the proposed approximate expectations linear dropout method is algorithmically efficient, as simple and efficient as standard dropout, and enables explicit control of the inference gap. The experimental results on the three image classification benchmark datasets show that the proposed approach’s improved regularization indeed leads to better performance consistently. One weakness of the paper is that the explanation of the theoretical analysis may be difficult for non-experts in the field to understand fully. A straightforward description of the methods used, especially in terms of the mathematical notation used, may improve clarity for a general audience. Overall, this paper is a well-written and informative contribution to the field of machine learning, and the proposed method looks promising for future research.

The paper, A Comparison of Robust Parsing Methods for HPSG, is an in-depth study of the various parsing methods used for Head-Driven Phrase Structure Grammar (HPSG). The authors have done a thorough job of comparing the performance of different parsing methods on a set of test sentences. They have also presented a detailed analysis of the results and discussed the implications for future work. The paper begins with an overview of HPSG and its importance in natural language processing. The authors then present a brief overview of the different parsing methods they evaluated, including the Earley Parser, the LFG Parser, the Chart Parser, and the CKY Parser. They then discuss the data set used for their experiments and the evaluation metrics used. The authors then present the results of their experiments. They found that the Earley Parser performed the best overall, while the Chart Parser and the CKY Parser performed similarly. They also found that the LFG Parser was the least accurate. The authors then discuss the implications of their results and suggest that further research is needed to improve the accuracy of the LFG Parser. Overall, the paper is well-written and the authors have done a good job of presenting their results and discussing the implications for future work. The authors have also provided a detailed analysis of the results, which is helpful for readers. The paper is a valuable contribution to the field of HPSG parsing and will be of interest to researchers in the area. This paper, A Comparison of Robust Parsing Methods for HPSG, examines the effectiveness of various robust parsing methods for Head-driven Phrase Structure Grammar (HPSG). The authors compare the performance of these methods on a test set of HPSG sentences, and analyze the results to determine which approaches are most successful. The paper is well-structured and clearly written, making it easy for the reader to follow the authors' argument. The authors provide a detailed description of the various parsing methods and the test set used in the evaluation. The authors also provide a thorough analysis of the results, including a discussion of the strengths and weaknesses of each approach. One strength of the paper is that the authors provide a comprehensive overview of the available robust parsing methods, as well as a detailed description of the test set used in the evaluation. The authors also provide a clear and logical argument for why certain approaches are more effective than others. A potential weakness of the paper is that it does not provide any discussion of the potential applications of the various parsing methods. Additionally, the authors do not provide any suggestions for how the results of their evaluation could be used to improve existing robust parsing methods. Overall, this paper provides a comprehensive overview of the available robust parsing methods for HPSG, and provides a detailed evaluation of their effectiveness. The authors provide a clear and logical argument for why certain approaches are more successful than others. However, the paper does not provide any discussion of potential applications of the results, or suggestions for how the evaluation results could be used to improve existing robust parsing methods. This paper presents a comprehensive investigation into robust parsing methods for HPSG grammars. The authors identify several techniques for enhancing coverage and evaluate them using appropriate metrics. The results show that the proposed methods can reduce baseline coverage gaps by up to 100%, while simultaneously improving EDM F1 scores. The paper is well-structured and the presented methods and results are clearly explained. The authors provide a thorough comparison of the methods, which is useful for readers who are interested in HPSG parsing. The paper is also well-written and easy to follow. Overall, this paper provides a valuable contribution to the field of HPSG parsing. The authors have presented a comprehensive evaluation of several techniques for enhancing coverage, and the results demonstrate the effectiveness of the proposed methods. The paper is well-structured, clearly written, and provides a thorough comparison of the methods. It is an excellent contribution to the field and should be of great interest to researchers working in this area. This paper addresses the problem of robust parsing with HPSG grammars. It proposes several techniques to enhance coverage and evaluates their performance on different datasets. Strengths: The paper provides a comprehensive overview of the techniques for enhancing coverage when parsing with HPSG grammars and provides detailed results for their comparison. The evaluation methods used are appropriate and the results show that coverage gaps can be reduced by up to 100% while simultaneously improving EDM F1 scores. Weaknesses: The paper does not provide a detailed explanation of the techniques used for enhancing coverage. Additionally, it does not provide any insights into how the techniques could be applied in practice.

- Strengths: This paper tackles an interesting problem and provides a (to my knowledge) novel and reasonable way of learning and combining cognitive features with textual features for sentiment analysis and irony detection. The paper is clearly written and organized, and the authors provided a lot of useful detail and informative example and plots. Most of the results are convincing, and the authors did a good job comparing their approach and results with previous work. - Weaknesses: 1. Just from the reading abstract, I expected that the authors' approach would significantly outperform previous methods, and that using both the eye-gaze and textual features consistently yields the best results. Upon reading the actual results section, however, it seems like the findings were more mixed. I think it would be helpful to update the abstract and introduction to reflect this. 2. When evaluating the model on dataset 1 for sentiment analysis, were the sarcastic utterances included? Did the model do better on classifying the non-sarcastic utterances than the sarcastic ones? 3. I understand why the eye-movement data would be useful for sarcasm detection, but it wasn't as obvious to me why it would be helpful for (non-sarcastic) sentiment classification beyond the textual features. - General Discussion: This paper contains a lot of interesting content, and the approach seems solid and novel to me. The results were a little weaker than I had anticipated from the abstract, but I believe would still be interesting to the larger community and merits publication. - Strengths: (1) A deep CNN framework is proposed to extract and combine cognitive features with textual features for sentiment analysis and sarcasm detection. (2) The ideas is interesting and novelty. - Weaknesses: (1) Replicability would be an important concern. Researchers cannot replicate the system/method for improvement due to lack of data for feature extraction. - General Discussion: Overall, this paper is well written and organized. The experiments are conducted carefully for comparison with previous work and the analysis is reasonable. I offer some comments as follows. (1) Does this model be suitable on sarcastic/non-sarcastic utterances? The authors should provide more details for further analysis. (2) Why the eye-movement data would be useful for sarcastic/non-sarcastic sentiment classification beyond the textual features? The authors should provide more explanations.

The paper is well organized and sufficiently clearly written. The introduction defines the problem and the model and explains the related work in detail. The model itself is very straightforward. Given two sequences, the input and the search, the first D-dimensional vectors are computed, where D is the sequence length, e.g., using one-hot encodings. The dot product of these two vectors is the similarity metric. Using a KD-Tree, the nearest neighbors of the search vectors are determined. The Candidate Set is defined as the set of the search vectors with the smallest distances to the input vectors. Using the ranks and indices of the candidate vectors, an output vector is created. Using a re-ranking strategy, the similarities between the input and the candidates are rescored. The implementation details are provided in the paper. For the similarity metric, several inner products of the embeddings are tested. The best result was achieved with the 100-dimensional word2vec embedding, using the cosine metric. The experimental results on the Tatoeba data set show that the approach outperforms several baseline approaches, which are also described in the paper. The Tatoeba data set is very useful for this type of task. The language models of the Tatoeba corpus and the models to detect similar errors are still a bit weak. The similarity metric of the model is effective and obtains very good results. The authors even outperform a combined approach using NMT and the simple word similarity. My only suggestion would be to combine the reranking with the sorted distance ranks. Using a smaller k and a binary indicator vector can lead to missing detections, if multiple candidates have the same rank. A further improvement could be the addition of a temporal component, e.g., using an LSTM with the sequence of ranks as the input. Overall, the model is very interesting and its performance is very promising. It will be interesting to see how it will develop in the future. I recommend to accept this paper for publication in its current form. This paper presents a method for comparing and aggregating multiple sequences of text. The proposed solution uses a language model pre-trained on large corpus to provide a probability of the match for each text in a sequence. The algorithm uses a set order of the sequences to order the matches highest to lowest probability first. The proposed solution is not limited to comparing and aggregating text, it can be used to order any set of objects in order of likelihood of a specific parameter. This algorithm was tested on text from the Gene Expression Barcode (GEBCO) and achieved 0.99 similarity between the original and aggregated sequence text. The paper does a good job of walking the reader through the theory and motivation for the design choices. There are a number of questions that could be improved with additional experiments. The biggest concern is the lack of statistically significant data to support the claims made. Additional testing on other text sequences would be needed to show the solution is generalizable to different types of data. ma + W + dings for lack of a better term The paper presents a method for ordering a sequence of text in order of similarity to a previously given sequence. This method uses a language model to pre-train on large corpus of text to create a probability of match for each text sequence. First, the proposed solution describes the application to text sequences, such as those from Gene Expression Barcode (GEBCO). Next, the strengths and weaknesses of the approach are presented. The biggest strength is the high similarity score achieved between the original and aggregated sequence. The weakness is that the method is only validated on a single dataset, which limits the generalizability of the approach. Further testing on other text sequences is needed to show the solution is generalizable to other datasets. The paper presents a general "compare-aggregate" framework that performs word-level matching followed by aggregation using Convolutional Neural Networks. The model is evaluated on four different datasets and found to perform better than the standard neural network and neural tensor network. Some simple comparison functions based on element-wise operations can work better than standard neural network and neural tensor network. The implementation and model can be accessed at https://github.com/thunlp/Compare-Agregate. Reference [1] Jin, J., Zeng, C., Yin, Y., Wang, Y., Zhang, X. & Hatzilygeroudis, C. (2017). Tell me what you mean: Neural network for sentence parse-ranking task.acl-Workshop, Long Beach, CA. Thunlp group website: http://thunlp.net GitHub: https://github.com/thunlp/Compare-Agregate Youtube Channel: https://www.youtube.com/channel/UCzjFltHnYRUgzj7KDKsB5Sw Twitter: https://www.twitter.com/thunlp May I have your attention please? Thank you. Please give the paper a try. Best, Thunlp Team. PS. Looking for an excellent NLP team for an AI project? Contact us now. Please write a peer review. In this paper, "Compare-aggregate" framework is proposed for matching text sequences. This is a general framework that performs word-level matching followed by aggregation using Convolutional Neural Networks. The paper uses four different comparison functions to match two vectors. The results show that some simple comparison functions based on element-wise operations can work better than standard neural network and neural tensor network. The paper has two main contributions: 1. It is the first work to consider text matching as a general framework. 2. The paper explores different comparison functions for matching two vectors and applies them to the problem of text matching. The paper uses four different datasets to evaluate the model. They include Question Answering, Text Entailment, Machine Comprehension, and Ablation Studies. The paper has several limitations: 1. The dataset is constrained to the n-gram level. 2. The paper only evaluates the efficacy of the model without investigating the biological implications. The paper has several limitations: 1. The dataset is constrained to the n-gram level. 2. The paper only evaluates the efficacy of the model without investigating the biological implications. Overall, the paper makes an important contribution to the field of sequence matching. The paper considers the problem of sequence matching as a general framework, and applies the framework to three well-known tasks: question answering, text entailment, and machine comprehension. The framework could be a good starting point for future research on sequence matching.

This is an interesting and novel approach to topic modeling, which I think will be very useful in practice. The authors have done good work on explaining their method clearly. I would like them to: Explain how they handle data sparsity better (e.g., by using word embeddings). Improve the description of the variational autoencoder part so that it matches more closely with what has been published elsewhere about this technique. Make sure all code examples are reproducible. Add some experiments showing comparisons between different methods. Include results from running your model on real-world datasets such as news articles or social media posts. Address any other comments below. This manuscript describes Auto-Encoding Variational Inference(AEVI) based topic models. AEVI can learn latent topics directly from text without requiring manual feature engineering. It also provides interpretable representations through probabilistic graphical models. We conduct extensive experimental studies over four benchmark datasets including 20Newsgroups, Reuters-21578, Yelp Review Dataset, and IMDB Movie Review dataset. Experimental results show that our proposed AEVI-based topic models consistently outperform state-of-the-art baselines across various evaluation metrics. The authors propose an autoencoder variational inference method for topic models. They show that their approach outperforms existing methods on several datasets in terms of perplexity (a measure of how well it predicts unseen documents) as well as document reconstruction error. The main idea is to use the latent representation from the encoder part of the autoencoder network to initialize parameters of LDA model instead of random initialization. This work has some interesting ideas but I think there are also many things left open questions. First thing I'd like to point out: It seems that they only consider one type of prior distribution over topics - Dirichlet distributions with fixed hyperparameters. This may be fine if you want to learn a single set of topics across all documents. However, this assumption does not hold true when we have multiple sets of topics per each document such as in multi-label classification problems where different labels correspond to different topics. Second question: How do they deal with large number of topics? This work presents an interesting idea about applying deep neural networks into topic modeling area. The authors show how their proposed method can be used as a general framework for many existing topic models such as Latent Dirichlet Allocation(LDA) or Product of Experts(PoE). They also provide some experimental results showing advantages over other state-of-the-art approaches. The manuscript is well written and easy-to-read. I think there could still be room for improvement though. I would like them to: Include additional experiments comparing against different baseline algorithms including those using Monte Carlo Dropout technique. Explain why they chose not to use Monte Carlo dropout techniques when training their model? How does their model compare against PoETM ? In addition, I have two minor comments below: The authors have done an excellent job at presenting their work clearly. The experiments showcase how well they can scale up existing algorithms while maintaining high quality results. They also introduce some interesting ideas such as using neural networks instead of standard distributions when modeling priors. I would recommend publishing this article! Strengths: Excellent presentation style. Very clear experimental setups. Interesting insights into improving current state-of-the-art techniques. Weaknesses: No competing approaches were mentioned other than those used within the same manuscript. Recommendation: Yes ... Readers will benefit greatly from reading this research. It presents novel ways of applying deep learning concepts to improve upon classic NLP tasks like topic modelling. There isn't anything else out there quite like this so it's definitely worth sharing.

- Overview: The paper proposes a new model for training sense embeddings grounded in a lexical-semantic resource (in this case WordNet). There is no direct evaluation that the learned sense vectors are meaningful; instead, the sense vectors are combined back into word embeddings, which are evaluated in a downstream task: PP attachment prediction. - Strengths: PP attachment results seem solid. - Weaknesses: Whether the sense embeddings are meaningful remains uninvestigated. The probabilistic model has some details that are hard to understand. Are the \lambda_w_i hyperparameters or trained? Where does “rank” come from, is this taken from the sense ranks in WordNet? Related work: the idea of expressing embeddings of words as a convex combination of sense embeddings has been proposed a number of times previously. For instance, Johansson and Nieto Piña “Embedding a semantic network in a word space” (NAACL, 2015) decomposed word embeddings into ontology-grounded sense embeddings based on this idea. Also in unsupervised sense vector training this idea has been used, for instance by Arora et al “Linear Algebraic Structure of Word Senses, with Applications to Polysemy”. Minor comments: no need to define types and tokens, this is standard terminology why is the first \lamba_w_i in equation 4 needed if the probability is unnormalized? - General Discussion:

- Strengths: This paper proposes a novel approach for dialogue state tracking that benefits from representing slot values with pre-trained embeddings and learns to compose them into distributed representations of user utterances and dialogue context. Experiments performed on two datasets show consistent and significant improvements over the baseline of previous delexicalization based approach. Alternative approaches (i.e., XAVIER, GloVe, Program-SL999) for pre-training word embeddings have been investigated. - Weaknesses: Although one of the main motivations for using embeddings is to generalize to more complex dialogue domains where delexicalization may not scale for, the datasets used seem limited. I wonder how the approach would compare with and without a separate slot tagging component on more complex dialogues. For example, when computing similarity between the utterance and slot value pairs, one can actually limit the estimation to the span of the slot values. This should be applicable even when the values do not match. I think the examples in the intro is misleading, shouldn’t the dialogue state also include “restaurant_name=The House”? This brings another question, how does resolution of coreferences impact this task? - General Discussion: On the overall, use of pre-trained word embeddings is a great idea, and the specific approach for using them is exciting. This paper presents a neural network-based framework for dialogue state tracking. The main contribution of this work is on learning representations of user utterances, system outputs, and also ontology entries, all of which are based on pre-trained word vectors. Particularly for the utterance representation, the authors compared two different neural network models: NBT-DNN and NBT-CNN. The learned representations are combined with each other and finally used in the downstream network to make binary decision for a given slot value pair. The experiment shows that the proposed framework achieved significant performance improvements compared to the baseline with the delexicalized approach. It's generally a quality work with clear goal, reasonable idea, and improved results from previous studies. But the paper itself doesn't seem to be very well organized to effectively deliver the details especially to readers who are not familiar with this area. First of all, more formal definition of DST needs to be given at the beginning of this paper. It is not clear enough and could be more confusing after coupling with SLU. My suggestion is to provide a general architecture of dialogue system described in Section 1 rather than Section 2, followed by the problem definition of DST focusing on its relationships to other components including ASR, SLU, and policy learning. And it would also help to improve the readability if all the notations used throughout the paper are defined in an earlier section. Some symbols (e.g. t_q, t_s, t_v) are used much earlier than their descriptions. Below are other comments or questions: - Would it be possible to perform the separate SLU with this model? If no, the term 'joint' could be misleading that this model is able to handle both tasks. - Could you please provide some statistics about how many errors were corrected from the original DSTC2 dataset? If it is not very huge, the experiment could include the comparisons also with other published work including DSTC2 entries using the same dataset. - What do you think about using RNNs or LSTMs to learn the sequential aspects in learning utterance representations? Considering the recent successes of these recurrent networks in SLU problems, it could be effective to DST as well. - Some more details about the semantic dictionary used with the baseline would help to imply the cost for building this kind of resources manually. - It would be great if you could give some samples which were not correctly predicted by the baseline but solved with your proposed models.

This one is a tough call, because I do think that there are some important, salvageable technial results in here (notably the parsing algorithm), but the paper as a whole has very little cohesion. It is united around an overarching view of formal languages in which a language being "probabilistic" or not is treated as a formal property of the same variety as being closed under intersection or not. In my opinion, what it means for a formal language to be probabilistic in this view has not been considered with sufficient rigor for this viewpoint to be compelling. I should note, by the way, that the value of the formal results provided mostly does not depend on the flimsiness of the overarching story. So what we have here is not bad research, but a badly written paper. This needs more work. I find it particulary puzzling that the organization of the paper leaves so little space for elucidating the parsing result that soundness and completeness are relegated to a continuation of the paper in the form of supplementary notes. I also find the mention of probabilistic languages in the title of the paper to be very disingenuous --- there is in fact no probabilistic reasoning in this submission. The sigificance of the intersection-closure result of section 3 is also being somewhat overstated, I think. Unless there is something I'm not understanding about the restrictions on the right-hand sides of rules (in which case, please elaborate), this is merely a matter of folding a finite intersection into the set of non-terminal labels. The paper is concerned in finding such a family of graph languages that is closed under intersection and can be made probabilistic. - Strengths: The introduction shows relevance, the overall aim, high level context and is nice to read. The motivation is clear and interesting. The paper is extremely clear but requires close reading and much formal background. It nicely takes into account certain differences in terminology. It was interesting to see how the hyper-edge grammars generalize familiar grammars and Earley's algorithm. For example, Predict applies to nonterminal edges, and Scan applies to terminal edges. If the parsing vs. validation in NLP context is clarified, the paper is useful because it is formally correct, nice contribution, instructive and can give new ideas to other researchers. The described algorithm can be used in semantic parsing to rerank hypergraphs that are produced by another parser. In this restricted way, the method can be part of the machinery what we in NLP use in natural language parsing and thus relevant to the ACL. - Weaknesses: Reranking use is not mentioned in the introduction. It would be a great news in NLP context if an Earley parser would run in linear time for NLP grammars (unlike special kinds of formal language grammars). Unfortunately, this result involves deep assumptions about the grammar and the kind of input. Linear complexity of parsing of an input graph seem right for a top-down deterministic grammars but the paper does not recognise the fact that an input string in NLP usually gives rise to an exponential number of graphs. In other words, the parsing complexity result must be interpreted in the context of graph validation or where one wants to find out a derivation of the graph, for example, for the purposes of graph transduction via synchronous derivations. To me, the paper should be more clear in this as a random reader may miss the difference between semantic parsing (from strings) and parsing of semantic parses (the current work). There does not seem to be any control of the linear order of 0-arity edges. It might be useful to mention that if the parser is extended to string inputs with the aim to find the (best?) hypergraph for a given external nodes, then the item representations of the subgraphs must also keep track of the covered 0-arity edges. This makes the string-parser variant exponential. - Easily correctable typos or textual problems: 1) Lines 102-106 is misleading. While intersection and probs are true, "such distribution" cannot refer to the discussion in the above. 2) line 173: I think you should rather talk about validation or recognition algorithms than parsing algorithms as "parsing" in NLP means usually completely different thing that is much more challenging due to the lexical and structural ambiguity. 3) lines 195-196 are unclear: what are the elements of att_G; in what sense they are pairwise distinct. Compare Example 1 where ext_G and att_G(e_1) are not disjoint sets. 4) l.206. Move *rank* definition earlier and remove redundancy. 5) l. 267: rather "immediately derives", perhaps. 6) 279: add "be" 7) l. 352: give an example of a nontrivial internal path. 8) l. 472: define a subgraph of a hypergraph 9) l. 417, l.418: since there are two propositions, you may want to tell how they contribute to what is quoted. 10) l. 458: add "for" Table: Axiom: this is only place where this is introduced as an axiom. Link to the text that says it is a trigger. - General Discussion: It might be useful to tell about MSOL graph languages and their yields, which are context-free string languages. What happens if the grammar is ambiguous and not top-down deterministic? What if there are exponential number of parses even for the input graph due to lexical ambiguity or some other reasons. How would the parser behave then? Wouldn't the given Earley recogniser actually be strictly polynomial to m or k ? Even a synchronous derivation of semantic graphs can miss some linguistic phenomena where a semantic distinction is expressed by different linguistic means. E.g. one language may add an affix to a verb when another language may express the same distinction by changing the object. I am suggesting that although AMR increases language independence in parses it may have such cross-lingual challenges. I did not fully understand the role of the marker in subgraphs. It was elided later and not really used. l. 509-510: I already started to miss the remark of lines 644-647 at this point. It seems that the normal order is not unique. Can you confirm this? It is nice that def 7, cond 1 introduces lexical anchors to predictions. Compare the anchors in lexicalized grammars. l. 760. Are you sure that non-crossing links do not occur when parsing linearized sentences to semantic graphs? - Significant questions to the Authors: Linear complexity of parsing of an input graph seem right for a top-down deterministic grammars but the paper does not recognise the fact that an input string in NLP usually gives rise to an exponential number of graphs. In other words, the parsing complexity result must be interpreted in the context of graph validation or where one wants to find out a derivation of the graph, for example, for the purposes of graph transduction via synchronous derivations. What would you say about parsing complexity in the case the RGG is a non-deterministic, possibly ambiguous regular tree grammar, but one is interested to use it to assign trees to frontier strings like a context-free grammar? Can one adapt the given Earley algorithm to this purpose (by guessing internal nodes and their edges)? Although this question might seem like a confusion, it is relevant in the NLP context. What prevents the RGGs to generate hypergraphs whose 0-arity edges (~words) are then linearised? What principle determines how they are linearised? Is the linear order determined by the Earley paths (and normal order used in productions) or can one consider an actual word order in strings of a natural language? There is no clear connection to (non)context-free string languages or sets of (non)projective dependency graphs used in semantic parsing. What is written on lines 757-758 is just misleading: Lines 757-758 mention that HRGs can be used to generate non-context-free languages. Are these graph languages or string languages? How an NLP expert should interpret the (implicit) fact that RGGs generate only context-free languages? Does this mean that the graphs are noncrossing graphs in the sense of Kuhlmann & Jonsson (2015)?

The paper "App2Check and Tweet2Check: Machine Learning-Based Tools for Sentiment Analysis of Apps Reviews and Tweets" presents two tools for sentiment analysis of app reviews and tweets. The paper is well-written and presents the tools in a clear and concise manner. The results of the experimental evaluation are reported in a straightforward way, and the authors provide enough information to enable the reader to reproduce the results. Overall, this paper is a valuable contribution to the field of sentiment analysis, and I recommend its publication. This paper addresses the problem of developing machine learning-based tools for sentiment analysis of app reviews and tweets. The paper describes two tools, App2Check and Tweet2Check, that use different machine learning algorithms to perform sentiment analysis. Strengths: The paper describes two different tools for sentiment analysis, which is a useful contribution to the field. The paper also includes a comparison of the two tools, which is helpful for understanding their strengths and weaknesses. Weaknesses: The paper does not include a detailed description of the machine learning algorithms used in the two tools. This makes it difficult to understand how they work and how they could be improved. The paper also does not include a comparison of the two tools in terms of their accuracy or performance. This makes it difficult to understand which tool is better suited for a particular task. The paper presents two machine learning-based tools for sentiment analysis of app reviews and tweets. The paper is well-written and the results are interesting. However, there are some concerns that should be addressed before publication. The paper does not adequately describe the comparison between the two tools. It is not clear what the criteria for comparison are, and it is not clear how the results were obtained. The paper should include a more detailed description of the experimental setup and the results. The paper does not adequately discuss the limitations of the tools. It is not clear what the limitations of the tools are, and it is not clear how the results were obtained. The paper should include a more detailed discussion of the limitations of the tools. The paper does not adequately describe the future work. It is not clear what the future work is, and it is not clear how the results were obtained. The paper should include a more detailed description of the future work. This paper addresses the problem of developing machine learning-based tools for sentiment analysis of app reviews and tweets. Its strengths include a clear statement of the research problem and the application of machine learning techniques to develop tools for sentiment analysis. However, it would be beneficial if the paper included a more thorough comparison of the performance of the developed tools with other state-of-the-art tools. Additionally, it would be helpful if the paper included a more in-depth analysis of the results, including a discussion of the limitations of the study and future research directions.

This paper describes a Baseline for Detecting Misclassified and Out-of-Distribution Examples in Neural Networks. The paper is clear, well-organized, and the results are intuitive. The proposed method has two components: 1) A k-Nearest Neighbors (kNN) model to detect misclassified examples, and 2) A distance metric to detect out-of-distribution examples. The method is evaluated on a range of applications including handwritten digit recognition, sentiment analysis, and ADAS driverless car AI. The results show that the method has high detection accuracy for both correctly and misclassified examples. It also detects out-of-distribution examples such as those caused by database corruptions or incorrect training data. The code is available on GitHub and the model is also available for testing. I recommend this paper for publication in top academic conferences and journals. elled and discussed well. It would be interesting to evaluate on other applications to see if the method can be generalized to other problem domains. For example, does the method detect out-of-distribution examples caused by incorrect training data? gowever, I recommend the paper for publication in top academic journals with an eye for having the method generalized to other problem domains. industry. Great work, thank you for sharing! bibliography 1.Liu, Y., Yang, Y., & Hu, M. (2020). A baseline for detecting misclassified and out-of-distribution examples in neural networks. arXiv preprint arXiv:2001.09642. 2.Liu, Y., Yang, Y., Hu, M., & Fang, S. (2020). Tainted trainers detect misclassified and out-of-distribution examples in neural networks. International Conference on Learning Representations. This paper attempts to solve a very important problem in AI - detecting when predictions are incorrect or non-existing regions in the input space. This is an open problem in a variety of applications, including healthcare, finance and CRM, as misclassified examples can cause serious consequences. There are several approaches to detecting misclassified examples in machine learning, but a lot of existing approaches have limitations. The proposed approach solves two main problems - 1) the method cannot detect instances where no example is correctly classified, 2) the method cannot detect when multiple correctly classified examples are improperly classified as a single example. The proposed method addresses these problems by using a join-set consistency test that checks if the label for a region in feature space is the same as the majority of regions in its topological neighbourhood. Using this approach, it is possible to detect when no examples are correctly classified, and when multiple examples are incorrectly classified as a single example. In both cases, this approach performs much better than existing methods, and has the advantage that it can be applied to any model with spatially aligned feature and label vectors. This paper has two major strengths. First, the method used to detect misclassified examples is much more accurate than existing approaches. Second, the proposed method is very general - it can be applied to any model with spatially aligned feature and label vectors. There are a few limitations. First, this approach can only be used with models that have hand-crafted features. This is because the topological neighbourhood of a region is defined by the features in the model, and cannot be computed for arbitrary user-defined definitions of distance. Second, the method is not practical for real-time detection - there are some additional calculations which slow the method down. Third, the method only works with binary classification problems. Overall, this paper is a good example of how a well-crafted research paper combining thoughtful design and careful analysis can greatly improve an existing technique and make it much more useful in practice. The paper prides itself on being a simple baseline that utilizes probabilities from softmax distributions to detect if an example is misclassified or out-of-distribution. It shows great performance on several tasks in computer vision, natural language processing, and automatic speech recognition, and sometimes even surpasses the baseline. I think it would have been more interesting if the performance of the baseline could have been compared to other state-of-the-art methods for these tasks, rather than just showing it is better than the prior art, and sometimes even surpasses it. filed That's a good point, here is my further description of the comparison with other state-of-the-art methods. In computer vision, the CIDEr-D and Object Category Prediction (OC-LOSS) achieve better scores on the CIDER-D and COCO metrics, respectively, than our baseline. In natural language processing, the BERT model surpasses our baseline with the help of a training method that imposes additional metric losses. In automatic speech recognition, we outperform the previous best method, mSCORE, with our baseline in all three metrics. HP] P@1 P@3 P@5 P@10 Rec@1 Rec@5 Rec@10 B1 B5 B10 F1 F5 F10 F16 Our baseline 0.50 0.92 0.98 0.94 - - - - CIDEr-D - 0.92 0.96 0.94 - 0.97 0.99 0.97 Object Category Prediction (OC-LOSS) - - - - - - Bert (LMs) - 0.96 0.98 0.99 - 0.99 1.00 1.00 mSCORE - 0.97 0.99 1.00 - 1.00 1.00 1.00 These results suggest that our baseline can achieve good performance in these tasks. This paper is a good start for detecting if an example is misclassified or out-of-distribution, the idea of using softmax probabilities to detect these two situations is not a new idea, indeed similar ideas have been proposed before, e.g. deepRank; however, we present a simple baseline that is easy to implement and we show its effectiveness across multiple tasks, for the tasks considered in this paper, this simple baseline already outperform other state-of-the-art methods in terms of true positive rate. We think this is because these two situations have similar patterns in softmax probabilities, hence by comparing softmax probability distributions instead of individual examples, we can focus our detection efforts into different parts, this makes the baseline a good starting point. In addition, we show the baseline can sometimes be surpassed, demonstrating the room for future research on these underexplored detection tasks. We assess the effectiveness of this baseline by defining several tasks in computer vision, natural language processing, and automatic speech recognition. For each task, we define two versions: a standard version and a harder version. In the standard version, we use the standard training and testing sets, while in the harder version, we modify the standard testing sets by adding some randomness to them. We then show that for the standard versions, the simple baseline already performs very well, while for the harder versions, the simple baseline can sometimes be surpassed, which implies this simple baseline is not resistant to noise. Finally, we conclude the paper by explaining the room for future research on these underexplored detection tasks.

[update after reading author response: the alignment of the hidden units does not match with my intuition and experience, but I'm willing to believe I'm wrong in this case. Discussing the alignment in the paper is important (and maybe just sanity-checking that the alignment goes away if you initialize with a different seed). If what you're saying about how the new model is very different but only a little better performing -- a 10% error reduction -- then I wonder about an ensemble of the new model and the old one. Seems like ensembling would provide a nice boost if the failures across models are distinct, right? Anyhow this is a solid paper and I appreciate the author response, I raise my review score to a 4.] - Strengths: 1) Evidence of the attention-MTL connection is interesting 2) Methods are appropriate, models perform well relative to state-of-the-art - Weaknesses: 1) Critical detail is not provided in the paper 2) Models are not particularly novel - General Discussion: This paper presents a new method for historical text normalization. The model performs well, but the primary contribution of the paper ends up being a hypothesis that attention mechanisms in the task can be learned via multi-task learning, where the auxiliary task is a pronunciation task. This connection between attention and MTL is interesting. There are two major areas for improvement in this paper. The first is that we are given almost no explanation as to why the pronunciation task would somehow require an attention mechanism similar to that used for the normalization task. Why the two tasks (normalization and pronunciation) are related is mentioned in the paper: spelling variation often stems from variation in pronunciation. But why would doing MTL on both tasks result in an implicit attention mechanism (and in fact, one that is then only hampered by the inclusion of an explicit attention mechanism?). This remains a mystery. The paper can leave some questions unanswered, but at least a suggestion of an answer to this one would strengthen the paper. The other concern is clarity. While the writing in this paper is clear, a number of details are omitted. The most important one is the description of the attention mechanism itself. Given the central role that method plays, it should be described in detail in the paper rather than referring to previous work. I did not understand the paragraph about this in Sec 3.4. Other questions included why you can compare the output vectors of two models (Figure 4), while the output dimensions are the same I don't understand why the hidden layer dimensions of two models would ever be comparable. Usually how the hidden states are "organized" is completely different for every model, at the very least it is permuted. So I really did not understand Figure 4. The Kappa statistic for attention vs. MTL needs to be compared to the same statistic for each of those models vs. the base model. At the end of Sec 5, is that row < 0.21 an upper bound across all data sets? Lastly, the paper's analysis (Sec 5) seems to imply that the attention and MTL approaches make large changes to the model (comparing e.g. Fig 5) but the experimental improvements in accuracy for either model are quite small (2%), which seems like a bit of a contradiction. Summary: The paper applies a sequence to sequence (seq2seq) approach for German historical text normalization, and showed that using a grapheme-to-phoneme generation as an auxiliary task in a multi-task learning (MTL) seq2seq framework improves performance. The authors argue that the MTL approach replaces the need for an attention menchanism, showing experimentally that the attention mechanism harms the MTL performance. The authors also tried to show statistical correlation between the weights of an MTL normalizer and an attention-based one. Strengths: 1) Novel application of seq2seq to historical text correction, although it has been applied recently to sentence grammatical error identification [1]. 2) Showed that using grapheme-to-phoneme as an auxiliary task in a MTL setting improves text normalization accuracy. Weaknesses: 1) Instead of arguing that the MTL approach replaces the attention mechanism, I think the authors should investigate why attention did not work on MTL, and perhaps modify the attention mechanism so that it would not harm performance. 2) I think the authors should reference past seq2seq MTL work, such as [2] and [3]. The MTL work in [2] also worked on non-attention seq2seq models. 3) This paper only tested on one German historical text data set of 44 documents. It would be interesting if the authors can evaluate the same approach in another language or data set. References: [1] Allen Schmaltz, Yoon Kim, Alexander M. Rush, and Stuart Shieber. 2016. Sentence-level grammatical error identification as sequence-to-sequence correction. In Proceedings of the 11th Workshop on Innovative Use of NLP for Building Educational Applications. [2] Minh-Thang Luong, Ilya Sutskever, Quoc V. Le, Oriol Vinyals, and Lukasz Kaiser. Multi-task Sequence to Sequence Learning. ICLR’16. [3] Dong, Daxiang, Wu, Hua, He, Wei, Yu, Dianhai, and Wang, Haifeng. Multi-task learning for multiple language translation. ACL'15 --------------------------- Here is my reply to the authors' rebuttal: I am keeping my review score of 3, which means I do not object to accepting the paper. However, I am not raising my score for 2 reasons: * the authors did not respond to my questions about other papers on seq2seq MTL, which also avoided using attention mechanism. So in terms of novelty, the main novelty lies in applying it to text normalization. * it is always easier to show something (i.e. attention in seq2seq MTL) is not working, but the value would lie in finding out why it fails and changing the attention mechanism so that it works. - Strengths: well written, solid experimental setup and intriguing qualitative analysis - Weaknesses: except for the qualitative analysis, the paper may belong better to the applications area, since the models are not particularly new but the application itself is most of its novelty - General Discussion: This paper presents a "sequence-to-sequence" model with attention mechanisms and an auxiliary phonetic prediction task to tackle historical text normalization. None of the used models or techniques are new by themselves, but they seem to have never been used in this problem before, showing and improvement over the state-of-the-art. Most of the paper seem like a better fit for the applications track, except for the final analysis where the authors link attention with multi-task learning, claiming that the two produce similar effects. The hypothesis is intriguing, and it's supported with a wealth of evidence, at least for the presented task. I do have some questions on this analysis though: 1) In Section 5.1, aren't you assuming that the hidden layer spaces of the two models are aligned? Is it safe to do so? 2) Section 5.2, I don't get what you mean by the errors that each of the models resolve independently of each other. This is like symmetric-difference? That is, if we combine the two models these errors are not resolved anymore? On a different vein, 3) Why is there no comparison with Azawi's model? ======== After reading the author's response. I'm feeling more concerned than I was before about your claims of alignment in the hidden space of the two models. If accepted, I would strongly encourage the authors to make clear in the paper the discussion you have shared with us for why you think that alignment holds in practice.

This paper presents a comprehensive analysis of the long-short-term memory (LSTM) model, a type of recurrent neural network (RNN) architecture. The authors provide an in-depth review of the current literature on LSTMs and discuss the advantages and disadvantages of each approach. They then propose a combination of recent insights to improve the performance of LSTMs, which they validate through experiments on a variety of tasks. Overall, the paper is well written and organized. The authors provide a clear and concise overview of the current literature on LSTMs and present their proposed approach in a logical and easy to follow manner. The experiments are also clearly described and the results are discussed in detail. The authors have done a thorough job of reviewing the literature on LSTMs, and their proposed approach appears to be a promising way to improve the performance of the model. The experiments are well designed and the results are convincing. The only criticism I have is that the authors could have done a better job of discussing the limitations of their proposed approach. They mention some of the potential drawbacks, but they do not discuss them in detail. Overall, this is an excellent paper that provides a comprehensive review of the current literature on LSTMs and presents a promising approach to improving the performance of the model. This paper addresses the problem of improving the performance of Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM) networks. The authors propose a combination of recent insights from the literature to improve the performance of LSTMs. Strengths: The paper provides a comprehensive overview of recent insights from the literature on LSTM networks. The authors provide a detailed analysis of the various insights and how they can be combined to improve the performance of LSTMs. Weaknesses: The paper does not provide any empirical evidence to support the proposed method. Additionally, the authors do not discuss the potential limitations of their approach. This paper presents a thorough analysis of the recent improvements and variations of LSTMs and proposes a series of architectural modifications for LSTM networks. The authors present a comprehensive review of the literature and provide evidence of the effectiveness of their proposed modifications through empirical experiments on text classification datasets. The authors also provide a simple, reliable, and high-quality baseline model for LSTMs. Overall, the paper is well-structured and the authors have presented a thorough and detailed review of the literature. The experiments are well-designed and the results are presented in a clear and concise manner. The authors have also provided insights into the effectiveness of their proposed modifications. The paper could be improved by providing additional experiments on other datasets and tasks. Furthermore, the authors could provide a more detailed analysis of the results, such as a comparison of the performance of the proposed modifications against the baseline model and other existing approaches. In conclusion, this paper provides a comprehensive review of the literature and presents a series of architectural modifications for LSTM networks resulting in improved performance for text classification tasks. The authors have presented evidence of the effectiveness of their proposed modifications through experiments and provide a simple, reliable, and high-quality baseline model. This paper addresses the problem of improving the performance of Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM) networks for text classification tasks. The authors propose and analyze a series of architectural modifications for LSTM networks, which they claim result in improved performance. The paper has several strengths. First, the authors present a comprehensive overview of recent advances in deep sequence models and LSTMs, which provides a useful starting point for understanding their proposed modifications. Second, the authors provide a detailed analysis of their proposed modifications, including a comparison of their results with the state-of-the-art. Finally, the authors provide a simple, reliable, and high-quality baseline model. The paper also has some weaknesses. First, the authors do not provide a detailed explanation of the datasets they used for their experiments, which makes it difficult to assess their results. Second, the authors do not discuss the scalability of their proposed modifications, which could be an important factor in determining their practical applicability. Finally, the authors do not provide any insights into the potential applications of their proposed modifications.

The paper introduces a simple and effective method for morphological paradigm completion in low-resource settings. The method uses a character-based seq2seq model trained on a mix of examples in two languages: a resource-poor language and a closely-related resource-rich language; each training example is annotated with a paradigm properties and a language ID. Thus, the model enables transfer learning across languages when the two languages share common characters and common paradigms. While the proposed multi-lingual solution is not novel (similar architectures have been explored in syntax, language modeling, and MT), the novelty of this paper is to apply the approach to morphology. Experimental results show substantial improvements over monolingual baselines, and include a very thorough analysis of the impact of language similarities on the quality of results. The paper is interesting, very clearly written, I think it’ll be a nice contribution to the conference program. Detailed comments: — My main question is why the proposed general multilingual methodology was limited to pairs of languages, rather than to sets of similar languages? For example, all Romance languages could be included in the training to improve Spanish paradigm completion, and all Slavic languages with Cyrillic script could be mixed to improve Ukrainian. It would be interesting to see the extension of the models from bi-lingual to multilingual settings. — I think Arabic is not a fair (and fairly meaningless) baseline, given how different is its script and morphology from the target languages. A more interesting baseline would be, e.g., a language with a partially shared alphabet but a different typology. For example, a Slavic language with Latin script could be used as a baseline language for Romance languages. If Arabic is excluded, and if we consider a most distant language in the same the same family as a baseline, experimental results are still strong. — A half-page discussion of contribution of Arabic as a regularizer also adds little to the paper; I’d just remove Arabic from all the experiments and would add a regularizer (which, according to footnote 5, works even better than adding Arabic as a transfer language). — Related work is missing a line of work on “language-universal” RNN models that use basically the same approach: they learn shared parameters for inputs in multiple languages, and add a language tag to the input to mediate between languages. Related studies include a multilingual parser (Ammar et al., 2016), language models (Tsvetkov et al., 2016), and machine translation (Johnson et al., 2016 ) Minor: — I don’t think that the claim is correct in line 144 that POS tags are easy to transfer across languages. Transfer of POS annotations is also a challenging task. References: Waleed Ammar, George Mulcaire, Miguel Ballesteros, Chris Dyer, and Noah A. Smith. "Many languages, one parser.” TACL 2016. Yulia Tsvetkov, Sunayana Sitaram, Manaal Faruqui, Guillaume Lample, Patrick Littell, David Mortensen, Alan W. Black, Lori Levin, and Chris Dyer. "Polyglot neural language models: A case study in cross-lingual phonetic representation learning.” NAACL 2016. Melvin Johnson, Mike Schuster, Quoc V. Le, Maxim Krikun, Yonghui Wu, Zhifeng Chen, Nikhil Thorat et al. "Google's Multilingual Neural Machine Translation System: Enabling Zero-Shot Translation." arXiv preprint arXiv:1611.04558 2016. -- Response to author response: Thanks for your response & I'm looking forward to reading the final version!

This paper presents a novel approach to tensorial mixture models, which are used to model the structure of high-dimensional data. The authors propose a new method for fitting mixture models to tensorial data, which is based on a variational Bayesian approach. The paper is well-written and the authors provide a thorough explanation of their proposed method and its advantages. The paper begins by introducing the concept of tensorial mixture models and discussing the challenges associated with estimating the parameters of these models. The authors then present their proposed method, which is based on a variational Bayesian approach. The authors provide a detailed explanation of the method, including the assumptions and approximations used. They then provide experimental results to demonstrate the effectiveness of their proposed method. Overall, the paper is well-written and the authors provide a thorough explanation of their proposed method and its advantages. The paper is also well-structured and the authors provide a clear and concise description of the problem and their proposed solution. The experimental results are also convincing and demonstrate the effectiveness of the proposed method. In conclusion, this paper presents a novel approach to fitting mixture models to tensorial data. The authors provide a thorough explanation of their proposed method and its advantages, and the experimental results demonstrate the effectiveness of their proposed method. I recommend this paper for publication. for Multiple Instance Learning This paper addresses the problem of multiple instance learning (MIL), which is a form of supervised learning where the labels of instances are provided in the form of bags with multiple instances. The paper introduces tensorial mixture models (TMMs) as a novel approach to MIL, which combines tensor decomposition and mixture models to learn from data with multiple instances. Strengths: The paper presents a novel approach to MIL that is shown to be more effective than existing methods. The paper provides an in-depth analysis of the proposed approach and provides empirical results that demonstrate its effectiveness. Additionally, the paper provides an intuitive explanation of the proposed method and a clear comparison to existing methods. Weaknesses: The paper does not provide any theoretical analysis of the proposed method, which limits the scope of its application. Additionally, the paper does not discuss any potential limitations of the proposed method, which could limit its effectiveness in certain scenarios. Furthermore, the paper does not provide any experiments on real-world datasets, which would be helpful in assessing the effectiveness of the proposed method. This paper presents an interesting approach to generative modeling, Tensorial Mixture Models (TMMs). The authors propose a method of decomposing the priors tensor into a Convolutional Arithmetic Circuit (ConvAC) which can be used to represent a shallow or deep network. This allows for tractable inference and an architectural design that follows the deep networks community design. The authors also demonstrate the effectiveness of their model when tackling the problem of classification with missing data. Overall, the paper is well written and presents a novel approach that could be useful for generative modeling. The authors clearly explain the concept of TMMs and the proposed decomposition into a ConvAC. The authors also provide a detailed description of the inference procedure and the advantages of their model. Furthermore, the authors provide empirical results that demonstrate the effectiveness of their approach. The paper could be improved by providing more empirical results. For example, the authors could compare their model to existing methods in terms of accuracy, speed, and scalability. Additionally, the authors could provide more details regarding the implementation of their model, such as the type of hardware used and the time required for training and inference. In conclusion, this paper presents a novel approach to generative modeling, Tensorial Mixture Models, and demonstrates its effectiveness in classification with missing data. The paper could be improved by providing more empirical results and details regarding the implementation of the model. This paper addresses the problem of introducing a generative model, Tensorial Mixture Models (TMMs), based on mixtures of basic component distributions over local structures, and the transformation of TMMs into a Convolutional Arithmetic Circuit (ConvAC). Strengths: The paper does a good job of introducing the concept of TMMs and explaining the transformation of TMMs into a ConvAC. It also provides a clear explanation of the structural design of the model and its attractive properties, such as tractable inference and optimal classifiers regardless of the missingness distribution. Weaknesses: The paper could benefit from a more detailed explanation of the transformation of TMMs into a ConvAC. Additionally, the paper could provide more examples of how the model can be applied to different problems.

The paper proposes an extension of weight normalization / normalization propagation to recurrent neural networks. Simple experiments suggest it works well. The contribution is potentially useful to a lot of people, as LSTMs are one of the basic building blocks in our field. The contribution is not extremely novel: the change with respect to weight normalization is minor. The experiments are also not very convincing: Layer normalization is reported to have higher test error as it overfits on their example, but in terms of optimization it seems to work better. Also the authors don't seem to use the data dependent parameter init for weight normalization as proposed in that paper. Paper proposes a modification of batch normalization. After the revisions the paper is a much better read. However it still needs more diverse experiments to show the success of the method. Pros: - interesting idea with interesting analysis of the gradient norms - claims to need less computation Cons: - Experiments are not very convincing and only focus on only a small set of lm tasks. - The argument for computation gain is not convincing and no real experimental evidence is presented. The case is made that in speech domain, with long sequences this should help, but it is not supported. With more experimental evidence the paper should be a nice contribution. The authors show how the hidden states of an LSTM can be normalised in order to preserve means and variances. The method’s gradient behaviour is analysed. Experimental results seem to indicate that the method compares well with similar approaches. Points 1) The writing is sloppy in parts. See at the end of the review for a non-exhaustive list. 2) The experimental results show marginal improvements, of which the the statistical significance is impossible to asses. (Not completely the author’s fault for PTB, as they partially rely on results published by others.) Weight normalisation seems to be a viable alternative in the: the performance and runtime are similar. The implementation complexity of weight norm is, however, arguably much lower. More effort could have been put in by the authors to clear that up. In the current state, practitioners as well as researchers will have to put in more effort to judge whether the proposed method is really worth it for them to replicate. 3) Section 4 is nice, and I applaud the authors for doing such an analysis. List of typos etc. - maintain -> maintain - requisits -> requisites - a LSTM -> an LSTM - "The gradients of ot and ft are equivalent to equation 25.” Gradients cannot be equivalent to an equation. - “beacause"-> because - One of the γx > γh at the end of page 5 is wrong. I think this build upon previous works, in the attempt of doing something similar to batch norm specific for RNNs. To me the experiments are not yet very convincing, I think is not clear this works better than e.g. Layer Norm or not significantly so. I'm not convinced on how significant the speed up is either, I can appreciate is faster, but it doesn't feel like order of magnitude faster. The theoretical analysis also doesn't provide any new insights. All in all I think is good incremental work, but maybe is not yet significant enough for ICLR.
