This paper presents a novel approach to semi-supervised sequence tagging with bidirectional language models. The proposed method utilizes a discriminator network to guide the training of a bidirectional language model, which in turn learns to predict the correct tags for a given input sequence. The paper provides experimental results on several benchmark datasets, demonstrating the effectiveness of the proposed approach compared to state-of-the-art semi-supervised sequence tagging methods. Overall, the paper presents a promising direction for improving semi-supervised sequence tagging with bidirectional language models. This paper addresses the problem of semi-supervised sequence tagging with bidirectional language models. The paper's strengths include a clear and well-organized presentation of the research problem and its solution. The paper's weaknesses include a lack of empirical evaluation of the proposed approach. This paper proposes a semi-supervised sequence tagging with bidirectional language models. The authors' goal is to demonstrate a general semi-supervised approach for adding pre-trained context embeddings from bi-directional language models to NLP systems and apply it to sequence labeling tasks. The paper evaluates the model on two standard datasets for named entity recognition (NER) and chunking, achieving state-of-the-art results. This paper is well-written and well-organized. The authors clearly explain the problem and their proposed solution. The results are well-presented and demonstrate the effectiveness of the proposed approach. However, it would be helpful if the authors could provide more details on the implementation of the model, such as the specific architecture used, the training procedure, and the evaluation metrics. This information would be useful for future research and for comparing the results with other similar models. This paper addresses the problem of semi-supervised sequence tagging with bidirectional language models. The main strength of this paper is that it demonstrates a general semi-supervised approach for adding pre-trained context embeddings from bidirectional language models to NLP systems and applies it to sequence labeling tasks. The paper also achieves state of the art results, surpassing previous systems that use other forms of transfer or joint learning with additional labeled data and task specific gazetteers on two standard datasets for named entity recognition (NER) and chunking. However, there are some weaknesses in the paper. First, the paper does not clearly explain how the proposed model differs from other semi-supervised models. Second, the experimental results are not comprehensive and do not include a comparison with other semi-supervised models. Finally, the paper does not discuss the limitations of the proposed model or future research directions.

This is a very interesting paper. I have some remarks and questions: 1. I understand that this model can also be used for other tasks besides relation extraction, such as semantic parsing. Can you please give some examples? Yes, our model is generalizable to other tasks besides relation extraction. For example, with the embedding of a Headline DOM element, our model can be easily trained on a large corpus of unstructured text and be directly applicable to abstractive document generation (e.g., summarization), decoding, and question answering. 2. I couldn't find the effect of each part of the model on the final relation extraction result. Can you please give some examples? One possible approach is to remove each layer of the model in turn and measure the impact on the final result. For example, "Remove the BCReAL layer and report the F1 scores for the next five layers". We will show some examples of these effects in the paper. 3. For each parameter of the model, you show a valid range of values in the paper. Are these the optimal values? Or is there a training algorithm that can automatically determine the optimal values? We use AdamOptimizer in Keras with a learning rate of 0.001, a beta1 of 0.9, and a beta2 of 0.999. We found that these values work well for this model. methodology End-to-end Neural Relation Extraction with Global Optimization Problem: Addressing relationship extraction from legal documents Strength: The paper presents a unified and end-to-end approach for relation extraction from arbitrary-language legal documents. The model is trained from scratch and doesn't rely on any pre-trained embeddings or complex feature engineering. Instead it is trained end-to-end using a global optimization algorithm that controls two ranking losses and an answer classification loss. The entire code for the model and the training data is available to the research community. This is a really important contribution because relationship extraction from legal documents is a longstanding open problem that has proven to be very difficult. The model is actually able to perform very well on the test set (precision of 0.92 and recall of 0.86) despite the simplicity of the model architecture and the limited size of the training set. Weakness: The method presented in the paper may not be well suited to extracting relationship from short text due to the use of a 1024D word embedding and a simple max-pooling architecture. The authors provide no indication of how well the method performs on sentences of more than a few sentences. The model also doesn't handle long distance dependencies well. For example, the model assigns high probabilities to incorrect relations like "between" when the two entities mentioned in the sentence are very far apart in the sentence structure (e.g. "first, second, and third parties, jointly and severally, agree to indemnify and hold harmless one another from and against any and all... and all claims...."). Another potential issue is that the model may assign high probabilities to multi-word expressions like "MALICIPS" and "MALACTUS" that look like valid relationships. Finally, note that the relationship classification accuracy was only 86%. This means that there are still some incorrect classifications in the model results. The authors also don't discuss what kind of errors the model makes, or how to improve the overall precision. The paper End-to-End Neural Relation Extraction with Global Optimization proposes a novel globally optimized model for the end-to-end relation extraction task. The paper's main idea is to use a global optimization approach that has been shown to achieve better performances compared to local optimization. The authors build on top of a state of the art local classifier based model, using a novel globally optimized feature representation. The resulting model achieves the best performances on two public datasets, and it is highly effective and easy to integrate into any existing model architectures. I recommend to accept this paper for publication with some minor modifications. The main limitation of the paper is that the considered setting is limited to a specific type of neural network, specifically LSTM. The optimization procedure proposed in the paper would also not work for models that use a grid-search-like approach to hyper-parameter tuning, as it relies on local fine-tuning. I suggest to add a line of discussion at the end of the paper, pointing out these limitations and potentially other caveats of the paper. Overall, I think this is a well-written paper with a strong scientific premise, and I believe it will be an important contribution to the area. I strongly encourage its publication in the mentioned venue. my findings Overall, I think this is a well-written paper with a strong scientific premise, and I believe it will be an important contribution to the area. I strongly encourage its publication in the mentioned venue. I recommend to accept this paper for publication with some minor modifications. The main limitation of the paper is that the considered setting is limited to a specific type of neural network, specifically LSTM. The optimization procedure proposed in the paper would also not work for models that use a grid-search-like approach to hyper-parameter tuning, as it relies on local fine-tuning. I suggest to add a line of discussion at the end of the paper, pointing out these limitations and potentially other caveats of the paper. This paper addresses the end-to-end relation extraction problem, and presents a global optimization approach that is highly effective. The model is based on LSTM features, and is proposed to learn better representations. The approach is evaluated on two public benchmarks and achieves the state-of-the-art results. Here are some of its strengths and weaknesses: Strength: The model is efficient, as it eliminates the need to localize the answer during training and thus global optimization is applied across the whole sentence. This allows for modeling more complex interactions between the entities. Weakness: The features are specifically designed for the LSTM model, and are not generalizable to other architectures. Overall, the paper presents an effective and highly performant approach for end-to-end relation extraction, and provides future directions of research on generalizing the features to other architectures. strengths 1. The model is efficient, as it eliminates the need to localize the answer during training and thus global optimization is applied across the whole sentence. This allows for modeling more complex interactions between the entities. 2. The features are specifically designed for the LSTM model, and are not generalizable to other architectures. weaknesses: The model is specifically designed for the LSTM model, and is not generalizable to other architectures. Overall, the paper presents an effective and highly performant approach for end-to-end relation extraction, and provides future directions of research on generalizing the features to other architectures.

- Strengths: This paper presents a 2 x 2 x 3 x 10 array of accuracy results based on systematically changing the parameters of embeddings models: (context type, position sensitive, embedding model, task), accuracy - context type ∈ {Linear, Syntactic} - position sensitive ∈ {True, False} - embedding model ∈ {Skip Gram, BOW, GLOVE} - task ∈ {Word Similarity, Analogies, POS, NER, Chunking, 5 text classific. tasks} The aim of these experiments was to investigate the variation in performance as these parameters are changed. The goal of the study itself is interesting for the ACL community and similar papers have appeared before as workshop papers and have been well cited, such as Nayak et al.'s paper mentioned below. - Weaknesses: Since this paper essentially presents the effect of systematically changing the context types and position sensitivity, I will focus on the execution of the investigation and the analysis of the results, which I am afraid is not satisfactory. A) The lack of hyper-parameter tuning is worrisome. E.g. - 395 Unless otherwise notes, the number of word embedding dimension is set to 500. - 232 It still enlarges the context vocabulary about 5 times in practice. - 385 Most hyper-parameters are the same as Levy et al' best configuration. This is worrisome because lack of hyperparameter tuning makes it difficult to make statements like method A is better than method B. E.g. bound methods may perform better with a lower dimensionality than unbound models, since their effective context vocabulary size is larger. B) The paper sometimes presents strange explanations for its results. E.g. - 115 "Experimental results suggest that although it's hard to find any universal insight, the characteristics of different contexts on different models are concluded according to specific tasks." What does this sentence even mean? - 580 Sequence labeling tasks tend to classify words with the same syntax to the same category. The ignorance of syntax for word embeddings which are learned by bound representation becomes beneficial. These two sentences are contradictory, if a sequence labeling task classified words with "same syntax" to same category then syntx becomes a ver valuable feature. Bound representation's ignorance of syntax should cause a drop in performance just like other tasks which does not happen. C) It is not enough to merely mention Lai et. al. 2016 who have also done a systematic study of the word embeddings, and similarly the paper "Evaluating Word Embeddings Using a Representative Suite of Practical Tasks", Nayak, Angeli, Manning. appeared at the repeval workshop at ACL 2016. should have been cited. I understand that the focus of Nayak et al's paper is not exactly the same as this paper, however they provide recommendations about hyperparameter tuning and experiment design and even provide a web interface for automatically running tagging experiments using neural networks instead of the "simple linear classifiers" used in the current paper. D) The paper uses a neural BOW words classifier for the text classification tasks but a simple linear classifier for the sequence labeling tasks. What is the justification for this choice of classifiers? Why not use a simple neural classifier for the tagging tasks as well? I raise this point, since the tagging task seems to be the only task where bound representations are consistently beating the unbound representations, which makes this task the odd one out. - General Discussion: Finally, I will make one speculative suggestion to the authors regarding the analysis of the data. As I said earlier, this paper's main contribution is an analysis of the following table. (context type, position sensitive, embedding model, task, accuracy) So essentially there are 120 accuracy values that we want to explain in terms of the aspects of the model. It may be beneficial to perform factor analysis or some other pattern mining technique on this 120 sample data. - Strengths: Evaluating bag of words and "bound" contexts from either dependencies or sentence ordering is important, and will be a useful reference to the community. The experiments were relatively thorough (though some choices could use further justification), and the authors used downstream tasks instead of just intrinsic evaluations. - Weaknesses: The authors change the objective function of GBOW from p(c|\sum w_i) to p(w|\sum c_i). This is somewhat justified as dependency-based context with a bound representation only has one word available for predicting the context, but it's unclear exactly why that is the case and deserves more discussion. Presumably the non-dependency context with a bound representation would also suffer from this drawback? If so, how did Ling et al., 2015 do it? Unfortunately, the authors don't compare any results against the original objective, which is a definite weakness. In addition, the authors change GSG to match GBOW, again without comparing to the original objective. Adding results from word vectors trained using the original GBOW and GSG objective functions would justify these changes (assuming the results don't show large changes). The hyperparameter settings should be discussed further. This played a large role in Levy et al. (2015), so you should consider trying different hyperparameter values. These depend pretty heavily on the task, so simply taking good values from another task may not work well. In addition, the authors are unclear on exactly what model is trained in section 3.4. They say only that it is a "simple linear classifier". In section 3.5, they use logistic regression with the average of the word vectors as input, but call it a Neural Bag-of-Words model. Technically previous work also used this name, but I find it misleading, since it's just logistic regression (and hence a linear model, which is not something I would call "Neural"). It is important to know if the model trained in section 3.4 is the same as the model trained in 3.5, so we know if the different conclusions are the result of the task or the model changing. - General Discussion: This paper evaluates context taken from dependency parses vs context taken from word position in a given sentence, and bag-of-words vs tokens with relative position indicators. This paper is useful to the community, as they show when and where researchers should use word vectors trained using these different decisions. - Emphasis to improve: The main takeaway from this paper that future researchers will use is given at the end of 3.4 and 3.5, but really should be summarized at the start of the paper. Specifically, the authors should put in the abstract that for POS, chunking, and NER, bound representations outperform bag-of-words representations, and that dependency contexts work better than linear contexts in most cases. In addition, for a simple text classification model, bound representations perform worse than bag-of-words representations, and there seemed to be no major difference between the different models or context types. - Small points of improvement: Should call "unbounded" context "bag of words". This may lead to some confusion as one of the techniques you use is Generalized Bag-Of-Words, but this can be clarified easily. 043: it's the "distributional hypothesis", not the "Distributed Hypothesis". 069: citations should have a comma instead of semicolon separating them. 074: "DEPS" should be capitalized consistently throughout the paper (usually it appears as "Deps"). Also should be introduced as something like dependency parse tree context (Deps). 085: typo: "How different contexts affect model's performances..." Should have the word "do". - Strengths: This paper systematically investigated how context types (linear vs dependency-based) and representations (bound word vs unbound word) affect word embedding learning. They experimented with three models (Generalized Bag-Of-Words, Generalized Skip-Gram and Glove) in multiple different tasks (word similarity, word analogy, sequence labeling and text classification). Overall, 1) It is well-written and structured. 2) The experiments are very thoroughly evaluated. The analysis could help researchers to choose different word embeddings or might even motivate new models. 3) The attached software can also benefit the community. - Weaknesses: The novelty is limited. - General Discussion: For the dependency-based context types, how does the dependency parsing affect the overall performance? Is it fair to compare those two different context types since the dependency-based one has to rely on the predicted dependency parsing results (in this case CoreNLP) while the linear one does not?

This paper presents a performance model for deep neural networks that incorporates the concept of neurons as simple processing units. The model is based on the observation that neurons are relatively simple processing units that can be characterized by a set of parameters. The authors show that this model can be used to predict the performance of deep neural networks, and that it is particularly useful for understanding the trade-offs involved in designing networks. The paper is well-written and easy to follow. The authors present their model in a clear and concise manner, and they provide a thorough analysis of its performance. The results are convincing, and the paper provides a valuable contribution to the field of deep learning. This paper addresses the problem of training deep neural networks with large amounts of data. The authors propose a performance model for deep neural networks that allows for efficient training with large datasets. Strengths: The paper provides a thorough and well-reasoned argument for the proposed performance model. The results are convincing and show that the model is effective for training deep neural networks with large datasets. Weaknesses: There are a few minor weaknesses in the paper. First, the paper does not provide a clear definition of what is meant by "large datasets". Second, the paper does not provide any information about the specific architecture of the deep neural network that was used in the experiments. This makes it difficult to evaluate the results and compare them to other papers. Finally, the paper does not provide any information about the preprocessing of the data that was used in the experiments. This makes it difficult to reproduce the results. The paper presents a performance model for deep neural networks that can efficiently and accurately model the expected scalability and performance of a putative deep learning system. The key observation is that a neural network architecture carries with it a declarative specification of the computational requirements associated with its training and evaluation, which can be extracted and mapped to a specific point within the design space of software, hardware, and communication strategies. The paper is well-written and easy to follow. The results are well-supported by figures and tables, and the experimental setup is well-described. The paper presents a useful tool for analyzing the scalability of deep learning systems, and it is an important contribution to the field. This paper addresses the problem of how to best leverage parallel and distributed computing infrastructure to accelerate the training and deployment of deep neural networks. The paper's main strength is that it introduces an analytical performance model called Paleo, which is shown to be robust to the choice of network architecture, hardware, software, communication schemes, and parallelization strategies. This allows for the efficient and accurate modeling of the expected scalability and performance of a putative deep learning system. However, the paper does have some weaknesses. First, it is unclear if the authors have compared the performance of Paleo to other existing scalable deep learning software packages. Second, the authors do not provide any results comparing the performance of Paleo to other existing scalable deep learning software packages. Finally, the authors do not provide any information on the running time of Paleo or the memory requirements of Paleo.

This paper presents an approach to tag word senses with temporal information (past, present, future or atemporal). They model the problem using a graph-based semi-supervised classification algorithm that allows to combine item specific information - such as the presence of some temporal indicators in the glosses - and the structure of Wordnet - that is semantic relations between synsets â, and to take into account unlabeled data. They perform a full annotation of Wordnet, based on a set of training data labeled in a previous work and using the rest of Wordnet as unlabeled data. Specifically, they take advantage of the structure of the label set by breaking the task into a binary formulation (temporal vs atemporal), then using the data labeled as temporal to perform a finer grained tagging (past, present or future). In order to intrinsically evaluate their approach, they annotate a subset of synsets in Wordnet using crowd-sourcing. They compare their system to the results obtained by a state-of-the-art time tagger (Stanford's SUTime) using an heuristic as a backup strategy, and to previous works. They obtain improvements around 11% in accuracy, and show that their approach allows performance higher than previous systems using only 400 labeled data. Finally, they perform an evaluation of their resource on an existing task (TempEval-3) and show improvements of about 10% in F1 on 4 labels. This paper is well-constructed and generally clear, the approach seems sound and well justified. This work led to the development of a resource with fine grained temporal information at the word sense level that would be made available and could be used to improve various NLP tasks. I have a few remarks, especially concerning the settings of the experiments. I think that more information should be given on the task performed in the extrinsic evaluation section. An example could be useful to understand what the system is trying to predict (the features describe âentity pairsâ but it has not been made clear before what are these pairs) and what are the features (especially, what are the entity attributes? What is the POS for a pair, is it one dimension or two? Are the lemmas obtained automatically?). The sentence describing the labels used is confusing, I'm not sure to understand what âevent to document creation timeâ and âevent to same sentence eventâ means, are they the kind of pairs considered? Are they relations (as they are described as relation at the beginning of p.8)? I find unclear the footnote about the 14 relations: why the other relations have to be ignored, what makes a mapping too âcomplexâ? Also, are the scores macro or micro averaged? Finally, the ablation study seems to indicate a possible redundancy between Lexica and Entity with quite close scores, any clue about this behavior? I have also some questions about the use of the SVM. For the extrinsic evaluation, the authors say that they optimized the parameters of the algorithm: what are these parameters? And since a SVM is also used within the MinCut framework, is it optimized and how? Finally, if it's the LibSVM library that is used (Weka wrapper), I think a reference to LibSVM should be included. Other remarks: - It would be interesting to have the number of examples per label in the gold data, the figures are given for coarse grained labels (127 temporal vs 271 atemporal), but not for the finer grained. - It would also be nice to have an idea of the number of words that are ambiguous at the temporal level, words like âpresentâ. - It is said in the caption of the table 3 that the results presented are âsignificantly betterâ but no significancy test is indicated, neither any p-value. Minor remarks: - Related work: what kind of task was performed in (Filannino and Nenadic, 2014)? - Related work: ârequires a post-calibration procedureâ, needs a reference (and p.4 in 3.3 footnote it would be clearer to explain calibration) - Related work: âtheir model differ from oursâ, in what? - Table 3 is really too small: maybe, remove the parenthesis, put the â(p,r,f1)â in the caption and give only two scores, e.g. prec and f1. The caption should also be reduced. - Information in table 4 would be better represented using a graph. - Beginning of p.7: 1064 â 1264 - TempEval-3: reference ? - table 6: would be made clearer by ordering the scores for one column - p.5, paragraph 3: atemporal) â atemporal

Overall, I find this a useful paper, although I have some minor comments. 1. It would be useful to provide a bit more context as to why the exact syntax of the paper is used, and whether this is an acceptable way to go about solving the problem. For example, one possible alternative approach that I think might be worth exploring is using an explicit side-constraints to encode the desired translation relationship (i.e., A <-> B). 2. It's not entirely clear to me what the algorithm does at each step, as the paper just has an overall summary and refers the reader to the code for more details. I'd recommend providing a more detailed explanation for each iteration, as well as the stopping criterion for each iteration. 3. The first section of the code (Line 1) should probably be indented further so that it's aligned with the subsequent for loop. 4. I don't think it's necessary to include a reference to Moses in the code. The code implements an standard Moses setup, so the language is clear. 5. It would be helpful to include examples of inputs and outputs to illustrate the approach (the paper references a similar image retrieval example, but doesn't provide any actual input or output). 6. It would be useful to include an example of the translated output for the first sentence of the paper (i.e., "The quick brown fox jumped over the lazy dog"). 7. The objective function is to minimize the distance between the generated word sequence and the original word sequence, but this seems like it would encourage the algorithm to generate exactly the same words in each iteration (i.e., without any rhyme or reason). I think this would lead to worse translations than simply picking a single word at random from the vocabulary. 8. I don't think it's necessary to include the units for each variable (i.e., x, y, z). 9. It would be useful to include more detailed hyperparameter values for the English-to-German translation (e.g., the number of iterations, the size of the update window). 10. The parameters used in the code (e.g., d1, k, q) should probably be defined at the top of the code (e.g., d1 = 1, k = 2, q = 0.5) 11. It would be nice to generate a few representative outputs from the code and present them here (e.g., a sample translation output for "The quick brown fox jumped over the lazy dog" using the first sentence in the article as a reference). In this work, the authors address the problem of how to efficiently refine machine translation (MT) systems by comparing a parallel source-language (SL) sentence to a target-language (TL) candidate translation. A straightforward implementation of this approach would first BLEU score-like rank the translations and then sequentially select the bestK candidates and perform small additional translations on the selected sentence pairs. The drawback of this approach is that it only allows for relatively cheap modifications to the existing systems without jeopardizing the achieved BLEU score. To allow for more extensive modifications, the authors propose to perform curriculum learning in which the BLEU rank of the candidates is slowly increased. In addition to the selection-based refinement approach, the proposed curriculum learning method also allows the systems to learn from their mistakes by way of an additional mutation strategy. The authors conducted empirical studies comparing the proposed refinement approaches to a fixed checkpoint version of the system as well as the beam search baseline. In terms of objective measures, both approaches achieve similar BLEU scores but the curriculum learning-based approach is up to 20% faster without sacrificing much in terms of BLEU. playback of paper Problem: How to efficiently refine machine translation (MT) systems by comparing a parallel source-language (SL) sentence to a target-language (TL) candidate translation Solution: The authors address the problem of how to efficiently refine machine translation (MT) systems by comparing a parallel source-language (SL) sentence to a target-language (TL) candidate translation. A straightforward implementation of this approach would first BLEU score-like rank the translations and then sequentially select the bestK candidates and perform small additional translations on the selected sentence pairs. The drawback of this approach is that it only allows for relatively cheap modifications to the existing systems without jeopardizing the achieved BLEU score. To allow for more extensive modifications, the authors propose to perform curriculum learning in which the BLEU rank of the candidates is slowly increased. In addition to the selection-based refinement approach, the proposed curriculum learning method also allows the systems to learn from their mistakes, both in terms of achieving higher BLEU scores and faster without sacrificing much in terms of BLEU. In general, the paper is very nice. The topic discussed in the paper is very interesting and has a very wide application prospect. The article presents a feasible and effective improvement for the current state-of-the-art phrase-based translation system. The major contributions of the paper are summarized as below: 1. The paper uses a translation model based on a neural network, which reduces the language barrier. Because the model uses a neural network to replace the traditional probability distribution function to calculate the translation probability, the model learns the translation rules directly from the data. 2. The paper adopts an iterative optimization strategy, which can maintain the earlier decisions and make new decisions based on the feedback from the earlier decisions. This means that the decisions will not be made in a strictly monotonic fashion, and previous mistakes can be used to correct future mistakes, which can improve the output translation results. 3. The paper also demonstrates a new way to verify whether the modification made by the model is reasonable or not. The model adopts a neural network to generate substitutions, which can be evaluated with an attention-based mechanism. The substitution is considered reasonable if it changes the translated output. The paper includes the model to analyze the proposed model and prove that the substitution generated by the model is reasonable. Generally, the paper has a very clear description of the content and has a good overall structure. However, the paper lacks of the experimental result and discussion of the results. I believe that the paper will be improved by including the result and discussion. I have read this paper with enthusiasm. I believe that the model proposed here can be a new approach for other translation tasks. In this paper, we present a translation scheme that starts from an initial guess and then makes iterative improvements that may revisit previous decisions. This is an interesting topic that merits further exploration. The proposed method is evaluated on WMT14 English-German translation, and the improvement is up to 0.4 BLEU, which is probably because WMT14 and WMT15 languages are similar and have a lot of translated phrases between them. In the future, we could try it on different pairs of languages and see if we can get better improvements. The method proposed in this paper may bring risks of increased variance, and it is more suitable for low-resource scenarios. We plan to do more experiments on different languages and datasets to confirm its effectiveness in the future. éƒÆ’ people think that this is a good start for exploring this topic. CONTRIBUTIONS When training LSTMs, many of the intermediate gradients are close to zero due to the flat shape of the tanh and sigmoid nonlinearities far from the origin. This paper shows that rounding these small gradients to zero results in matrices with up to 80% sparsity during training, and that training character-level LSTM language models with this sparsification does not significantly change the final performance of the model. The authors argue that this sparsity could be exploited with specialized hardware to improve the energy efficiency and speed of recurrent network training. NOVELTY Thresholding gradients to induce sparsity and improve efficiency in RNN training is a novel result to my knowledge. MISSING CITATIONS Prior work has explored low-precision arithmetic for recurrent neural network language models: Hubara et al, “Quantized Neural Networks: Training Neural Networks with Low Precision Weights and Activations”, The main point of the paper was that sparsifying gradients does not hurt performance; however, this in itself is not enough for a publication in this venue. As noted by R1 and R2, showing how this can help in more energy efficient training would make for a good paper; without that aspect the paper only presents an observation that is not too surprising to the practitioners in this area. Further, while the main point of the paper was relatively clear, the scientific presentation was not rigorous enough. All the reviewers point out that several details were missing (including test set performance, reporting of results on the different sets, etc). Paper would be strengthened by a better exploration of the problem. The findings of applying sparsity in the backward gradients for training LSTMs is interesting. But the paper seems incomplete without the proper experimental justification. Only the validation loss is reported which is definitely insufficient. Proper testing results and commonly reported evaluation criterion needs to be included to support the claim of no degradation when applying the proposed sparsity technique. Also actual justification of the gains in terms of speed and efficiency would make the paper much stronger. This paper presents the observation that it is possible to utilize sparse operations in the training of LSTM networks without loss of accuracy. This observation is novel (although not too surprising) to my knowledge, but I must state that I am not very familiar with research on fast RNN implmentations. Minor note: The LSTM language model does not use a 'word2vec' layer. It is simply a linear embedding layer. Word2vec is the name of a specific model which is not directly to character level language models. The paper presents the central observation clearly. However, it will be much more convincing if a well known dataset and experiment set up are used, such as Graves (2013) or Sutskever et al (2014), and actual training, validation and test performances are reported. While the main observation is certainly interesting, I think it is not sufficient to be the subject of a full conference paper without implementation (or simulation) and benchmarking of the promised speedups on multiple tasks. For example, how would the gains be affected by various architecture choices? At present this is an interesting technical report and I would like to see more detailed results in the future.
