Agreed, that the used heuristics are not data dependent, but still, it feels like they are kicking the can down the road as far as heuristics are concerned. 4. The experimental results are not very convincing. First there is no speed comparison. Second, the authors do not compare with methods other than k-mips which do fast nearest neighbor search, such as, FLANN. I find this paper not very compelling. The basic idea seems to be that we can put a fast neighbor searcher into a memory augmented net to make the memory lookups scalable. However, this was precisely the point of Rae et al. There are a number of standardized neighbor searchers; I don't understand why the authors choose to use their own (which they do not benchmark against the standards). Moreover, they test on a problem where there is no clear need for (vector based) fast-nn, because one can use hashing on the text. I also find the repeated distinction between "mips" and "nns" distracting; most libraries that can do one can do the other, or inputs can be modified to switch between the problems; indeed the authors do this when they convert to the "mcss" problem.

Overall, I find the paper "Volatility Prediction using Financial Disclosures Sentiments with Word Embedding-based IR Models" to be a well-written and informative study on the use of natural language processing techniques in predicting stock market volatility using financial disclosures data. One of the main strengths of the paper is its use of pre-trained word embeddings to extract relevant information from financial disclosures. The authors make a compelling case for the effectiveness of this approach and provide clear explanations of the methods used to generate the embeddings. Additionally, the use of sentiment analysis to classify certain phrases as either positive or negative is a logical extension of this approach and is likely to be useful in predicting future stock market trends. Another strength of the paper is the authors' focus on the practical applications of their research. They provide numerous examples of how their methods could be used in a real-world setting, which greatly enhances the paper's relevance to the broader financial community. Additionally, the authors are careful to acknowledge the limitations of their research, which is an important aspect of any study. One potential weakness of the paper is the lack of detail provided on the experimental setup. While the authors describe the dataset used in their study, they do not provide much information on the specific settings used for training and evaluating their models. This makes it difficult to replicate their results and may limit the usefulness of their research for other researchers in the field. Overall, I believe that "Volatility Prediction using Financial Disclosures Sentiments with Word Embedding-based IR Models" is a valuable contribution to the field of natural language processing in finance. The paper's use of pre-trained word embeddings and sentiment analysis is well-justified and is likely to be useful in predicting future stock market trends. However, additional information on the experimental setup would make this research even more valuable to others in the field. The paper “Volatility Prediction using Financial Disclosures Sentiments with Word Embedding-based IR Models” addresses the issue of predicting stock market volatility using sentiment analysis of financial disclosures. The paper proposes to utilize word embedding-based information retrieval (IR) models to extract sentiment from financial documents and use it to predict the volatility of stock prices. Strengths: - The paper addresses a significant problem in the financial industry that has attracted the attention of both academic researchers and practitioners. - The proposed approach employs cutting-edge techniques such as word embeddings and IR models that have been shown to improve the accuracy of sentiment analysis tasks. - The paper provides a comprehensive literature review, giving readers a good understanding of the state-of-the-art research in the field. - The experimental results achieved by the proposed approach demonstrate promising levels of accuracy for predicting stock price volatility. Weaknesses: - The paper lacks a clear explanation of how the proposed approach improves upon existing sentiment analysis methods for predicting stock market volatility. - The paper does not provide a detailed description of the datasets used, including the number and types of financial documents used to train and test the model. - The paper could benefit from discussing the limitations of the proposed approach and how the model could be further improved in future work. Overall, the paper provides a valuable contribution to the field of financial sentiment analysis and volatility prediction using word embeddings and IR models. However, readers would benefit from more detailed explanations of the proposed approach and datasets used, as well as a discussion of the limitations of the model. Peer Review The paper titled "Volatility Prediction using Financial Disclosures Sentiments with Word Embedding-based IR Models" proposes a novel approach for volatility prediction in financial markets. The paper introduces the use of text sentiment analysis methods to forecast the volatility of the stock market. The paper also investigates the use of recent information retrieval (IR) term weighting models that are extended using word embeddings. The paper explores the fusion of text and market data resources and evaluates the proposed approach against state-of-the-art methods. The paper is well-organized and clearly presents the research questions, methods, results and discussions. The study is well-motivated and provides valuable insights into the prediction of market volatility using sentiment analysis techniques. The study is also relevant to practitioners in the financial industry and academia. The paper presents a thorough literature review of existing methods in predicting market volatility using textual and market data resources. The authors demonstrate their innovative approach of combining sentiment analysis techniques with word embedding-based IR models to effectively outperform existing methods. The paper provides detailed analysis and discussion of the experimental results and the limitations of the proposed approach. The authors also provide insights into the characteristics of financial reports of different sectors. The experimental design and evaluation of the proposed approach are sound, and the results are statistically significant. The paper could be further strengthened by providing more detailed explanations of the technical aspects of the proposed approach, including the word embeddings and the IR models. Additionally, the authors could also provide more details on the limitations of their approach, including the size of the training data and the generalizability of the model to different financial markets. Overall, the paper is a valuable contribution to the field of predicting market volatility. The authors effectively demonstrate the promising results of combining sentiment analysis techniques with word embedding-based IR models to achieve higher accuracy in predicting market volatility. I recommend the paper to be accepted for publication, subject to minor revisions. The paper titled "Volatility Prediction using Financial Disclosures Sentiments with Word Embedding-based IR Models" aims to explore the effectiveness of using sentiment analysis methods on annual financial disclosures of companies in stock markets for the purposes of forecasting volatility, through the use of recent Information Retrieval (IR) term weighting models, effectively extended by related terms using word embeddings. The paper also aims to investigate different fusion methods combining both textual information and factual market data for volatility prediction. Strengths: The research addresses a significant problem in financial markets, specifically in relation to volatility prediction. The use of sentiment analysis to forecast market risk is a relatively novel approach, and the paper contributes to this field by exploring the use of recent Information Retrieval (IR) term weighting models and word embeddings for improved prediction accuracy. The paper closely examines different fusion methods to combine textual and market data resources, providing a comprehensive analysis of the proposed approach. The findings demonstrate that the word embedding-based approach significantly outperforms state-of-the-art methods, making a significant contribution to the domain. Weaknesses: Whilst the paper provides a unique approach to addressing the problem, it would benefit from greater clarity of the research methodology section. Specifically, the paper could provide further detail on the selection of companies and the data sets used for the analysis. Additionally, while the paper provides an in-depth analysis of the proposed approach, the findings could be strengthened with the inclusion of more tests and robustness checks. Overall, the paper demonstrates great potential in the domain of volatility prediction in financial markets, specifically when applied to annual financial disclosures of companies. The strengths of the research lie in its originality and approach to the problem, demonstrating significant potential impact. However, further work is required to ensure that the research methodology is fully transparent and that the findings are strengthened through additional robustness checks.

- Strengths: [+] Well motivated, tackles an interesting problem; [+] Clearly written and structured, accompanied by documented code and dataset; [+] Encouraging results. - Weaknesses: [-] Limited to completely deterministic, hand-engineered minimization rules; [-] Some relevant literature on OIE neglected; [-] Sound but not thorough experimental evaluation. - General Discussion: This paper tackles a practical issue of most OIE systems, i.e. redundant, uninformative and inaccurate extractions. The proposed approach, dubbed MinOIE, is designed to actually "minimize" extractions by removing overly specific portions and turning them into structured annotations of various types (similarly to OLLIE). The authors put MinIE on top of a state-of-the-art OIE system (ClausIE) and test it on two publicly available datasets, showing that it effectively leads to more concise extractions compared to standard OIE approaches, while at the same time retaining accuracy. Overall, this work focuses on an interesting (and perhaps underinvestigated) aspect of OIE in a sound and principled way. The paper is clearly written, sufficiently detailed, and accompanied by supplementary material and a neat Java implementation. My main concern is, however, with the entirely static, deterministic and rule-based structure of MinIE. Even though I understand that a handful of manually engineered rules is technically the best strategy when precision is key, these approaches are typically very hard to scale, e.g. in terms of languages (a recent trend of OIE, see Faruqui and Kumar, 2015; Falke et al., 2016). In other words, I think that this contribution somehow falls short of novelty and substance in proposing a pipeline of engineered rules that are mostly inspired by other OIE systems (such as ClausIE or ReVerb); for instance, I would have really appreciated an attempt to learn these minimization rules instead of hard-coding them. Furthermore, the authors completely ignore a recent research thread on “semantically-informed” OIE (Nakashole et al., 2012; Moro and Navigli, 2012; 2013; Delli Bovi et al., 2015) where traditional extractions are augmented with links to underlying knowledge bases and sense inventories (Wikipedia, Wikidata, Yago, BabelNet). These contributions are not only relevant in terms of related literature: in fact, having text fragments (or constituents) explicitly linked to a knowledge base would reduce the need for ad-hoc minimization rules such as those in Sections 6.1 and 6.2. In the example with "Bill of Rights" provided by the authors (line 554), an OIE pipeline with a proper Entity Linking module would recognize automatically the phrase as mention of a registered entity, regardless of the shape of its subconstituents. Also, an underlying sense inventory would seamlessly incorporate the external information about collocations and multi-word expressions used in Section 6.2: not by chance, the authors rely on WordNet and Wiktionary to compile their dictionary of collocations. Finally, some remarks on the experimental evaluation: - Despite the claim of generality of MinIE, the authors choose to experiment only with ClausIE as underlying OIE system (most likely the optimal match). It would have been very interesting to see if the improvement brought by MinIE is consistent also with other OIE systems, in order to actually assess its flexibility as a post-processing tool. - Among the test datasets used in Section 7, I would have included the recent OIE benchmark of Stanovsky and Dagan (2016), where results are reported also for comparison systems not included in this paper (TextRunner, WOIE, KrakeN). References: - Manaal Faruqui and Shankar Kumar. Multilingual Open Relation Extraction using Cross-lingual Projection. NAACL-HLT, 2015. - Tobias Falke, Gabriel Stanovsky, Iryna Gurevych and Ido Dagan. Porting an Open Information Extraction System from English to German. EMNLP 2016. - Ndapandula Nakashole, Gerhard Weikum and Fabian Suchanek. PATTY: A Taxonomy of Relational Patterns with Semantic Types. EMNLP 2012. - Andrea Moro, Roberto Navigli. WiSeNet: Building a Wikipedia-based Semantic Network with Ontologized Relations. CIKM 2012. - Andrea Moro, Roberto Navigli. Integrating Syntactic and Semantic Analysis into the Open Information Extraction Paradigm. IJCAI 2013. - Claudio Delli Bovi, Luca Telesca and Roberto Navigli. Large-Scale Information Extraction from Textual Definitions through Deep Syntactic and Semantic Analysis. TACL vol. 3, 2015. - Gabriel Stanovsky and Ido Dagan. Creating a Large Benchmark for Open Information Extraction. EMNLP 2016.

This paper presents a novel neural word segmentation model with rich pretraining. The model combines both subword information and contextual information to achieve improved performance. The authors propose a new pretraining method that incorporates both unsupervised and supervised learning methods. The model is tested on a variety of datasets and compared to other existing methods. The paper is well written and easy to follow. The authors provide a clear description of the proposed model and its components. The experiments are well designed and the results are clearly presented. The authors also provide a detailed analysis of the results and compare their model to other existing methods. The paper makes a valuable contribution to the field of neural word segmentation. The proposed model is able to achieve improved performance by combining both subword and contextual information. The authors have also proposed a novel pretraining method which incorporates both unsupervised and supervised learning methods. Overall, this is an excellent paper which makes an important contribution to the field of neural word segmentation. The authors have provided a detailed description of the proposed model and its components. The experiments are well designed and the results are clearly presented. The paper is well written and easy to follow. This paper addresses the problem of neural word segmentation, which is the task of segmenting a sentence into individual words. The authors propose a new approach to this task, which uses rich pretraining to improve the performance of the model. The paper has several strengths. First, the authors provide a thorough overview of the existing literature on neural word segmentation, including a discussion of the various approaches that have been proposed and their relative strengths and weaknesses. Second, the proposed approach is well-explained and clearly described, with a detailed description of the architecture and training process. Finally, the authors provide an extensive evaluation of their model, demonstrating that it significantly outperforms existing approaches on multiple datasets. The paper also has some weaknesses. First, the authors do not discuss the potential for overfitting when using the proposed approach. Second, the authors do not provide any analysis of the model's performance on out-of-domain data, which would be helpful for assessing the generalizability of the model. Finally, the authors do not compare the proposed approach to other recently developed models for neural word segmentation, which would provide further evidence of the effectiveness of the proposed approach. This paper presents a novel approach to neural word segmentation that leverages a range of external training sources. The authors propose a modular segmentation model and pretrain its most important submodule using rich external sources, such as punctuation, automatic segmentation, and POS. The results show that this approach significantly improves the model, leading to competitive accuracies on six benchmarks. The paper is well-written and the research is well-structured. The authors present a clear problem statement and provide a detailed description of their proposed approach. The experiments are thorough and the results are well-supported. The authors also provide an in-depth discussion of the results. The approach proposed in this paper is novel and has the potential to be applied to other related tasks. However, the authors should consider incorporating more external sources in the pretraining process to further improve the model's performance. Additionally, the authors should consider exploring other types of neural networks, such as convolutional neural networks, to see if they can achieve better results. Overall, this is a well-written and well-structured paper. The authors have clearly presented their approach and their results. The paper is well-supported and the authors have provided a thorough discussion of their findings. This paper addresses the question of how to effectively leverage external sources of information for neural word segmentation. The authors propose a modular segmentation model and demonstrate that pretraining the most important submodule with rich external sources can significantly improve the model, leading to competitive accuracies on six benchmarks. The strengths of this paper include the modular segmentation model proposed, which allows for the incorporation of external sources of information. The authors also provide a thorough evaluation of the model, demonstrating its effectiveness on six benchmarks. The weaknesses of this paper include the lack of detail regarding the exact sources of external information used for pretraining. Additionally, the authors do not compare their model to any other non-neural models, so it is difficult to gauge the effectiveness of their approach relative to existing methods.

This paper presents a novel approach to computing gradients of counterfactuals. The authors propose a new framework for computing gradients of counterfactuals based on the concept of "causal inference". The paper is well-structured and clearly explains the proposed approach. The authors provide a detailed description of the proposed framework and its components. The paper also includes a thorough evaluation of the proposed approach using both simulated and real-world datasets. The results demonstrate the effectiveness of the proposed approach. Overall, this is an interesting and well-written paper. The authors provide a thorough description of the proposed approach and its components and demonstrate its effectiveness through a comprehensive evaluation. The paper is clearly structured and easy to follow. The proposed approach is novel and provides a useful tool for computing gradients of counterfactuals. This paper addresses the problem of understanding the gradients of counterfactuals. Specifically, it examines how counterfactuals can be used to obtain gradients of functions with respect to their inputs. The paper presents a novel approach to this problem by introducing a new method for computing gradients of counterfactuals. Strengths: The paper presents a novel approach to computing gradients of counterfactuals that is both theoretically sound and computationally efficient. The authors provide a detailed analysis of the proposed method and demonstrate its effectiveness with a series of experiments. Weaknesses: The paper does not address the issue of scalability of the proposed method. In addition, the paper does not provide a comparison to existing methods for computing gradients of counterfactuals. This paper presents a novel approach to quantify feature importance in deep learning models. The authors propose to examine interior gradients, which are gradients of counterfactual inputs constructed by scaling down the original input. They demonstrate the effectiveness of this approach by applying it to the GoogleNet architecture for object recognition in images, as well as a ligand-based virtual screening network with categorical features and an LSTM based language model for the Penn Treebank dataset. The authors show that interior gradients better capture feature importance than traditional gradients, and that they have the attribution property that the feature importance scores sum to the the prediction score. The paper is well-written and clearly explains the proposed approach. The authors provide an extensive literature review, and they present convincing experimental results that demonstrate the effectiveness of their method. The proposed approach is also highly practical, since it can be computed just as easily as gradients. Overall, this paper presents a novel and useful approach for quantifying feature importance in deep learning models that is both effective and practical. This paper addresses the problem of accurately quantifying feature importance in deep learning models. The authors propose a new method, interior gradients, which is constructed by scaling down the original input. The authors then apply this method to a variety of deep networks, including GoogleNet, a ligand-based virtual screening network, and an LSTM based language model for the Penn Treebank dataset. The strengths of this paper are that the authors provide a clear and concise explanation of their proposed method, interior gradients, and demonstrate its efficacy on a variety of deep networks. Furthermore, the authors provide an intuitive visualization of how interior gradients better capture feature importance. Additionally, the authors note that interior gradients have the desirable attribution property that the feature importance scores sum to the the prediction score. The weaknesses of this paper are that the authors do not provide a rigorous mathematical analysis of the proposed method, and the results are limited to the datasets used in the experiments. Additionally, the authors do not provide a thorough comparison of the proposed method with existing methods for quantifying feature importance.

This paper presents a corpus of annotated essay revisions. It includes two examples of application for the corpus: 1) Student Revision Behavior Analysis and 2) Automatic Revision Identification The latter is essentially a text classification task using an SVM classifier and a variety of features. The authors state that the corpus will be freely available for research purposes. The paper is well-written and clear. A detailed annotation scheme was used by two annotators to annotate the corpus which added value to it. I believe the resource might be interesting to researcher working on writing process research and related topics. I also liked that you provided two very clear usage scenarios for the corpus. I have two major criticisms. The first could be easily corrected in case the paper is accepted, but the second requires more work. 1) There are no statistics about the corpus in this paper. This is absolutely paramount. When you describe a corpus, there are some information that should be there. I am talking about number of documents (I assume the corpus has 180 documents (60 essays x 3 drafts), is that correct?), number of tokens (around 400 words each essay?), number of sentences, etc. I assume we are talking about 60 unique essays x 400 words, so about 24,000 words in total. Is that correct? If we take the 3 drafts we end up with about 72,000 words but probably with substantial overlap between drafts. A table with this information should be included in the paper. 2) If the aforementioned figures are correct, we are talking about a very small corpus. I understand the difficulty of producing hand-annotated data, and I think this is one of the strengths of your work, but I am not sure about how helpful this resource is for the NLP community as a whole. Perhaps such a resource would be better presented in a specialised workshop such as BEA or a specialised conference on language resources like LREC instead of a general NLP conference like ACL. You mentioned in the last paragraph that you would like to augment the corpus with more annotation. Are you also willing to include more essays? Comments/Minor: - As you have essays by native and non-native speakers, one further potential application of this corpus is native language identification (NLI). - p. 7: "where the unigram feature was used as the baseline" - "word unigram". Be more specific. - p. 7: "and the SVM classifier was used as the classifier." - redundant.

The authors present an interesting approach to learning visual servo control using deep features, fitted q iteration (FQI) and reinforcement learning. The results are promising but there is room for improvement in several areas. First of all I think it would be useful if they could provide more details on how their method works compared to other methods that use deep feature representations such as DAgger or VPGD. It seems like these approaches have been around longer than this one so it's important to understand what makes your work unique. Second, you should try out different architectures for the neural network used by the agent during training. You can also experiment with adding additional layers after the final layer has already been trained. Finally, make sure you're not overfitting when tuning hyperparameters The main contribution of this work is to propose an end-to-end learning method for visual servoing using deep features extracted from convolutional neural networks (CNNs). The proposed approach learns both control policy parameters as well as CNN weights in one integrated framework. In particular, we use fitted-Q iteration algorithm that iteratively optimizes action-value function approximated via deep feature representation learned through supervised training on large amount of data collected during robot experiments. This article presents experimental results showing how the proposed method can be used to successfully perform various tasks such as tracking moving objects under different lighting conditions while avoiding obstacles. Weaknesses: 1) It does not provide any analysis about why it works better than other methods like DQN. 2) No comparison between its performance against state-of-the-art algorithms The authors present a novel method combining several techniques including RL (reinforcement learning), DNNs (deep neural networks) and visual servo control. The proposed method has been tested successfully both on simulated environments as well as real-world experiments involving autonomous driving cars. I think it would make sense if they also mention some related works such as: - Learning-based visual servoing: e.g., [1] , [2] . - Reinforcement learning methods: e.g., [3] . -Theoretical analysis of stability properties [4] Overall I find the manuscript very interesting and recommend publication after minor revisions. Minor comments include: 1. Introduction: It might help readers who don't know much about RL by providing references to introductory papers/books like [5] 2. Figure 1: Please add labels The authors present a novel method combining several techniques including RL (reinforcement learning), DNNs (deep neural networks) and visual servo control. Their results indicate significant improvements compared to previous methods both quantitatively as well as qualitatively. The proposed method has been tested extensively against different types of disturbances such as varying lighting conditions, viewpoint change etc., showing good performance across all scenarios considered. One drawback might be its computational complexity due to the need to compute forward predictions during each step. Overall I think it would make sense if they could provide some additional insights into why certain choices were made e.g. regarding network architecture/parameters selection. This will help readers understand better where exactly these ideas come from and whether there may exist alternative ways to achieve similar goals

Overall, the paper is technically sound and interesting. Below are my suggestions to improve the paper. 1. The LSTM based models require a large training data for training. In the validation phase of the paper, the model is tested on a small data (~500 sentences). I would recommend testing on a larger data set (eg. ~1000 sentences) to see if the performance is similar to the reported results. 2. Also, I would recommend conducting a sanity check on the global averaged pooling output from the LSTM layer. Since the sentences have different lengths, I expect the length of the sequences to be the same length. The LSTM layer learns this and outputs a sequence of vectors of the same length as the input sequence. The global averaged pooling uses the same vector for each index in the sequence, which results in zero padding or empty values for the corresponding indices if the input sequence is of different lengths. A simple check could be to check if there are any zero padding values in the output layer. 3. The paper uses the binary classification task. Although the model is not provided, I assume the the layer is logistic regression. Since the model is a sequence to sequence model, the label of the sequence should be provided as additional input to the model. The cross entropy loss will be affected by the order of the label and the sequence. One way to deal with this is to shuffle the order of the labels before giving them to the model. 4. The full stop (period) is usually used as the decimal point in English. The check should be done for all the occurrences of decimal points. Paper Linguistically Regularized LSTM for Sentiment Classification: Matthieu Barla, Ahmed Elsayed, Hervé Bourhis and Sebastien Ourselin. This paper presents a LSTM model with a linguistic regularizer for sentiment classification. The model can be seen as an extension of the classic LSTM, introducing a new layer that adds a linguistic component to the usual vector of word embeddings. The linguistic component is computed from training data with a MaxEnt model. It measures how much each word is affected by each tense, using a one-vs-all scheme. The final word embedding is a combination of the common word vector and the sentiment-affected vector. The model is evaluated on two benchmark datasets for Spanish and French. On both datasets, the model outperforms the baseline and other state-of-the-art methods. The main benefit of the model is its simplicity: adding a linguistic component is simple, and the model can be trained end-to-end. Also, the model can be applied to other languages without retraining. The paper also presents an analysis of the effect of the different components of the model. In particular, the analysis shows that the model was not able to capture the effect of the past tense and negative polarity items. The paper is well-written and clear. It presents the model and the approach in a concise way, and analyzes the effect of different components of the model. I have some comments and suggestions. First, because this is a Machine Learning paper, it would be good to present the model’s generalization ability. For example, it would be good to present the model’s accuracy on the test set for the different values of the hyperparameter alpha. Second, because the model can be applied to other languages, it would be good to present an example of application to other languages. It would also be interesting to discuss the limitations of the model and its application to other languages. Overall, this is a well-written paper presenting a good approach for sentiment analysis. Some comments and suggestions: The paper only presents results on two benchmarks. One could argue that it would be interesting to compare the model to other approaches on a larger number of datasets. The paper only presents results for two languages. The results could be presented for both languages. The paper only describes the model in the case of Spanish and French. For these two languages, the reader could think that the model could be implemented for other languages. However, it would be interesting to discuss whether this approach could be extended to other languages. The paper does not show results for the effect of the different components of the model, such as the model without the linguistic component, or the effect of the number of layers in the model. Overall, this is a well-written paper presenting a good approach for sentiment analysis. Adding the generalization ability of the model: How does the model perform for different values of the alpha parameter? Adding application to other languages: Could the model be applied to other languages than Spanish and French? Why or why not? Limitation and discussion: Would the approach presented in the paper be applicable to other languages or not? Why? And what are the limitations of the approach? Overall, this is a well-written paper presenting a good approach for sentiment analysis. I have some comments and suggestions. First, because this is a Machine Learning paper, it would be good to present the model’s generalization ability. Title: Sarcasm SIGN: Interpreting Sarcasm with Sentiment Based Monolingual Machine Translation The paper presents an interesting approach to detecting sarcasm in text using sentiment-based monolingual machine translation. The authors propose Sarcasm SIGN, which is a technique for identifying sarcastic statements in a given language by leveraging sentiment changes that occur within them. The paper is well-structured and has a clear introduction, methodology, experimental set up, and results interpretation. The authors' experimental results were encouraging and demonstrated the effectiveness of their approach. However, I have a few comments regarding the paper: 1. The authors should provide more details about how they selected the datasets used in their experiments. They should explain the rationale behind the choice of the datasets and how they ensured the diversity of the data. 2. The paper would be improved if the authors provided more detailed information about the translation models employed in their study. They only mentioned that the models were based on OpenNMT, but there are several variants of OpenNMT like ConvS2S and Transformer, and it is not clear which one was used in this paper. 3. Another area for improvement is the comparison of the proposed approach with existing methods. The authors could have compared the performance of their method with other state-of-the-art sarcasm detection techniques to show how their approach fares in comparison. Overall, I believe that this paper has presented a valuable contribution to the field of sarcasm detection with an innovative approach. I recommend that the authors address the issues raised above before publication. The paper "Sarcasm SIGN: Interpreting Sarcasm with Sentiment Based Monolingual Machine Translation" addresses the challenge of accurately detecting and interpreting sarcasm in natural language. The paper proposes a novel approach to sarcasm detection that leverages sentiment-based monolingual machine translation. The paper presents experimental results that demonstrate the effectiveness of the approach. The main strength of this paper is the innovative approach proposed for sarcasm detection. The use of sentiment-based machine translation is a novel way to interpret sarcastic utterances, allowing for improved accuracy in detecting and understanding the intended meaning. The use of a large dataset and the evaluation of multiple machine learning models is another strength of this paper, providing robustness to the results. However, there are also some weaknesses in the paper. Firstly, the paper lacks clarity in the description of the experimental setup and methodology, making it difficult to understand how the results were obtained. The paper could benefit from providing more details on the feature selection process, model training, and evaluation metrics used. Additionally, although the results show improvement in sarcasm detection accuracy, the study lacks a comparison with existing state-of-the-art approaches, making it difficult to draw conclusions on the effectiveness of the proposed approach. Overall, the paper "Sarcasm SIGN: Interpreting Sarcasm with Sentiment Based Monolingual Machine Translation" presents an interesting and innovative approach to sarcasm detection. Despite some weaknesses, the results suggest that the proposed approach is effective for this task. The paper titled "Sarcasm SIGN: Interpreting Sarcasm with Sentiment Based Monolingual Machine Translation" presents a novel task of sarcasm interpretation by generating a non-sarcastic utterance conveying the same message as the original sarcastic one. The paper introduces a novel dataset of 3000 sarcastic tweets, each interpreted by five human judges, and experiments with monolingual machine translation algorithms and evaluation measures. The authors also present the MT-based sarcasm interpretation algorithm SIGN, which targets sentiment words, a defining element of textual sarcasm. Overall, the paper is well written and presents a novel and interesting approach to the task of sarcasm interpretation. The dataset used in this paper is relevant and provides a good ground truth for evaluating the proposed algorithm. The authors' discussion on future research directions for the new task is insightful and invites further exploration. The authors' approach of addressing the task as monolingual machine translation is innovative. The use of MT algorithms and evaluation measures allows for a systematic comparison of different interpretation models. The proposed algorithm, SIGN, which targets sentiment words, is able to generate interpretations that score higher for adequacy and sentiment polarity. However, there are a few issues that need to be addressed. First, the authors could have provided more details on the MT algorithms used and how they were optimized for the task of sarcasm interpretation. Second, while the proposed dataset of 3000 sarcastic tweets is a good start, it is limited to English and may not be representative of sarcasm use in other languages. Finally, the authors could have discussed the limitations of their approach, such as the reliance on sentiment words and the potential loss of other contextual cues that contribute to the overall sarcasm interpretation. Overall, the paper presents a valuable contribution to the field of NLP by introducing a new task of sarcasm interpretation and proposing an MT-based algorithm SIGN that targets sentiment words. The authors' approach is innovative, and their results show promise for future research in sarcasm interpretation. Peer review: The paper "Sarcasm SIGN: Interpreting Sarcasm with Sentiment Based Monolingual Machine Translation" addresses the problem of sarcasm interpretation, which is defined as generating a non-sarcastic utterance that conveys the same message as the original sarcastic one. This task is challenging, as the meaning of the sarcastic utterance is opposite to what is being conveyed. The paper presents a novel dataset of 3000 sarcastic tweets, each interpreted by five human judges. Additionally, the authors propose a new sarcasm interpretation algorithm called SIGN, which is based on sentiment words, a defining element of textual sarcasm. The paper then presents a comparison of the SIGN algorithm with other MT algorithms, using n-gram-based automatic measures for evaluation. Finally, the authors discuss future research directions for sarcasm interpretation. The paper has several strengths. The introduction of the new task of sarcasm interpretation is interesting and important in the context of natural language understanding. The proposed dataset of sarcastic tweets, and the assessment by multiple human judges, is a valuable resource for future research in this area. Additionally, the proposed SIGN algorithm is innovative and takes into account the crucial role of sentiment words in sarcasm interpretation. However, there are some weaknesses in the paper. Firstly, the paper does not provide enough details about the different MT algorithms experimented with, making it difficult to understand the extent of the comparison with the proposed SIGN algorithm. Secondly, the paper's evaluation of the SIGN algorithm is limited to n-gram-based automatic measures, which may not be sufficient to capture the full complexity of sarcasm interpretation. Finally, the paper does not thoroughly discuss the limitations of the proposed SIGN algorithm and the dataset. Overall, the paper is a valuable contribution to the field of natural language processing and sets a foundation for future research in sarcasm interpretation. However, the study could be improved by providing more in-depth evaluation and discussion of the proposed algorithm and dataset.

The paper proposes a task of selecting the most appropriate textual description for a given scene/image from a list of similar options. It also proposes couple of baseline models, an evaluation metrics and human evaluation score. - Strengths: The paper is well-written and well-structured. It is clear with its contributions and well supports them by empirical evidence. So the paper is very easy to read. The paper is well motivated. A method of selecting the most appropriate caption given a list of misleading candidates will benefit other image-caption/understanding models, by acting as a post-generation re-ranking method. - Weaknesses: I am not sure if the proposed algorithm for decoys generation is effective, which as a consequence puts the paper on questions. For each target caption, the algorithm basically picks out those with similar representation and surface form but do not belong to the same image. But a fundamentally issue with this approach is: not belonging to the image-A does not mean not appropriate to describe image-A, especially when the representation and surface form are close. So the ground-truth labels might not be valid. As we can see in Figure-1, the generated decoys are either too far from the target to be a *good* decoy (*giraffe* vs *elephant*), or fair substitutes for the target (*small boy playing kites* vs *boy flies a kite*). Thus, I am afraid that the dataset generated with this algorithm can not train a model to really *go beyond key word recognition*, which was claimed as contribution in this paper. As shown in Figure-1, most decoys can be filtered by key word mismatch---*giraffe vs elephant*, *pan vs bread*, *frisbee vs kite*, etc. And when they can not be separated by *key word match*, they look very tempting to be a correct option. Furthermore, it is interesting that humans only do correctly on 82.8% on a sampled test set. Does it mean that those examples are really too hard even for human to correctly classify? Or are some of the *decoys* in fact good enough to be the target's substitute (or even better) so that human choose them over ground-truth targets? - General Discussion: I think this is a well-written paper with clear motivation and substantial experiments. The major issue is that the data-generating algorithm and the generated dataset do not seem helpful for the motivation. This in turn makes the experimental conclusions less convincing. So I tend to reject this paper unless my concerns can be fully addressed in rebuttal. - Strengths: Authors generate a dataset of “rephrased” captions and are planning to make this dataset publicly available. The way authors approached DMC task has an advantage over VQA or caption generation in terms of metrics. It is easier and more straightforward to evaluate problem of choosing the best caption. Authors use accuracy metric. While for instance caption generation requires metrics like BLUE or Meteor which are limited in handling semantic similarity. Authors propose an interesting approach to “rephrasing”, e.g. selecting decoys. They draw decoys form image-caption dataset. E.g. decoys for a single image come from captions for other images. These decoys however are similar to each other both in terms of surface (bleu score) and semantics (PV similarity). Authors use lambda factor to decide on the balance between these two components of the similarity score. I think it would be interesting to employ these for paraphrasing. Authors support their motivation for the task with evaluation results. They show that a system trained with the focus on differentiating between similar captions performs better than a system that is trained to generate captions only. These are, however, showing that system that is tuned for a particular task performs better on this task. - Weaknesses: It is not clear why image caption task is not suitable for comprehension task and why author’s system is better for this. In order to argue that system can comprehend image and sentence semantics better one should apply learned representation, e.g. embeddings. E.g. apply representations learned by different systems on the same task for comparison. My main worry about the paper is that essentially authors converge to using existing caption generation techniques, e.g. Bahdanau et al., Chen et al. They way formula (4) is presented is a bit confusing. From formula it seems that both decoy and true captions are employed for both loss terms. However, as it makes sense, authors mention that they do not use decoy for the second term. That would hurt mode performance as model would learn to generate decoys as well. The way it is written in the text is ambiguous, so I would make it more clear either in the formula itself or in the text. Otherwise it makes sense for the model to learn to generate only true captions while learning to distinguish between true caption and a decoy. - General Discussion: Authors formulate a task of Dual Machine Comprehension. They aim to accomplish the task by challenging computer system to solve a problem of choosing between two very similar captions for a given image. Authors argue that a system that is able to solve this problem has to “understand” the image and captions beyond just keywords but also capture semantics of captions and their alignment with image semantics. I think paper need to make more focus on why chosen approach is better than just caption generation and why in their opinion caption generation is less challenging for learning image and text representation and their alignment. For formula (4). I wonder if in the future it is possible to make model to learn “not to generate” decoys by adjusting second loss term to include decoys but with a negative sign. Did authors try something similar? - Strengths: The DMC task seems like a good test of understanding language and vision. I like that the task has a clear evaluation metric. The failure of the caption generation model on the DMC task is quite interesting. This result further demonstrates that these models are good language models, but not as good at capturing the semantics of the image. - Weaknesses: The experiments are missing a key baseline: a state-of-the-art VQA model trained with only a yes/no label vocabulary. I would have liked more details on the human performance experiments. How many of the ~20% of incorrectly-predicted images are because the captions are genuinely ambiguous? Could the data be further cleaned up to yield an even higher human accuracy? - General Discussion: My concern with this paper is that the data set may prove to be easy or gameable in some way. The authors can address this concern by running a suite of strong baselines on their data set and demonstrating their accuracies. I'm not convinced by the current set of experiments because the chosen neural network architectures appear quite different from the state-of-the-art architectures in similar tasks, which typically rely on attention mechanisms over the image. Another nice addition to this paper would be an analysis of the data set. How many tokens does the correct caption share with distractors on average? What kind of understanding is necessary to distinguish between the correct and incorrect captions? I think this kind of analysis really helps the reader understand why this task is worthwhile relative to the many other similar tasks. The data generation technique is quite simple and wouldn't really qualify as a significant contribution, unless it worked surprisingly well. - Notes I couldn't find a description of the FFNN architecture in either the paper or the supplementary material. It looks like some kind of convolutional network over the tokens, but the details are very unclear. I'm also confused about how the Veq2Seq+FFNN model is applied to both classification and caption generation. Is the loglikelihood of the caption combined with the FFNN prediction during classification? Is the FFNN score incorporated during caption generation? The fact that the caption generation model performs (statistically significantly) *worse* than random chance needs some explanation. How is this possible? 528 - this description of the neural network is hard to understand. The final paragraph of the section makes it clear, however. Consider starting the section with it.

The authors present an interesting study on how readers make sense out of sentences with multiple clauses and conjunctions, using vector representations to represent sentence meaning. The results are intriguing but I am not sure that they provide much insight into what is going on during reading comprehension. I think it would be useful if there were some discussion about whether this kind of representation can capture all aspects of language processing or only certain kinds (e.g., those related to prediction). It seems like these models could also be used as part of a larger model which includes other types of information such as world knowledge. In addition, while the data presented here suggests that people do use predictive strategies when reading complex sentences, it's unclear exactly why that's happening - perhaps because the task was too easy? The authors present an interesting study on how readers make sense out of sentences that are not completely understood. The main idea is to use vector representations for neural networks as input into a reader model which can predict word sequences based upon context. This work has several important contributions including (1) introducing new methods for training these models, (2) showing that this approach works well with real world data from human reading experiments, (3) demonstrating that it performs better than previous approaches using n-grams Finally they show some promising results when applying their method to other tasks such as machine translation. Strengths: I think there were two major strengths about this research. First was the novelty of the proposed solution - I have never seen anyone else try something like this before so it's very exciting! Second strength was the experimental validation through actual human subjects testing. Weaknesses: There weren't many obvious weak points but one thing stood out to me. In order to train your network you need large amounts of text labeled according to its predication structure. This means that if you're trying to apply this technique to another language you'll either be required to collect enough unlabeled text yourself or find someone who already did! If neither option exists you'd probably want to consider alternative solutions instead The authors make some interesting observations regarding how different types of reader work by looking at their internal representations. The idea behind using linguistic information seems promising but it would help if they could provide more details or examples showing what exactly these features are doing. Overall I think it's worth publishing because there isn't much research done with respect to reading comprehension models so any new insights into them will likely get attention from researchers working in related fields. However, I'm also concerned whether the ideas presented here really add anything substantial beyond what's already known since most of the claims made seem fairly obvious once you know why those particular readers were designed. So I'd recommend making sure your conclusions aren't too broad before submitting. Overall positive feedback! I am happy to see that my comments helped improve the manuscript considerably The authors make several interesting observations regarding how different types of reader work together when reading text. The most important observation seems to me to be that there are two distinct ways that information from one part of the sentence may affect another - either directly via explicit references between parts of the sentence, or indirectly through aggregation mechanisms which combine all relevant pieces into some sort of summary before making their way back out again. I think it would also help if they could provide more details on exactly why adding linguistic features helps so much with these models; it's clear enough intuitively but I'd like them to explain better what's going on here. Overall though, I'm very impressed at both the quality of research presented here and its potential impact. Strengths: 1) Very well written 2) Interesting insights 3) Excellent experimental design 4) Highly original 5) Potential practical applications

This paper presents a novel approach to deep learning called Boosted Residual Networks (BRN). The authors propose a new architecture that combines residual connections with boosting techniques to improve the accuracy of deep learning models. The authors present a thorough explanation of the proposed architecture and its advantages over existing deep learning models. They also provide a detailed description of the experiments they conducted to evaluate the performance of the BRN model. The results of their experiments demonstrate that the BRN model outperforms existing deep learning models in terms of accuracy and generalization ability. The paper is well-structured and the authors have provided a clear explanation of the proposed architecture. The experiments and results are also presented in a clear and concise manner. The authors have also discussed the limitations of their proposed architecture and have provided suggestions for future work. Overall, this is a well-written paper that provides a novel approach to deep learning. The authors have provided a thorough explanation of their proposed architecture and have demonstrated its effectiveness through their experiments. The paper is well-structured and easy to follow. for Image Classification This paper addresses the problem of image classification using a boosted residual network. The authors propose a novel architecture, which combines the boosting and residual learning techniques to improve the accuracy of image classification. Strengths: - The paper presents a novel architecture for image classification which combines boosting and residual learning techniques. - The authors provide a comprehensive analysis of the proposed architecture and its performance. - The authors provide an extensive evaluation of the proposed architecture on several benchmark datasets. Weaknesses: - The paper does not provide an in-depth comparison of the proposed architecture with other existing architectures. - The paper does not discuss the potential applications of the proposed architecture. - The paper does not provide any discussion on the scalability of the proposed architecture. This paper presents an interesting approach to ensemble learning, combining the recent developments in Residual Networks with the Deep Incremental Boosting technique. The authors demonstrate that their proposed Boosted Residual Networks approach has superior performance compared to boosting a Residual Network of fixed structure or using the equivalent Deep Incremental Boosting without the shortcut layers. The paper is well-written and clearly explains the proposed approach. The authors provide a detailed description of the Boosted Residual Networks methodology and demonstrate its effectiveness through experimental results. The experimental results are comprehensive and provide a good insight into the performance of the proposed approach. Overall, this paper provides a good contribution to the field of ensemble learning. The authors have presented an interesting and promising approach that combines the recent developments in Residual Networks with the Deep Incremental Boosting technique. The proposed approach is demonstrated to have superior performance compared to existing methods and should be of interest to researchers in the field. This paper addresses the problem of combining recent developments in Residual Networks with Deep Incremental Boosting to create an ensemble of Residual Networks. The paper presents a new ensemble method, called Boosted Residual Networks, which builds an ensemble of Residual Networks by growing the member network at each round of boosting. The paper has several strengths. First, the authors provide a clear and concise description of the proposed ensemble method. Second, the authors provide a thorough analysis of the results, which shows that the proposed approach has better potential than either boosting a Residual Network of fixed structure or using the equivalent Deep Incremental Boosting without the shortcut layers. Finally, the authors provide a detailed discussion of the implications of the proposed approach. The paper also has some weaknesses. First, the authors do not provide any experiments to compare the proposed approach to other methods. Second, the authors do not provide any insights into how the proposed approach could be used in practical applications. Finally, the authors do not provide any discussion of potential limitations of the proposed approach.

The paper proposes a method to adapt a model trained on source domain to new samples from the target domain. The main problem is that the source and target domain may have different data distributions, thus this problem is called domain adaptation. The main idea is to regularize the Adversarial Discriminative Domain Adaptation (ADDAC) framework with a variational approach. The variational approach prevents the solution from moving towards the source distribution and instead learn a compromise solution that combines both source and target domain. Strength The proposed method is effective and easily applicable. Additionally, the proposed method is applied to a vanilla Adversarial Discriminative Domain Adaptation (ADDAC) which is a popular approach to domain adaptation. The code for this method is publicly available. There are several problems that the proposed variational method can be extended to. For example, the proposed method can be applied to a dueling discriminative domain adaptation framework. The dueling adaptation approach is to generate negative samples for source samples, which are used to train the target domain only classifier. dueling approach is applied to, for example, UTFN and BAM. Another possible extension is to apply the method to other adversarial learning frameworks. For example, the authors can replace the original non-saturating loss with another loss function (for example, triplet loss) and apply the method to triplet discriminative domain adaptation. Weaknesses The following problems can be considered as weaknesses of the paper: 1. the method is evaluated on sentiment analysis task and the quantitative results are not promising. The suggested implementation uses ADDA code by the authors, but the code is not released. This reduces the transparency of the results. 2. the proposed method is applied to a vanilla ADDAC framework, while the method can be also applied to dueling and triplin discriminative domain adaptation. It would be useful to study whether the method can improve the results of these approaches. 3. the method is applied to a BPRM which is widely used for knowledge transfer. It would be useful to study whether the method can be applied to other transfer learning frameworks, for example, LSTMA and DAN. Overall, the paper provides a general method to adapt a source model to a new target domain. The suggested method is easy to apply and the code is publicly available. The method can be easily extended to other adaptation frameworks and applications. The paper is well written, and the topic is extremely important and useful in practice. The experiments results are convincing, and I believe this paper will be published in a well-known journal. I really enjoyed reading this paper. First, why the paper only discuss the time-series data domain adaptation problem? Why don't we discuss the sequence data domain adaptation problem? As we all know, sequence data is also important in real-world applications. For example, we can use text data to predict the user’s ratings for items in an e-commerce system. In addition, recent years have seen great success in applying domain adaptation techniques to sequence data, such as NLP tasks. For example, the ICONIA 2018 Challenge on Neural Sentiment Analysis also includes a domain adaptation task. Second, the VRADA model is good for time-series domain adaptation, but it only considers the domain transfer direction from the source domain to the target domain. In many real-world applications, we also need to perform domain adaptation in the opposite direction. For example, VRADA needs to learn the domain invariant features from the target domain, to perform domain adaptation in the source domain. Similarly, we can also transfer the domain information from the source domain to the domain. Therefore, the model should not only consider the direction of transfer from the source domain to the target domain, but also consider the direction of transfer from the source domain to the target domain. I think this directionless based on only one direction of transfer is a significant limitation of this paper. The topic of the paper, model-based RL with a learned model, is important and timely. The paper is well written. I feel that the presented results are too incremental. Augmenting the frame prediction network with another head that predicts the reward is a very sensible thing to do. However neither the methodology not the results are novel / surprising, given that the original method of [Oh et al. 2015] already learns to successfully increment score counters in predicted frames in many games. I’m very much looking forward to seeing the results of applying the learned joint model of frames and rewards to model-based RL as proposed by the authors. The authors have combined two known areas of research - frame prediction and reward prediction - and combined them in a feedforward network trained on sequences from Atari games. The fact that this should train well is unsurprising for this domain, and the research yields no other interesting results. Pros - the paper is clearly written and the experiments are sound. Cons - there is very little novelty or contribution. This paper introduces an additional reward-predicting head to an existing NN architecture for video frame prediction. In Atari game playing scenarios, the authors show that this model can successfully predict both reward and next frames. Pros: - Paper is well written and easy to follow. - Model is clear to understand. Cons: - The model is incrementally different than the baseline. The authors state that their purpose is to establish a pre-condition, which they achieve. But this makes the paper quite limited in scope. This paper reads like the start of a really good long paper, or a good short paper. Following through on the future work proposed by the authors would make a great paper. As it stands, the paper is a bit thin on new contributions. The paper extends a recently proposed video frame prediction method with reward prediction in order to learn the unknown system dynamics and reward structure of an environment. The method is tested on several Atari games and is able to predict the reward quite well within a range of about 50 steps. The paper is very well written, focussed and is quite clear about its contribution to the literature. The experiments and methods are sound. However, the results are not really surprising given that the system state and the reward are linked deterministically in Atari games. In other words, we can always decode the reward from a network that successfully encodes future system states in its latent representation. The contribution of the paper is therefore minor. The paper would be much stronger if the authors could include experiments on the two future work directions they suggest in the conclusions: augmenting training with artificial samples and adding Monte-Carlo tree search. The suggestions might decrease the number of real-world training samples and increase performance, both of which would be very interesting and impactful. The topic of the paper, model-based RL with a learned model, is important and timely. The paper is well written. I feel that the presented results are too incremental. Augmenting the frame prediction network with another head that predicts the reward is a very sensible thing to do. However neither the methodology not the results are novel / surprising, given that the original method of [Oh et al. 2015] already learns to successfully increment score counters in predicted frames in many games. I’m very much looking forward to seeing the results of applying the learned joint model of frames and rewards to model-based RL as proposed by the authors. The topic of the paper, model-based RL with a learned model, is important and timely. The paper is well written. I feel that the presented results are too incremental. Augmenting the frame prediction network with another head that predicts the reward is a very sensible thing to do. However neither the methodology not the results are novel / surprising, given that the original method of [Oh et al. 2015] already learns to successfully increment score counters in predicted frames in many games. I’m very much looking forward to seeing the results of applying the learned joint model of frames and rewards to model-based RL as proposed by the authors. The authors have combined two known areas of research - frame prediction and reward prediction - and combined them in a feedforward network trained on sequences from Atari games. The fact that this should train well is unsurprising for this domain, and the research yields no other interesting results. Pros - the paper is clearly written and the experiments are sound. Cons - there is very little novelty or contribution. This paper introduces an additional reward-predicting head to an existing NN architecture for video frame prediction. In Atari game playing scenarios, the authors show that this model can successfully predict both reward and next frames.
