This paper addresses the problem of code suggestion in dynamic programming languages. Its strength lies in the fact that it uses a large-scale code suggestion corpus of 41M lines of Python code crawled from GitHub, and the qualitative analysis shows the model indeed captures interesting long-range dependencies. However, there are some weaknesses in the paper. First, the paper does not provide any comparison with other state-of-the-art code suggestion systems. Second, the paper does not provide any evaluation on code written by developers, which is the ultimate goal of code suggestion systems.

This paper improves SGD using a simple feedback mechanism. The paper finds the optimal rate of decreasing learning rate based on previous iterations, and show that with this optimal decreasing learning rate, SGD converges faster than constant decreasing learning rate. The method is experimentally validated on logistic regression and accelerated convergence is shown on simple linear model as well as complex models such as language modeling. my own opinions: This is a very interesting paper. The idea of optimal decreasing learning rate is very creative and may help accelerate the convergence of SGD. I have a few concerns about the implementation and logic in the paper. First, the implementation in the paper is slightly flawed. The implementation on linear models (i.e., logistic regression, XOR problem, autoencoding model) shows that using optimal decreasing learning rate leads to faster convergence, but on complex models (i.e., language modeling), it does not show any improvement over constant decreasing learning rate. The reason may be that optimal decreasing learning rate doesn’t decrease fast enough when iterations get far away from the optimal point. In the implementation, the paper uses early stopping to deal with this issue, but this is risky as it assumes the model is being learned in a convex region. A more reasonable way to do this is by properly choosing the decay rate for the learned model. Second, I am not convinced by the purpose of the paper. The main purpose of the paper is to show that optimal decreasing learning rate leads to faster convergence. But it does not prove that it is better than constant decreasing learning rate. In fact, there could be some cases that constant decreasing learning rate is better than optimal decreasing learning rate. For example, when the model is getting close to overfitting, optimal decreasing learning rate may be too aggressive and lead to slow convergence, while constant decreasing learning rate is conservative and may help avoid overfitting. I think in general, the trade-off between fast convergence and good model accuracy is not a single number, but a curve. Such a paper is far from being comprehensive. The authors improved on Stochastic Gradient Descent by introducing a dynamic aperture scheme that allows for more consistent and faster SGD training. In addition to that, the authors also provided empirical results on how the proposed method performs on the CIFAR10 and ImageNet datasets. My major concern with the paper is the lack of theoretical analysis on the advantages of the dynamic aperture scheme. Without such an analysis, it is not clear if the additional benefits of the scheme over standard SGD are because of the scheme itself, or merely statistical noise. impressio hard the problem or question this paper addresses, then strengths and weaknesses, for the paper Improving Stochastic Gradient Descent with Feedback research question the authors improved on Stochastic Gradient Descent by introducing a dynamic aperture scheme that allows for more consistent and faster SGD training. In addition to that, the authors also provided empirical results on how the proposed method performs on the CIFAR10 and ImageNet datasets. My major concern with the paper is the lack of theoretical analysis on the advantages of the dynamic aperture scheme. Without such an analysis, it is not clear if the additional benefits of the scheme over standard SGD are because of the scheme itself, or merely statistical noise. strengths: 1. Introduced dynamic aperture scheme that allows for more consistent and faster SGD training Strength: The proposed dynamic aperture scheme has consistently lower validation error on both CIFAR10 and ImageNet. However, I am not sure if this is because of the scheme itself or merely statistical noise. 2. Theoretical analysis on the advantages of the dynamic aperture scheme The authors claim that the dynamic aperture scheme has the following advantages over standard SGD: 1. Enabled faster training without increasing the number of epochs 2. Generated better starting point for subsequent epoches 3. Eased dependence on the step size selection 4. Eased the accommodation for decreasing learning rate. However, I am not sure if these theoretical advantages are because of the scheme itself or merely statistical noise w.r.t. weakness: The authors use CIFAR10 and ImageNet to showcase the advantages of the dynamic aperture scheme, however, a more rigorous analysis on other datasets would strengthen the paper. The main contributions of this work are: (1) proposing a feedback-based method for improving SGD, (2) applying the method to modify Adam, a state of the art optimizer for deep learning, and (3) showing that Eve outperforms all state of the art methods for training deep learning models on three different datasets. We will discuss the following aspects of the paper: (1) its main contribution, (2) its potential impact and interest, and (3) the limitations and future work. The main contribution of this work is proposing a feedback-based method for improving SGD. A simple but effective method for improving SGD is highly relevant and has a lot of potential impact on the field. There are many potential uses for this approach beyond modifying Adam, such as using the method for other types of stochastic optimization, or developing more complex forms of feedback. The potential impact and interest of this work can be seen in the improvements to the standard SGD algorithm, which are generally applicable to different types of optimization. In particular, the proposed method can be applied in a wide range of machine learning and data mining tasks, such as training large deep neural networks, training large language models, or optimizing hyperparameter settings. These tasks often require the use of high-performance computing resources. Thus, feedback-based methods for improving SGD can be used to further reduce the costs of training these models. The limitations of this work and future work can be seen in the modification of Adam. As discussed above, the feedback-based method has potential for other types of optimization besides Adam. Additionally, Adam is a very popular optimization method, and modifying it in such a way that it becomes a simple one-parameter approach can be challenging. The potential to further improve Adam by taking into account the feedback from the objective function remains. This work is a well-conducted empirical study of Adam. The empirical evaluation is particularly novel, as few works have tested the improvement of Adam on large-scale datasets such as ImageNet. The adoption of multiple benchmarks also adds to the value of this study. However, we note that the improvements achieved by Eve are modest, as reflected in its reported training speed, in comparison to the standard SGD algorithm. One possible explanation for this is that Adam achieved state-of-the-art performance by annealing the learning rate over time. We believe that this behavior makes it resistant to the proposed method of adjusting the learning rate on the fly. In the future, it may be possible to combine the improvement methods proposed here and Adam in a way that achieves much better performance and training speed. In this paper, the author presents a simple and efficient method for improving stochastic gradient descent (SGD) methods by using the feedback from the objective function. The proposed algorithm, called Eve, is tested on a few deep learning tasks and shown to be superior to state-of-the-art methods. A couple of sentences are included here to describe the main idea and motivation behind the proposed algorithm. One of the challenges in training deep neural networks is to update the learning rates in SGD such that the optimization procedure is stable and fast. In this paper we present a simple and efficient method to adaptively tune the learning rates using the running average of the objective values. Our method is based on the observation that the relative changes in the objective function often signal the regions of the parameter space that are difficult to optimize. We refer to these changes as the descent percents, and the proposed algorithm tracks the descent percent values throughout the training process. When the learning rate is set to be a large value in normal circumstances, the proposed algorithm reduces the learning rate when the descendent percents are small. On the contrary, when the descendent percent is large, the proposed algorithm increases the learning rate to fast track the descent. We compared our method with state of the art methods and shown that it outperforms them on several deep learning tasks. In the paper, the author also adapts the Adam algorithm to the proposed method. Instead of separately tuning the lower and higher learning rates, the proposed algorithm sets the first and second moments of the exponential moving average of the parameters to same value. This modification reduces the initial overhead of the proposed algorithm without loss of performance.

The paper titled "Leveraging Behavioral and Social Information for Weakly Supervised Collective Classification of Political Discourse on Twitter" presents an interesting approach to classify political discourse on Twitter using weakly supervised learning techniques. The authors propose to use social and behavioral information, such as user profile, follower network, hashtags, and mentions, in addition to the content of tweets, to train a collective classifier. The paper presents a thorough review of related work and highlights the limitations of existing approaches that rely solely on content-based features. The proposed approach addresses these limitations by leveraging social and behavioral information to improve the accuracy of the classification task, especially in the case of weakly labeled data. The paper also presents a well-structured experimental evaluation, including data collection, preprocessing, feature engineering, and classification tasks. The results indicate that the proposed approach outperforms existing state-of-the-art methods, both in terms of accuracy and F1 score. However, there are some limitations in the proposed approach that need to be addressed. Firstly, the paper doesn't discuss the potential biases that may arise from using social and behavioral information. For example, user profile and follower network may reflect the user's socio-demographic characteristics or political beliefs, which may affect the classification results. Secondly, the paper doesn't provide details about the interpretability of the classifiers. It is important to know how the classifiers make decisions and what features are most informative for the classification task, especially in the case of political discourse. In conclusion, the paper provides a valuable contribution to the field of political discourse analysis on Twitter using weakly supervised learning techniques. However, the limitations discussed above need to be addressed before the proposed approach can be applied in practical settings. The paper "Leveraging Behavioral and Social Information for Weakly Supervised Collective Classification of Political Discourse on Twitter" addresses the problem of automatically classifying political discourse on Twitter with weakly-labeled data. The authors propose a method that leverages both behavioral and social information to improve the accuracy of the weakly supervised collective classification. One of the main strengths of the paper is the use of both behavioral and social information to improve the classification of political discourse on Twitter. The authors use a Hidden Markov Model to capture information about the behavioral patterns of Twitter users in relation to political topics, and then incorporate this information into a collective classification algorithm. The paper also provides a detailed evaluation of the proposed method on a real-world dataset of political tweets, which suggests that the method can achieve good performance with low amounts of labeled data. However, there are some weaknesses in the paper that could be addressed. Firstly, the paper does not provide sufficient details about the experiment design for the evaluation of the proposed method. Specifically, the authors do not provide information about the selection of the labeled data, the size of the labeled and unlabeled data used, and how the performance metrics were calculated. This lack of information makes it difficult to reproduce the experiments and replicate the results. Secondly, the paper does not provide a comparison of the proposed method with other state-of-the-art methods for weakly supervised classification on Twitter. A comparison with other methods could better demonstrate the effectiveness of the proposed approach. Overall, I think that the proposed method in the paper is a promising approach to improve the accuracy of weakly supervised classification of political discourse on Twitter. However, the paper could benefit from providing more details about the experiment design and including a comparison with other state-of-the-art methods. The paper “Leveraging Behavioral and Social Information for Weakly Supervised Collective Classification of Political Discourse on Twitter” is a well-written and informative study on the use of weakly supervised models for predicting frames used in political discourse on the social media platform, Twitter. The authors present an engaging introduction to the concept of “framing,” which is a strategy used by politicians to shape public perception of issues through carefully worded statements. The study focuses on the particular challenge of studying political framing on Twitter. The paper reports the use of global probabilistic models that combine both lexical features of tweets and network-based behavioral features of Twitter to predict frames used in political discourse. The authors find that these models significantly improve the unsupervised F1 score by 21.52 points over a lexical baseline alone. One of the key strengths of the paper is the use of weakly supervised models which brings several advantages, such as reducing the burden of manual labelling of data and improving the scalability of the approach. Additionally, the paper highlights the importance of considering behavioral features of Twitter users in the classification task. However, the paper could benefit from more detailed explanation of the methodology chosen by the authors. For instance, the authors do not provide much detail on the use of the global probabilistic models and how they were integrated with the various features used for classification. Furthermore, it would have been helpful to explain in greater detail how the findings are useful for researchers and practitioners working in the field of political discourse analysis. Overall, this study presents an interesting contribution to the field of political discourse analysis on Twitter, and the use of weakly supervised models is a promising approach for future research in this area. The paper "Leveraging Behavioral and Social Information for Weakly Supervised Collective Classification of Political Discourse on Twitter" addresses the problem of classifying political framing in Twitter discourse. Specifically, the authors aim to predict the frames used in political discourse on Twitter using a collection of weakly supervised models that leverage both lexical and network-based behavioral features of Twitter. The strength of this paper lies in its novel approach to incorporating both lexical and network-based features in classification tasks. The authors' use of collective classification to make predictions based on the opinions of the community rather than individual classifiers allows for a more nuanced understanding of political framing on social media. Additionally, the results of this study are significant, showing an increase in the average, unsupervised F1 score by 21.52 points over a lexical baseline alone. This indicates that the inclusion of network-based behavioral features is highly valuable in predicting political framing. However, there are also some weaknesses in this paper. The authors fail to discuss the limitations of their approach, including the possible biases in the user behavior they are using to train their models. Additionally, the authors do not explicitly address the ethical implications of accurately predicting political frames on social media. Overall, the paper presents an innovative approach to predicting political framing in social media discourse. However, further discussion of the study's limitations and ethical considerations is necessary.

The authors present an interesting approach to paragraph vector representation, which is based on binary codes and has been shown in experiments to be competitive with state-of-the-art methods. I have some comments about this work that I hope will help improve it further. First of all, there are several typos throughout the text (e.g., "paragraph" instead of "paraphrase"; "sentence" instead of "sequence"). Second, although the proposed method can handle paragraphs longer than 256 tokens by using multiple codewords per token, it does not seem very efficient because each word needs to go through two layers of coding/decoding before being used as input features or output labels. Finally, since the main idea behind the proposed model is similar to Word2Vec's skip-gram architecture but applied at sentence level rather than word-level, one may wonder whether the performance gain over other models comes from the use of binary codes only? The authors propose to use paragraph vectors for text classification tasks. Paragraph vector is an extension of word2vec that represents each document as a fixed-length feature vector instead of bag-of-words representation. The proposed method can be used in many applications such as sentiment analysis on social media posts where users express their opinions using paragraphs rather than sentences. This work has several advantages over existing methods including better performance with less training data (fewer labeled examples); it also allows us to learn from unlabeled documents which are abundant but not always available. However there are some issues I would like them to address before publication. 1) How does this approach compare against other state-of-the-art approaches? It should include comparisons between different models trained under various settings so we know how much improvement they have achieved compared to previous works. 2) What do you think about its scalability? This model requires more computation resources when dealing with large datasets since all documents need to go through the same process at once. They may want to consider parallelizing the algorithm across multiple machines if necessary. 3) Are these results reproducible? In order to make sure your findings are reliable, it's important to provide detailed information regarding experimental setups along with code samples and scripts... This is an interesting article on how to use deep learning techniques with text data. The authors propose new methods based on recent research into word embeddings (word2vec) which have been shown to produce good results when applied to natural language processing tasks such as sentiment analysis or machine translation. The proposed method uses a combination of convolutional layers followed by max pooling operations similar to those found in image recognition systems but adapted here so they operate over sequences of characters rather than pixels. In addition there is some attention mechanism introduced at different stages within the network architecture. It seems like it could potentially provide very useful insights if implemented correctly... In general I think it's well written although I'm not sure about the style - sometimes sentences seem too long and complicated compared to what would normally be expected in scientific papers The authors propose an alternative approach to text representation based on Paragraph Vector (PV) proposed recently by Le&Mikolov. PV is a method which uses deep learning techniques to represent texts as dense vectorial features with fixed length. The idea behind PV is similar to word2vec but instead of representing each word individually it represents whole paragraphs at once. In order to make PV more efficient they use Huffman coding technique to compress the output into shorter bit sequences. They compare performance between different types of encoding schemes including Huffman coded PV, AutoEncoder-Based Binarization(AEB), Random Code Generation(RCG). Their results suggest that PV performs better than AEB even though it has less number of bits per codeword. However RCG does not perform well compared to other methods because its generated random codes do not have any semantic meaning. Overall I think it's interesting research topic especially when you consider how much data there will be available online over time. One thing I'd like them to clarify further would be whether the same type of encoder/decoder network architecture was used across all three approaches?

The aim of this paper is to show that distributional information stored in word vector models contain information about POS labels. They use a version of the BNC annotated with UD POS and in which words have been replaced by lemmas. They train word embeddings on this corpus, then use the resulting vectors to train a logistic classifier to predict the word POS. Evaluations are performed on the same corpus (using cross-validation) as well as on other corpora. Results are clearly presented and discussed and analyzed at length. The paper is clear and well-written. The main issue with this paper is that it does not contain anything new in terms of NLP or ML. It describe a set of straightforward experiments without any new NLP or ML ideas or methods. Results are interesting indeed, in so far that they provide an empirical grounding to the notion of POS. In that regard, it is certainly worth being published in a (quantitative/emprirical) linguistic venue. On another note, the literature on POS tagging and POS induction using word embeddings should be cited more extensively (cf. for instance Lin, Ammar, Duer and Levin 2015; Ling et al. 2015 [EMNLP]; Plank, SÃ¸gaard and Goldberg 2016...). ## General comments: This paper presents an exploration of the connection between part-of-speech tags and word embeddings. Specifically the authors use word embeddings to draw some interesting (if not somewhat straightforward) conclusions about the consistency of PoS tags and the clear connection of word vector representations to PoS. The detailed error analysis (outliers of classification) is definitely a strong point of this paper. However, the paper seems to have missing one critical main point: the reason that corpora such as the BNC were PoS tagged in the first place. Unlike a purely linguistic exploration of morphosyntactic categories (which are underlined by a semantic prototype theory - e.g. see Croft, 1991), these corpora were created and tagged to facilitate further NLP tasks, mostly parsing. The whole discussion could then be reframed as whether the distinctions made by the distributional vectors are more beneficial to parsing as compared to the original tags (or UPOS for that matter). Also, this paper is missing a lot of related work in the context of distributional PoS induction. I recommend starting with the review Christodoulopoulos et al. 2010 and adding some more recent non-DNN work including Blunsom and Cohn (2011), Yatbaz et al. (2012), etc. In light of this body of work, the results of section 5 are barely novel (there are systems with more restrictions in terms of their external knowledge that achieve comparable results). ## Specific issues In the abstract one of the contributed results is that "distributional vectors do contain information about PoS affiliation". Unless I'm misunderstanding the sentence, this is hardly a new result, especially for English: every distributionally-based PoS induction system in the past 15 years that presents "many-to-one" or "cluster purity" numbers shows the same result. The assertion in lines 79-80 ("relations between... vectors... are mostly semantic") is not correct: the <MIKOLOV or COLOBERT> paper (and subsequent work) shows that there is a lot of syntactic information in these vectors. Also see previous comment about cluster purity scores. In fact you revert that statement in the beginning of section 2 (lines 107-108). Why move to UPOS? Surely the fine-grained distinctions of the original tagset are more interesting. I do not understand footnote 3. Were these failed attempts performed by you or other works? Under what criteria did they fail? What about Brown cluster vectors? They almost perfectly align with UPOS tags. Is the observation that "proper nouns are not much similar to common nouns" (lines 331-332) that interesting? Doesn't the existence of "the" (the most frequent function word) almost singlehandedly explain this difference? While I understand the practical reasons for analysing the most frequent word/tag pairs, it would be interesting to see what happens in the tail, both in terms of the vectors and also for the types of errors the classifier makes. You could then try to imagine alternatives to pure distributional (and morphological - since you're lemmatizing) features that would allow better generalizations of the PoS tags to these low-frequency words. ## Minor issues Change the sentential references to \newcite{}: e.g. "Mikolov et al. (2013b) showed"

- Strengths: The deviation between "vocal" users and "average users" is an interesting discovery that could be applied as a way to identify different types of users. - Weaknesses: I see it as an initial work on a new topic that should be expanded in the future. A possible comparison between matrix factorization and similar topics in distributional semantics (e.g. latent semantic analysis) would be useful. - General Discussion: In this paper, the authors describe an approach for modeling the stance/sentiment of Twitter users about topics. In particular, they address the task of inter-topic preferences modeling. This task consists of measuring the degree to which the stances about different topics are mutually related.This work is claimed to advance state of the art in this task, since previous works were case studies, while the proposed one is about unlimited topics on real-world data.The adopted approach consists of the following steps: A set of linguistic patterns was manually created and, through them, a large number of tweets expressing stance towards various topics was collected. Next, the texts were expressed as triples containing user, topic, and evaluation. The relationships represented by the tuples were arranged as a sparse matrix. After matrix factorization, a low-rank approximation was performed. The optimal rank was identified as 100. The definition of cosine similarity is used to measure the similarity between topics and, thus, detect latent preferences not represented in the original sparse matrix. Finally, cosine similarity is also used to detect inter-topic preferences.A preliminary empirical evaluation shows that the model predicts missing topics preferences. Moreover, predicted inter-topic preferences moderately correlate with the corresponding values from a crowdsourced gold-standard collection of preferences. According to the overview discussed in the related work section, there are no previous systems to be compared in the latter task (i.e. prediction of inter-topic preferences) and, for this reason, it is promising. I listed some specific comments below. - Rows 23 and 744, "high-quality": What makes them high-quality? If not properly defined, I would remove all the occurrences of "high-quality" in the paper. - Row 181 and caption of Figure 1: I would remove the term "generic." - Row 217, "This section collect": -> "We collected" or "This section explains how we collected"- Row 246: "ironies" -> "irony" - Row 269, "I support TPP": Since the procedure can detect various patterns such as "to A" or "this is A," maybe the author should explain that all possible patterns containing the topic are collected, and next manually filtered? - Rows 275 and 280, "unuseful": -> useless - Row 306, "including": -> are including - Row 309: "of" or "it" are not topics but, I guess, terms retrieved by mistakes as topics. - Rows 317-319: I would remove the first sentence and start with "Twitter user..." - Rows 419-439: "I like the procedure used to find the optimal k. In previous works, this number is often assumed, while it is useful to find it empirically." - Row 446, "let": Is it "call"?

- Strengths: i. Motivation is well described. ii. Provides detailed comparisons with various models across diverse languages - Weaknesses: i. The conclusion is biased by the selected languages. ii. The experiments do not cover the claim of this paper completely. - General Discussion: This paper issues a simple but fundamental question about word representation: what subunit of a word is suitable to represent morphologies and how to compose the units. To answer this question, this paper applied word representations with various subunits (characters, character-trigram, and morphs) and composition functions (LSTM, CNN, and a simple addition) to the language modeling task to find the best combination. In addition, this paper evaluated the task for more than 10 languages. This is because languages are typologically diverse and the results can be different according to the word representation and composition function. From their experimental results, this paper concluded that character-level representations are more effective, but they are still imperfective in comparing them with a model with explicit knowledge of morphology. Another conclusion is that character-trigrams show reliable perplexity in the majority of the languages. However, this paper leaves some issues behind. - First of all, there could be some selection bias of the experimental languages. This paper chose ten languages in four categories (up to three languages per a category). But, one basic question with the languages is “how can it be claimed that the languages are representatives of each category?” All the languages in the same category have the same tendency of word representation and composition function? How can it be proved? For instance, even in this paper, two languages belonging to the same typology (agglutinative) show different results. Therefore, at least to me, it seems to be better to focus on the languages tested in this paper instead of drawing a general conclusions about all languages. - There is some gap between the claim and the experiments. Is the language modeling the best task to prove the claim of this paper? Isn’t there any chance that the claim of this paper breaks in other tasks? Further explanation on this issue is needed. - In Section 5.2, this paper evaluated the proposed method only for Arabic. Is there any reason why the experiment is performed only for Arabic? There are plenty of languages with automatic morphological analyzers such as Japanese and Turkish. - This paper considers only character-trigram among various n-grams. Is there any good reason to choose only character-trigram? Is it always better than character-bigram or character-fourgram? In general, language modeling with n-grams is affected by corpus size and some other factors. Minor typos: - There is a missing reference in Introduction. (88 line in Page 1) - root-and-patter -> root-and-pattern (524 line in Page 6) tldr: The authors compare a wide variety of approaches towards sub-word modelling in language modelling, and show that modelling morphology gives the best results over modelling pure characters. Further, the authors do some precision experiments to show that the biggest benefit towards sub-word modelling is gained after words typically exhibiting rich morphology (nouns and verbs). The paper is comprehensive and the experiments justify the core claims of the paper. - Strengths: 1) A comprehensive overview of different approaches and architectures towards sub-word level modelling, with numerous experiments designed to support the core claim that the best results come from modelling morphemes. 2) The authors introduce a novel form of sub-word modelling based on character tri-grams and show it outperforms traditional approaches on a wide variety of languages. 3) Splitting the languages examined by typology and examining the effects of the models on various typologies is a welcome introduction of linguistics into the world of language modelling. 4) The analysis of perplexity reduction after various classes of words in Russian and Czech is particularly illuminating, showing how character-level and morpheme-level models handle rare words much more gracefully. In light of these results, could the authors say something about how much language modelling requires understanding of semantics, and how much it requires just knowing various morphosyntactic effects? - Weaknesses: 1) The character tri-gram LSTM seems a little unmotivated. Did the authors try other character n-grams as well? As a reviewer, I can guess that character tri-grams roughly correspond to morphemes, especially in Semitic languages, but what made the authors report results for 3-grams as opposed to 2- or 4-? In addition, there are roughly 26^3=17576 possible distinct trigrams in the Latin lower-case alphabet, which is enough to almost constitute a word embedding table. Did the authors only consider observed trigrams? How many distinct observed trigrams were there? 2) I don't think you can meaningfully claim to be examining the effectiveness of character-level models on root-and-pattern morphology if your dataset is unvocalised and thus doesn't have the 'pattern' bit of 'root-and-pattern'. I appreciate that finding transcribed Arabic and Hebrew with vowels may be challenging, but it's half of the typology. 3) Reduplication seems to be a different kind of phenomenon to the other three, which are more strictly morphological typologies. Indonesian and Malay also exhibit various word affixes, which can be used on top of reduplication, which is a more lexical process. I'm not sure splitting it out from the other linguistic typologies is justified. - General Discussion: 1) The paper was structured very clearly and was very easy to read. 2) I'm a bit puzzled about why the authors chose to use 200 dimensional character embeddings. Once the dimensionality of the embedding is greater than the size of the vocabulary (here the number of characters in the alphabet), surely you're not getting anything extra? ------------------------------- Having read the author response, my opinions have altered little. I still think the same strengths and weakness that I have already discussed hold.

Overview: The paper presents a scalable Bayesian approach for learning Recurrent Neural Networks (RNNs) for language modeling. The proposed approach aims to tackle the challenge of overfitting that often arises in training RNNs on large datasets, by incorporating Bayesian inference for weight regularization. The authors provide empirical evidence that the proposed approach outperforms existing methods in terms of both perplexity and sample quality on the Penn Treebank dataset. Strengths: The paper provides a clear motivation for using Bayesian approaches to address overfitting in RNNs for language modeling. The presentation of the proposed approach is well written, and the authors provide a thorough formalization of the problem and the Bayesian inference procedure used. The experimental evaluation of the approach is comprehensive, including comparisons to state-of-the-art methods and an analysis on the impact of hyperparameter settings on performance. Weaknesses: Although the performance gains of the proposed approach are significant for the Penn Treebank dataset, it would be helpful if the authors could provide more context on the generalizability of the approach to other datasets and use cases. Specifically, it is not clear if the benefits of Bayesian regularization outweigh the additional computational cost required for large-scale training. Additionally, the manuscript is relatively technical in nature, which could make it difficult for readers without a strong background in Bayesian methods and RNNs to understand. Recommendations: Overall, the paper presents a well-researched and well-executed approach for addressing overfitting in RNNs for language modeling. To enhance the contribution of this work, I suggest the authors consider the following revisions: - Provide additional discussion regarding the generalizability of the proposed approach to other datasets and use cases, along with any limitations that may exist. - Consider including a more detailed overview of the Bayesian approach and RNNs for readers who may not have a strong background in these areas. - Include a discussion on the computational requirements and efficiency gain of the proposed approach. In summary, I recommend this paper for acceptance, as it provides a valuable contribution to the field of scalable Bayesian learning for RNNs in language modeling. The paper titled "Scalable Bayesian Learning of Recurrent Neural Networks for Language Modeling" addresses the problem of developing a scalable approach to learning Recurrent Neural Networks (RNNs) for Language Modeling (LM). The authors propose a Bayesian approach to training RNNs that improves upon the state-of-the-art in terms of predicting unseen sentences in text corpora. The strengths of the paper include its clear and concise presentation of the problem and its proposed solution. The authors provide a thorough literature review that outlines the current state-of-the-art and highlights the limitations of existing approaches. The proposed model uses a Bayesian framework that enables the integration of prior knowledge in the learning process, resulting in improved performance compared to other existing methods on benchmark datasets. The paper also discusses the importance of hyperparameter tuning, and provides practical advice on how to choose appropriate values for the model. However, there are some potential weaknesses to the paper. Firstly, while the paper emphasizes the scalability of the proposed approach, it does not provide a detailed analysis of the computational resources required to train the model. Without such information, it is difficult to fully evaluate the practicality of the approach for large-scale applications. Secondly, while the proposed method shows improved performance on language modeling tasks, it is not clear how well it will generalize to other tasks or domains. Further experiments on different tasks and datasets could provide more insight into the applicability of the approach. Overall, the paper makes a valuable contribution to the field of NLP by proposing a scalable Bayesian approach to learning RNNs for LM tasks. Its strengths lie in its presentation of the problem, thorough related work review, and practical advice on hyperparameter tuning. While it could benefit from more extensive analysis of computational resources required and further experimentation on different tasks, the proposed approach shows potential for improving the state-of-the-art in language modeling. The paper presents a Bayesian approach for learning recurrent neural networks (RNNs) that addresses the problem of overfitting that arises during traditional back-propagation through time training. The proposed approach relies on recent advances in stochastic gradient Markov Chain Monte Carlo, which provides a principled way of capturing weight uncertainty in RNN models. This is achieved by adding gradient noise during training to enhance exploration of the model-parameter space, and performing model averaging during testing to obtain improved predictions. The paper is well-structured and clearly written, and the proposed approach is theoretically grounded and motivated. The authors provide extensive experimental results that demonstrate the superiority of the proposed approach over stochastic optimization in various applications involving different RNN models. The results indicate that the proposed approach obtains better performance in terms of perplexity and accuracy, while also mitigating overfitting. Overall, the paper presents a valuable contribution to the field of natural language processing by leveraging Bayesian learning to improve the performance of RNNs in language modeling. The methodology is well-motivated and the experimental results are convincing. A potential limitation of the paper is that it mainly focuses on language modeling, and it would be interesting to investigate the generalization of the proposed approach to other NLP tasks. Nonetheless, the paper constitutes a significant advance towards scalable Bayesian learning of RNNs, and I recommend it for publication. This paper addresses the problem of overfitting in training recurrent neural networks (RNNs) for language modeling, which is a well-known issue when using traditional back-propagation through time. The authors propose a scalable Bayesian approach that explicitly models uncertainty in the weights of RNNs through stochastic gradient Markov Chain Monte Carlo (SG-MCMC) methods. One of the key strengths of this paper is the detailed explanation and analysis of the proposed Bayesian learning algorithm. The authors give clear and concise descriptions of the algorithm, its underlying assumptions, and its benefits. They also provide a thorough evaluation of the algorithm's performance on a wide range of tasks and benchmark datasets, demonstrating its superiority compared to stochastic optimization. Another strength of this paper is the experimental results, which are very convincing. The authors show that their approach yields better results than traditional methods in terms of perplexity and accuracy on various datasets for language modeling tasks. They also perform extensive ablation studies to investigate the contribution of each component of the proposed algorithm. However, one potential weakness of the paper is that it may not be easy to implement for practitioners without a strong background in Bayesian methods. Despite the clear description of the algorithm, some readers may still find it challenging to follow the mathematical notation and concepts used. In summary, this paper presents a well-designed Bayesian learning algorithm for RNNs that addresses the problem of overfitting in language modeling tasks. The experimental results are very promising, and the authors provide a clear explanation of the proposed algorithm. However, it may be challenging for practitioners without a strong background in Bayesian methods to implement the algorithm.

The authors present an interesting approach to zero pronoun resolution by generating pseudo training data from existing corpora, which is then used in combination with neural network models. I think that this work has several strengths including (1) it provides insights into how different types of information can be combined effectively, (2) it shows promise on real-world datasets such as Reddit comments, and (3) it presents results using multiple evaluation metrics showing consistent improvements over baseline systems. However I also have some concerns about their experimental setups. First, they use two separate sets of experiments - one where they generate new sentences containing pronouns based on original sentence templates; another experiment where they replace all nouns within a sentence template with pronouns. The former seems more realistic but both are not ideal since there may be subtle differences between these generated sentences compared to actual user-generated content. Second, while they report significant improvement across many measures when comparing against state-of-the-art baselines, it's unclear whether or not any of those gains were statistically significant due to lack of statistical tests reported throughout most sections of the manuscript. The authors propose to generate pseudo training data from existing corpora in order to improve zero pronoun resolution performance. They show that their approach outperforms state-of-the-art methods on two datasets (the OntoNotes 5 corpus of English news articles). The proposed method is simple yet effective; it can be easily extended beyond its current scope as well. Strengths: 1) Simple but powerful idea. 2) Easy-to-extend framework. Weaknesses: No discussion about how this could scale up with larger amounts of text. No comparison against other approaches using synthetic data generation techniques such as those described in: Synthetic data has been used extensively within NLP research communities over many years now - see e.g., http://aclweb.org/anthology-new/ The authors have done some good work here. The idea behind generating large scale pseudodata from Wikipedia articles seems very promising. However, I think there should be more discussion about how they generated their dataset (e.g., what was used as input? How were sentences selected?) so readers can understand exactly why these datasets may perform well or not. In addition, if possible, please provide examples of your outputted data along side other similar works such as those produced using WikiZH. Finally, you might want to consider adding additional experiments where you compare different methods of creating pseudodatasets against each other - e.g., do all types produce equally useful outputs? This would help demonstrate whether any particular method performs best overall. Overall though, it's great research! The authors present their work as follows: The current research status; Their own contribution (the new method); A comparison against other methods. This structure makes it easy to understand how they contribute over previous works. They also provide references so you can find more information about each topic if needed. Strengths: It provides clear explanations throughout the text. Weaknesses: ... In addition, I think there should be some discussion regarding why using only unlabeled data may not lead to good results when compared to labeled ones. For example, "However, since no explicit clue exists in these sentences,..." could have been written as: However, since no explicit clue exists within those sentences, ...". In fact, most people would probably agree that without any clues at all it's hard to guess where pronouns might appear!

This paper delves into the mathematical properties of the skip-gram model, explaining the reason for its success on the analogy task and for the general superiority of additive composition models. It also establishes a link between skip-gram and Sufficient Dimensionality Reduction. I liked the focus of this paper on explaining the properties of skip-gram, and generally found it inspiring to read. I very much appreciate the effort to understand the assumptions of the model, and the way it affects (or is affected by) the composition operations that it is used to perform. In that respect, I think it is a very worthwhile read for the community. My main criticism is however that the paper is linguistically rather naive. The authors' use of 'compositionality' (as an operation that takes a set of words and returns another with the same meaning) is extremely strange. Two words can of course be composed and produce a vector that is a) far away from both; b) does not correspond to any other concept in the space; c) still has meaning (productivity wouldn't exist otherwise!) Compositionality in linguistic terms simply refers to the process of combining linguistic constituents to produce higher-level constructs. It does not assume any further constraint, apart from some vague (and debatable) notion of semantic transparency. The paper's implication (l254) that composition takes place over sets is also wrong: ordering matters hugely (e.g. 'sugar cane' is not 'cane sugar'). This is a well-known shortcoming of additive composition. Another important aspect is that there are pragmatic factors that make humans prefer certain phrases to single words in particular contexts (and the opposite), naturally changing the underlying distribution of words in a large corpus. For instance, talking of a 'male royalty' rather than a 'king' or 'prince' usually has implications with regard to the intent of the speaker (here, perhaps highlighting a gender difference). This means that the equation in l258 (or for that matter the KL-divergence modification) does not hold, not because of noise in the data, but because of fundamental linguistic processes. This point may be addressed by the section on SDR, but I am not completely sure (see my comments below). In a nutshell, I think the way that the authors present composition is flawed, but the paper convinces me that this is indeed what happens in skip-gram, and I think this is an interesting contribution. The part about Sufficient Dimensionality Reduction seems a little disconnected from the previous argument as it stands. I'm afraid I wasn't able to fully follow the argument, and I would be grateful for some clarification in the authors' response. If I understand it well, the argument is that skip-gram produces a model where a word's neighbours follow some exponential parametrisation of a categorical distribution, but it is unclear whether this actually reflects the distribution of the corpus (as opposed to what happens in, say, a pure count-based model). The fact that skip-gram performs well despite not reflecting the data is that it implements some form of SDR, which does not need to make any assumption about the underlying form of the data. But then, is it fair to say that the resulting representations are optimised for tasks where geometrical regularities are important, regardless of the actual pattern of the data? I.e. there some kind of denoising going on? Minor comments: - The abstract is unusually long and could, I think, be shortened. - para starting l71: I think it would be misconstrued to see circularity here. Firth observed that co-occurrence effects were correlated with similarity judgements, but those judgements are the very cognitive processes that we are trying to model with statistical methods. Co-occurrence effects and vector space word representations are in some sense 'the same thing', modelling an underlying linguistic process we do not have direct observations for. So pair-wise similarity is not there to break any circularity, it is there because it better models the kind of judgements humans known to make. - l296: I think 'paraphrase' would be a better word than 'synonym' here, given that we are comparing a set of words with a unique lexical item. - para starting l322: this is interesting, and actually, a lot of the zipfian distribution (the long tail) is fairly uniform. - l336: it is probably worth pointing out that the analogy relation does not hold so well in practice and requires to 'ignore' the first returned neighbour of the analogy computation (which is usually one of the observed terms). - para starting l343: I don't find it so intuitive to say that 'man' would be a synonym/paraphrase of anything involving 'woman'. The subtraction involved in the analogy computation is precisely not a straightforward composition operation, as it involves an implicit negation. - A last, tiny general comment. It is usual to write p(w|c) to mean the probability of a word given a context, but in the paper 'w' is actually the context and 'c' the target word. It makes reading a little bit harder... Perhaps change the notation? Literature: The claim that Arora (2016) is the only work to try and understand vector composition is a bit strong. For instance, see the work by Paperno & Baroni on explaining the success of addition as a composition method over PMI-weighted vectors: D. Paperno and M. Baroni. 2016. When the whole is less than the sum of its parts: How composition affects PMI values in distributional semantic vectors. Computational Linguistics 42(2): 345-350. *** I thank the authors for their response and hope to see this paper accepted.

The paper "Towards Principled Methods for Training Generative Adversarial Networks" proposes a new framework for training Generative Adversarial Networks (GANs). The authors propose a new loss function for GANs that is based on the Wasserstein metric. This new loss function is designed to be more stable and consistent than the traditional GAN loss function. The authors also propose a new training method for GANs that is based on the theory of cursed distributions. This new training method is designed to improve the quality of the generated images. Overall, the paper presents a new and promising approach to training GANs. However, there are some concerns about the proposed method. The Wasserstein loss function has been shown to be less stable than the traditional GAN loss function when used in combination with the proposed training method. This paper addresses the problem of training Generative Adversarial Networks (GANs) in a principled manner. The authors propose a new method for training GANs that is based on the principle of maximum mean discrepancy (MMD). The MMD is a measure of the distance between two distributions, and the authors use it to measure the distance between the real data distribution and the generated data distribution. The paper is well-written and the authors make a clear case for the importance of training GANs in a principled manner. However, there are a few weaknesses in the paper. First, the authors do not provide a clear definition of what they mean by a "principled" training method. Second, the paper does not provide any new theoretical results or proofs. Finally, the paper does not provide any experimental results to back up the claims made in the paper. This paper presents a theoretical analysis of the problems that arise when training Generative Adversarial Networks (GANs), and proposes a practical solution based on these theoretical results. The authors introduce new tools for analyzing and evaluating GAN training processes, and demonstrate their effectiveness in addressing issues such as instability and saturation. Overall, this paper makes significant contributions to the understanding of GAN training dynamics and provides a practical approach for improving their performance. This paper addresses the problem of instability and saturation when training generative adversarial networks. The authors introduce a theoretical framework for understanding these issues and provide evidence to support their claims. Strengths: - This paper takes a rigorous approach to understanding the problems of instability and saturation in training generative adversarial networks. The authors present a theoretical framework that explains these issues and provides evidence to support their claims. Weaknesses: - The authors do not provide a complete solution to the problem of instability and saturation in training generative adversarial networks. Rather, they present a direction towards solving these problems. Overall, this paper makes important theoretical contributions towards understanding the training dynamics of generative adversarial networks.

Overall this is a well-written paper. I have a few suggestions for the improve the paper. 1) You need to justify the choice of evaluating on CIFAR-10. CIFAR-10 is not a standard image classification dataset. There are many standard image classification datasets, such as ImageNet. CIFAR-10 was popular a few years ago, but it has been superseded by more modern datasets such as ImageNet. 2) The numerical analysis for the paper could be strengthened. In particular, the analysis of the “winner” operations should be included in the paper. The “winner” operations are commonly used in numerical computing, and they have a big impact on numerical precision. I would be interested to know the impact of these operations on the precisions of the tuned network. 183 155 147 139 129 115 101 95 93 91 89 87 85 83 81 79 77 75 71 67 65 63 61 59 59 55 51 47 45 43 39 37 35 33 31 29 27 25 23 21 19 17 15 13 11 9 7 5 3 America/New_York winner: lg(2*x+1). loser: 1/abs(x) aly findings: When using winner operations, the maximum absolute difference is less than 1e-14. When not using winner operations, the maximum absolute difference is greater than 1e-16. 2) The overhead of using winner operations could be eliminated by wrapping all functions in the compute graph in a domain specific compute context. Tartan: Accelerating Fully-Connected and Convolutional Layers in Deep Learning Networks by Exploiting Numerical Precision Variability is an interesting paper that explores the idea of improving the performance of deep learning models by representing weights and gradients with higher precision. The proposed Tartan protocol is to increase the bit width of precisions used to representation weight and gradient values. More specifically, it uses a decimal (base 10) floating point representation for weights and gradians with precision of digits like 0.1 and uses binary (base 2) floating point representation with higher precision digits like 0.5. By switching to a higher precision representation for weights and gradians the authors show that they are able to speed up training of certain models by 10% or more. The major strength of this approach is that it shows promise of a broad class of models for improvement. There are only a limited number of special cases where it works and the authors don't offer much explanation as to why it works in those limited special cases. This paper has some important limitations too. The authors only tested their approach on a relatively small subset of models and network architectures. They also didn't show that the performance gains from switching to Tartans representation were statistically significant. Overall, I think this is an interesting idea and it's something worth exploring for those interested in the trade offs between algorithmic implementation and numerical precision in neural network training. Tartan {TRT} a hardware accelerator for inference with Deep Neural Networks (DNNs) is presented and evaluated on Convolutional Neural Networks. TRT exploits the variable per layer precision requirements of DNNs to deliver execution time that is proportional to the precision p in bits used per layer for convolutional and fully-connected layers. Prior art has demonstrated an accelerator with the same execution performance only for convolutional layers. Experiments on image classification CNNs show that on average across all networks studied, TRT outperforms a state-of-the-art bit-parallel accelerator by 1.90x without any loss in accuracy while it is 1.17x more energy efficient. TRT requires no network retraining while it enables trading off accuracy for additional improvements in execution performance and energy efficiency. For example, if a 1% relative loss in accuracy is acceptable, TRT is on average 2.04x faster and 1.25x more energy efficient than the bit-parallel accelerator. This revision includes post-layout results and a better configuration that processes 2bits at time resulting in better efficiency and lower area overhead. The main contents of the paper are well described. I recommend to publish this paper in the IJCAI Proceedings. Nevertheless, I have some minor points that could be improved: 1- In page 3, first line, I don't understand why the author doesn't use "S FP" instead of "S/he FP" (or maybe "S FP"). 2- I would like to see some results for other types of DNNs than Convolutional Neural Networks (e.g. Recurrent Neural Networks). Otherwise, I recommend to accept this paper. Best regards, Juan F. Santos. PS: I assume the Tartan {TRT} stands for "TartanRunTime". Reference: 1.
