The following is the main content: Tartan {TRT} a hardware accelerator for inference with Deep Neural Networks (DNNs) is presented and evaluated on Convolutional Neural Networks. TRT exploits the variable per layer precision requirements of DNNs to deliver execution time that is proportional to the precision p in bits used per layer for convolutional and fully-connected layers. Prior art has demonstrated an accelerator with the same execution performance only for convolutional layers. Experiments show that on average across all networks studied, TRT outperforms a state-of-the-art bit-parallel accelerator by 1.90x without any loss in accuracy while it is 1.17x more energy efficient. TRT requires no network retraining while it enables trading off accuracy for additional improvements in execution performance and energy efficiency. For example, if a 1% relative loss in accuracy is acceptable, TRT is on average 2.04x faster and 1.25x more energy efficient than the bit-parallel accelerator. Post-layout results and a better configuration that processes 2bits at a time resulting in better efficiency and lower area overhead are included. This work was done during the post-doc period of Aaron Schultz at UC Berkeley.

Overall, the paper presents an interesting approach to coarse pruning of convolutional neural networks using random masks. The authors propose a method for selecting and applying these masks to reduce the size of the network with minimal loss in accuracy. One strength of the paper is the thorough evaluation of the proposed approach on several benchmark datasets such as CIFAR-10, CIFAR-100, and ImageNet. The results clearly show that the approach is effective in reducing the complexity of the networks while maintaining high accuracy. However, there are a few areas where the paper could be improved. Firstly, the authors need to provide more detailed information regarding the computational cost of applying the random masks. This would help readers better understand the practical implications of this approach and its feasibility for real-world applications. In addition, the paper would be improved by providing more detailed analysis on the effects of different mask selection methods on network accuracy. The authors could perform statistical analysis to identify which mask selection method is the most effective in achieving the desired level of accuracy. Overall, this paper presents an interesting approach to pruning convolutional neural networks and provides valuable insights into the effects of using random masks. With some minor improvements, this paper has the potential to be a significant contribution to the field of deep learning. The paper "Coarse Pruning of Convolutional Neural Networks with Random Masks" addresses the problem of reducing the computational and storage requirements of deep convolutional neural networks (CNNs) without sacrificing their performance. The authors proposed a new approach called "coarse pruning" that randomly removes entire convolutional channels from a CNN, and showed that it can achieve comparable accuracy to fine-grained pruning while being much faster and easier to implement. The strengths of this paper include its clear and concise presentation of the proposed method, as well as the thorough experimental evaluation of the method on several benchmark datasets and CNN architectures. The paper also provides a detailed analysis of the trade-off between pruning rate, accuracy, and computational cost, which can be helpful for practitioners to choose the appropriate pruning strategy for their specific application. However, there are also a few weaknesses in this paper. Firstly, the authors did not compare their method with other state-of-the-art pruning methods, such as structured pruning or weight pruning, which could potentially have higher compression rates or even better accuracy. Secondly, the paper did not provide a theoretical justification for why the proposed random mask-based pruning works, which could make the method less convincing to some readers. Finally, the paper could benefit from providing more detailed explanations of some technical terms and methods used in the paper, such as "batch normalization" and "re-parameterization". Overall, "Coarse Pruning of Convolutional Neural Networks with Random Masks" is a well-written and interesting paper that proposes a novel method for CNN compression and achieves promising results. Yet, there are also some areas that could be improved in future iterations of the paper. This paper presents a technique for reducing the computational complexity of deep convolutional neural networks through feature map and kernel level pruning. The authors propose a strategy for selecting the least adversarial pruning masks by generating random masks and selecting the best performing one using the evaluation set. The proposed approach is evaluated on CIFAR-10, SVHN, and MNIST datasets, showing promising results with more than 85% sparsity induced in the convolution layers and less than 1% increase in the misclassification rate of the baseline network. Overall, the paper is well-written and the proposed approach is clearly explained. The evaluation on three different datasets adds to the generalizability of the proposed approach. However, there are a few areas that could be improved. First, the authors could provide more details on the selection criteria for the evaluation set. How was the evaluation set selected and what was the reasoning behind this selection? Second, it would be helpful if the authors provided more information on the computational complexity reduction achieved by their approach. For instance, how does the proposed approach compare to other state-of-the-art pruning techniques? Lastly, it would be valuable to include a discussion on potential limitations of the proposed approach. For example, do the proposed random masks always result in good pruning? Are there any scenarios in which this approach might not be effective? In summary, the proposed approach shows promising results and is well-explained. However, additional information on the evaluation set selection criteria, comparison with other state-of-the-art techniques, and potential limitations would strengthen the paper. The paper "Coarse Pruning of Convolutional Neural Networks with Random Masks" proposes a novel approach to reduce the computational complexity of deep convolutional neural networks by pruning the feature maps and kernels. The motivation behind this work is that deep neural networks with a large number of layers and dense kernel connectivity patterns require higher computational costs, which may hinder real-time inference. This paper claims to address this issue by introducing a simple and generic strategy for selecting good pruning masks. The authors have presented their arguments effectively and demonstrated the effectiveness of the proposed approach through experimental results. The authors used three datasets - CIFAR-10, SVHN, and MNIST - to conduct extensive evaluations and demonstrated that more than 85% sparsity can be induced in the convolution layers with less than 1% increase in the misclassification rate of the baseline network. One of the strengths of this paper is the use of multiple datasets for evaluation and experimentally showing that the proposed approach can provide substantial reduction in computational complexity without compromising accuracy. Besides, the proposed approach is generic, simple, and can be easily integrated with GPUs and VLSI based implementations. However, there are also some weaknesses in the paper that need to be addressed. First, the paper does not give detailed information on the selection procedure of the hyperparameters used in the experiments, which raises concerns about the reproducibility of the results. Second, the complexity reduction ratios that are analyzed in this work (between 40% and 80%) are relatively high. Therefore, the effectiveness and applicability of this approach for smaller complexity reductions may be limited. Finally, the paper's title implies that it relates to coarse pruning; however, the proposed approach does not explicitly focus on coarse-level pruning. That is, the paper focuses on both coarse and fine-grained pruning, which should be clarified. Overall, the proposed approach seems promising in reducing the computational complexity of deep convolutional neural networks, and the authors have demonstrated its effectiveness through experimental evidence. The paper's strengths outweigh its weaknesses, and it would be valuable for researchers interested in reducing computational complexity of deep learning models. However, the paper needs some revisions to clarify the experimental setup and the focus of the proposed technique.

This paper investigates three simple weight-pruning techniques for NMT, and shows that pruning weights based on magnitude works best, and that retraining after pruning can recover original performance, even with fairly severe pruning. The main strength of paper is that the technique is very straightforward and the results are good. Itâs also clearly written and does a nice job covering previous work. A weakness is that the work isnât very novel, being just an application of a known technique to a new kind of neural net and application (namely NMT), with results that arenât very surprising. Itâs not clear to me what practical significance these results have, since to take advantage of them you would need sparse matrix representations, which are trickier to get working fast on a GPU - and after all, speed is the main problem with NMT, not space. (There may be new work that changes this picture, since the field is evolving fast, but if so you need to describe it, and generally do a better job explaining why we should care about pruning.) A suggestion for dealing with the above weakness would be to use the pruning results to inform architecture changes. For instance, figure 3 suggests that you might be able to reduce the number of hidden layers to two, and also potentially reduce the dimension of source and target embeddings. Another suggestion is that you try to make a link between pruning+retraining and dropout (eg âA Theoretically Grounded Application of Dropout in Recurrent Neural Networksâ, Gal, arXiv 2016). Detailed comments: Line 111: âsoftmax weightsâ - âoutput embeddingsâ may be a preferable term S3.2: Itâs misleading to call n the âdimensionâ of the network, and specify all parameter sizes as integer multiples of this number as if this were a logical constraint. Line 319: You should cite Bahdanau et al here for the attention idea, rather than Luong for their use of it. S3.3: Class-uniform and class-distribution seem very similar (and naturally get very similar results); consider dropping one or the other. Figure 3 suggestion that you could hybridize pruning: use class-blind for most classes, but class-uniform for the embeddings. Figure 4 should show perplexity too. What pruning is used in section 4.2 & figure 6? Figure 7: does loss pertain to training or test corpora? Figure 8: This seems to be missing softmax weights. I found this diagram somewhat hard to interpret; it might be better to give relevant statistics, such as the proportion of each class that is removed by class-blind pruning at various levels. Line 762: You might want to cite Le et al, âA Simple Way to Initialize Recurrent Networks of Rectified Linear Unitsâ, arXiv 2015. This paper applies the idea of translation model pruning to neural MT. The authors explore three simple threshold and histogram pruning schemes, two of which are applied separately to each weight class, while the third is applied to the entire model. The authors also show that retraining the models produces performance equal to the full model, even when 90% of the weights are pruned. An extensive analysis explains the superiority of the class-blind pruning scheme, as well as the performance boost through retraining. While the main idea of the paper is simple, it seems quite useful for memory-restricted applications of NMT. I particularly liked the analysis section which gives further insight into the model components that are usually treated like black boxes. While these insights are interesting by themselves, the paper's main motivation is model compression. This argument would be stronger if the paper included some numbers on actual memory consumption of the compressed model in comparison to the uncompressed model. Some minor remarks: - There is a substantial amount of work on pruning translation models in phrase-based SMT, which could be referenced in related work, e.g. Johnson, J., Martin, J., Foster, G. and Kuhn, R.: Improving Translation Quality by Discarding Most of the Phrasetable. EMNLP 07 or Zens, R., Stanton, D. and Peng X.: A Systematic Comparison of Phrase Table Pruning Techniques. EMNLP 12 - It took me a while to understand Figure 5. I would find it more informative to add an additional barplot under figure 4 showing highest discarded weight magnitude by class. This would also allow a comparison across all pruning methods.

The authors present an interesting approach to compress deep neural networks using homological hashing, which is based on computing persistent cohomology of graphs representing DNNs. I think that this work can be improved in several ways. First and foremost I would like them to provide more details about how they compute persistence diagrams (PD) from PDEs. The main idea behind their algorithm seems very promising but it lacks some explanation as far as what exactly happens during each step. Second thing I'd suggest improving are experiments with different types of data sets - currently only MNIST was used so it's hard to judge whether or not there will be any problems when applying HFH method to other datasets such as CIFAR-10/100 etc. Finally, one could try adding additional layers into the network before compression takes place since these might contain useful information too. The authors propose an algorithm to compress deep neural networks using homological functional hashing (HFH). The main idea is that they use HFH as a hash function in order to map similar neurons into one neuron. This work makes several contributions compared with previous works on network compression algorithms such as: 1) It can be applied directly without any preprocessing of data. 2) It does not require training another model before applying it. 3) It provides better results than other state-of-the-art methods when tested against MNIST dataset. However there are some issues which need further investigation: 1) How do we know if this method will perform well enough? This needs more experiments testing different datasets. 2) What happens during the process of mapping similar neurons together? Does it affect accuracy? Strengths: The authors present an interesting idea about using "homologous" information across different layers to achieve higher compression rates while maintaining good quality predictions. I think this work could be further extended along two directions. First, I would like to see how well does their method perform when applied directly at inference time rather than training-time only. In other words, can we use the compressed model without retraining? Second, what happens if you don't have access to the original uncompressed weights? Can your algorithm still produce reasonable results or do they need to know exactly which layer's output should go where before starting up? Overall, it's very promising research! Thanks again! Yes thanks for reading our manuscript ! We will try these questions soon . Thanks again! The authors present an interesting idea about using homology theory to reduce storage cost while maintaining model quality. They also provide some experimental results showing their method works well compared against other state-of-the-art methods. However, there are still many open questions regarding this work. First, how does the author choose which layer should be compressed? It seems like they pick random weights at different depths during training time but I am not sure if that's true. Second, why do we need two separate models - one for fetching value and another for recovering them? Why can't these two steps happen simultaneously? Third, what's the advantage over existing techniques such as quantization? I think it's worthwhile to explore further! Strengths: Weaknesses: 1. How can you select which layer will get compressed? 2. What happens when both fetching and recovery occur together?

General Comments: The paper "Gated-Attention Readers for Text Comprehension" presents a novel approach for text comprehension using a hierarchical neural network architecture that leverages both word-level and sentence-level attention mechanisms. The authors compare their approach to several state-of-the-art models and show significant improvements in comprehension accuracy on several datasets. The paper is well-written and provides a thorough explanation of the proposed method, experiments, and results. Overall, the paper presents a promising approach for text comprehension and is a valuable contribution to the field of NLP. Specific Comments: - The introduction section of the paper provides a clear motivation and rationale for the proposed method. However, it would be helpful to add a brief summary of related work in this area. - The hierarchical attention mechanism proposed in this paper is well-motivated and explained. However, it would be helpful to provide additional justification for the use of gated attention mechanisms and how they differ from traditional attention mechanisms. - The experiments in the paper are well-designed, and the results support the effectiveness of the proposed approach. However, the authors could provide additional insights into why the proposed approach works better than the baseline models. For example, what specific aspects of the hierarchical attention mechanism are critical for achieving better performance. - The paper could potentially benefit from additional visual aids, such as diagrams or tables, to help illustrate the proposed architecture and results more clearly. Overall, the paper provides a valuable contribution to the field of NLP and presents a promising approach for text comprehension. With some minor revisions and additional insights, this paper could become a significant reference for researchers working in this area. This paper by Dhingra et al., titled "Gated-Attention Readers for Text Comprehension" addresses the problem of text comprehension. The authors introduce a model that combines two techniques: gated attention and a character-level convolutional neural network (CNN) for better text comprehension. The strengths of this paper lie in the thorough evaluation of the proposed model with various benchmark datasets, where the proposed model significantly outperformed existing state-of-the-art models. The authors also effectively explain the motivations behind the proposed model and their design choices for creating gated-attention readers. However, one of the weaknesses of this paper is that it could benefit from more detailed and explicit explanations of how certain design choices affect the overall performance of the model. For instance, in presenting the results, it would be helpful if the authors specifically analyze how different parts of the model contribute to the overall performance. Additionally, though the paper cites previous work, it does not provide adequate references or comparative analysis to competing works in the field, which could help readers establish the uniqueness and superiority of the proposed model. Overall, the proposed model in this paper represents a significant contribution to the field of text comprehension. With its strong empirical results, the model has the potential to be widely adopted by researchers and industry practitioners seeking to improve text comprehension in various domains. The paper "Gated-Attention Readers for Text Comprehension" proposes a novel approach for answering cloze-style questions over documents using the Gated-Attention (GA) Reader. The model integrates a multi-hop architecture with a novel attention mechanism that leverages multiplicative interactions between the query embedding and the intermediate states of a recurrent neural network document reader. The results demonstrate that the proposed approach achieves state-of-the-art results on three benchmarks for this task: CNN & Daily Mail news stories and the Who Did What dataset. The paper is well-written, and the proposed approach is motivated by previous work on attention mechanisms for text comprehension tasks. The evaluation section is thorough, and the authors provide a detailed analysis of the performance of the proposed approach. In particular, the ablation study and comparison to alternative compositional operators help to demonstrate the effectiveness of multiplicative interaction used in the attention mechanism. One potential limitation of the approach is that the model requires the input document to be in the form of individual tokens, rather than word vectors or contextualized representations such as BERT. However, the authors note that this is common in the cloze-style question answering task and provide a detailed comparison with alternative models that use contextualized embeddings. Overall, the proposed approach is promising and achieves state-of-the-art results on the evaluated benchmarks. The paper provides a clear motivation for the approach, a detailed description of the model, and a thorough evaluation section. The results demonstrate that the proposed approach can effectively leverage attention mechanisms to improve text comprehension models. The paper "Gated-Attention Readers for Text Comprehension" addresses the problem of answering cloze-style questions over documents. The authors propose a novel attention mechanism, based on multiplicative interactions between query embeddings and the intermediate states of a recurrent neural network document reader, to build query-specific representations for accurate answer selection. The authors evaluate their Gated-Attention (GA) Reader on three benchmarks, including the CNN & Daily Mail news stories and the Who Did What dataset, and show that it achieves state-of-the-art results. The paper has several strengths. First, the authors clearly explain the motivation, problem statement, and proposed method. Second, the paper is technically sound, and the approach is well-designed and thoroughly evaluated. Third, the evaluation covers multiple datasets and shows significant improvements over previous state-of-the-art models. Finally, the paper includes an ablation study to demonstrate the effectiveness of the proposed approach and compare it against alternative compositional operators. However, there are also some weaknesses in the paper. First, the paper could benefit from more extensive analysis of the experiment results to provide insights into why and how the proposed model works better than previous state-of-the-art models. Second, the authors do not compare their approach against non-deep learning methods or non-attention-based models, limiting the comparison scope. Finally, although the proposed model achieves state-of-the-art performance on the chosen benchmarks, the paper does not discuss how this approach can scale beyond the particular datasets used in the experiments, and whether there could be potential limitations or challenges when applying this approach to other domains or tasks. Overall, "Gated-Attention Readers for Text Comprehension" is a well-written and well-executed paper that presents a novel approach to document comprehension and achieves state-of-the-art results on the chosen benchmarks. The strengths of the paper outweigh the weaknesses, and I recommend it for publication with minor revisions.

The paper describes an MT training data selection approach that scores and ranks general-domain sentences using a CNN classifier. Comparison to prior work using continuous or n-gram based language models is well done, even though it is not clear of the paper also compared against bilingual data selection (e.g. sum of difference of cross-entropies). The motivation to use a CNN instead of an RNN/LSTM was first unclear to me, but it is a strength of the paper to argue that certain sections of a text/sentence are more important than others and this is achieved by a CNN. However, the paper does not experimentally show whether a BOW or SEQ (or the combination of both( representation is more important and why. The textual description of the CNN (one-hot or semi-supervised using pre-trained embeddings) is clear, detailed, and points out the important aspects. However, a picture of the layers showing how inputs are combined would be worth a thousand words. The paper is overall well written, but some parentheses for citations are not necessary (\citet vs. \citep) (e.g line 385). Experiments and evaluation support the claims of the paper, but I am a little bit concerned about the method of determining the number of selected in-domain sentences (line 443) based on a separate validation set: - What validation data is used here? It is also not clear on what data hyperparameters of the CNN models are chosen. How sensitive are the models to this? - Table 2 should really compare scores of different approaches with the same number of sentences selected. As Figure 1 shows, the approach of the paper still seems to outperform the baselines in this case. Other comments: - I would be interested in an experiment that compares the technique of the paper against baselines when more in-domain data is available, not just the development set. - The results or discussion section could feature some example sentences selected by the different methods to support the claims made in section 5.4. - In regards to the argument of abstracting away from surface forms in 5.4: Another baseline to compare against could have been the work of Axelrod, 2015, who replace some words with POS tags to reduce LM data sparsity to see whether the word2vec embeddings provide an additional advantage over this. - Using the sum of source and target classification scores is very similar to source & target Lewis-Moore LM data selection: sum of difference of cross-entropies. A reference to this work around line 435 would be reasonable. Finally, I wonder if you could learn weights for the sum of both source & target classification scores by extending the CNN model to the bilingual/parallel setting. The paper describes a method for in-domain data selection for SMT with a convolutional neural network classifier, applying the same framework as Johnson and Zhang, 2015. The method performs about 0.5 BLEU points better than language model based data selection, and, unlike the other methods, is robust even if only a very small in-domain data set is provided. The paper claims improvements of 3.1 BLEU points. However, from the results we see that improvements of this magnitude are only achieved if there are in-domain data in the training set - training only on the in-domain data already produces +2.8 BLEU. It might be interesting to also compare this to a system which interpolates separate in- and out-domain models. The more impressive result, in my opinion, comes from the second experiment, which demonstrates that the CNN classifier is still effective if there is very little in-domain data. However, the second experiment is only run on the zh2en task which includes actual in-domain data in the training set, possibly making selection easier. Would the result also hold for the other tasks, where there is no in-domain data in the training set? The results for the en2es and en2zh task already point in this direction, since the development sets only contain a few hundred sentence pairs. I think the claim would be better supported if results were reported for all tasks when only 100 sentence pairs are used for training. When translating social media text one often has to face very different problems from other domains, the most striking being a high OOV rate due to non-conventional spelling (for Latin scripts, at least). The texts can also contain special character sequences such as usernames, hashtags or emoticons. Was there any special preprocessing or filtering step applied to the data? Since data selection cannot address the OOV problem, it would be interesting to know in more detail what kinds of improvements are made through adaptation via data selection, maybe by providing examples. The following remarks concern specific sections: Section 3.2: - It could be made clearer how the different vectors (word embeddings, segment vectors and one-hot vectors) are combined in the model. An illustration of the architecture would be very helpful. - What was the "designated loss function"? Section 5.2: For completeness' sake, it could be mentioned how the system weights were tuned.

Important task (attention models), interesting distillation application, well-written paper. The authors have been responsive in updating the paper, adding new experiments, and being balanced in presenting their findings. I support accepting this paper. We would like to thank the reviewers for their comments and for providing us with valuable feedback. We already updated the paper with new results on ImageNet, and are also going to further update the paper based on the reviewer comments as explained in our responses below. We also plan to release the code for our experiments next week. The paper proposes a new way of transferring knowledge. I like the idea of transferring attention maps instead of activations. However, the experiments don’t show a big improvement compared with knowledge distillation alone and I think more experiments are required in IMAGENET section. I would consider updating the score if the authors extend the last section 4.2.2. The paper presented a modified knowledge distillation framework that minimizes the difference of the sum of statistics across the a feature map between the teacher and the student network. The authors empirically demonstrated the proposed methods outperform the fitnet style distillation baseline. Pros: + The author evaluated the proposed methods on various computer vision dataset + The paper is in general well-written Cons: - The method seems to be limited to the convolutional architecture - The attention terminology is misleading in the paper. The proposed method really just try to distill the summed squared(or other statistics e.g. summed lp norm) of activations in a hidden feature map. - The gradient-based attention transfer seems out-of-place. The proposed gradient-based methods are never compared directly to nor are used jointly with the "attention-based" transfer. It seems like a parallel idea added to the paper that does not seem to add much value. - It is also not clear how the induced 2-norms in eq.(2) is computed. Q is a matrix \in \mathbb{R}^{H \times W} whose induced 2-norm is its largest singular value. It seems computationally expensive to compute such cost function. Is it possible the authors really mean the Frobenius norm? Overall, the proposed distillation method works well in practice but the paper has some organization issues and unclear notation. This paper proposes to investigate attention transfers between a teacher and a student network. Attention transfer is performed by minimising the l2 distance between the teacher/student attention maps at different layers, in addition to minimising the classification loss and optionally a knowledge distillation term. Authors define several activation based attentions (sum of absolute feature values raise at the power p or max of values raised at the power p). They also propose a gradient based attention (derivative of the Loss w.r.t. inputs). They evaluate their approaches on several datasets (CIFAR, Cub/Scene, Imagenet) showing that attention transfers does help improving the student network test performance. However, the student networks performs worst than the teacher, even with attention. Few remarks/questions: - in section 3 authors claim that networks with higher accuracy have a higher spatial correlation between the object and the attention map. While Figure 4 is compelling, it would be nice to have quantitative results showing that as well. - how did you choose the hyperparameter values, it would be nice to see what is the impact of $\beta$. - it would be nice to report teacher train and validation loss in Figure 7 b) - from the experiments, it is not clear what at the pros/cons of the different attention maps - AT does not lead to better result than the teacher. However, the student networks have less parameters. It would be interesting to characterise the corresponding speed-up. If you keep the same architecture between the student and the teacher, is there any benefit to the attention transfer? In summary: Pros: - Clearly written and well motivated. - Consistent improvement of the student with attention compared to the student alone. Cons: - Students have worst performances than the teacher models. - It is not clear which attention to use in which case? - Somewhat incremental novelty relatively to Fitnet Hi, Sergey. I am confused about the definition of activation tensor in section 3.1. Is it obtained before or after ReLU activation function? If it's got after ReLu, there is no need adding absolute function. Thanks!

This paper proposes a novel approach to training group orthogonal neural networks with privileged information. The authors introduce a new regularization technique that utilizes privileged information to improve the generalization performance of group orthogonal neural networks. The proposed method is evaluated on three benchmark datasets, and the results show that the proposed approach significantly improves the generalization performance of the model. Overall, the paper presents a promising approach to training group orthogonal neural networks with privileged information. However, there are some areas that could be improved. - The paper does not clearly explain how the privileged information is incorporated into the model. It is not clear how the privileged information is used to regularize the model. - The evaluation of the proposed approach is limited to three benchmark datasets. It would be helpful to evaluate the performance of the model on a broader range of datasets. - The authors do not compare the performance of the proposed approach to other state-of-the-art regularization techniques. It would be helpful to include a comparison to other regularization techniques to assess the effectiveness of the proposed approach. This paper addresses the problem of training group orthogonal neural networks with privileged information. The paper does a good job of introducing the problem and providing a clear motivation for why this problem is important. The paper also provides a good overview of the state-of-the-art in this area. However, the paper does not provide any new results or insights that are not already known. In addition, the paper does not provide any practical guidance on how to train group orthogonal neural networks with privileged information. The paper proposes a novel convolutional neural network (CNN) model that is able to maximize inherent diversity of a CNN model such that the model can learn better feature representation with a stronger generalization ability. The paper's main contribution is a group orthogonal convolutional neural network (GoCNN) that learns features from foreground and background in an orthogonal way by exploiting privileged information for optimization, which automatically emphasizes feature diversity within a single model. The reviewers felt that the paper is overall well-written and the proposed method is interesting and has potential. However, they had several concerns and suggestions for improvement. They suggested that the paper should include a more thorough literature review, discussing previous work in the field of privileged learning. The reviewers also suggested that the paper should provide more details on how the privileged information is incorporated into the training process, and how it helps to improve the model's generalization ability. Additionally, the reviewers suggested that the paper should include more quantitative results and comparisons to other state-of-the-art models to demonstrate the effectiveness of the proposed method. This paper addresses the problem of how to learn rich and diverse feature representations for deep convolutional neural networks (CNNs) while making use of auxiliary annotations that are available for specific data. The authors propose a novel CNN model that is able to maximize the inherent diversity of a CNN model such that the model can learn better feature representation with stronger generalization ability. More specifically, they propose a group orthogonal convolutional neural network (GoCNN) to learn features from foreground and background in an orthogonal way by exploiting privileged information for optimization, which automatically emphasizes feature diversity within a single model. Experiments on two benchmark datasets, ImageNet and PASCAL VOC, well demonstrate the effectiveness and high generalization ability of the proposed GoCNN models. Strengths: - The paper presents a novel CNN model that is able to maximize the inherent diversity of a CNN model while making use of auxiliary annotations that are available for specific data. This is done by proposing a group orthogonal convolutional neural network (GoCNN) that learns features from foreground and background in an orthogonal way by exploiting privileged information for optimization, which automatically emphasizes feature diversity within a single model. - The experimental results on two benchmark datasets, ImageNet and PASCAL VOC, demonstrate the effectiveness and high generalization ability of the proposed GoCNN models. Weaknesses: - The paper does not provide any theoretical background or analysis of the proposed model. - The paper does not present any comparison with other state-of-the-art CNN models on the same datasets. - The code and pre-trained models are not made available to the public.

This paper presents a novel approach to kernel machines, called Compositional Kernel Machines (CKMs). The authors propose a method for combining multiple kernels into a single kernel, which can then be used to construct a kernel machine. The authors demonstrate the effectiveness of CKMs on a variety of datasets, showing that CKMs can outperform traditional kernel machines. The paper is well-written and clearly presented. The authors begin by introducing the concept of CKMs and providing an overview of the method. They then discuss the construction of CKMs, including the different types of kernels that can be used, the combination of kernels, and the optimization of the parameters of the CKM. The authors then present the results of their experiments, showing that CKMs can outperform traditional kernel machines on a variety of datasets. Overall, the paper is a well-written and comprehensive introduction to the concept of CKMs. The authors provide a clear explanation of the method and demonstrate its effectiveness with a variety of experiments. The paper is well-structured and easy to follow, and the authors have provided a thorough discussion of their results. I would recommend this paper to anyone interested in learning more about CKMs. This paper addresses the problem of using kernel machines for compositional data. Kernel machines have the potential to be used for data with varying composition, but existing algorithms are not designed to handle this type of data. The authors propose a new method, called Compositional Kernel Machines (CKMs), which can handle compositional data. The paper has several strengths. The authors provide a clear and concise description of the problem they are trying to address and the proposed solution. The paper also includes a detailed explanation of the CKM algorithm, including a description of the steps involved and a discussion of the complexity of the algorithm. Additionally, the authors provide a thorough evaluation of the proposed method, including a comparison to existing methods. The paper has several weaknesses. The authors do not provide any theoretical analysis of the proposed method, which would be useful in understanding the performance of the algorithm. Additionally, the authors do not discuss the potential applications of the proposed method or how it could be used in practice. Finally, the authors do not provide any discussion of the potential limitations of the proposed method. This paper presents a novel approach to computer vision, Compositional Kernel Machines (CKMs). The authors provide an in-depth description of CKMs and how they improve upon existing methods such as convolutional neural networks (convnets) and support vector machines (SVMs). The authors demonstrate that CKMs can learn nonlinear concepts with easier training and architecture selection than convnets. In addition, CKMs can be used to combat the curse of dimensionality and provide invariance to translations and other symmetries. The paper is well-written and the authors provide a comprehensive overview of the CKM approach. The authors clearly explain the advantages of CKMs over existing methods and provide a detailed description of how CKMs work. The authors also provide convincing experimental results on the NORB datasets to demonstrate the effectiveness of CKMs. Overall, this paper provides an interesting and novel approach to computer vision. The authors present a well-written and comprehensive overview of the CKM approach and provide convincing experimental evidence of its effectiveness. This paper explores the potential of a new machine learning technique, compositional kernel machines (CKMs), as an alternative to convolutional neural networks (convnets) for object recognition and other vision problems. The paper examines the properties of CKMs, which are able to learn nonlinear concepts with fewer samples and without data augmentation, and presents promising results on NORB datasets. The paper's strengths include a clear description of the CKM technique and its potential advantages compared to convnets and SVMs. The paper also provides a thorough exploration of the properties of CKMs, and experiments that demonstrate the potential of the technique. Additionally, the authors provide an analysis of the results and discuss potential future directions for the research. The paper's main weakness is that it does not provide a detailed comparison of CKMs to other state-of-the-art methods for object recognition. While the results presented are promising, it would be beneficial to have a more comprehensive comparison to other methods. Additionally, the paper does not discuss any potential limitations of the technique, which could be addressed in future research.

Firstly, the paper by Pruning Filters for Efficient ConvNets is well-written and presents a clear methodology for pruning filters in deep convolutional neural networks (ConvNets). The paper explores the idea of reducing the computational cost of the ConvNets by eliminating the filters that are not necessary for the training of the network. One of the strengths of the paper is that it presents an extensive evaluation of the proposed approach on different datasets and architectures, showing promising results in terms of compression rate and accuracy. The authors provide a detailed analysis of the pruned models, showing that their approach allows to achieve a significant reduction in the number of filters, while preserving the accuracy of the original models. However, some points should be addressed to improve the quality of the paper. Firstly, the authors should provide more details on the rationale behind the choice of the hyperparameters used in their experiments, such as the threshold for the filter selection and the pruning rate. Secondly, it would be beneficial to provide a more in-depth discussion of the potential limitations and future directions of their approach. Overall, the proposed approach presented in this paper is interesting and provides an effective method for pruning filters in ConvNets. The evaluation results are promising, and the presented methodology is worth further exploration. Therefore, I would recommend the paper for publication, provided the authors address the above concerns. The paper "Pruning Filters for Efficient ConvNets" presents a novel approach to reducing the computational cost of convolutional neural networks. The problem the paper addresses is the high computational cost required to train and deploy convolutional neural networks (CNNs), which makes them unsuitable for some real-world applications. The authors propose an effective method for pruning the number of filters in a CNN during the training process to make it more computationally efficient. The strengths of this paper are numerous. Firstly, the authors thoroughly explain the concept of CNNs and the challenges faced in making them more efficient. They provide a comprehensive literature review and show how their proposed method differs from previous approaches. Additionally, the authors present a detailed explanation of their pruning algorithm and provide extensive experimental results on various datasets, demonstrating substantial improvements in computational efficiency without any significant loss in classification accuracy. However, there are some weaknesses in the paper that should be addressed. Firstly, the paper lacks clarity in some of its explanations. For example, the authors briefly mention some technical aspects of their pruning algorithm without providing enough detail for readers to fully understand the process. Additionally, while the authors claimed that their method can be applied to various CNN architectures, they only demonstrate the results on a few well-known architectures; therefore, a more comprehensive evaluation across different architectures is desirable. Overall, the paper presents a novel and effective method for reducing the computational cost of CNNs. However, some further explanations and a more comprehensive evaluation is needed to clarify some technical details and strengthen the generalizability of the proposed method. The paper "Pruning Filters for Efficient ConvNets" presents a novel acceleration method for Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) by pruning filters that have minor effects on output accuracy. The paper argues that magnitude-based pruning of weights cannot adequately reduce computation costs in the convolutional layers due to irregular sparsity in the pruned networks. The proposed filter pruning technique does not result in sparse connectivity patterns, making it suitable for existing efficient BLAS libraries for dense matrix multiplication. The paper provides a systematic experimental evaluation of the proposed filter pruning technique on VGG-16 and ResNet-110 models on CIFAR10 dataset. The results show significant improvements in computational efficiency (34% for VGG-16 and 38% for ResNet-110) while maintaining close to original accuracy. The experiments are thorough and well documented, noting the hyperparameters used to train the models and the evaluation metrics. The contributions in this paper are clearly presented and justified. The approach is both practical and effective, and the results are impressive. The paper is well written, and the authors have done a good job in explaining the proposed approach and its significance. However, the paper could be strengthened by providing a comparison with state-of-the-art pruning techniques in current literature. Overall, this paper is a valuable contribution to the field of CNNs and can be considered for publication with minor revisions. This paper addresses the challenge of reducing computational and parameter storage costs associated with Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs). The authors propose a method that prunes filters from CNNs, instead of weights, which are identified as having a small impact on the output accuracy. The significance of this research is that it offers an alternative approach to pruning weights that reduces the computational overhead significantly and maintains the original accuracy. One of the strengths of this paper is its clear presentation of the problem that the authors aim to solve, backed by a thorough literature review. The idea of filter pruning offers a unique and intuitive approach that emphasizes the importance of layer-wise pruning. Additionally, the authors provide detailed illustrations and algorithms for filter pruning, which makes the method easy to understand and replicate. Furthermore, the authors provide experimental results on the effectiveness of the proposed filter pruning method on both VGG-16 and ResNet-110 CNN architectures. This provides solid evidence for the practical application of the approach. The experiments demonstrate significant reductions in inference costs while keeping computational and storage efficiency. However, the paper is not without its weaknesses. While the authors provide a well-motivated and straightforward approach to filter pruning, more detailed analysis and implementation consideration are necessary to assess its performance in other domains. Another limitation is that the experiments only consider two CNN architectures for a specific application. It would be valuable to explore the application of the proposed method with other architectures and datasets. In conclusion, the proposed filter pruning method presents a novel approach to reduce computational and parameter storage costs by pruning filters from CNNs. The paper provides convincing evidence for the success of the approach on two architectures for CIFAR-10. Despite some limitations, this research has several strengths in its approach, presentation, and experimental results, making it a valuable contribution to the field of CNNs optimization.

Networks The paper “Implicit ReasoNet: Modeling Large-Scale Structured Relationships with Shared Memory Networks” presents a novel approach for modeling large-scale structured relationships using shared memory networks. The authors propose a novel memory-augmented neural network architecture called Implicit ReasoNet, which uses a shared memory network to represent complex relationships between entities. The authors demonstrate the effectiveness of this approach on two knowledge graph completion tasks, showing that Implicit ReasoNet outperforms existing methods. The paper is well-written and clearly organized. The authors provide a thorough introduction to the problem and a detailed description of the proposed architecture. The experiments are well-designed and the results are presented clearly. The authors also provide a thorough discussion of the implications of their work and potential future directions. Overall, the paper makes a convincing argument for the effectiveness of Implicit ReasoNet for modeling large-scale structured relationships. The proposed architecture is novel and provides a promising approach for addressing this problem. The authors provide a convincing demonstration of the effectiveness of the proposed approach, and the results are promising. The paper is well-written and clearly organized, and the authors provide a thorough discussion of the implications of their work and potential future directions. This paper addresses the problem of modeling large-scale structured relationships with shared memory. The authors propose a novel approach, Implicit ReasoNet, which combines deep learning and relational reasoning to learn the relationships between entities in a large-scale knowledge graph. The paper has several strengths. The authors clearly explain their proposed approach and provide a detailed description of the architecture, training process, and evaluation. Additionally, the authors demonstrate the effectiveness of their approach on several large-scale datasets, showing that it outperforms existing methods. However, there are also some weaknesses. The authors do not provide a detailed discussion of the limitations of their approach, which may make it difficult for readers to understand the potential applications of the method. Additionally, the authors do not provide a detailed comparison of their approach with existing methods, which could have provided further insight into the effectiveness of their approach. This paper presents an interesting approach to knowledge base completion, namely Implicit ReasoNets (IRNs). The authors demonstrate that IRNs are able to outperform previous approaches by more than 5.7% on the popular FB15k benchmark. The paper is well-written and the proposed approach is well-explained. The authors provide a detailed description of the proposed approach, which is based on a search controller and a shared memory. The authors explain how the search controller is used to perform multi-step inference and how the shared memory is jointly updated during training. The authors also provide a thorough description of the experiments conducted to evaluate the performance of the proposed approach. However, there are some areas that could be improved. For example, the authors could provide a more detailed description of the datasets used in the experiments. Additionally, the authors could provide a more detailed comparison of the proposed approach to existing approaches. Overall, this is an interesting paper and the authors have provided a thorough description of the proposed approach. The authors should consider the suggestions mentioned above to further improve the paper. This paper addresses the problem of knowledge base completion, the task of recovering missing relationships based on recorded relations. The paper proposes Implicit ReasoNets (IRNs), which is designed to perform large-scale inference implicitly through a search controller and shared memory. Strengths: The paper provides a novel approach to knowledge base completion, using a search controller and shared memory to perform inference. The proposed model is shown to outperform all previous approaches on the popular FB15k benchmark by more than 5.7%. Weaknesses: The paper does not provide any discussion of the scalability of the proposed model, or any comparison to other models in terms of scalability. Additionally, the paper does not provide any discussion of the potential applications of the proposed model.

This paper presents an analysis of the behavior of neural machine translation (NMT) models in terms of their ability to learn morphological features of languages. The authors use a number of experiments to determine the extent to which NMT models can learn morphological features, and compare the results to those obtained from a statistical machine translation (SMT) model. The authors find that NMT models can learn some morphological features, but not as effectively as the SMT model. This paper is well-written and well-structured. The authors provide a clear introduction to the topic, and provide a thorough discussion of the related work. The methodology used to test the NMT models is also clearly explained, and the authors provide a detailed analysis of the results. The paper also provides a clear conclusion and discussion of the implications of the results. The experiments conducted by the authors are well-designed, and the results are presented in a clear and concise manner. The authors provide a good comparison between the results obtained from the NMT and SMT models, and the results are discussed in depth. Overall, this paper is a well-written and well-structured paper, and provides a good analysis of the behavior of NMT models in terms of their ability to learn morphological features. The authors provide a thorough discussion of the related work, and the experiments and results are well-presented. This paper is a valuable contribution to the field of NMT research. This paper addresses the question of what neural machine translation models learn about morphology. The authors use an empirical approach to investigate the extent to which these models are able to learn the morphology of a language, and how the models’ performance is affected by the amount of training data. The paper’s strengths include its use of an empirical approach to investigate the question, its thorough analysis of the results, and its discussion of the implications of the results. In addition, the authors provide an extensive review of related work, which helps to place the research in context. The paper’s weaknesses include its lack of detail in the description of the models used and the data sets used. Additionally, the authors do not discuss the implications of their findings for the development of machine translation models. Furthermore, the paper does not address the question of whether the models are able to learn the morphology of a language without the aid of explicit morphological features. This paper presents an interesting analysis of what neural machine translation (MT) models learn about source and target languages during the training process. The authors investigate the quality of representations learned by neural MT models at various levels of granularity and evaluate their ability to capture word structure through extrinsic part-of-speech and morphological tagging tasks. The authors provide a thorough investigation along several parameters, such as word-based vs. character-based representations, depth of the encoding layer, the identity of the target language, and encoder vs. decoder representations. The paper is well-written and organized, providing a clear overview of the research and its findings. The authors have used a variety of methods to analyze the data and provide a comprehensive evaluation of the results. The authors also provide an insightful discussion of the implications of their findings and how they can be used to improve neural MT systems. The only suggestion I have for the authors is to provide more detail about the methods used to analyze the data. It would be helpful to have a better understanding of the techniques used to evaluate the quality of the representations and the methods used to measure the ability of the models to capture word structure. Overall, this is a well-researched and interesting paper. It provides a valuable insight into what neural MT models learn about source and target languages during the training process and provides a useful evaluation of the quality of the representations learned. I highly recommend it to anyone interested in understanding the inner workings of neural MT systems. This paper addresses the question of what neural machine translation (MT) models learn about source and target languages during the training process. The authors analyze the representations learned by neural MT models at various levels of granularity and evaluate the quality of the representations for learning morphology through extrinsic part-of-speech and morphological tagging tasks. The strength of this paper is that it provides a thorough investigation along several parameters, such as word-based vs. character-based representations, depth of the encoding layer, the identity of the target language, and encoder vs. decoder representations. The authors also provide a data-driven, quantitative evaluation that sheds light on important aspects in the neural MT system and its ability to capture word structure. A potential weakness of this paper is that it does not provide any discussion on the implications of the results for future research. Additionally, the authors could have further discussed the implications of their findings for the development of more efficient neural MT systems.

This paper proposes the new (to my knowledge) step of proposing to treat a number of sentence pair scoring tasks (e.g. Answer Set Scoring, RTE, Paraphrasing, among others) as instances of a more general task of understanding semantic relations between two sentences. Furthermore, they investigate the potential of learning generally- applicable neural network models for the family of tasks. I find this to be an exciting proposal that's worthy of both presentation at CoNLL and further discussion and investigation. The main problem I have with the paper is that it in fact feels unfinished. It should be accepted for publication only with the proviso that a number of updates will be made for the final version: 1 - the first results table needs to be completed 2 - given the large number of individual results, the written discussion of results is terribly short. Much more interpretation and discussion of the results is sorely needed. 3 - the abstract promises presentation of a new, more challenging dataset which the paper does not seem to deliver. This incongruity needs to be resolved. 4 - the results vary quite a bit across different tasks - could some investigation be made into how and why the models fail for some of the tasks, and how and why they succeed for others? Even if no solid answer is found, it would be interesting to hear the authors' position regarding whether this is a question of modeling or rather dissimilarity between the tasks. Does it really work to group them into a unified whole? 5 - please include example instances of the various datasets used, including both prototypical sentence pairs and pairs which pose problems for classification 6 - the Ubu. RNN transfer learning model is recommended for new tasks, but is this because of the nature of the data (is it a more general task) or rather the size of the dataset? How can we determine an answer to that question? Despite the unpolished nature of the paper, though, it's an exciting approach that could generate much interesting discussion, and I'd be happy to see it published IN A MORE FINISHED FORM. I do recognize that this view may not be shared by other reviewers! Some minor points about language: * "weigh" and "weighed" are consistently used in contexts that rather require "weight" and "weighted" * there are several misspellings of "sentence" (as "sentene") * what is "interpunction"? * one instance of "world overlap" instead of "word overlap"

Overall the paper is interesting, well written and clear. I have a couple of comments and suggestions. In the second paragraph of the introduction you mention that we need to understand the behavior of the network to debug it. Wouldn't it be easier to debug if we knew what the network was actually learning? For example, why did the training stop at a certain point? What were the learning curves like? Why did the loss curve have local minima? This type of information can help you understand what is going on in the model and thus help debug it. You could also try to link the behavior of the network to some specifics in the data. For example, is there a reason why the model sometimes learns to predict -1 and sometimes it predicts 0? Did the data change recently? Was it fit with the wrong parameters? You could try to figure out what is going on by looking at what the model is learning and the loss curves. If the loss went down but then started to increase again, it might be a indication that the model started overfitting the data. The results in the last paragraph: activation pattern, number of parameters, FLOPs. This can be moved to the supplemental material. I don't think it's necessary to explain what dropout is. The paper is already quite long, you can skip this. In the section about scaling the model to larger problems you mention hyperparameter optimization, but you don't actually show the result of hyperparameter optimization. The only hyperparameter mentioned in the text is the number of neurons in the hidden layer. As another suggestion, you could have tried other activation functions such as ReLu. It could be that none of them were able to find a minimum. This paper explores local minima in the training of deep networks. The authors propose a novel Hessian-based optimization method to detect and skip local minima, as local minima are not necessarily minima but can correspond to high local maxima. The method is simple, has memory and parallelizability advantages over conventional second-order methods, and is efficient in both simulation and real world datasets. A weakness of the method is that it can only test for global minima, not local minima, and it is unclear how well it performs on hard to train problems. The authors provide a promising new approach for monitoring training and finding good starting points for training. brainsci formation/question: What problem or question this paper addresses? The authors explore local minima in the training of deep networks. The authors propose a novel Hessian-based optimization method to detect and skip local minima, as local minima are not necessarily minima but can correspond to high local maxima. The method is simple, has memory and parallelizability advantages over conventional second-order methods, and is efficient in both simulation and real world datasets. The main weakness of the method is that it can only test for global minima, not local minima, and it is unclear how well it performs on hard to train problems. It would be interesting to know how well this method performs on hard to train problems. Strengths: The method is simple, has memory and parallelizability advantages over conventional second-order methods, and is efficient in both simulation and real world datasets. Weaknesses: It only tests for global minima, not local minima, and it is unclear how well it performs on hard to train problems. Dear Author, In the paper Local minima in training of deep networks, its main content is as below: There has been a lot of recent interest in trying to characterize the error surface of deep models. This stems from a long standing question. Given that deep networks are highly nonlinear systems optimized by local gradient methods, why do they not seem to be affected by bad local minima? It is widely believed that training of deep models using gradient methods works so well because the error surface either has no local minima, or if they exist they need to be close in value to the global minimum. It is known that such results hold under strong assumptions which are not satisfied by real models. In this paper we present examples showing that for such theorem to be true additional assumptions on the data, initialization schemes and/or the model classes have to be made. We look at the particular case of finite size datasets. We demonstrate that in this scenario one can construct counter-examples (datasets or initialization schemes) when the network does become susceptible to bad local minima over the weight space. I think the content is very interesting and certainly worthy of attention. The counter-examples provided in this case are well chosen. It is useful to have examples of networks (with various architectures, hyper-parameters and/or training datasets) that do exhibit bad local minima. The counter-example construction methodology can be used to define a “region of vulnerability” within the space of possible models. This should inform the search for good local minima. I look forward to seeing more papers from the authors on this topic. This paper looks at the local minima question for deep networks. Specifically, the authors construct a simple dataset and show that a deep network with a specific initialization and learning rule will get stuck in a local minimum that does not generalize well to new data. The exact method they use to construct the data and model is not too important, since the point is that it is easy to generate examples of finite size datasets where the method works. The key take away from this is that even though deep networks are trained using gradient-based methods, they are not always able to escape from bad local minima in training. The strength of this paper is that it is a well-crafted proof of concept that illustrates a specific weakness in the assumption that deep networks are trained only local minima, globally optimal. The work does also raise new questions about how the particular example constructed could be modified to not have this effect. Strengths: 1. Simple counterexample to a widely-believed assumption about local minima in deep learning training. 2. Demonstrates the care is needed in applying theory from statistical learning to deep learning applications. 3. Generalizes beyond the specific example used. Weaknesses: 1. Demonstrates a local minima in a specific local search algorithm, but not necessarily in general. 2. Some of the theory cannot be applied, e.g. the assumption that the dataset is generated from a probability distribution. 3. The example could be modified to work around the weaknesses in the first two points. Overall, a well-crafted example showing that deep learning is not always safe from local minima. This work raises important issues about the generalization of deep networks and how they are trained and how a theoretical framework like statistical learning can be applied.

Overall, the paper "Sarcasm SIGN: Interpreting Sarcasm with Sentiment Based Monolingual Machine Translation" is an interesting paper that studies a new sarcasm interpretation task. The major contributions of this work are: (1) introducing a new and large-scale sarcasm dataset, (2) proposing a sentiment based interpretation model for this task, (3) conducting an automatic metrics evaluation for the task, (4) and conducting human evaluation to compare different interpretation models. My only concern is that the "human evaluation" is based on a small number of samples. I recommend to expand the testing set or do human evaluation on a held out test set. Another point to consider is that the performance reported in this paper may not be sustainable in the long term. For example, the model that employs n-gram based language model in the start-of-the-sentence(SOS) position performs well in the automatic metrics evaluation stage, but its performance is generally below the basic MT model in human evaluation. I think the poor performance is mainly due to the overfitting to the training set. addressing the above two points, I recommend to include the following directions in this paper: 1. Expand the testing set or do human evaluation on a held out test set; 2. Evaluate the sustainability of the performance over time; 3. Extend this work to address the negative and ironical forms of sarcasm; 4. Incorporate other techniques to further improve the interpretation model.
