This paper presents a novel neural programming architecture that is able to generalize through recursion. The authors propose a recursive neural network (RNN) that is able to learn recursive functions, which can then be used to generalize to unseen data. The authors present an evaluation of the proposed architecture on a number of benchmark datasets and demonstrate its generalizability. The paper is well-written and the authors present their ideas in a clear and concise manner. The paper does a good job of explaining the concept of recursion and how it can be used to generalize neural programming architectures. The authors also provide a thorough evaluation of the proposed architecture on a number of benchmark datasets, which demonstrates its effectiveness. Overall, this paper presents a novel neural programming architecture that is able to generalize through recursion. The authors provide a clear explanation of the concept of recursion and how it can be used to generalize neural programming architectures. The evaluation of the proposed architecture on a number of benchmark datasets is thorough and demonstrates its effectiveness. I recommend this paper for publication. This paper addresses the problem of making neural programming architectures generalize better by using recursion. The authors propose a new approach to neural programming architectures that uses recursion to better generalize. Strengths: -The paper presents a novel approach to making neural programming architectures generalize better. -The authors provide a detailed explanation of their proposed approach and analyze the results of their experiments. -The paper includes a thorough discussion of related work in the field. Weaknesses: -The experiments are limited in scope and could benefit from a more comprehensive evaluation. -The paper could benefit from a more comprehensive discussion of the implications of their proposed approach. -The paper does not provide a clear conclusion or summary of the findings. This paper presents an interesting approach to improving the generalizability and interpretability of neural programming architectures by incorporating recursion. The authors demonstrate the effectiveness of their approach on four tasks: grade-school addition, bubble sort, topological sort, and quicksort. The authors also provide evidence that incorporating recursion into neural architectures can help to reduce the domain of each neural network component, making it easier to prove guarantees about the overall system’s behavior. The paper is well written and the authors provide a clear explanation of their approach. The experiments are well designed and the results are presented in an easy to understand way. The authors also provide a detailed discussion of their findings and implications. The authors could have done a better job of discussing the limitations of their approach. Additionally, it would have been useful to compare the results of their approach to other existing approaches to improving generalizability and interpretability in neural programming architectures. Overall, this is a well written and thought-provoking paper. The authors have presented an interesting approach to improving the generalizability and interpretability of neural programming architectures and have provided evidence that their method is effective. This paper addresses the problem of poor generalizability and difficulty in reasoning about the behavior of neural networks that attempt to learn programs from data. The authors propose augmenting neural architectures with recursion, which they demonstrate to be effective on four tasks: grade-school addition, bubble sort, topological sort, and quicksort. The paper's strengths include a clear and concise explanation of the problem and proposed solution, as well as a thorough description of the experiments and results. The authors also provide a compelling argument for the need to incorporate a concept like recursion in order for neural architectures to robustly learn program semantics. A potential weakness of the paper is that it does not provide a comprehensive discussion of related work in the field. Additionally, the experiments are limited to four tasks, so it is unclear to what extent the results can be generalized to other tasks.

This paper describes a new deterministic dependency parsing algorithm and analyses its behaviour across a range of languages. The core of the algorithm is a set of rules defining permitted dependencies based on POS tags. The algorithm starts by ranking words using a slightly biased PageRank over a graph with edges defined by the permitted dependencies. Stepping through the ranking, each word is linked to the closest word that will maintain a tree and is permitted by the head rules and a directionality constraint. Overall, the paper is interesting and clearly presented, though seems to differ only slightly from Sogaard (2012), "Unsupervised Dependency Parsing without Training". I have a few questions and suggestions: Head Rules (Table 1) - It would be good to have some analysis of these rules in relation to the corpus. For example, in section 3.1 the fact that they do not always lead to a connected graph is mentioned, but not how frequently it occurs, or how large the components typically are. I was surprised that head direction was chosen using the test data rather than training or development data. Given how fast the decision converges (10-15 sentences), this is not a major issue, but a surprising choice. How does tie-breaking for words with the same PageRank score work? Does it impact performance significantly, or are ties rare enough that it doesn't have an impact? The various types of constraints (head rules, directionality, distance) will lead to upper bounds on possible performance of the system. It would be informative to include oracle results for each constraint, to show how much they hurt the maximum possible score. That would be particularly helpful for guiding future work in terms of where to try to modify this system. Minor: - 4.1, "we obtain [the] rank" - Table 5 and Table 7 have columns in different orders. I found the Table 7 arrangement clearer. - 6.1, "isolate the [contribution] of both" The authors proposed an unsupervised algorithm for Universal Dependencies that does not require training. The tagging is based on PageRank for the words and a small amount of hard-coded rules. The article is well written, very detailed and the intuition behind all prior information being added to the model is explained clearly. I think that the contribution is substantial to the field of unsupervised parsing, and the possibilities for future work presented by the authors give rise to additional research. This paper presents a way to parse trees (namely the universal dependency treebanks) by relying only on POS and by using a modified version of the PageRank to give more way to some meaningful words (as opposed to stop words). This idea is interesting though very closed to what was done in SÃ¸gaard (2012)'s paper. The personalization factor giving more weight to the main predicate is nice but it would have been better to take it to the next level. As far as I can tell, the personalization is solely used for the main predicate and its weight of 5 seems arbitrary. Regarding the evaluation and the detailed analyses, some charts would have been beneficial, because it is sometimes hard to get the gist out of the tables. Finally, it would have been interesting to get the scores of the POS tagging in the prediction mode to be able to see if the degradation in parsing performance is heavily correlated to the degradation in tagging performance (which is what we expect). All in all, the paper is interesting but the increment over the work of SÃ¸gaard (2012) is small. Smaller issues: ------------------- l. 207 : The the main idea -> The main idea

This paper proposes an approach for multi-lingual named entity recognition using features from Wikipedia. By relying on a cross-lingual Wikifier, it identifies English Wikipedia articles for phrases in a target language and uses features based on the wikipedia entry. Experiments show that this new feature helps not only in the monolingual case, but also in the more interesting direct transfer setting, where the English model is tested on a target language. I liked this paper. It proposes a new feature for named entity recognition and conducts a fairly thorough set of experiments to show the utility of the feature. The analysis on low resource and the non-latin languages are particularly interesting. But what about named entities that are not on Wikipedia? In addition to the results in the paper, it would be interesting to see results on how these entities are affected by the proposed method. The proposed method is strongly dependent on the success of the cross-lingual wikifier. With this additional step in the pipeline, how often do we get errors in the prediction because of errors in the wikifier? Given the poor performance of direct transfer on Tamil and Bengali when lexical features are added, I wonder if it is possible to regularize the various feature classes differently, so that the model does not become over-reliant on the lexical features. This paper is concerned with cross-lingual direct transfer of NER models using a very recent cross-lingual wikification model. In general, the key idea is not highly innovative and creative, as it does not really propose any core new technology. The contribution is mostly incremental, and marries the two research paths: (1) direct transfer for downstream NLP tasks (such as NER, parsing, or POS tagging), and (2) very recent developments in the cross-lingual wikification technology. However, I pretty much liked the paper, as it is built on a coherent and clear story with enough experiments and empirical evidence to support its claims, with convincing results. I still have several comments concerning the presentation of the work. Related work: a more detailed description in related work on how this paper relates to work of Kazama and Torisawa (2007) is needed. It is also required to state a clear difference with other related NER system that in one way or another relied on the encyclopaedic Wikipedia knowledge. The differences are indeed given in the text, but they have to be further stressed to facilitate reading and placing the work in context. Although the authors argue why they decided to leave out POS tags as features, it would still be interesting to report experiments with POS tags features similar to Tackstrom et al.: the reader might get an overview supported by empirical evidence regarding the usefulness (or its lack) of such features for different languages (i.e., for the languages for which universal POS are available at least). Section 3.3 could contribute from a running example, as I am still not exactly sure how the edited model from Tsai and Roth works now (i.e., the given description is not entirely clear). Since the authors mention several times that the approaches from Tackstrom et al. (2012) and Nothman et al. (2012) are orthogonal to theirs and that they can be combined with the proposed approach, it would be beneficial if they simply reported some preliminary results on a selection of languages using the combination of the models. It will add more flavour to the discussion. Along the same line, although I do acknowledge that this is also orthogonal approach, why not comparing with a strong projection baseline, again to put the results into more even more context, and show the usefulness (or limitations) of wikification-based approaches. Why is Dutch the best training language for Spanish, and Spanish the best language for Yoruba? Only a statistical coincidence or something more interesting is going on there? A paragraph or two discussing these results in more depth would be quite interesting. Although the idea is sound, the results from Table 4 are not that convincing with only small improvements detected (and not in all scenarios). A statistical significance test reported for the results from Table 4 could help support the claims. Minor comments: - Sect. 2.1: Projection can also be performed via methods that do not require parallel data, which makes such models more widely applicable (even for languages that do not have any parallel resources): e.g., see the work of Peirsman and Pado (NAACL 2009) or Vulic and Moens (EMNLP 2013) which exploit bilingual semantic spaces instead of direct alignment links to perform the transfer. - Several typos detected in the text, so the paper should gain quite a bit from a more careful proofreading (e.g., first sentence of Section 3: "as a the base model"; This sentence is not 'parsable', Page 3: "They avoid the traditional pipeline of NER then EL by...", "to disambiguate every n-grams" on Page 8)

The authors propose to use deep learning techniques in order to predict future frames of video sequences, which is an interesting problem with many applications. In particular they focus on using LSTM networks (Long Short Term Memory) that are able to learn long-term dependencies between successive frames. They show how this can be used both as a prediction model or also as unsupervised feature extraction method by training it directly from raw data without any labels. I think it's very well written but I would have liked more details about some aspects such as: How do you deal with overfitting? How does your approach compare against other state-of-the-art methods? Overall I'm positive towards publication after minor revisions. The manuscript has been revised according to comments received during the first round of reviews The authors propose Deep Predictive Coding Network (DPCN) to predict future frames in video sequences with high accuracy. They also show that DPCNs can be used as an unsupervised learning method on natural images without any ground truth labels. This is very interesting work which has potential applications both in computer vision research area and industry. The main contribution of this paper lies in: 1). proposing new network architecture; 2). showing its effectiveness through experiments. However there are some issues I would like them to address before publication. 1. In Figure 3 , it seems that the proposed model outperforms other methods significantly when predicting next frame from previous two frames only. However if we use more than three consecutive frames together, performance drops dramatically compared to LSTM based models such as RNNs. This may indicate that using too many past information at once might not help improve prediction results much but rather hurt our final goal - making accurate predictions about future events. 2. It should be noted that although they claim their approach does not require training data, all experimental settings still rely heavily on pre-trained networks trained on ImageNet dataset. This work presents interesting ideas on how one could use prediction-based methods to perform unsupervised learning. The authors present some compelling experimental evidence supporting their claims. The manuscript would benefit if it were more self-contained; there seems to be no clear explanation why they chose certain architectures over others or what exactly was done during experiments such as "pose estimation". This makes it hard to assess whether all necessary details needed to reproduce the presented results are available. In addition, I think the discussion section should include references to related works which might help put the current contribution into context. Overall, it's a good start but needs further development before publication. 3 stars ************************** Authors: Please revise your submission based on our comments above! Thank you The authors present their work on Deep Predictive Coding Networks: A novel approach towards unsupervised learning via predicting future frames in a video stream. The proposed method has several advantages over existing approaches such as: 1) It does not require any labeled data during training; 2) it allows one to infer information regarding the underlying motion dynamics; 3) it scales well even when applied to real-world datasets containing large number of images/video sequences. In addition, they provide extensive experiments demonstrating state-of-the-art performance across different benchmarks including rendered scenes, car mounted cameras etc... Overall I think it's very interesting research which will be highly cited once published! Strengths include: Weaknesses: 1. There seems no discussion whether there exists some other better way than CNNs to achieve similar goals?

The paper presents a method for learning symmetric collaborative dialogue agents with dynamic knowledge graph embeddings. The method uses a dynamic knowledge graph to represent the relationship between dialogue states and the corresponding utterances. The dynamic knowledge graph is then used to learn embeddings for the dialogue states and utterances. The embeddings are used to represent the dialogue states and utterances in a collaborative dialogue system. The paper presents experimental results that show that the proposed method is effective in learning symmetric collaborative dialogue agents with dynamic knowledge graph embeddings. This paper addresses the problem of learning symmetric collaborative dialogue agents with dynamic knowledge graph embeddings. The strengths of this paper are that it proposes a new approach for learning symmetric collaborative dialogue agents with dynamic knowledge graph embeddings, and that it evaluates the proposed approach on a large-scale corpus of human-written dialogue data. The weaknesses of this paper are that it does not include a detailed comparison of the proposed approach to other state-of-the-art dialogue systems, and that it does not explore the impact of the dynamic knowledge graph embeddings on the performance of the dialogue system. This paper proposes a symmetric collaborative dialogue system in which two agents, each with private knowledge, must strategically communicate to achieve a common goal. The dialogue state in this setting is open-ended and poses new challenges for existing dialogue systems. The paper collects a dataset of 11K human-human dialogues, which exhibits interesting lexical, semantic, and strategic elements. To model both structured knowledge and unstructured language, the paper proposes a neural model with dynamic knowledge graph embeddings that evolve as the dialogue progresses. Automatic and human evaluations show that the model is both more effective at achieving the goal and more human-like than baseline neural and rule-based models. The results are interesting and the paper is well-written. However, there are some points that need to be improved: 1. It is not clear how the proposed model is different from other neural models for dialogue systems. It would be helpful to have a more thorough comparison with existing work. 2. The experimental results are not fully convincing. It is not clear why the proposed model is more effective than other neural models, especially when there is no human evaluation to back up the claim. 3. The human evaluation is not well-designed. It would be helpful to have a more thorough evaluation of the proposed model's performance in terms of its ability to understand and respond to dialogue. This paper addresses the problem of symmetric collaborative dialogue, in which two agents with private knowledge must strategically communicate to achieve a common goal. The open-ended dialogue state in this setting poses new challenges for existing dialogue systems. To address this problem, the paper proposes a neural model with dynamic knowledge graph embeddings that evolve as the dialogue progresses. Strengths: The paper proposes a new approach to symmetric collaborative dialogue, which is a difficult problem in dialogue systems. The approach is evaluated on a new dataset of 11K human-human dialogues, which exhibits interesting lexical, semantic, and strategic elements. The results show that the proposed model is more effective at achieving the goal and more human-like than baseline neural and rule-based models. Weaknesses: The evaluation of the proposed model is limited to a single task. It is not clear how well the model would perform on other tasks or in different dialogue settings. There is also no comparison to other recent dialogue systems, which could have helped to better evaluate the proposed model.

This paper presents an exploration of the application of deep learning for supervised learning problems. The authors provide a comprehensive overview of the current state of deep learning, exploring the various types of deep learning models and their applications. They also discuss the challenges and opportunities that deep learning presents and how it can be used to solve supervised learning problems. The paper is well-structured and clearly written. It provides a thorough overview of the current state of deep learning and its various applications. The authors have done an excellent job of summarizing the various types of deep learning models and their applications. They have also provided an in-depth discussion of the challenges and opportunities that deep learning presents. The paper is well-researched and the authors have provided a comprehensive review of the relevant literature. They have also included a number of examples to illustrate their points. The paper is also well-referenced, with a wide range of sources cited. Overall, this is an excellent paper that provides a comprehensive overview of the current state of deep learning and its applications. It is well-structured and clearly written, and the authors have done an excellent job of summarizing the various types of deep learning models and their applications. The paper is well-referenced and provides a comprehensive review of the relevant literature. I recommend this paper for publication. This paper examines the application of deep learning for supervised learning problems. Deep learning is a subset of machine learning that is based on artificial neural networks. The paper discusses the advantages and disadvantages of using deep learning for supervised learning problems. It also provides a review of recent research in this area and outlines the potential applications of deep learning in supervised learning. Strengths: The paper provides a comprehensive review of the current state of deep learning for supervised learning problems. It discusses the advantages and disadvantages of using deep learning for supervised learning problems, and provides a clear outline of potential applications. The paper also provides a good overview of recent research in this area. Weaknesses: The paper does not provide any concrete examples of how deep learning can be used for supervised learning problems. Additionally, the paper does not discuss any potential limitations of deep learning for supervised learning problems. This paper presents a comprehensive analysis of deep neural networks (DNNs) across multiple domains and their effectiveness. The authors provide insights into the characteristics of various DNNs, and explore the application of meta-learning to the problem of architecture ranking. The authors demonstrate that by using topological features and modeling the changes in its weights, biases, and activation functions layers of the initial training steps, they are able to rank architectures based on their predicted performance. The paper is well-written and organized. The authors provide a clear explanation of the methods and results. The authors also provide a thorough discussion of the implications of the results, and their contributions to the field. The authors have done an excellent job of exploring the application of deep learning for supervised learning problems. The paper provides a valuable contribution to the field and should be of interest to researchers in the field of deep learning. However, the authors could have provided more detail on the datasets used in the experiments and the results of the experiments. Additionally, the authors could have provided more discussion on the potential applications of their findings. This paper addresses the problem of exploring the application of deep learning for supervised learning problems. It examines a large set of deep neural networks across multiple domains and analyzes their characteristics, as well as the general effect they may have on performance. The authors then explore the application of meta-learning to the problem of architecture ranking, demonstrating that by using topological features and modeling the changes in its weights, biases and activation functions layers of the initial training steps, they are able to rank architectures based on their predicted performance. The paper's strengths include its comprehensive analysis of deep neural networks across multiple domains and its exploration of meta-learning for architecture ranking. Additionally, the authors provide a clear explanation of their methodology, which is well-supported by relevant data and research. The paper's main weakness is that the results of the analysis and exploration are not discussed in detail. The authors should provide more insight into the implications of their findings and suggest potential applications of their work. Additionally, the authors should discuss possible limitations of their approach and offer suggestions for future research.

- Strengths: A nice, solid piece of work that builds on previous studies in a productive way. Well-written and clear. - Weaknesses: Very few--possibly avoid some relatively "empty" statements: 191 : For example, if our task is to identify words used similarly across contexts, our scoring function can be specified to give high scores to terms whose usage is similar across the contexts. 537 : It is educational to study how annotations drawn from the same data are similar or different. - General Discussion: In the first sections I was not sure that much was being done that was new or interesting, as the methods seemed very reminiscent of previous methods used over the past 25 years to measure similarity, albeit with a few new statistical twists, but conceptually in the same vein. Section 5, however, describes an interesting and valuable piece of work that will be useful for future studies on the topic. In retrospect, the background provided in sections 2-4 is useful, if not necessary, to support the experiments in section 5. In short, the work and results described will be useful to others working in this area, and the paper is worthy of presentation at ACL. Minor comments: Word, punctuation missing? 264 : For word annotations, we used PPMI, SVD, and SGNS (skipgram with negative sampling from Mikolov et al. (2013b)) word vectors released by Hamilton et al. (2016). Unclear what "multiple methods" refers to : 278 : some words were detected by multiple methods with CCLA This paper propose a general framework for analyzing similarities and differences in term meaning and representation in different contexts. - Strengths: * The framework proposed in this paper is generalizable and can be applied to different applications, and accommodate difference notation of context, different similarity functions, different type of word annotations. * The paper is well written. Very easy to follow. - Weaknesses: * I have concerns in terms of experiment evaluation. The paper uses qualitative evaluation metrics, which makes it harder to evaluate the effectiveness, or even the validity of proposed method. For example, table 1 compares the result with Hamilton et, al using different embedding vector by listing top 10 words that changed from 1900 to 1990. It's hard to tell, quantitatively, the performances of CCLA. The same issue also applies to experiment 2 (comparative lexical analysis over context). The top 10 words may be meaningful, but what about top 20, 100? what about the words that practitioner actually cares? Without addressing the evaluation issue, I find it difficult to claim that CCLA will benefit downstream applications.

Sorry to pop up late, but I've been looking over ICLR accepted papers, and I noticed this one. Something very similar, by combining word and character information at the feature level using sigmoid gating, has been done before, see The consensus amongst reviewers is that this paper's proposal for combining character level information with word-level information is sound, well presented, and well evaluated. The main negative sentiment was that the approach was perhaps a little increment, although the conceptual size of the increment was sufficient to warrant publication, and will be relevant to the interests of ICLR attendees working on the intersection of deep learning and NLP. After a cursory reading of the paper, I see no reason to disagree, and recommend acceptance. This paper proposes a new gating mechanism to combine word and character representations. The proposed model sets a new state-of-the-art on the CBT dataset; the new gating mechanism also improves over scalar gates without linguistic features on SQuAD and a twitter classification task. Intuitively, the vector-based gate working better than the scalar gate is unsurprising, as it is more similar to LSTM and GRU gates. The real contribution of the paper for me is that using features such as POS tags and NER help learn better gates. The visualization in Figure 3 and examples in Table 4 effectively confirm the utility of these features, very nice! In sum, while the proposed gate is nothing technically groundbreaking, the paper presents a very focused contribution that I think will be useful to the NLP community. Thus, I hope it is accepted. I think the problem here is well motivated, the approach is insightful and intuitive, and the results are convincing of the approach (although lacking in variety of applications). I like the fact that the authors use POS and NER in terms of an intermediate signal for the decision. Also they compare against a sufficient range of baselines to show the effectiveness of the proposed model. I am also convinced by the authors' answers to my question, I think there is sufficient evidence provided in the results to show the effectiveness of the inductive bias introduced by the fine-grained gating model. SUMMARY. The paper proposes a gating mechanism to combine word embeddings with character-level word representations. The gating mechanism uses features associated to a word to decided which word representation is the most useful. The fine-grain gating is applied as part of systems which seek to solve the task of cloze-style reading comprehension question answering, and Twitter hashtag prediction. For the question answering task, a fine-grained reformulation of gated attention for combining document words and questions is proposed. In both tasks the fine-grain gating helps to get better accuracy, outperforming state-of-the-art methods on the CBT dataset and performing on-par with state-of-the-art approach on the SQuAD dataset. ---------- OVERALL JUDGMENT This paper proposes a clever fine-grained extension of a scalar gate for combining word representation. It is clear and well written. It covers all the necessary prior work and compares the proposed method with previous similar models. I liked the ablation study that shows quite clearly the impact of individual contributions. And I also liked the fact that some (shallow) linguistic prior knowledge e.g., pos tags ner tags, frequency etc. has been used in a clever way. It would be interesting to see if syntactic features can be helpful. Sorry to pop up late, but I've been looking over ICLR accepted papers, and I noticed this one. - Strengths: - The paper is clearly written and well-structured. - The system newly applied several techniques including global optimization to end-to-end neural relation extraction, and the direct incorporation of the parser representation is interesting. - The proposed system has achieved the state-of-the-art performance on both ACE05 and CONLL04 data sets. - The authors include several analyses. - Weaknesses: - The approach is incremental and seems like just a combination of existing methods. - The improvements on the performance (1.2 percent points on dev) are relatively small, and no significance test results are provided. - General Discussion: - Major comments: - The model employed a recent parser and glove word embeddings. How did they affect the relation extraction performance? - In prediction, how did the authors deal with illegal predictions? - Minor comments: - Local optimization is not completely "local". It "considers structural correspondences between incremental decisions," so this explanation in the introduction is misleading. - Points in Figures 6 and 7 should be connected with straight lines, not curves. - How are entities represented in "-segment"? - Some citations are incomplete. Kingma et al. (2014) is accepted to ICLR, and Li et al. (2014) misses pages.

Title: "Massively Multilingual Word Embeddings" Authors: John Doe, Jane Smith, and Mark Lee Summary: The paper presents a novel approach for creating massively multilingual word embeddings by leveraging a large corpus of text from multiple languages. The proposed approach involves training a single model on a concatenated corpus of text in multiple languages, followed by a language-adaptive projection of embeddings to preserve the inherent semantic and syntactic properties of words across different languages. The authors demonstrate the effectiveness of their approach through several experiments on word similarity and word translation tasks in multiple languages. Strengths: This paper presents a well-structured and well-written approach to generate massively multilingual word embeddings that are robust and effective in preserving semantic and syntactic similarities and differences of words across languages. The proposed approach incorporates a language-adaptive projection of embeddings that further enhances the performance of word similarity and translation tasks. The authors have conducted thorough experiments on various datasets and languages to show the effectiveness of their approach. Weaknesses: The study lacks a thorough comparison with other state-of-the-art multilingual word embedding models. The authors have only compared their approach with a few baseline models from previous studies. Additionally, the paper could benefit from a more detailed description of the proposed approach, as some technical details are not well explained. Suggestions: To strengthen the paper, the authors should conduct a more detailed comparison of their approach with other state-of-the-art multilingual word embedding models. They should also provide more technical details to make it easier for readers to reproduce the results. Finally, the paper could benefit from a more in-depth analysis of why the proposed approach works well and how it can be applied in other NLP tasks beyond word similarity and translation. Overall, I found this paper to be an interesting and valuable contribution to the field of NLP, and the proposed approach has the potential to improve the performance of multilingual NLP tasks. The paper "Massively Multilingual Word Embeddings" addresses the problem of developing word embeddings for multiple languages at once. This is a challenging task because each language has its own unique linguistic and syntactic features that need to be accounted for in order to develop accurate word embeddings. Previous approaches have either focused on developing embeddings for individual languages or using a combination of translation and alignment techniques, which can be inefficient and fail to capture the linguistic complexity of each language. The authors propose a novel method for developing word embeddings that takes into account the similarities and differences between languages. They use a neural network-based approach that models the underlying linguistic structure of each language and applies a multilingual objective function to learn shared representations across languages. The authors also make their pre-trained embeddings available for download, which is a valuable resource for the research community. One strength of the paper is its thorough evaluation of the proposed method on several benchmark datasets. The results show that their approach outperforms previous state-of-the-art methods in terms of accuracy and efficiency. The authors also discuss the limitations of their approach, such as the trade-off between model complexity and performance, which is important for future research. One weakness of the paper is that it relies heavily on the choice of hyperparameters, such as the size of the network and the learning rate, and there is little guidance on how to choose these values for different languages. Additionally, the evaluation focuses on word similarity tasks and does not test the embeddings on downstream NLP tasks, such as sentiment analysis or machine translation. Overall, "Massively Multilingual Word Embeddings" presents a promising approach to developing word embeddings for multiple languages. The paper provides valuable insights into the challenges of multilingual embedding learning and demonstrates the effectiveness of their proposed method. The availability of pre-trained embeddings is a valuable asset for the research community, and further research on how to generalize this approach to other languages and tasks would be valuable. Dear Editor, I have reviewed the paper titled "Massively Multilingual Word Embeddings". The authors introduce novel methods for estimating and evaluating word embeddings in over fifty languages in a shared embedding space. The authors use multiCluster and multiCCA estimation methods that utilize monolingual data and dictionaries instead of parallel data. They also introduce a state-of-the-art evaluation method, multiQVECCCA, which produces better correlations with downstream tasks, such as text categorization and parsing. Overall, the paper is well-written and structured, and the research is novel and significant. The use of monolingual data and dictionaries instead of parallel data is significant as parallel data is not always available for all languages. Additionally, the multiQVECCCA evaluation method outperforms previous methods and is critical for understanding the effectiveness of the embeddings on downstream tasks. However, there are a few points that need to be addressed. First, the paper briefly introduces the web portal but does not provide enough details about it. It would be helpful if the authors expand on the web portal and its features. Secondly, the authors should provide more information about the open-source releases of their methods. Specifically, how to obtain and use these releases should be provided. Lastly, the authors should provide a more detailed comparison between their methods and previous methods. Apart from these minor issues, the paper is significant and well-done, and I recommend it for publication. Sincerely, [Your Name] The paper "Massively Multilingual Word Embeddings" presents novel approaches for estimating and evaluating word embeddings in more than 50 languages in a single shared embedding space. Specifically, the paper proposes two estimation methods (multiCluster and multiCCA) that rely on dictionaries and monolingual data without the need for parallel data, as well as a new evaluation method (multiQVECCCA) that outperforms previous ones in downstream tasks such as text categorization and parsing. Strengths: - The paper addresses an important problem of multilingual natural language processing, particularly in the area of word embeddings, which is still an ongoing research topic. - The proposed methods rely on existing resources such as monolingual data and dictionaries, which makes them easily applicable to a wide range of languages, including low-resource ones. - The evaluation of the proposed methods is thorough and systematic, and the proposed evaluation method outperforms previous ones. - The paper provides a web portal and open-source releases of all the methods, which will facilitate further research in this area. Weaknesses: - The paper lacks a clear explanation of how the proposed methods compare to existing state-of-the-art methods. Though the paper compares the proposed methods to previous works in evaluation, a comparison of the proposed estimation methods to existing ones would provide a more comprehensive understanding of their effectiveness. - The experiment design lacks a clear justification for the number of languages selected for the evaluation. - The paper could benefit from a more detailed discussion of the limitations and potential biases of the proposed methods. Overall, the paper significantly contributes to the field of multilingual word embeddings by proposing novel methods for estimation and evaluation.

- Strengths: The paper proposes an end-to-end neural model for semantic graph parsing, based on a well-designed transition system. The work is interesting, learning semantic representations of DMRS, which is capable of resolving semantics such as scope underspecification. This work shows a new scheme for computational semantics, benefiting from an end-to-end transition-based incremental framework, which resolves the parsing with low cost. - Weaknesses: My major concern is that the paper only gives a very common introduction for the definition of DMRS and EP, and the example even makes me a little confused because I cannot see anything special for DMRS. The description can be a little more detailed, I think. However, upon the space limitation, it is understandable. The same problem exists for the transition system of the parsing model. If I do not have any background of MRS and EP, I can hardly learn something from the paper, just seeing that this paper is very good. - General Discussion: Overall, this paper is very interesting to me. I like the DMRS for semantic parsing very much and like the paper very much. Hope that the open-source codes and datasets can make this line of research being a hot topic.

This paper shows: 1. Easy, constructive proofs to derive e-error upper-bounds on neural networks with O(log 1/e) layers and O(log 1/e) ReLU units. 2. Extensions of the previous results to more general function classes, such as smooth or vector-valued functions. 3. Lower bounds on the neural network size, as a function of its number of layers. The lower bound reveals the need of exponentially many more units to approximate functions using shallow architectures. The paper is well written and easy to follow. The technical content, including the proofs in the Appendix, look correct. Although the proof techniques are simple (and are sometimes modifications of arguments by Gil, Telgarsky, or Dasgupta), they are brought together in a coherent manner to produce sharp results. Therefore, I am leaning toward acceptance. The paper makes a solid technical contribution in proving that the deep networks are exponentially more efficient in function approximation compared to the shallow networks. They take the case of piecewise smooth networks, which is practically motivated (e.g. images have edges with smooth regions), and analyze the size of both the deep and shallow networks required to approximate it to the same degree. The reviewers recommend acceptance of the paper and I am happy to go with their recommendation. SUMMARY This paper contributes to the description and comparison of the representational power of deep vs shallow neural networks with ReLU and threshold units. The main contribution of the paper is to show that approximating a strongly convex differentiable function is possible with much less units when using a network with one more hidden layer. PROS The paper presents an interesting combination of tools and arrives at a nice result on the exponential superiority of depth. CONS The main result appears to address only strongly convex univariate functions. SPECIFIC COMMENTS - Thanks for the comments on L. Still it would be a good idea to clarify this point as far as possible in the main part. Also, I would suggest to advertise the main result more prominently. I still have not read the revision and maybe you have already addressed some of these points there. - The problem statement is close to that from [Montufar, Pascanu, Cho, Bengio NIPS 2014], which specifically arrives at exponential gaps between deep and shallow ReLU networks, albeit from a different angle. I would suggest to include that paper it in the overview. - In Lemma 3, there is an i that should be x - In Theorem 4, ``\tilde f'' is missing the (x). - Theorem 11, the lower bound always increases with L ? - In Theorem 11, \bf x\in [0,1]^d? The main contribution of this paper is a construction to eps-approximate a piecewise smooth function with a multilayer neural network that uses O(log(1/eps)) layers and O(poly log(1/eps)) hidden units where the activation functions can be either ReLU or binary step or any combination of them. The paper is well written and clear. The arguments and proofs are easy to follow. I only have two questions: 1- It would be great to have similar results without binary step units. To what extent do you find the binary step unit central to the proof? 2- Is there an example of piecewise smooth function that requires at least poly(1/eps) hidden units with a shallow network? This paper shows: 1. Easy, constructive proofs to derive e-error upper-bounds on neural networks with O(log 1/e) layers and O(log 1/e) ReLU units. 2. Extensions of the previous results to more general function classes, such as smooth or vector-valued functions. 3. Lower bounds on the neural network size, as a function of its number of layers. The lower bound reveals the need of exponentially many more units to approximate functions using shallow architectures. The paper is well written and easy to follow. The technical content, including the proofs in the Appendix, look correct. Although the proof techniques are simple (and are sometimes modifications of arguments by Gil, Telgarsky, or Dasgupta), they are brought together in a coherent manner to produce sharp results. Therefore, I am leaning toward acceptance. This paper shows: 1. Easy, constructive proofs to derive e-error upper-bounds on neural networks with O(log 1/e) layers and O(log 1/e) ReLU units. 2. Therefore, I am leaning toward acceptance. The reviewers recommend acceptance of the paper and I am happy to go with their recommendation. The paper "Counterpoint by Convolution" presents a new method for creating counterpoint in the style of Bach. The author has done an excellent job of explaining the new method and how it works. The results are very promising and the author should continue to develop this work. This paper addresses the problem of how to efficiently implement aconvolutional neural network (CNN) on a GPU. The authors present a novelalgorithm that uses a counterpoint-based convolution, which is shown tohave superior performance and memory efficiency compared to existingmethods. Strengths: The paper presents a novel algorithm that is shown to have superiorperformance and memory efficiency compared to existing methods. The authorsclearly explain the motivation behind their approach and how it differsfrom existing methods. The paper is well-written and easy to follow. Weaknesses: The experimental results are not as thorough as they could be. The authorsonly compare their algorithm to one other method, which is not enoughto show that their approach is truly superior. The paper would benefitfrom more extensive experimental results and comparisons. I have read the paper "Counterpoint by Convolution" and found that it has some interesting ideas about using blocked Gibbs sampling to improve the quality of samples from a convolutional neural network (CNET). The main contribution of the paper is the introduction of Coconet, a generative model that uses a blocked Gibbs sampling procedure to generate music in a nonlinear fashion, similar to how humans compose music. The paper presents evidence that the blocked Gibbs approach significantly improves sample quality compared to the ancestral sampling procedure used in previous NADE models. This is attributed to some conditional distributions being poorly modeled by the ancestral sampling procedure. The paper also shows that even a cheap approximate blocked Gibbs procedure can yield better samples than ancestral sampling. I found the paper to be well-written and the results to be interesting and promising. However, there are some aspects of the paper that could be improved. First, it is not clear how the blocked Gibbs procedure is implemented in practice. It would be helpful to have more details about how the procedure is performed, especially for those who are not familiar with the NADE framework. Second, it is not clear why the blocked Gibbs procedure improves sample quality. It would be helpful to have more details about why the conditional distributions are poorly modeled by the ancestral sampling procedure and how the blocked Gibbs procedure addresses this issue. Third, the paper only compares the blocked Gibbs procedure to the ancestral sampling procedure in the NADE framework. It would be helpful to have a comparison with other generative models, such as the GAN, to see how the Coconet performs in comparison. Overall, I found the paper to be an interesting contribution to the field of music generation, and I look forward to seeing more work from the authors in the future. This paper addresses the problem of generating high-quality music using deep learning techniques. The main strength of this paper is its originality. The authors introduce a new model called Coconet, which generates music in a nonlinear fashion, similar to how human composers write music. This is a novel approach to music generation, and the authors provide evidence that it yields better samples than previous methods. Another strength of this paper is its thorough experimental evaluation. The authors evaluate their model on a variety of tasks, including unconditioned polyphonic music generation, and provide evidence that it performs well across a range of music generation tasks. However, there are some weaknesses in this paper. First, the authors do not provide a clear explanation of how their model works. While they do provide some explanation of the key components of their model, they do not provide a detailed overview of how these components work together to generate music. Second, the authors do not provide a comprehensive evaluation of their model. While they do evaluate their model on a range of tasks, they do not provide a thorough evaluation of its performance across a wide range of music generation tasks.

This paper proposes a joint model of salient phrase selection and discourse relation prediction in spoken meeting. Experiments using meeting corpora show that the proposed model has higher performance than the SVM-based classifier. - Strengths: The paper is written to be easy to read. Technical details are described fully, and high performance is also shown in experimental evaluation. It also shows useful comparisons with related research in the field of discourse structure analysis and key phrase identification. It is interesting to note that not only the performance evaluation of phrase selection from discourse, discourse relation labeling, and summary generation as their applications, but also application to the prediction of the consistency of understanding by team members is also verified . - Weaknesses: Jointly Modeling salient phrase extraction and discourse relationship labeling between speaker turns has been proposed. If intuitive explanation about their interactivity and the usefulness of considering it is fully presented. - General Discussion: SVM-based classifier is set as a comparative method in the experiment. It would be useful to mention the validity of the setting.

The paper describes a novel technique to improve the efficiency of computation graphs in deep learning frameworks. An impressive speedup can be observed in their implementation within TensorFlow. The content is presented with sufficient clarity, although some more graphical illustrations could be useful. This work is relevant in order to achieve highest performance in neural network training. Pros: - significant speed improvements through dynamic batching - source code provided Cons: - the effect on a large real-world (ASR, SMT) would allow the reader to put the improvements better into context - presentation/vizualisation can be improved Adds \iclrfinalcopy and a link to the github repo which is now live All reviewers viewed the paper favorably as a nice/helpful contribution to the implementation of this important class of methods. I've just uploaded a minor revision in response to reviewer comments, in particular clarifying the intent of sections 3 and 3.5.1 with new/revised initial paragraphs. This pushed us a bit over the page limit so I moved the code example from 3.4 into an appendix per AnonReviewer1's suggestion. Thanks again AnonReviewers for your time and attention, I think this version is certainly an improvement over the original draft. Authors describe implementation of TensorFlow Fold which allows one to run various computations without modifying computation graph. They achieve this by creating a generic scheduler as a TensorFlow computation graph, which can accept graph description as input and execute it. They show clear benefits to this approach for tasks where computation changes for each datapoint, such as the case with TreeRNN. In the experiments, they compare against having static batch (same graph structure repeated many times) and batch size 1. The reason my score is 7 and not higher is because they do not provide comparison to the main alternative of their method -- someone could create a new TensorFlow graph for each dynamic batch. In other words, instead of using their graph as the scheduling algorithm, one could explicitly create each non-uniform batch as a TensorFlow graph, and run that using standard TensorFlow. The paper presents a novel strategy to deal with dynamic computation graphs. They arise, when the computation is dynamically influenced by the input data, such as in LSTMs. The authors propose an `unrolling' strategy over the operations done at every step, which allows a new kind of batching of inputs. The presented idea is novel and the results clearly indicate the potential of the approach. For the sake of clarity of the presentation I would drop parts of Section 3 ("A combinator library for neural networks") which presents technical details that are in general interesting, but do not help the understanding of the core idea of the paper. The presented experimental results on the "Stanford Sentiment Treebank" are in my opinion not supporting the claim of the paper, which is towards speed, than a little bit confusing. It is important to point out that even though the presented ensemble "[...] variant sets a new state-of-the-art on both subtasks" [p. 8], this is not due to the framework, not even due to the model (comp. lines 4 and 2 of Tab. 2), but probably, and this can only be speculated about, due to the ensemble averaging. I would appreciate a clearer argumentation in this respect. Update on Jan. 17th: after the authors update for their newest revision, I increase my rating to 8 due to the again improved, now very clear argumentation. The content is presented with sufficient clarity, although some more graphical illustrations could be useful. This work is relevant in order to achieve highest performance in neural network training.
